# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Modes of Data Communication

Data communication between two devices (DTE ŌĆō Data Terminal Equipment) can be classified into **three canonical modes** based on the directionality of information flow:

| # | Mode | Direction | Reverse Path? | Real-world Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simplex (SX)** | A ŌåÆ B only | ŌØī No | TV broadcast, radio, keyboard |
| 2 | **Half-Duplex (HDX)** | A Ōåö B (alternating) | Ō£ģ Yes, but **not simultaneously** | Walkie-Talkie, CB radio, old Ethernet |
| 3 | **Full-Duplex (FDX)** | A Ōćä B (concurrent) | Ō£ģ Yes, **simultaneously** | Telephone, modern Ethernet, fiber links |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 1 Anchor Concept:** The classification of communication modes into Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex is a **high-weightage topic** in university exams. Always quote the direction-of-flow explicitly in your answer.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Simplex Mode ŌĆö Step-by-Step

Let us deconstruct the Simplex mode operation into a precise, ordered sequence:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Channel Allocation:**
A physical communication medium (copper wire, optical fiber, RF spectrum band) is provisioned **only in the forward direction** between the Tx and Rx.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Role Assignment:**
- The source device is hard-wired / configured as **Transmitter (Tx)**.
- The destination device is hard-wired / configured as **Receiver (Rx)**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Signal Encoding:**
The Tx encodes digital bits into analog signals using line-coding schemes (NRZ, Manchester, AMI, etc.) or passband modulation (ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Continuous Forward Propagation:**
The encoded signal traverses the medium **continuously** in the forward direction. There is no contention, no token passing, and no carrier sensing required (because only one device ever transmits).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Reception & Decoding:**
The Rx demodulates / decodes the signal and presents the bits to the upper layers (DLC, Network, etc.).

**Step 6 ŌĆö No Reverse Action:**
The Rx **does not** generate any response on the data channel. There is no ACK frame, no NACK, no RTS/CTS, no synchronization pulse from Rx to Tx.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Simplex Design:**
> Simplex is chosen when:
> - The application is inherently **broadcast-style** (one sender, many listeners).
> - **Cost** is a constraint (no need for bidirectional transceivers, full-duplex cables, echo cancellers).
> - **Spectrum** is scarce and must be conserved (e.g., a satellite downlink uses Simplex to maximize downlink bandwidth).
> - **Security / asymmetry** is intentional (e.g., a sensor sends telemetry to a controller but never needs commands back).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Simplex-Specific)

The following equations are **exam-critical** for KTU PCCGT302. Memorize both the formula and the units.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Applicable Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2(M)$ | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = signal levels | Noiseless Simplex channel |
| 2 | $C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless) | Noisy Simplex channel |
| 3 | $C_{\text{SX}} = C_{\text{Nyquist}}$ or $C_{\text{Shannon}}$ | $C_{\text{SX}}$ = Simplex capacity | Entire $C$ flows A ŌåÆ B |
| 4 | $\text{Bandwidth Efficiency } \eta = \dfrac{R}{B}$ | $R$ = actual data rate (bps) | Spectral efficiency of Simplex link |
| 5 | $\text{SNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S$ = signal power (W), $N$ = noise power (W) | dB conversion for $C_{\text{Shannon}}$ |
| 6 | $N = kTB$ | $k$ = Boltzmann constant $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}}$, $T$ = temperature (K) | Thermal noise on Simplex channel |
| 7 | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | $T_{\text{prop}}$ = propagation, $T_{\text{trans}}$ = transmission, $T_{\text{queue}}$ = queuing | End-to-end Simplex delay |
| 8 | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{v}$ | $v$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$ in fiber) | One-way propagation in Simplex |
| 9 | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{\text{Frame Size}}{R}$ | $R$ = data rate (bps) | Time to push one frame A ŌåÆ B |
| 10 | $\text{Throughput}_{\text{SX}} = R$ (theoretical) | $R$ = channel bit rate | No ACK overhead, no collisions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> In **Simplex**, there is **no ACK traffic** consuming bandwidth. Therefore, the **effective throughput** equals the **raw bit rate** (unlike Half-Duplex, where ACK consumes a portion of the round-trip time, and Full-Duplex, where ACK overhead can be hidden but requires echo cancellation).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Simplex Mode

Simplex is **not a "primitive" mode** ŌĆö it is the **most bandwidth-efficient** mode for asymmetric applications. Major engineering applications include:

| Domain | Simplex Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Broadcast Television** | Terrestrial & satellite TV downlink |
| **Satellite Communication** | GPS downlink (satellite ŌåÆ receiver), DTH TV |
| **Public Safety Pagers** | One-way alert paging systems |
| **Telemetry & SCADA** | Remote sensor ŌåÆ control center (no commands back) |
| **Radio Astronomy** | One-way RF data streaming |
| **Printer Communication** | Computer ŌåÆ printer (basic, pre-bidirectional) |
| **Fiber-Optic Distribution** | PON (Passive Optical Network) OLT ŌåÆ ONU downstream |
| **Sensor Networks** | Battery-powered IoT sensor ŌåÆ gateway (to save Tx power on the gateway side) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Simplex channels achieve **100% channel utilization** for the forward direction. Half-Duplex wastes bandwidth on turn-taking; Full-Duplex wastes complexity on echo cancellation. Simplex is **optimal** when the application is naturally asymmetric.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Maximum Forward Capacity of a Noiseless Simplex Channel (Nyquist)

We begin with the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**, which states that a band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from samples taken at a rate of at least $2B$ samples per second.

**Starting Equation (Nyquist Bit Rate for a noiseless channel):**
$$C = 2B \log_2(M) \;\;\text{bits/second}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the variables:**
- $B$ = bandwidth of the Simplex channel (Hz)
- $M$ = number of distinct signal levels (symbols) the Tx can generate
- $C$ = maximum bit rate (bps)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply to Simplex mode:**
In Simplex, the **entire bandwidth $B$** is dedicated to the forward (Tx ŌåÆ Rx) direction. There is no reverse channel, so no bandwidth is reserved for ACK/NACK.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SX, forward}} &= 2B \log_2(M) \;\;\text{bits/second} \\[6pt]
C_{\text{SX, reverse}} &= 0 \;\;\text{bits/second (no channel)} \\[6pt]
C_{\text{SX, total}} &= 2B \log_2(M) \;\;\text{bits/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem type):**
> *Given:* $B = 4000\;\text{Hz}$, $M = 16$ signal levels, Simplex mode.
> *Find:* Maximum data rate in bps.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SX}} &= 2B \log_2(M) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(16) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 4000 \times 4 \\[4pt]
&= 32{,}000\;\text{bps} \\[4pt]
&= 32\;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 4 kHz voice-grade Simplex channel with 16-level signaling can carry **32 kbps** ŌĆö exactly the capacity of a PCM voice channel in one direction. Ō£ģ

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Capacity of a Noisy Simplex Channel (Shannon-Hartley)

For a channel corrupted by **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)**, the maximum achievable bit rate is bounded by the **Shannon-Hartley Theorem**.

**Starting Equation (Shannon Channel Capacity):**
$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\;\text{bits/second}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert SNR from dB to linear scale (if given in dB):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{linear}} &= 10^{\;\left(\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}}{10}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply to Simplex mode:**
In Simplex, the **signal power $S$** and **noise power $N$** both belong to the **forward direction**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SX}} &= B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S_{\text{fwd}}}{N_{\text{fwd}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Example:**
> *Given:* $B = 3000\;\text{Hz}$, SNR $= 30\;\text{dB}$, Simplex mode.
> *Find:* Maximum data rate.

**Step 3a ŌĆö Convert SNR to linear:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{N} &= 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3b ŌĆö Apply Shannon:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SX}} &= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\[4pt]
&= 3000 \times \log_2(1001) \\[4pt]
&\approx 3000 \times 9.967 \\[4pt]
&\approx 29{,}901\;\text{bps} \\[4pt]
&\approx 29.9\;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 3 kHz Simplex voice channel at 30 dB SNR can support nearly **30 kbps** in one direction. Ō£ģ

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö End-to-End Latency in Simplex Transmission

Total latency from the moment a bit leaves the Tx until it is fully received at the Rx is the sum of **three orthogonal delay components**.

**Starting Equation (Total Latency):**
$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Propagation Delay:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $d$ = link length (m), $v$ = propagation velocity (m/s). For fiber, $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Transmission Delay (Time to push all bits of a frame onto the wire):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $L$ = frame size (bits), $R$ = Simplex bit rate (bps).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Queuing Delay:**
Time spent in buffer waiting for the channel. In pure Simplex, $T_{\text{queue}} = 0$ **if the buffer is empty and the channel is idle**, but in practice, $T_{\text{queue}} > 0$ during bursts.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Example:**
> *Given:* $d = 1000\;\text{km}$ (fiber), $v = 2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$, $L = 1500\;\text{bytes}$, $R = 1\;\text{Gbps}$, $T_{\text{queue}} = 0$.
> *Find:* Total latency.

**Step 4a ŌĆö Convert units:**
- $d = 10^6\;\text{m}$
- $L = 1500 \times 8 = 12{,}000\;\text{bits}$
- $R = 10^9\;\text{bps}$

**Step 4b ŌĆö Compute each component:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prop}} &= \frac{10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} = 5\;\text{ms} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{trans}} &= \frac{12{,}000}{10^{9}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{s} = 0.012\;\text{ms} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{queue}} &= 0\;\text{ms} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{total}} &= 5 + 0.012 + 0 = 5.012\;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** In Simplex, **propagation delay dominates** for long-haul links. Ō£ģ

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Simplex vs Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex

This matrix is **directly exam-relevant** and frequently appears in 7-mark questions.

| Parameter | Simplex (SX) | Half-Duplex (HDX) | Full-Duplex (FDX) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction of flow** | One direction only | Both, but alternately | Both, simultaneously |
| **Channel count** | 1 (forward only) | 1 (shared) | 2 (forward + reverse) or 1 with FDM/EC |
| **Reverse channel** | ŌØī Absent | Ō£ģ Present, time-shared | Ō£ģ Always present |
| **ACK/NACK possible?** | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes (must wait for turn) | Ō£ģ Yes (no waiting) |
| **Collision risk** | ŌØī None (only 1 Tx ever) | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Yes (both can try to Tx) | ŌØī None (separated channels) |
| **Throughput (theoretical max)** | $C = 2B\log_2 M$ | $\approx 0.5 \times C$ (turn-taking) | $\approx C$ in **each** direction (total $2C$) |
| **Hardware complexity** | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** (echo cancellers) |
| **Latency for ACK** | N/A (no ACK) | High (wait for next turn) | Low (immediate) |
| **Bandwidth utilization** | 100% in forward | ~50% in each direction (avg) | 100% in each direction |
| **Example** | TV broadcast, radio | Walkie-talkie | Telephone, full-duplex Ethernet |
| **Power consumption at Rx** | Rx only ŌĆö low | Rx + occasional Tx | Rx + simultaneous Tx ŌĆö high |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Why Simplex Throughput Equals the Raw Bit Rate

Unlike Half-Duplex (where ACK frames consume channel time) or Full-Duplex (where echo cancellation overhead exists), in Simplex there is **zero protocol overhead** in the data path.

**Throughput Equation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{\text{SX}} &= \frac{\text{Useful data bits delivered}}{\text{Total time}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{L}{T_{\text{trans}}} = \frac{L}{L/R} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof:** Since the entire channel capacity $R$ bps is used for user data (no ACK, no headers beyond link-layer framing), **Throughput = R bps exactly.** This is the **upper bound** of channel efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, link-layer framing (HDLC, PPP) adds a small overhead (e.g., flag bytes, FCS), so practical throughput is slightly less than $R$, but the **structural efficiency** of Simplex remains 100% in the forward direction.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Q: Define Simplex mode of communication. Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Simplex communication is a unidirectional mode of data transmission in which data flows in **only one fixed direction** between two devices. The sender and receiver roles are permanently assigned, and the reverse channel is either non-existent or unused.

**Two Real-World Examples:**
1. **Television Broadcasting:** A TV transmitter sends audio/video signals to TV sets. The TV set cannot transmit anything back to the broadcaster over the same broadcast channel.
2. **Radio Station / FM Broadcast:** The radio station transmits audio signals; the listener's radio receives them. No return transmission is possible on the FM band.

**Key Properties (for 3-mark completeness):**
- One-way only, no ACK, no flow control, 100% bandwidth to forward direction.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + 2 Examples (1) + Key Property (1) = **3 marks** Ō£ģ

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Dec 2024]**
**Q: How does Simplex differ from Half-Duplex communication?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Simplex | Half-Duplex |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | One direction only (A ŌåÆ B) | Both directions, but alternately |
| Reverse channel | Absent | Present, shared via time-division |
| Simultaneous bidirectional? | ŌØī No | ŌØī No (one at a time) |
| Example | TV broadcast | Walkie-Talkie ("over" protocol) |
| Bandwidth utilization | 100% in forward | ~50% in each direction (turn-taking) |

**Key Point:** In Simplex, both devices cannot transmit at any time; in Half-Duplex, **both can transmit, but never at the same time.**

**Marks Distribution:** 2 distinguishing points (2 marks) + example (1 mark) = **3 marks** Ō£ģ

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the three modes of data communication ŌĆö Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex ŌĆö with neat diagrams and suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Simplex channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz** and uses **8 signal levels**. Compute the maximum data rate using the **Nyquist criterion**. If the channel is noisy with an **SNR of 30 dB**, also compute the Shannon capacity. Which one is the **practical upper bound** and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The three modes of data communication are:

**1. Simplex Mode (SX):**
- Communication is **strictly one-way**, from Station A to Station B.
- The roles of sender and receiver are **permanently fixed**.
- **No acknowledgment** is possible; the receiver cannot send any feedback.

```
A  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║  B
(Tx only)     (Rx only)
```

**Examples:** TV broadcast, FM radio, keyboard-to-monitor (one-way data), satellite TV downlink.

**2. Half-Duplex Mode (HDX):**
- Communication is **bidirectional**, but **not simultaneous** ŌĆö only one station transmits at a time.
- Both stations have transceiver capability, but the channel is **time-shared**.
- A "**turn-taking**" protocol (e.g., "over" in walkie-talkies) is used.

```
A  ŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║  B
(alternating)
```

**Examples:** Walkie-Talkie, CB radio, old Ethernet (10BASE5 with CSMA/CD in half-duplex mode).

**3. Full-Duplex Mode (FDX):**
- Communication is **bidirectional and simultaneous** ŌĆö both stations transmit at the same time.
- Achieved using **two separate physical channels**, or **one channel with FDM/EC** (echo cancellation).

```
A  ŌŚäŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌĢÉŌ¢║  B
(simultaneous)
```

**Examples:** Standard telephone system, modern full-duplex Ethernet (100BASE-TX, 1 Gbps), fiber-optic links with separate Tx/Rx wavelengths.

**Comparison Table (for 7-mark depth):**

| Feature | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | One-way | Two-way (alternate) | Two-way (simultaneous) |
| Channel Count | 1 (one-way) | 1 (shared) | 2 (or 1 with FDM) |
| Efficiency | 100% (forward) | ~50% (avg) | 100% in each direction |
| Hardware | Cheapest | Medium | Most expensive |

**Marks Distribution (a):** 3 modes ├Ś 2 marks each (defn + diagram + example) + comparison table (1 mark) = **7 marks** Ō£ģ

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 4\;\text{kHz} = 4000\;\text{Hz}$
- Signal levels $M = 8$
- SNR $= 30\;\text{dB}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Capacity):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Nyquist}} &= 2B \log_2(M) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(8) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 4000 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&= 24{,}000\;\text{bps} = 24\;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{N} &= 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)} = 10^{(30/10)} = 10^3 = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Shannon Capacity (Noisy Capacity):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Shannon}} &= B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\[4pt]
&= 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \\[4pt]
&= 4000 \times 9.9668 \\[4pt]
&\approx 39{,}867\;\text{bps} \approx 39.87\;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify the Practical Upper Bound:**

The **Shannon Capacity (Ōēł 39.87 kbps)** is the **practical upper bound** for a noisy Simplex channel because:

- **Nyquist** assumes a **noiseless** channel and is achievable only with infinite-level or perfect signaling.
- **Shannon** accounts for **thermal noise, attenuation, and SNR**, and represents the **absolute theoretical maximum** that no coding/modulation scheme can ever exceed on a real channel.
- In a Simplex link, the **entire Shannon capacity is dedicated to the forward direction** (A ŌåÆ B), with **zero bits/second** allocated to the reverse.

**Final Answer:** $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 24\;\text{kbps}$; $C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 39.87\;\text{kbps}$; **Shannon capacity is the practical upper bound.**

**Marks Distribution (b):** Nyquist (2) + SNR conversion (1) + Shannon (2) + Justification (2) = **7 marks** Ō£ģ

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Simplex communication system** and explain the role of each block. Why is the reverse channel absent in Simplex? Justify with **two engineering applications** where Simplex is preferred over Half-Duplex. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Simplex link of length **2000 km** uses **fiber-optic cable** with a propagation speed of $2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$. The bit rate is **2 Mbps** and the frame size is **8000 bits**. Calculate:
- (i) Propagation delay
- (ii) Transmission delay
- (iii) Total delay
- (iv) Throughput in frames per second **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram of Simplex Communication System:**

```
ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ    ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
Ōöé  SOURCE  ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║Ōöé  ENCODER &   ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║Ōöé  PHYSICAL        ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║Ōöé  DECODER &   ŌöéŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ŌöéDESTINATIONŌöé
Ōöé  (Tx)    Ōöé    Ōöé  MODULATOR   Ōöé    Ōöé  MEDIUM          Ōöé    Ōöé  DEMODULATOR Ōöé    Ōöé  (Rx)    Ōöé
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś    Ōöé  (Forward only)  Ōöé    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                                    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                                      (No reverse path)
```

**Role of Each Block:**
1. **Source (Tx):** Originates the digital data (text, audio, video, sensor readings). It is configured as a **transmitter only** and never receives on the data channel.
2. **Encoder & Modulator:** Converts digital bits into analog signals using line coding (NRZ-L, Manchester) or passband modulation (ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM). Adds synchronization and framing bits.
3. **Physical Medium:** The transmission path (copper wire, optical fiber, RF spectrum). **Only the forward direction is provisioned**; no return medium exists.
4. **Decoder & Demodulator:** Recovers the original digital bit stream from the received analog waveform, performing clock recovery, equalization, and error detection.
5. **Destination (Rx):** Receives the data and presents it to the application layer. **Cannot transmit** on the data channel.

**Why Reverse Channel is Absent in Simplex:**
- The application is inherently **asymmetric** (one-way information dissemination).
- Eliminating the reverse channel **saves cost, spectrum, and hardware complexity** (no Rx-side transmitter, no echo canceller, no duplexer).
- For broadcast-style content, the sender does not need individual ACKs from millions of receivers.

**Two Engineering Applications Where Simplex is Preferred:**

1. **Satellite TV Downlink (DTH):** The satellite transmits TV channels to millions of subscribers. A reverse channel per subscriber is impractical and would exhaust the satellite's power budget. Simplex downlink maximizes bandwidth for video.

2. **GPS Receivers:** GPS satellites continuously broadcast navigation signals; the receiver **only listens**. A reverse channel from every GPS device back to the satellite is unnecessary ŌĆö the receiver computes position locally.

**Marks Distribution (a):** Block diagram (2) + 5 block explanations (2) + Why no reverse (1) + 2 applications (2) = **7 marks** Ō£ģ

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Distance $d = 2000\;\text{km} = 2 \times 10^{6}\;\text{m}$
- Propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$
- Bit rate $R = 2\;\text{Mbps} = 2 \times 10^{6}\;\text{bps}$
- Frame size $L = 8000\;\text{bits}$

**(i) Propagation Delay $T_{\text{prop}}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prop}} &= \frac{d}{v} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-2}\;\text{s} \\[4pt]
&= 10\;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Transmission Delay $T_{\text{trans}}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{trans}} &= \frac{L}{R} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8000}{2 \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt]
&= 4 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} \\[4pt]
&= 4\;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Delay $T_{\text{total}}$ (assuming $T_{\text{queue}} = 0$, $T_{\text{proc}} = 0$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} \\[4pt]
&= 10\;\text{ms} + 4\;\text{ms} \\[4pt]
&= 14\;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Throughput in Frames per Second:**

Since the channel is **Simplex** (no ACK, no collision, no turn-taking), the throughput in frames per second equals the rate at which frames can be pushed onto the wire:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{\text{fps}} &= \frac{R}{L} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{8000} \\[4pt]
&= 250\;\text{frames/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $T_{\text{prop}} = 10\;\text{ms}$
- (ii) $T_{\text{trans}} = 4\;\text{ms}$
- (iii) $T_{\text{total}} = 14\;\text{ms}$
- (iv) $\text{Throughput} = 250\;\text{frames/sec}$

**Marks Distribution (b):** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 1.5 marks, (iv) 2.5 marks = **7 marks** Ō£ģ

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist with Shannon:** Many students apply Nyquist formula even when SNR is given. **Rule:** If the question says "noiseless," use Nyquist. If SNR (or noise) is mentioned, use **Shannon**. The **smaller** of the two is the actual achievable rate.
> 2. **Forgetting that Simplex has NO reverse channel:** Students sometimes subtract an ACK bandwidth. In Simplex, **C_reverse = 0**, so total capacity = forward capacity only.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Converting kHz to Hz (multiply by $10^3$), Mbps to bps (multiply by $10^6$), and km to m (multiply by $10^3$) is the most common source of mark loss.
> 4. **dB vs Linear SNR:** A 30 dB SNR means $S/N = 1000$, **not 30**. Always convert: $(S/N)_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$.
> 5. **Mixing up Simplex Method (LPP) with Simplex Communication:** In PCCGT302, Simplex = Communication Mode. The Simplex Method of Linear Programming belongs to Operations Research, **not** this course.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember Ō£Ź’ĖÅ

Use this **rapid-revision checklist** before your KTU exam:

- ŌśÉ **Simplex = One-Way Only:** Data flows in exactly one fixed direction (A ŌåÆ B). No reverse path exists.
- ŌśÉ **Fixed Roles:** Sender is always sender; receiver is always receiver. Roles are **immutable**.
- ŌśÉ **Three Modes:** Simplex (SX), Half-Duplex (HDX), Full-Duplex (FDX) ŌĆö always quote direction explicitly.
- ŌśÉ **Nyquist Capacity (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ ŌĆö use when channel is **noiseless**.
- ŌśÉ **Shannon Capacity (Noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ ŌĆö use when **SNR is given**.
- ŌśÉ **dB Conversion:** $(S/N)_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$; e.g., 30 dB ŌåÆ 1000.
- ŌśÉ **Simplex Throughput = R bps exactly** (no ACK overhead in forward direction).
- ŌśÉ **Propagation Delay:** $T_{\text{prop}} = d/v$ where $v \approx 2 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}$ in fiber and $\approx 3 \times 10^8\;\text{m/s}$ in air/vacuum.
- ŌśÉ **Transmission Delay:** $T_{\text{trans}} = L/R$ where $L$ = frame bits, $R$ = bps.
- ŌśÉ **Total Latency:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$.
- ŌśÉ **Real-World Examples:** TV broadcast, radio, GPS, satellite TV downlink, telemetry, pagers.
- ŌśÉ **Simplex is preferred** for **broadcast**, **asymmetric**, **cost-sensitive**, and **power-constrained** applications.
- ŌśÉ **100% Bandwidth Utilization** in forward direction (compare to HDX Ōēł 50% and FDX = 100% in each direction with overhead).
- ŌśÉ **Hardware complexity:** Simplex < Half-Duplex < Full-Duplex.
- ŌśÉ **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$ ŌĆö used in thermal noise $N = kTB$ derivations.
- ŌśÉ **Diagrams to memorize:** Three-block Simplex architecture (Source ŌåÆ Encoder/Mod ŌåÆ Medium ŌåÆ Decoder/Demod ŌåÆ Destination) and the three-mode comparison chart.
- ŌśÉ **Exam mantra:** When asked "what is the capacity of a Simplex channel?" ŌĆö answer is **the full Nyquist or Shannon value**, because the **entire bandwidth** is one-directional.

> **Good luck with your KTU PCCGT302 examination! ­¤ÜĆ**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Half Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Half Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Data Communication and Computer Networks**, the term **Half Duplex** (also written as **Half-Duplex** or abbreviated as **HDX**) refers to a **bidirectional communication mode** in which data transmission is permitted in **both directions** between two communicating devices, but the channel is restricted to carry a signal in **only one direction at any given instant of time**.

According to the standard KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with foundational data communication literature, a Half Duplex channel is formally defined as:

> A communication system wherein **either** Device A can transmit while Device B receives, **or** Device B can transmit while Device A receives ŌĆö but **simultaneous two-way transmission is structurally impossible** on the shared physical medium.

The mode of operation is **time-shared** between the two endpoints. Control of the channel must be explicitly transferred from one station to the other, often requiring a **turn-taking protocol** (e.g., a Request-to-Send / Clear-to-Send handshake, a polling mechanism, or a contention-based access method such as **CSMA/CD**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCGT302 ŌĆō Module 1):**
> Half Duplex is studied under the unit covering **Data Communication fundamentals and Transmission Modes**. The examiner expects students to clearly *differentiate* Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex, and to *justify* the choice of Half Duplex in legacy Ethernet (10BASE5/10BASE2) and walkie-talkie systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology ŌĆö Bidirectional vs. Simultaneous:**
> Half Duplex is **bidirectional** (traffic flows both ways) but **not simultaneous**. This is the precise distinction from **Full Duplex**, which permits *simultaneous* bidirectional flow, and from **Simplex**, which permits *only one-way* flow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation)

To make this concept instantly memorable for a first-time learner, consider the following real-world analogy:

### ­¤ÜČ The "Single-Lane Bridge" Analogy
Imagine a **narrow one-lane bridge** connecting two villages, Village A and Village B. Cars can travel in **both directions** across the bridge ŌĆö but the bridge is **physically too narrow** to allow a car from A and a car from B to cross at the same time without colliding.

- If a car from Village A is crossing, all cars from Village B must **wait**.
- Once the bridge is clear, cars from Village B may cross.
- A **traffic policeman** (the protocol) decides whose turn it is.

That one-lane bridge is the **Half Duplex channel**. Both villages (end devices) can send and receive, but **never at the same time**.

### ­¤ÄÖ’ĖÅ Another Familiar Example ŌĆö The Walkie-Talkie
A **walkie-talkie** is the classic consumer example of Half Duplex. When you press the **Push-To-Talk (PTT)** button:
- Your voice is transmitted.
- You **cannot hear** the other person while you are speaking ŌĆö the speaker is muted.
- You must **release the button** to listen.

This "either you speak OR you listen" property is the defining behavioural fingerprint of Half Duplex.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot of the Three Transmission Modes

To crystallize the position of Half Duplex within the broader taxonomy of transmission modes, observe the following summary table:

| **Property** | **Simplex** | **Half Duplex** | **Full Duplex** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction of data flow | One direction only | Both directions, but **alternately** | Both directions, **simultaneously** |
| Number of channels | **1** | **1** (shared) | **2** (separate or effectively separate) |
| Channel capacity utilization | $\leq 50\%$ of theoretical | $\leq 50\%$ under symmetric traffic (up to 100% under asymmetric) | **100%** (theoretically) |
| Example devices | Keyboard ŌåÆ CPU, Radio broadcast, TV | Walkie-Talkie, CB Radio, Legacy Ethernet (10BASE5) | Telephone, Modern Switched Ethernet, Fiber full-duplex links |
| Complexity | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for the Exam:**
> **Simplex** = **S**ingle way (1-way)
> **Half Duplex** = **H**alf of a duplex (2-way, one at a time)
> **Full Duplex** = **F**ull 2-way (simultaneous)

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are **bolded** as they are high-yield for KTU derivations:

- **Bandwidth of the Half Duplex channel:** $B$ Hz (shared in time)
- **Effective unidirectional throughput:** at most $B$ bits/second when the entire channel is allocated to one direction
- **Bidirectional effective throughput (symmetric traffic):** $B/2$ bits/second in each direction on average
- **Propagation speed in copper:** approximately **$2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s**
- **Propagation speed in fiber:** approximately **$2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** to **$3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s**
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT) for Half Duplex turn-around:** $2 \times T_p + T_t$, where $T_p$ is propagation delay and $T_t$ is transmission delay

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Half Duplex System

The Half Duplex mode of communication can be decomposed into a **structured sequence of operational steps**. Each step is essential for the system to function correctly and to prevent collisions on the shared medium.

### Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

1. **Channel Acquisition (Channel Access Control):**
   - Before transmitting, a device must first **acquire the right to use the shared medium**.
   - This is governed by a **Medium Access Control (MAC)** protocol such as:
     - **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) ŌĆö used in legacy half-duplex Ethernet.
     - **Polling** ŌĆö a central controller asks each device in turn whether it has data.
     - **Token Passing** ŌĆö a special control frame (token) circulates; only the device holding the token may transmit.
     - **RTS/CTS Handshake** ŌĆö Request-to-Send / Clear-to-Send mechanism (used in wireless 802.11).

2. **Channel Sensing:**
   - The transmitting device first **listens** to the medium to determine whether it is **idle** (no carrier detected) or **busy** (another device is currently transmitting).
   - If the medium is **busy**, the device defers its transmission (backs off) for a random or deterministic interval.
   - If the medium is **idle**, the device proceeds to step 3.

3. **Directional Transmission:**
   - The transmitting device sends its data frame onto the shared medium.
   - During this interval, the medium is **reserved for this one direction**. The receiving device (and all other devices on the segment) operate in **receive-only mode**.

4. **Turn-Around / Channel Release:**
   - Upon completion of frame transmission, the device **releases the medium** (in CSMA/CD, the carrier is dropped; in RTS/CTS, a Clear-to-Send is sent).
   - There is a small **turn-around time** (channel propagation delay) before the other end can begin transmitting.

5. **Reverse Direction Transmission:**
   - Now the *other* device (or the same device) can begin a new acquisition cycle to send data back in the opposite direction.

6. **Collision Detection (if applicable):**
   - In CSMA/CD-based Half Duplex Ethernet, if two devices happened to begin transmitting simultaneously (despite sensing the medium idle due to propagation delay), a **collision** is detected. Both devices abort, send a **jam signal**, and back off for a random interval before retrying.

### The "Why" Behind the Steps

- **Why is sensing needed?** Because the medium is shared, a device must verify that no one else is transmitting to avoid corrupting an in-flight frame.
- **Why a turn-around delay?** Because even after the last bit is sent, the bit must physically propagate to the far end. During that propagation, the channel is still effectively "occupied."
- **Why collisions in Half Duplex Ethernet?** Because the **propagation delay is non-zero**, two devices physically distant from each other may both sense the medium as idle and transmit simultaneously, leading to a collision near the middle of the cable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Trade-Off:**
> Half Duplex *trades* channel efficiency for *physical simplicity and cost*. By using **one** channel instead of two, the cabling and hardware costs are roughly **halved** compared to Full Duplex ŌĆö at the price of reduced aggregate throughput and the need for collision-handling logic.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all** the key formulas, parameters, boundary conditions, and units that are essential for solving Half Duplex problems in the KTU examination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formatting Safeguard:** Absolute value and "such that" symbols in formulas use `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **#** | **Formula / Parameter** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Meaning / Boundary Conditions** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-way transmission delay | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length, $R$ = link bandwidth (channel rate) | seconds (s) |
| 2 | One-way propagation delay | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link length, $v$ = signal propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in copper) | seconds (s) |
| 3 | Round-trip propagation delay | $RTT = 2 \cdot T_p = \dfrac{2d}{v}$ | Time for a bit to travel to the far end and return | seconds (s) |
| 4 | Total frame delivery time (one-way) | $T_{frame} = T_t + T_p = \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d}{v}$ | From first bit sent to last bit received | seconds (s) |
| 5 | Half Duplex turn-around overhead | $T_{turn} = T_p$ | Time to release and re-acquire the medium | seconds (s) |
| 6 | Effective throughput per direction (symmetric traffic) | $\eta_{HDX} = \dfrac{B}{2}$ | Average bandwidth available to each direction | bits per second (bps) |
| 7 | Maximum link utilization (CSMA/CD, half duplex) | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ | Where $a = T_p / T_t$ (Ethernet efficiency model) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Parameter $a$ (ratio of propagation to transmission) | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$ | Determines collision window and efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | Minimum frame size (CSMA/CD collision detection) | $L_{min} = 2 \cdot d \cdot \dfrac{R}{v}$ | Frame must be long enough to detect a collision | bits |
| 10 | Channel utilization (Idle) | $U_{ideal} = 1$ | Only achievable in full duplex with no overhead | dimensionless |
| 11 | Collision window | $T_{cw} = 2 \cdot T_p$ | Time during which a collision is possible | seconds (s) |
| 12 | Slot time (Ethernet) | $T_{slot} = 51.2 \,\mu s$ | For 10 Mbps Ethernet; equal to 512 bit times | seconds (s) |
| 13 | Maximum network diameter (10 Mbps Ethernet) | $d_{max} = 2500$ m | 5 segments of 500 m each with repeaters | meters (m) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Half Duplex communication is not merely a textbook concept ŌĆö it has **substantial engineering utility** in the following domains:

1. **Legacy Ethernet Networks (10BASE5, 10BASE2, 10BASE-T with hubs):**
   - The original IEEE 802.3 Ethernet standard specified Half Duplex operation over a shared coaxial cable.
   - Multiple stations share a single collision domain; CSMA/CD resolves contention.

2. **Two-Way Radio Communication (Walkie-Talkies, CB Radios, Marine Radios, Aviation Radios):**
   - Spectrum is a scarce resource; using a single frequency for both directions (Half Duplex) is far more bandwidth-efficient than dedicating two separate frequencies (Full Duplex).

3. **Industrial Control Buses (RS-485, CAN Bus):**
   - Many industrial fieldbuses operate Half Duplex over a twisted pair to minimize wiring cost in noisy factory environments.

4. **Satellite Communication (Legacy Bent-Pipe Transponders):**
   - Early satellite transponders were Half Duplex ŌĆö the satellite could only receive from Earth Station A and re-transmit to Earth Station B, or vice versa, on a single frequency band at a time.

5. **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) and RFID:**
   - Low-power, low-cost transceivers in IoT devices frequently use Half Duplex radios (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 at the physical layer) to conserve battery life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** All transitions are written out explicitly. No shortcuts, no skipped steps. Every algebraic manipulation is accompanied by a text row explaining the reasoning. This is the KTU examiner-expected level of rigor for a 14-mark problem.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throughput of a Half Duplex Link Under Symmetric Traffic

### Problem Statement
A Half Duplex communication channel has a raw physical bandwidth of $B$ bits/second. If two endpoints **A** and **B** exchange equal volumes of data in both directions (symmetric traffic), determine the **average effective throughput** in each direction, assuming the channel is perfectly utilized with no idle time and no collisions.

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1: Define the raw bandwidth.**
The channel supports a maximum bit rate of $B$ bits per second in whichever direction is currently active.

$$B_{raw} = B \;\; \text{bits/second}$$

**Step 2: Observe the time-sharing property.**
In Half Duplex, the channel is **alternately** allocated to direction AŌåÆB and direction BŌåÆA. Let $T_A$ be the total time in one observation window used by AŌåÆB, and $T_B$ the total time used by BŌåÆA.

$$T_{total} = T_A + T_B$$

**Step 3: Apply the symmetric traffic assumption.**
By the symmetry of the problem, A and B use the channel for **equal durations**.

$$T_A = T_B = \dfrac{T_{total}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Compute throughput in the AŌåÆB direction.**
The bits successfully delivered from A to B in the observation window $T_{total}$ equal $B \cdot T_A$ (channel operates at full rate $B$ during A's slots). Therefore:

$$\eta_{A \to B} = \dfrac{B \cdot T_A}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{B \cdot (T_{total}/2)}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{B}{2}$$

**Step 5: By symmetry, throughput in the BŌåÆA direction.**

$$\eta_{B \to A} = \dfrac{B}{2} \;\; \text{bits/second}$$

**Step 6: State the final compact result.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{HDX, \text{symmetric}} = \dfrac{B}{2} \;\; \text{bits/second per direction}}$$

### Interpretation
This is the canonical "halving" result. A **$B$ bps Half Duplex** link delivers the **same per-direction throughput as a $B/2$ bps Full Duplex** link ŌĆö provided traffic is symmetric. In asymmetric scenarios (e.g., web browsing where downlink Ōē½ uplink), the throughput approaches $B$ in the dominant direction, approaching the performance of Simplex.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Frame Size for Collision Detection in Half Duplex CSMA/CD

### Problem Statement
In a half-duplex CSMA/CD Ethernet network, the maximum cable length between the two farthest stations is $d$ meters. The link operates at a bit rate of $R$ bits/second, and signals propagate at speed $v$ m/s. Derive the **minimum frame size** $L_{min}$ (in bits) that a station must transmit so that a collision occurring at the *farthest* point of the network is still detected before the sender finishes transmitting.

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1: Visualize the worst-case scenario.**
Consider two stations, **A** and **B**, at the two extreme ends of the cable, separated by distance $d$.

- At time $t = 0$, A begins transmitting.
- The first bit from A takes $T_p = d/v$ seconds to reach B.
- Just before the first bit arrives at B, B senses the medium as **idle** (since A's signal has not yet reached it) and **starts transmitting**.
- The two signals collide somewhere along the cable.

**Step 2: Compute the time for the collision signal to propagate back to A.**
The collision occurs essentially at B's location (worst case). The collision-energized signal must now travel **back to A** ŌĆö a distance of $d$ ŌĆö taking another $T_p = d/v$ seconds.

**Step 3: Compute the total time for A to detect the collision.**
From A's perspective, the entire event ŌĆö A's transmission, the propagation to B, B's mistaken transmission, and the collision echo back to A ŌĆö takes:

$$T_{detect} = 2 \cdot T_p = \dfrac{2d}{v}$$

**Step 4: State the constraint on frame size.**
For A to *still be transmitting* when the collision echo arrives, the frame transmission time $T_t$ must satisfy:

$$T_t \geq T_{detect}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the expression for $T_t$.**
Frame transmission time = (frame length) / (bit rate) = $L / R$. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{L}{R} \geq \dfrac{2d}{v}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $L$.**

$$L \geq \dfrac{2d \cdot R}{v}$$

**Step 7: State the minimum frame size.**

$$\boxed{L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot d \cdot R}{v} \;\; \text{bits}}$$

### Numerical Sanity Check (Ethernet)
For 10 Mbps Ethernet over a 2500 m maximum diameter (with $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s):

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \times 2500 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^8} = 250 \text{ bits}$$

The actual Ethernet minimum frame size is **512 bits (64 bytes)** ŌĆö a value chosen with safety margin and a 4-repeater network in mind. This matches the KTU textbook derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Link Utilization of Half Duplex CSMA/CD Ethernet

### Problem Statement
For a half-duplex CSMA/CD Ethernet network, derive the maximum channel utilization $U$ as a function of the parameter $a = T_p / T_t$.

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1: Define the parameter $a$.**
Let $T_p$ = one-way propagation delay, $T_t$ = transmission time of one frame.

$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$$

**Step 2: Express the total cycle time.**
In the best case (no collisions), one frame of length $L$ bits is transmitted, and during the propagation delay $T_p$, the channel is "wasted" (the sender is waiting for acknowledgment in the original Ethernet model, or simply the channel is busy propagating the last bit). The total "busy" time for one frame is:

$$T_{busy} = T_t + T_p$$

But the channel is held for slightly longer to allow the last bit to propagate fully. In the simplified model:

$$T_{cycle} = T_t + 2 \cdot T_p$$

**Step 3: Compute the utilization.**
Utilization is the ratio of *useful* transmission time to the total cycle time:

$$U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2 \cdot T_p}$$

**Step 4: Normalize by dividing numerator and denominator by $T_t$.**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot (T_p / T_t)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$$

**Step 5: Apply the standard Ethernet correction factor.**
The classical Ethernet efficiency model (as per the original MetcalfeŌĆōBoggs paper) introduces the factor **6.44** to account for collision recovery, back-off, jam signal, and inter-frame gap:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$$

**Step 6: State the final result.**

$$\boxed{U_{CSMA/CD} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}}$$

**Step 7: Limiting cases.**
- If $a \to 0$ (very long frames or very short cable): $U \to 1$ (100% utilization).
- If $a \to \infty$ (very short frames or very long cable): $U \to 0$ (essentially zero utilization ŌĆö the channel is dominated by collisions and idle time).

### Real-World Insight
This derivation shows *why* Ethernet mandates a **minimum frame size of 64 bytes** ŌĆö to keep $a$ small (and thus $U$ high) on the maximum-diameter network. Shorter frames would devastate throughput.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Half Duplex vs. Full Duplex

| **Scenario** | **Half Duplex Advantageous?** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Two-way radio over a single frequency band | Ō£ģ **Yes** | Spectrum is scarce; a single frequency is reused in time. |
| Low-cost industrial sensor bus (RS-485) | Ō£ģ **Yes** | One twisted pair; minimal wiring cost. |
| Legacy 10BASE2 / 10BASE5 Ethernet | Ō£ģ **Yes** (historically) | Single coaxial cable shared by all stations. |
| Modern switched Ethernet (full-duplex NICs) | ŌØī **No** | Dedicated switch-to-station links; full duplex doubles throughput. |
| Fiber-optic backbone | ŌØī **No** | Two fiber strands (or two wavelengths) trivially support full duplex. |
| Cellular voice (4G LTE / 5G NR) | ŌØī **No** | Separate downlink and uplink channels (FDD) or carefully time-shared slots (TDD). |
| Underwater acoustic communication | Ō£ģ **Yes** | Half duplex is often mandatory due to extreme propagation delay making echo cancellation impractical. |

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Half Duplex Turn-Taking State Machine

This diagram illustrates the **state transitions** of two devices (Device A and Device B) communicating over a Half Duplex channel. Note the **mutual exclusion** ŌĆö only one device may be in the TRANSMITTING state at any given instant.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> A_TRANSMITS : A_HAS_DATA_AND_CHANNEL_IDLE
    IDLE --> B_TRANSMITS : B_HAS_DATA_AND_CHANNEL_IDLE
    A_TRANSMITS --> IDLE : A_FINISHES_FRAME_TRANSMISSION
    B_TRANSMITS --> IDLE : B_FINISHES_FRAME_TRANSMISSION
    A_TRANSMITS --> COLLISION_DETECTED : SIMULTANEOUS_TX_DETECTED
    B_TRANSMITS --> COLLISION_DETECTED : SIMULTANEOUS_TX_DETECTED
    COLLISION_DETECTED --> BACKOFF : SEND_JAM_SIGNAL
    BACKOFF --> IDLE : WAIT_RANDOM_INTERVAL_THEN_RETRY
    IDLE --> [*]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The system is initially in the **IDLE** state.
- Exactly one device enters the **TRANSMITTING** state at a time.
- A **COLLISION_DETECTED** state is reachable from either transmit state, illustrating that CSMA/CD Half Duplex Ethernet is susceptible to collisions.
- The **BACKOFF** state implements the exponential back-off algorithm (in standard Ethernet, the wait time is a random multiple of the 51.2 ┬Ąs slot time, up to 1023 slots).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Functional Block Architecture of a Half Duplex Communication System

This block diagram maps the **internal functional modules** of a generic Half Duplex transceiver, showing the data flow, control logic, and the shared physical medium.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA1[APPLICATION_LAYER_DATA_SOURCE] --> nodeA2[DATA_LINK_LAYER_FRAMING_AND_MAC]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[HALF_DUPLEX_TX_BUFFER]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[TRANSMIT_ENABLE_CONTROL_LOGIC]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[PHYSICAL_LAYER_ENCODER_AND_DAC]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6[SHARED_PHYSICAL_MEDIUM_SINGLE_CHANNEL]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7[PHYSICAL_LAYER_DECODER_AND_ADC]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA8[HALF_DUPLEX_RX_BUFFER]
    nodeA8 --> nodeA9[DATA_LINK_LAYER_FRAME_DISASSEMBLY]
    nodeA9 --> nodeA10[APPLICATION_LAYER_DATA_SINK]
    nodeB1[CARRIER_SENSE_MODULE] --> nodeA4
    nodeB2[COLLISION_DETECTION_MODULE] --> nodeA4
    nodeB3[CHANNEL_BUSY_FLAG] --> nodeB1
    nodeA6 --> nodeB1
    nodeA6 --> nodeB2
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7
    nodeA6 --> nodeA3
    nodeA10 --> nodeA2
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **SHARED_PHYSICAL_MEDIUM_SINGLE_CHANNEL** is the heart of Half Duplex ŌĆö a single channel that is *time-shared* between transmit and receive.
- The **TRANSMIT_ENABLE_CONTROL_LOGIC** acts as the gatekeeper. It consults the **CARRIER_SENSE_MODULE** and the **COLLISION_DETECTION_MODULE** before granting permission to transmit.
- The dashed-style control flows (e.g., `nodeB1 ŌåÆ nodeA4` and `nodeB2 ŌåÆ nodeA4`) represent **control signals**, not data flow.
- Notice the **bidirectional** connection between the shared medium and the TX/RX buffers ŌĆö this visually emphasizes that the *same* physical medium handles both directions, but **not simultaneously**.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Comparative Timing of Three Transmission Modes

This diagram (a block-level functional timeline) compares how data flows in Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex over a 4-time-unit observation window.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIMPLEX_TIMELINE
        sim1[UNIT_1_A_TO_B] --> sim2[UNIT_2_A_TO_B] --> sim3[UNIT_3_A_TO_B] --> sim4[UNIT_4_A_TO_B]
    end
    subgraph HALF_DUPLEX_TIMELINE
        hd1[UNIT_1_A_TO_B] --> hd2[UNIT_2_B_TO_A] --> hd3[UNIT_3_A_TO_B] --> hd4[UNIT_4_B_TO_A]
    end
    subgraph FULL_DUPLEX_TIMELINE
        fd1[UNIT_1_A_TO_B_AND_B_TO_A] --> fd2[UNIT_2_A_TO_B_AND_B_TO_A] --> fd3[UNIT_3_A_TO_B_AND_B_TO_A] --> fd4[UNIT_4_A_TO_B_AND_B_TO_A]
    end
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- In **Simplex**, every time unit is allocated to AŌåÆB (4 units of AŌåÆB traffic).
- In **Half Duplex**, time alternates: AŌåÆB, BŌåÆA, AŌåÆB, BŌåÆA (2 units in each direction).
- In **Full Duplex**, every time unit carries *both* AŌåÆB and BŌåÆA simultaneously (4 units in each direction).

This compactly demonstrates the **throughput scaling**: Simplex delivers $4B$, Half Duplex delivers $2B$ per direction, and Full Duplex delivers $4B$ per direction ŌĆö over the same physical channel of bandwidth $B$.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: CSMA/CD Collision Detection Timeline in Half Duplex

This functional flow shows the **time-domain sequence** of events when two stations (A and B) transmit simultaneously in a Half Duplex CSMA/CD network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[TIME_T_ZERO_STATION_A_STARTS_TX] --> step2[TIME_TP_BIT_PROPAGATES_TO_STATION_B]
    step2 --> step3[STATION_B_SENSES_IDLE_AND_STARTS_TX]
    step3 --> step4[COLLISION_OCCURS_NEAR_STATION_B]
    step4 --> step5[COLLISION_SIGNAL_PROPAGATES_BACK_TO_STATION_A]
    step5 --> step6[STATION_A_DETECTS_COLLISION_AND_ABORTS]
    step6 --> step7[BOTH_STATIONS_SEND_JAM_SIGNAL]
    step7 --> step8[BACKOFF_FOR_RANDOM_SLOT_TIME]
    step8 --> step9[STATION_A_AND_B_RETRY_AFTER_BACKOFF]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The collision is *invisible* to B (B's transmission begins before A's signal arrives).
- The collision is *detected* by A only after the collision-echo travels the full link length.
- The **jam signal** alerts all stations on the segment that a collision occurred.
- The **backoff** (binary exponential back-off in standard Ethernet) is essential to prevent repeated collisions.

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, PCCGT302]`
**Differentiate between Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex modes of data transmission. Give one real-world example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| **Mode** | **Direction of Data Flow** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | Communication is **unidirectional** ŌĆö data flows from sender to receiver only. The receiver cannot send data back on the same channel. | A **television broadcast** ŌĆö the TV station transmits, your TV set only receives. |
| **Half Duplex** | Communication is **bidirectional**, but only **one direction at a time**. Both devices can transmit and receive, but not simultaneously. The channel is time-shared. | A **walkie-talkie** ŌĆö when you press the Push-To-Talk button, you transmit; you must release it to listen. |
| **Full Duplex** | Communication is **bidirectional and simultaneous**. Both devices can transmit and receive at the same time over the channel (using two separate physical channels or echo-cancellation). | A **telephone conversation** ŌĆö both parties can speak and hear at the same time. |

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- 1 mark for each correctly differentiated mode with a valid example.
- Award partial credit (0.5) for an example that is not the most standard but is technically correct.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō December 2023, PCCGT302]`
**Why is a minimum frame size of 64 bytes mandated in traditional half-duplex 10 Mbps Ethernet?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **64-byte (512-bit) minimum frame size** is mandated to ensure reliable **collision detection** in a half-duplex CSMA/CD Ethernet network.

1. **Reason:** A station must still be transmitting when the collision signal returns from the farthest point of the network. Otherwise, the station would finish transmitting *before* learning that a collision occurred, and the corrupted frame would go undetected.

2. **Derivation sketch:** For a maximum network diameter of $d = 2500$ m and propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper, the round-trip propagation delay is:
$$2 \cdot T_p = \dfrac{2d}{v} = \dfrac{2 \times 2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 25 \, \mu s$$

3. **Minimum frame size:** For 10 Mbps Ethernet, the minimum frame transmission time must exceed this round-trip delay:
$$L_{min} = R \times 2 T_p = 10^7 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 250 \text{ bits}$$

   To provide a safety margin (4 repeaters, longer propagation, clock drift), the IEEE 802.3 standard rounds this up to **512 bits = 64 bytes**.

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- 1 mark for stating the reason (collision detection requirement).
- 1 mark for the relationship $L_{min} = 2 \cdot T_p \cdot R$.
- 1 mark for the numerical computation and the 64-byte conclusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Pattern:** The KTU question paper presents an "OR" choice. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below as full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question B1 (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, PCCGT302]`

#### **(a) Explain the Half Duplex mode of transmission in detail. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the CSMA/CD protocol used in traditional half-duplex Ethernet. Also derive the expression for the minimum frame size required for collision detection.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Half Duplex:**
Half Duplex is a mode of communication in which both stations can transmit and receive, but **not simultaneously**. The shared physical medium is **time-shared** ŌĆö at any instant, the medium is allocated entirely to one direction.

**CSMA/CD Protocol ŌĆö Operational Steps:**
1. **Carrier Sense:** Before transmitting, a station listens to the medium to check whether it is idle (no carrier signal detected).
2. **Multiple Access:** Multiple stations share the same medium; any station may attempt to transmit when it senses the medium as idle.
3. **Collision Detection:** While transmitting, the station continues to monitor the medium. If the signal on the medium differs from the transmitted signal (indicating another station's signal has collided), a **collision** is declared.
4. **Jam Signal:** Upon detecting a collision, the station transmits a brief **jam signal** (32 bits) to ensure all other stations also recognize the collision.
5. **Back-off:** The station waits for a random number of slot times (each slot = 51.2 ┬Ąs for 10 Mbps Ethernet) before attempting to retransmit. The random back-off is doubled in range after each successive collision (binary exponential back-off), up to a maximum of 1023 slots.
6. **Retransmission:** The station re-attempts transmission after the back-off interval.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes for the CSMA/CD collision detection timeline.)*

**Derivation of Minimum Frame Size:**
The sender must still be transmitting when the collision echo returns from the farthest station. Let:
- $d$ = maximum distance between two stations (network diameter)
- $R$ = link bit rate
- $v$ = propagation speed in the medium

Propagation delay one-way: $T_p = d/v$

For collision detection:
$$T_t \geq 2 \cdot T_p$$
$$\dfrac{L_{min}}{R} \geq \dfrac{2d}{v}$$
$$\boxed{L_{min} = \dfrac{2dR}{v}}$$

For 10 Mbps Ethernet with $d = 2500$ m and $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s:
$$L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \times 2500 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^8} = 250 \text{ bits} \rightarrow \text{Standard mandates 512 bits (64 bytes)}}$$

**Mark Allocation for Sub-part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
- 1 mark for the correct definition of Half Duplex.
- 2 marks for the CSMA/CD operational steps.
- 1 mark for the derivation setup.
- 1 mark for the final formula.
- 1 mark for the numerical example.
- 1 mark for the diagram (or block-level functional flow).

#### **(b) Consider a half-duplex CSMA/CD network operating at 1 Gbps over a cable of maximum length 200 m. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Determine:**
**(i) The minimum frame size required for proper collision detection.**
**(ii) If frames are 1500 bytes (typical Ethernet), calculate the link utilization using the formula $U = 1 / (1 + 6.44a)$ where $a = T_p / T_t$.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Link bit rate: $R = 10^9$ bits/second
- Cable length: $d = 200$ m
- Propagation speed: $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Frame size (for part ii): $L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 12000 \text{ bits}$

**Part (i) ŌĆö Minimum Frame Size:**

Compute one-way propagation delay:
$$T_p = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{200}{2 \times 10^8} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \, \mu s$$

Compute round-trip propagation delay:
$$2 \cdot T_p = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2 \, \mu s$$

Apply the minimum frame size formula:
$$L_{min} = 2 \cdot T_p \cdot R = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^9 = 2000 \text{ bits} = 250 \text{ bytes}$$

**Answer (i): $L_{min} = 2000$ bits = 250 bytes**

**Part (ii) ŌĆö Link Utilization for 1500-byte frames:**

Compute transmission time for one 1500-byte frame:
$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{12000}{10^9} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12 \, \mu s$$

Compute the parameter $a$:
$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$$

Apply the utilization formula:
$$U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0833} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.5365} = \dfrac{1}{1.5365} \approx 0.6508$$

**Answer (ii): $U \approx 0.6508$ or **65.08%****

**Mark Allocation for Sub-part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
- 1 mark for correctly computing $T_p$.
- 1 mark for correctly computing $L_{min}$.
- 1 mark for the correct formula application for $L_{min}$.
- 1 mark for correctly computing $T_t$.
- 1 mark for correctly computing $a$.
- 2 marks for the final utilization computation and interpretation.

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, PCCGT302, Alternative]`

#### **(a) Compare and contrast Half Duplex and Full Duplex communication with suitable diagrams. Under what circumstances is Half Duplex preferred over Full Duplex in practical systems? Provide three concrete engineering examples.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Analysis:**

| **Parameter** | **Half Duplex** | **Full Duplex** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Bidirectional, **alternating** | Bidirectional, **simultaneous** |
| Physical channels | **One** (shared) | **Two** (separate or echo-cancelled) |
| Aggregate throughput (symmetric) | $B$ bps total, $B/2$ per direction | $2B$ bps total, $B$ per direction |
| Collision handling | Required (e.g., CSMA/CD) | Not required (no shared medium) |
| Cabling cost | Lower | Higher (or requires echo canceller) |
| Complexity of MAC layer | Higher | Lower or trivial (point-to-point) |
| Latency | Higher (turn-around delay) | Lower (no turn-around needed) |
| Example | Walkie-talkie, 10BASE5 Ethernet | Telephone, modern full-duplex switch link |

**Diagrams:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for the comparative timeline diagrams of the three transmission modes.)*

**When Half Duplex is Preferred ŌĆö Three Engineering Examples:**

1. **Two-Way Radio Systems (Walkie-Talkies, Marine VHF, Aviation Radios):**
   Radio spectrum is an extremely scarce and regulated resource. A single frequency band is often allocated per channel. Using Half Duplex (with a Push-To-Talk button) **doubles the number of users** that a given spectrum allocation can support, compared to dedicating two separate frequency bands for Full Duplex. This is why nearly all professional two-way radio systems (police, fire, taxi dispatch, military) are Half Duplex.

2. **Industrial Fieldbuses (RS-485, CAN Bus, Modbus over twisted pair):**
   In factory automation, hundreds or thousands of sensors and actuators must be networked across long distances in electrically noisy environments. Using a **single twisted pair** (Half Duplex) instead of two twisted pairs (Full Duplex) reduces cabling cost by ~50%, simplifies connector pinouts, and is easier to install in tight cable trays. The bus topology of these protocols naturally fits Half Duplex with a token-passing or arbitration MAC.

3. **Underwater Acoustic Communication (Submarine Networks, AUV Communication):**
   Underwater acoustic channels have **enormous propagation delays** (sound travels at ~1500 m/s in water, not at the speed of light). For a 10 km link, the one-way delay is ~6.7 seconds. Full Duplex operation would require an **echo canceller** capable of subtracting the just-transmitted signal from the received signal ŌĆö a signal delayed by 6.7 seconds and severely distorted by multipath. Implementing such an echo canceller is **practically infeasible** with current technology. Hence, underwater acoustic modems (e.g., WHOI Micromodem, Teledyne Benthos) operate strictly in Half Duplex.

**Mark Allocation for Sub-part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
- 2 marks for the comparison table.
- 1 mark for the diagrams (or block-level functional flows).
- 3 marks for the three engineering examples (1 mark each).
- 1 mark for the concluding synthesis (e.g., "Half Duplex is preferred when spectrum, cabling, or echo cancellation constraints dominate").

#### **(b) A Half Duplex link has a raw bandwidth of 10 Mbps and is used to transfer a 1 MB (MegaByte) file from Station A to Station B, and a 1 MB file from Station B to Station A simultaneously. Compute the total time required for both transfers to complete. Assume the channel is always busy (no idle time, no collisions, no protocol overhead).** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Raw channel bandwidth: $B = 10 \text{ Mbps} = 10 \times 10^6$ bits/second
- File size (each direction): $1 \text{ MB} = 1 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 8 \times 10^6$ bits
- Both transfers are simultaneous (start at the same time).

**Step 1: Recognize the Half Duplex constraint.**
The channel can carry data in **only one direction at a time**. Since both stations need to send a file, the channel must be **time-shared** between the AŌåÆB and BŌåÆA transfers.

**Step 2: Compute transmission time for one file in one direction.**

$$\text{Time for 1 file} = \dfrac{\text{File size in bits}}{\text{Bandwidth}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Compute total time for both files.**
Since the two transfers must occur sequentially (Half Duplex constraint), the total time is the sum:

$$T_{total} = 0.8 + 0.8 = 1.6 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Alternative derivation (for verification).**
$$T_{total} = 2 \times \dfrac{8 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 0.8 = 1.6 \text{ s}$$

**Answer: Total time = 1.6 seconds**

**Mark Allocation for Sub-part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**
- 2 marks for recognizing the Half Duplex sequential constraint.
- 1 mark for unit conversion (MB to bits).
- 2 marks for the per-file transmission time calculation.
- 1 mark for the total time calculation.
- 1 mark for correct final answer with units.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Half Duplex Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Half Duplex with Full Duplex throughput:** Many students incorrectly state that a 10 Mbps Half Duplex link delivers 10 Mbps in *both* directions. It does not. In symmetric traffic, it delivers only **5 Mbps per direction**. Full 10 Mbps per direction requires **Full Duplex**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the round-trip factor in collision detection:** The minimum frame size derivation uses $2 T_p$, not $T_p$. The collision signal must travel to the far end *and back*. Students who write $L_{min} = d \cdot R / v$ will lose **half the marks** on that sub-question.
>
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion:** File sizes given in **MB (MegaBytes)** must be converted to **bits** (multiply by $8 \times 10^6$ for powers-of-10 MB, or $8 \times 2^{20}$ for powers-of-2 MiB). Mixing up MB and Mb (Megabits) is a frequent and costly error.
>
> 4. **Omitting the "simultaneous" qualifier in Half Duplex:** Half Duplex is **bidirectional but not simultaneous**. Students who write "Half Duplex is bidirectional" without the qualifier often lose 0.5ŌĆō1 mark on a definition question.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A KTU Part B question on transmission modes almost always expects a diagram. **A textual answer without a diagram typically loses 1ŌĆō2 marks** even if the text is correct.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong efficiency formula:** The formula $U = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$ is specific to **CSMA/CD Ethernet**. For a generic Half Duplex analysis (no collision protocol), the simpler $U = 1/(1 + 2a)$ applies. Mixing them up is a common pitfall.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 high-density revision block ŌĆö read this in the final 5 minutes before the exam.**

### ­¤öæ Core Concepts
- [x] **Half Duplex** = bidirectional but **NOT simultaneous** ŌĆö channel is time-shared.
- [x] **Simplex** = one-way only; **Full Duplex** = two-way simultaneous.
- [x] Half Duplex uses **one physical channel**; Full Duplex requires two (or echo-cancellation).

### ­¤öæ Key Formulas (Memorize!)
- [x] $T_t = L / R$ ŌĆö transmission delay
- [x] $T_p = d / v$ ŌĆö propagation delay
- [x] $L_{min} = 2dR / v$ ŌĆö minimum frame size for collision detection
- [x] $a = T_p / T_t$ ŌĆö efficiency parameter
- [x] $U = 1 / (1 + 6.44a)$ ŌĆö CSMA/CD Ethernet efficiency
- [x] $\eta_{HDX,symmetric} = B/2$ per direction

### ­¤öæ Canonical Examples to Quote in the Exam
- [x] **Walkie-Talkie** ŌĆö classic Half Duplex consumer example.
- [x] **10BASE5 / 10BASE2 Ethernet** ŌĆö Half Duplex with CSMA/CD.
- [x] **CB Radio, Marine VHF, Aviation Radio** ŌĆö Half Duplex due to spectrum scarcity.
- [x] **RS-485 / CAN Bus** ŌĆö Half Duplex industrial fieldbuses.
- [x] **Underwater acoustic modems** ŌĆö Half Duplex due to impractical echo cancellation.

### ­¤öæ Historical / Numerical Facts
- [x] **Minimum Ethernet frame size** = 64 bytes (512 bits).
- [x] **Maximum Ethernet frame size** = 1518 bytes (1522 with VLAN tag).
- [x] **Slot time (10 Mbps Ethernet)** = 51.2 ┬Ąs = 512 bit times.
- [x] **Maximum 10BASE5 segment length** = 500 m; **max network diameter** = 2500 m (with 4 repeaters).
- [x] **CSMA/CD acronym expansion:** Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection.

### ­¤öæ Common Comparisons (Always Cite in Answers)
- [x] Half Duplex vs. Full Duplex: **same cabling, half the per-direction throughput** (symmetric case).
- [x] Half Duplex vs. Simplex: **doubles the channel utilization** when bidirectional traffic exists.
- [x] CSMA/CD vs. Token Passing: **CSMA/CD is contention-based** (used in Ethernet); **Token Passing is deterministic** (used in Token Ring, FDDI).

### ­¤öæ Exam Strategy Tips
- [x] Always **draw a diagram** for transmission mode questions.
- [x] Always **state units explicitly** in numerical answers.
- [x] When asked to "differentiate," use a **table** for clarity and easy marking.
- [x] When asked to "derive," show **every step** ŌĆö partial marks are awarded for correct intermediate steps.
- [x] For "give an example" questions, give an **engineering example**, not a generic one (e.g., say "RS-485 industrial bus," not just "a network").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Full Duplex Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full Duplex Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Full Duplex Communication** (also called *duplex* or *full-duplex transmission*) is a bidirectional data communication mode in which **both connected devices can transmit and receive data simultaneously over the same physical link or channel**, without any temporal sharing of the medium. The two communication directions are physically or logically separated, allowing concurrent signal propagation in both directions without collision.

In the context of the **OSI Physical Layer (Layer 1)** and the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the TCP/IP hybrid model, full duplex is implemented through one of the following separation techniques:

- **Spatial Separation:** Independent wire pairs or fiber strands for each direction (e.g., separate TX and RX lines in 1000BASE-T Ethernet).
- **Frequency Division Separation:** Distinct frequency bands allocated to upstream and downstream traffic.
- **Time-Slot Reservation:** Predetermined non-overlapping time intervals (rare in true full duplex, more common in TDM).
- **Wavelength Division (WDM):** Different light wavelengths in fiber optic links.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Full duplex is a transmission mode in which two stations can send and receive data concurrently, effectively **doubling the aggregate throughput** of a single physical link when compared to half duplex under the same signal conditions.

## Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **two-lane road** where one lane carries cars from City A to City B, and a parallel lane carries cars from City B to City A **at the same time**. Traffic never has to stop and wait for the opposite direction to clear.

Now contrast this with a **single-lane rural bridge** where only one car can cross at a time, and cars from the other side must wait ŌĆö this is **half-duplex**.

A **simplex** connection is like a **one-way street** ŌĆö only the city traffic department can broadcast announcements, and drivers can only listen.

A **full-duplex** phone call is the perfect everyday analogy: you and your friend can both speak and listen at the same time, just like a real conversation in a noisy coffee shop. The voice from your microphone reaches the earpiece of your friend instantly while his voice reaches your earpiece ŌĆö there is no "your turn, my turn" protocol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters in KTU Exams:**
> Examiners frequently test the distinction between **Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex** as a 3-mark definition question. Remember: *Full duplex = simultaneous, two independent channels, double throughput potential.*

## Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Maximum theoretical channel capacity | $C$ | bits/second (bps) |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $S/N$ | Dimensionless ratio (linear) |
| Number of signal levels | $M$ | Pure number |
| Number of independent channels | $N$ | $N = 2$ (for true full duplex) |

> [!TIP]
> The single most important relationship for KTU problems is **Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem** combined with the **full-duplex multiplier** of **2**, giving total link throughput $C_{FD} = 2 \cdot B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown ŌĆö How Full Duplex Works

A full-duplex link is architecturally different from a half-duplex link in three fundamental ways:

- **Independent Receiver and Transmitter Hardware:** Each station has a dedicated **Transmitter (TX)** circuit and a dedicated **Receiver (RX)** circuit operating concurrently. There is no need to "switch" the line direction.
- **Physical Channel Isolation:** The medium must support two-way signal flow. In copper twisted pair, this is done by using **separate pairs** (e.g., Pins 1-2 for TX, Pins 3-6 for RX in 10/100BASE-TX). In fiber, it is done with **two strands** (one for each direction).
- **No Collision Detection Required:** Because the channels are physically separate, **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** becomes unnecessary. This is why **modern switched Ethernet operates in full duplex by default** and why it achieves near-wire-speed throughput.

## The "Why" Behind Full Duplex

The engineering motivation for full duplex is **link efficiency**. In half duplex, the link is idle roughly **50%** of the time because only one direction transmits at any moment. Full duplex removes this idle penalty, allowing the same physical hardware to carry **twice the useful data rate** without increasing the bandwidth.

## Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Transmission Modes

| Property | Simplex | Half Duplex | Full Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direction of Data Flow | One direction only | Both, but **not at the same time** | Both, **simultaneously** |
| Example | TV broadcast, Radio | Walkie-Talkie, Hub-based Ethernet | Telephone call, Switched Ethernet |
| Channel Count | 1 (unidirectional) | 1 (bidirectional, shared) | 2 (one per direction) |
| Throughput Multiplier | $1 \times C$ | $\approx 0.5 \times C$ (practical) | $2 \times C$ (ideal) |
| Collision Possible? | No | Yes (CSMA/CD required) | **No** (in modern switched full duplex) |
| Hardware Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shannon Capacity (single channel) | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Maximum error-free data rate in bps |
| Nyquist Bit Rate (noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | Maximum symbol rate without noise |
| Full Duplex Total Capacity | $C_{FD} = 2B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Both directions combined |
| Full Duplex Throughput Efficiency | $\eta_{FD} = \dfrac{C_{FD}}{C_{max}} \times 100\%$ | Often equals 100% in modern full duplex |
| Half Duplex Practical Capacity | $C_{HD} \approx B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Roughly half of full duplex |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) | $BDP = B \times t_{prop}$ | Pipeline depth for full-duplex streaming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT write $C_{FD} = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. The factor of **2** is mandatory. Forgetting it is the single most common error reported by KTU valuation teams on this topic.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Center Backbones:** 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps, and 400 Gbps Ethernet links operate exclusively in full duplex to maximize fabric throughput.
- **5G and LTE Cellular:** Use paired frequency bands (FDD ŌĆö Frequency Division Duplex) to achieve full-duplex-like simultaneous upload and download.
- **Submarine Fiber Cables:** Use Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing (DWDM) where one set of wavelengths goes East-to-West and another goes West-to-East, giving true full duplex over a single fiber pair.
- **Voice over IP (VoIP):** RTP streams require full-duplex audio paths for natural conversation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Total Capacity of a Full-Duplex Link from Shannon's Theorem

We begin with the standard Shannon-Hartley theorem for a single noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hertz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$.

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

This gives the **maximum achievable data rate** in one direction. For a full-duplex link, the engineering reality is that we deploy **two such channels** ŌĆö one for each direction. Because these channels are physically isolated (e.g., separate wire pairs, separate wavelengths), they are statistically independent, so their capacities **add linearly**.

$$C_{FD} = C_{forward} + C_{reverse}$$

Substituting Shannon's formula into each term, with the assumption that both directions have equal bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:

$$C_{FD} = B \log_2(1 + S/N) + B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Combine like terms using the distributive law of logarithms:**

$$C_{FD} = 2B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express units clearly:**

$$C_{FD} \; [\text{bits/second}] = 2 \times B \; [\text{Hz}] \times \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \; [\text{dimensionless}]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Real-world interpretation:**

The factor of **2** appears because full duplex doubles the *spatial* utilization of the medium. There is no change in $B$ or $S/N$ ŌĆö those are properties of the channel, not the direction of flow.

## Derivation 2: Throughput Efficiency Comparison (Half Duplex vs Full Duplex)
