We define throughput efficiency $\eta$ as the ratio of achieved useful data rate to the maximum theoretical rate for the given hardware.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define baseline.** For a single physical channel with capacity $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$, the hardware is *physically* capable of supporting $C$ bps in one direction. The factor-of-2 question is: how much of this capability is realized in each duplex mode?

**Step 2 ŌĆö Half-duplex case.** In a perfectly scheduled half-duplex link with no overhead, the link is used for transmission only **half the time per direction**. Therefore:

$$\eta_{HD} = \frac{C_{HD}}{2C} = \frac{B \log_2(1 + S/N)}{2B \log_2(1 + S/N)} = 0.5 \; \text{or} \; 50\%$$

In practice, due to guard bands, turnaround delays, and CSMA/CD backoff, $\eta_{HD}$ drops to **30%ŌĆō40%** in real Ethernet.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Full-duplex case.** In full duplex, both directions transmit simultaneously, so each direction realizes the full $C$ bps:

$$\eta_{FD} = \frac{C_{FD,per\_direction}}{C} = \frac{B \log_2(1 + S/N)}{B \log_2(1 + S/N)} = 1.0 \; \text{or} \; 100\%$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Net improvement.** The ratio of full-duplex to half-duplex throughput is:

$$\frac{\eta_{FD}}{\eta_{HD}} = \frac{1.0}{0.5} = 2.0$$

This **2├Ś** multiplier is the entire justification for upgrading from a shared hub (half duplex) to a switched network (full duplex).

## Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A full-duplex link has a bandwidth of $B = 4 \text{ kHz}$ and an $S/N$ ratio of $30 \text{ dB}$. Compute the total capacity of the link in both directions combined.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert $S/N$ from decibels to linear scale.**

The decibel formula for power ratio is:

$$(S/N)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$$

Solving for the linear ratio:

$$S/N = 10^{(S/N)_{dB}/10}$$

$$S/N = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Shannon's formula to a single channel.**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$C = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the logarithm.**

$$\log_2(1001) \approx \log_2(1024) = 10 \text{ bits/Hz}$$

(Using the exact value: $\log_2(1001) = 9.967$ bits/Hz, but KTU commonly accepts the approximation.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute single-channel capacity.**

$$C \approx 4000 \times 10 = 40{,}000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the full-duplex multiplier.**

$$C_{FD} = 2 \times 40{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ bps} = 80 \text{ kbps}$$

**Final Answer:** The full-duplex link supports a combined bidirectional throughput of **80 kbps**, with each direction independently capable of 40 kbps.

## Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Each Formula

| Scenario | Correct Formula | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Noiseless full-duplex link with $M$ levels | $C_{FD} = 2 \times 2B \log_2(M)$ | Use Nyquist, multiply by 2 |
| Noisy full-duplex link | $C_{FD} = 2B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Use Shannon, multiply by 2 |
| Half-duplex noisy link | $C_{HD} = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Single channel, time-shared |
| $S/N$ given in dB | Convert first: $S/N = 10^{dB/10}$ | Always convert before plugging in |
| Bandwidth-delay product for streaming | $BDP = B \times t_{prop}$ | Determines buffer size, not capacity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Full-Duplex Link Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph StationA["STATION A"]
        TX_A["Transmitter TX-A"]
        RX_A["Receiver RX-A"]
        AppA["Application Layer Data"]
    end

    subgraph MediumA["PHYSICAL MEDIUM"]
        FwdChan["Forward Channel A to B"]
        RevChan["Reverse Channel B to A"]
    end

    subgraph StationB["STATION B"]
        TX_B["Transmitter TX-B"]
        RX_B["Receiver RX-B"]
        AppB["Application Layer Data"]
    end

    AppA --> TX_A
    TX_A --> FwdChan
    FwdChan --> RX_B
    RX_B --> AppB

    AppB --> TX_B
    TX_B --> RevChan
    RevChan --> RX_A
    RX_A --> AppA

    FwdChan -.Independent Physical Path.-> RevChan
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **Forward Channel AŌåÆB** and the **Reverse Channel BŌåÆA** are drawn as two distinct paths.
- Each station has both a **Transmitter (TX)** and a **Receiver (RX)** active simultaneously.
- The dashed line between the two channels emphasizes that they are **physically independent** ŌĆö this is the defining property of true full duplex.

## Diagram 2: Comparison Flow ŌĆö Simplex, Half Duplex, Full Duplex

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Transmission Mode Selection"]
    Start --> S1{"Direction of Flow?"}

    S1 -->|"Only One Way"| Simplex["SIMPLEX MODE\nExample: TV Broadcast\nChannel: 1 unidirectional\nThroughput: 1x C"]
    S1 -->|"Both Ways"| S2{"Simultaneous?"}

    S2 -->|"No, Time-Shared"| HalfDup["HALF DUPLEX MODE\nExample: Walkie Talkie\nChannel: 1 bidirectional\nThroughput: ~0.5x C\nCollision Possible"]
    S2 -->|"Yes, Parallel"| FullDup["FULL DUPLEX MODE\nExample: Telephone Call\nChannels: 2 independent\nThroughput: 2x C\nNo Collision"]

    Simplex --> Result["Select Channel Type"]
    HalfDup --> Result
    FullDup --> Result

    Result --> R1["Single Pair Copper"]
    Result --> R2["Fiber Pair"]
    Result --> R3["FDD Frequency Bands"]
    Result --> R4["Separate Wavelengths WDM"]
```

## Diagram 3: Time-Line Visualization (Bidirectional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Timeline["TIME AXIS t0 to t5"]
        direction LR
        T0["t0"] --> T1["t1"] --> T2["t2"] --> T3["t3"] --> T4["t4"] --> T5["t5"]
    end

    subgraph Station_A_Channel["STATION A TRANSMITS"]
        A1["Frame 1"] --- A2["Frame 2"] --- A3["Frame 3"] --- A4["Frame 4"] --- A5["Frame 5"]
    end

    subgraph Station_B_Channel["STATION B TRANSMITS"]
        B1["Frame 1"] --- B2["Frame 2"] --- B3["Frame 3"] --- B4["Frame 4"] --- B5["Frame 5"]
    end
```

**Reading the timeline:**
- In the top channel, Station A sends Frame 1 at t0, Frame 2 at t1, and so on **without interruption**.
- In the bottom channel, Station B does the same **at the same instants**.
- This is the visual proof of **simultaneity** ŌĆö the hallmark of full duplex.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Full Duplex Communication. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper]

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Response):**

> **Full Duplex Communication** is a bidirectional data transmission mode in which two connected devices can **transmit and receive data simultaneously** over the link. Unlike half duplex, the channel is not time-shared between directions; instead, two independent physical or logical paths are used ŌĆö one for each direction. This allows the total link throughput to reach **twice** the capacity of a single channel, and it eliminates the need for collision detection mechanisms like CSMA/CD. A common real-world example is a **telephone conversation** or a **switched full-duplex Ethernet** link.

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
- (1 mark) Correct definition mentioning simultaneity
- (1 mark) Distinguishing from half-duplex (independent channels)
- (1 mark) Valid real-world example

---

### Question 2: Compare Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex with one example each. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (Tabular 3-Mark Response):**

| Mode | Data Flow | Simultaneous? | Example |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Simplex | One direction only | No | Television broadcast, Radio |
| Half Duplex | Both directions | No, time-shared | Walkie-Talkie, Hub-based Ethernet |
| Full Duplex | Both directions | Yes, simultaneous | Telephone, Switched Ethernet |

Full duplex offers the highest throughput efficiency and is the standard mode in all modern Layer-2 switched networks.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jul 2024, Module 1 Set A]

> **(a)** Explain the three modes of data transmission (Simplex, Half Duplex, Full Duplex) with suitable diagrams and one real-world example for each. Discuss the role of full duplex in modern switched Ethernet networks. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A communication channel has a bandwidth of $6 \text{ kHz}$ and a signal-to-noise ratio of $1023$. Compute the maximum data rate supported by: (i) a single channel using Shannon's theorem, (ii) a full-duplex link using the same channel, and (iii) the percentage improvement in throughput over a half-duplex link. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**1. Simplex Mode (2 marks):**
- Definition: One-way communication only.
- Diagram: A single arrow from Sender to Receiver.
- Example: TV broadcast, radio, keyboard-to-monitor.
- Hardware: Only TX at sender, only RX at receiver.

**2. Half Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
- Definition: Two-way communication, but only one direction at a time.
- Diagram: Bidirectional arrow with a time-slot indicator.
- Example: Walkie-talkie ("over" protocol), legacy hub-based 10BASE-T Ethernet.
- Mechanism: Both ends have TX and RX, but a single channel is shared using CSMA/CD.

**3. Full Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
- Definition: Two-way simultaneous communication.
- Diagram: Two parallel arrows between stations.
- Example: Telephone, modern switched Gigabit Ethernet, fiber links.
- Mechanism: Two physically independent channels (separate pairs or wavelengths).

**4. Role in Modern Ethernet (1 mark):**
Switched Ethernet uses full duplex to eliminate collisions. CSMA/CD is disabled, and the switch provides dedicated bandwidth to each port. This enables **wire-speed** forwarding with no contention, doubling the effective throughput per link.

#### Model Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Given:** $B = 6 \text{ kHz} = 6000 \text{ Hz}$, $S/N = 1023$.

**(i) Single-channel Shannon capacity (2 marks):**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$C = 6000 \times \log_2(1 + 1023)$$

$$C = 6000 \times \log_2(1024)$$

Since $2^{10} = 1024$, we have $\log_2(1024) = 10$ bits/Hz.

$$C = 6000 \times 10 = 60{,}000 \text{ bps} = 60 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Full-duplex total capacity (3 marks):**

$$C_{FD} = 2 \times B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$C_{FD} = 2 \times 60{,}000 = 120{,}000 \text{ bps} = 120 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Percentage improvement over half duplex (2 marks):**

Half-duplex effective throughput $= 60$ kbps (time-shared single channel).

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{C_{FD} - C_{HD}}{C_{HD}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{120{,}000 - 60{,}000}{60{,}000} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Final Answer:** Single channel = 60 kbps, Full duplex = 120 kbps, Improvement over half duplex = **100%**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jul 2024, Module 1 Set B]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how full-duplex communication is achieved in a 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet link. Discuss the role of separate transmit and receive pairs and how collisions are eliminated. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two stations A and B communicate over a full-duplex link with a bandwidth of $8 \text{ kHz}$ and an $S/N$ of $63$ (linear). (i) Determine the maximum theoretical data rate in each direction. (ii) If a file of 1.2 megabits is transmitted from A to B, calculate the minimum transmission time. (iii) Comment on the actual transmission time considering real-world protocol overhead. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
```
[Station A]                          [Station B]
   |                                       |
   |---TX Pair (Pins 1,2)---> [Switch] ---RX Pair (Pins 3,6)---|
   |                                       |
   |---RX Pair (Pins 3,6)---- [Switch] ---TX Pair (Pins 1,2)---|
```

In 1000BASE-T, **four twisted pairs** are used, each pair capable of 250 Mbps in full duplex. All four pairs operate simultaneously in both directions using **echo cancellation** and **PAM-5 signaling**. The physical separation of TX and RX paths (separate wire pairs) means that the two directions of communication never interfere with each other.

**Collision Elimination (2 marks):**
Because each direction has a dedicated physical path, two signals travelling in opposite directions **cannot collide on the wire**. CSMA/CD is therefore **disabled** in full-duplex switched Ethernet. This allows the link to operate at wire speed without backoff delays.

**Practical Advantages (2 marks):**
- 100% throughput efficiency.
- No half-duplex collision domain.
- Full wire-speed forwarding at the switch.
- Predictable latency.

#### Model Solution to (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Given:** $B = 8 \text{ kHz} = 8000 \text{ Hz}$, $S/N = 63$.

**(i) Maximum data rate per direction (3 marks):**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$C = 8000 \times \log_2(1 + 63) = 8000 \times \log_2(64)$$

Since $2^6 = 64$, we have $\log_2(64) = 6$ bits/Hz.

$$C = 8000 \times 6 = 48{,}000 \text{ bps} = 48 \text{ kbps per direction}$$

**(ii) Minimum transmission time for 1.2 Mb file (2 marks):**

$$t_{min} = \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Data Rate}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{48{,}000 \text{ bps}}$$

$$t_{min} = 25 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iii) Real-world considerations (2 marks):**
In practice, the actual transmission time will be **larger** than 25 seconds because of:
- **Protocol overhead:** Ethernet headers, IP headers, TCP headers, and ACKs.
- **Propagation delay:** Signal travel time across the medium.
- **Retransmissions:** TCP may retransmit lost packets.
- **Processing delay:** At intermediate switches and routers.
- A typical rule of thumb is that effective throughput is **70%ŌĆō80%** of theoretical capacity, so the actual time might be closer to **31ŌĆō36 seconds**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** When asked for full-duplex capacity, students often write $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ instead of $C_{FD} = 2B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. This loses **2 to 3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** $S/N$ in dB must be converted to linear scale **before** substitution. Writing $\log_2(1 + 30)$ instead of $\log_2(1 + 1000)$ is a classic mistake.
> 3. **Confusing bit rate with baud rate:** Always clarify whether the answer is in bps or symbols/sec.
> 4. **Confusing throughput with capacity:** Shannon gives the *maximum possible* rate, not the *guaranteed* rate. Real systems are always lower.
> 5. **Writing "half duplex = full duplex" in diagrams:** The diagram must clearly show the *simultaneity* of full duplex with two parallel arrows or two separate channels. A single bidirectional arrow is **half duplex**, not full duplex.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full Duplex = Simultaneous, Bidirectional, Two Independent Channels.**
- **Key Formula:** $C_{FD} = 2B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ ŌĆö never forget the multiplier of **2**.
- **Single Channel Formula:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ ŌĆö Shannon-Hartley Theorem.
- **dB to Linear Conversion:** $S/N = 10^{dB/10}$ (always convert first).
- **Real-World Examples:** Telephone call, Switched Ethernet, Fiber optic links, FDD cellular.
- **Comparison Shortcut:**
  - Simplex ŌåÆ 1-way (TV)
  - Half Duplex ŌåÆ 2-way time-shared (Walkie-Talkie)
  - Full Duplex ŌåÆ 2-way simultaneous (Phone)
- **Why Full Duplex Matters in Modern Networks:**
  - 100% channel efficiency per direction.
  - **Eliminates collisions** (CSMA/CD disabled).
  - **Doubles the aggregate link throughput** compared to half duplex.
- **Channel Separation Methods:** Spatial (wire pairs), Frequency (FDD), Wavelength (WDM).
- **Common KTU Tag Words:** "Bidirectional", "Simultaneous", "Independent", "No collision".
- **Quick Memory Hook:** "**FD = 2├ŚC, HD = 1├ŚC, Simplex = 1├ŚC one-way**".
- **Always draw two parallel arrows** for full duplex diagrams ŌĆö single arrow = half duplex.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Switching?

In any computer network, data must travel from a **source** to a **destination** through a mesh of intermediate nodes (routers/switches). The mechanism that determines **how this data is forwarded, routed, and managed** across these intermediate nodes is called **Switching**.

Switching is the process of **receiving incoming data, deciding the outgoing path, and forwarding it** to the next node toward its final destination. It is the foundational layer upon which entire networks like the Internet, PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network), and cellular systems operate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Switching is the technique used by network nodes to receive, buffer, process, and forward data units (messages, packets, or cells) from an incoming link to an appropriate outgoing link so that they reach the correct destination.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Classical Switching Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) focuses on three primary switching paradigms:

### ­¤ö╣ A) Circuit Switching
A **dedicated physical communication path** (called a *circuit*) is established between the sender and receiver **before any data is transmitted**. The path remains exclusively reserved for the entire duration of the conversation, even during silence.

### ­¤ö╣ B) Packet Switching
Data is broken into small, fixed-or-variable-size **packets**, each carrying a header with addressing information. Each packet is **independently routed** through the network using a **store-and-forward** mechanism.

### ­¤ö╣ C) Message Switching (Brief Mention)
The **entire message** is treated as one unit and stored at each intermediate node before being forwarded. It is essentially a *predecessor* of packet switching, rarely used in modern networks.

---

## 1.3 ­¤ÆĪ Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to **send a parcel from Delhi to Kochi**:

| Method | Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Circuit Switching** | You **book an entire dedicated truck** that drives straight from Delhi to Kochi, exclusively for your parcel. The truck waits for you to load/unload, regardless of how much you actually send. |
| **Packet Switching** | You **break the parcel into many small boxes**. Each box is shipped via the *fastest available courier* at that moment. Boxes may take different routes and arrive in any order, but the courier reassembles them. |
| **Message Switching** | The **whole parcel hops from one warehouse to another** (Bangalore ŌåÆ Chennai ŌåÆ Kochi), waiting in full at each warehouse before being dispatched. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Circuit switching = **predictable but wasteful** path. Packet switching = **efficient but variable** path.

---

## 1.4 Why is Switching Important in KTU 2024?

The choice of switching technique directly affects:
- **Latency (Delay)** ŌĆö measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**
- **Throughput** ŌĆö measured in **bits per second (bps)**
- **Resource Utilization** ŌĆö measured as a **percentage (%)**
- **Fairness & Quality of Service (QoS)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Public Telephone Network (PSTN) traditionally uses **Circuit Switching**, while the modern **Internet** predominantly uses **Packet Switching**. Understanding both is critical for Module 1's CO1 (Understand the fundamentals of data communication and network architectures).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Switching ŌĆö Operational Deep Dive

Circuit switching operates in **three distinct phases**:

### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Phase 1: Circuit Establishment
- The source sends a **setup request** to the destination through a series of intermediate switches.
- Each switch **reserves a dedicated physical channel** (a *circuit*) on each outgoing link for this connection.
- Once the destination acknowledges, a **dedicated end-to-end path** is locked in.

### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Phase 2: Data Transfer
- Data flows through the established circuit.
- **No addressing or routing decisions** are needed at intermediate nodes ŌĆö they simply forward bits.
- Bandwidth is **guaranteed** and **constant** throughout.

### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Phase 3: Circuit Teardown
- After transmission, a **teardown signal** is propagated to release all reserved resources.

### ­¤ö¼ Multiplexing in Circuit Switching
Since dedicating a physical wire to every user is wasteful, circuit-switched networks use **multiplexing** to share a single link:

- **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM):** The link's frequency spectrum is divided into **bands**, each carrying one circuit. *(Used in analog telephone networks.)*
- **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM):** Time is divided into **slots (frames)**, and each circuit gets one slot per frame. *(Used in digital telephone systems like T1/E1.)*
- **Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM):** Used in optical fiber, where multiple light **wavelengths** share one fiber. *(Modern optical backbones.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In TDM, if a user is silent, their slot is **wasted** ŌĆö this is a major disadvantage that packet switching solves.

---

## 2.2 Packet Switching ŌĆö Operational Deep Dive

Packet switching is **statistical** in nature ŌĆö resources are allocated **only when a packet is actively being transmitted**.

### ­¤ö╣ Sub-Type 1: Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless)
- Each packet is **independently routed**.
- Packets of the same message may take **different paths** and arrive **out of order**.
- Routers maintain a **routing table** and decide the next hop per packet.
- *Example:* The modern **Internet Protocol (IP)** uses this model.

### ­¤ö╣ Sub-Type 2: Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented)
- A **virtual path** (logical connection) is established *before* data transfer.
- All packets of the session carry a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** rather than full destination addresses.
- Packets follow the **same path**, so they arrive **in order**.
- *Examples:* **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)** networks, **Frame Relay**, and historically **X.25**.

### ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ Store-and-Forward Mechanism
Each router must **receive the ENTIRE packet** into its buffer before forwarding it to the next link. This is the **store-and-forward** paradigm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The store-and-forward delay is what gives packet switching its variable latency ŌĆö unlike circuit switching's predictable constant delay.

---

## 2.3 ­¤ōŖ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation used throughout:** 
> - $L$ = packet size (bits) 
> - $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) 
> - $N$ = number of links (hops) in path 
> - $P$ = number of packets in a message 
> - $d$ = physical distance (meters) 
> - $s$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber) 
> - $T_{setup}$ = circuit setup time (s) 
> - $T_{teardown}$ = teardown time (s)

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission Delay (per packet) | $t_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds (s) | Both CS & PS |
| 2 | Propagation Delay (per link) | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds (s) | Both CS & PS |
| 3 | Total End-to-End Delay (PS, 1 pkt, N hops) | $D = N \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d_{total}}{s}$ | seconds (s) | Packet Switching |
| 4 | Total Delay (PS, P pkts, N hops) | $D = \left(N + P - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d_{total}}{s}$ | seconds (s) | Packet Switching |
| 5 | Total Delay (Circuit Switching) | $D_{CS} = T_{setup} + \dfrac{L}{R} + T_{teardown}$ | seconds (s) | Circuit Switching |
| 6 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $B \cdot D = R \cdot t_{prop}$ | bits | Both |
| 7 | Throughput (bottleneck-limited) | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N)$ | bps | Packet Switching |
| 8 | Effective Throughput (P packets) | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{D_{total}}$ | bps | Packet Switching |
| 9 | Link Utilization (single packet) | $U = \dfrac{t_{trans}}{t_{trans} + t_{prop}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Both |
| 10 | Number of simultaneous FDM channels | $N_{FDM} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{B_{channel}}$ | dimensionless | Circuit Switching |
| 11 | Number of simultaneous TDM slots | $N_{TDM} = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{slot}}$ | dimensionless | Circuit Switching |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU:** In pipeline (store-and-forward with multiple packets), the second packet arrives at the second router **exactly when the first packet leaves** the first router, leading to the $(N + P - 1)$ factor.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Switching Used** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional Voice Calls (PSTN)** | Circuit | Need constant bandwidth for real-time audio |
| **Internet Browsing (HTTP)** | Packet | Bursty traffic; efficiency matters |
| **Live Video Conferencing** | Hybrid (VoIP over Packet) | Codecs handle packet loss |
| **Banking/Stock Trading** | Circuit (leased lines) | Ultra-low latency & guaranteed bandwidth |
| **Mobile Networks (4G/5G)** | Packet (with QoS) | Statistical multiplexing for scalability |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 ­¤¦« Derivation: Total Delay for P Packets through N Hops (Packet Switching)

**Problem Setup:** A message of size $M$ bits is broken into $P$ equal-sized packets of size $L$ bits each (so $M = P \cdot L$). The message is sent across a path of $N$ links, each of bandwidth $R$ bps, with negligible propagation delay.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Time for the first packet to reach the destination:**

The first packet must be **fully received** at each of the $N$ links before it can be forwarded (store-and-forward). Thus, the time for the first packet to arrive at the destination is:

$$
t_{first} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Time for the remaining $(P - 1)$ packets to drain through the pipeline:**

Once the first packet reaches the destination, the remaining packets are pipelined. Every $\dfrac{L}{R}$ seconds, a new packet reaches the destination. So, the remaining $(P - 1)$ packets need:

$$
t_{remaining} = (P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total transmission delay (neglecting propagation):**

$$
D_{trans} = t_{first} + t_{remaining} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify by factoring out $\dfrac{L}{R}$:**

$$
D_{trans} = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Add the propagation delay for the full path** ($d_{total}$ = total distance across all $N$ links, $s$ = propagation speed in medium):

$$
\boxed{D_{total} = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d_{total}}{s}}
$$

---

## 3.2 ­¤¦« Derivation: Circuit Switching Delay vs. Packet Switching Delay

**Scenario:** Same message of size $M$ bits, same path of $N$ links of bandwidth $R$ bps.

### For Circuit Switching:
The full message $M$ is sent as one continuous stream through the established circuit. The transmission time is just $\dfrac{M}{R}$ (since the circuit spans all $N$ links as one logical channel):

$$
D_{CS} = T_{setup} + \frac{M}{R} + T_{teardown}
$$

### For Packet Switching:
The message is split into $P$ packets, each of size $L = M / P$ bits:

$$
D_{PS} = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d_{total}}{s}
$$

### Comparative Analysis:
Substituting $L = M / P$:

$$
D_{PS} = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{M}{P \cdot R} + \frac{d_{total}}{s}
$$

For very large $P$ (small packets):

$$
\lim_{P \to \infty} D_{PS} = \frac{N \cdot M}{P \cdot R} + \frac{M}{R} \approx \frac{M}{R} \quad \text{(same as CS, but no setup cost)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Inference:** Packet switching *eliminates* the costly $T_{setup} + T_{teardown}$ overhead and shares the link statistically ŌĆö making it superior for **bursty data** traffic.

---

## 3.3 ­¤¦« Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Question:** A 40,000-bit message is to be sent from Host A to Host B over a path of 4 links, each of bandwidth 100 kbps. Propagation delay per link is 20 ms. The message is split into 5 packets using packet switching. Find the total time to deliver the message. Compare with circuit switching (assume $T_{setup} = 0.5$ s, $T_{teardown} = 0.5$ s).

### Solution ŌĆö Packet Switching:

**Step 1:** Size of each packet: $L = \dfrac{40{,}000}{5} = 8{,}000$ bits.

**Step 2:** Transmission time per packet per link: $\dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.08$ s.

**Step 3:** Apply the formula $D = (N + P - 1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d_{total}}{s}$:

$$
D_{trans} = (4 + 5 - 1) \cdot 0.08 = 8 \cdot 0.08 = 0.64 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4:** Total propagation delay = 4 links ├Ś 20 ms = 80 ms = **0.08 s**.

**Step 5:** Total Packet Switching delay:

$$
\boxed{D_{PS} = 0.64 + 0.08 = 0.72 \text{ s}}
$$

### Solution ŌĆö Circuit Switching:

$$
D_{CS} = T_{setup} + \frac{M}{R} + T_{teardown} + t_{prop}
$$

$$
D_{CS} = 0.5 + \frac{40{,}000}{100{,}000} + 0.5 + 0.08 = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.5 + 0.08
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{CS} = 1.48 \text{ s}}
$$

### ­¤Åå Conclusion:
Packet switching delivers the message in **0.72 s**, while circuit switching takes **1.48 s** ŌĆö a **2├Ś speedup** because the setup/teardown overhead and lack of pipelining dominate the circuit-switched path.

---

## 3.4 ­¤ōŖ Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Circuit Switching** | **Packet Switching (Datagram)** | **Packet Switching (Virtual Circuit)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | Required | Not required | Required (lightweight) |
| Path | Fixed & dedicated | Variable per packet | Fixed virtual path |
| Resource reservation | Yes (dedicated) | No (statistical) | Yes (logical) |
| Delay type | Constant after setup | Variable | Variable |
| Packet order at destination | In-order (stream) | May be out-of-order | In-order |
| Header overhead | None (after setup) | High (full address per packet) | Low (VCI per packet) |
| Failure resilience | Poor (whole path fails) | High (alternate routes) | Moderate |
| Suitable for | Voice, real-time | Burst data, web | Mixed (ATM) |
| Bandwidth utilization | Poor (wasted if silent) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Example | PSTN, ISDN | Internet (IP) | ATM, Frame Relay, X.25 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ­¤ōł Diagram 1: Circuit Switching ŌĆö Three-Phase Lifecycle

This diagram illustrates the **three operational phases** of circuit switching across a path of 4 switches between Source and Destination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SOURCE HOST A"] --> B["SWITCH 1"]
    B --> C["SWITCH 2"]
    C --> D["SWITCH 3"]
    D --> E["SWITCH 4"]
    E --> F["DESTINATION HOST B"]

    A -. "PHASE 1 SETUP REQUEST" .-> B
    B -. "RESERVE CHANNEL" .-> C
    C -. "RESERVE CHANNEL" .-> D
    D -. "RESERVE CHANNEL" .-> E
    E -. "CONNECT ACK" .-> F

    F -. "PHASE 3 TEARDOWN SIGNAL" .-> E
    E -. "RELEASE CHANNEL" .-> D
    D -. "RELEASE CHANNEL" .-> C
    C -. "RELEASE CHANNEL" .-> B
    B -. "CONFIRM RELEASE" .-> A

    A2["PHASE 2 DATA TRANSFER CONTINUOUS DEDICATED STREAM"] -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = the **physical/dedicated data path** used in Phase 2. Dashed arrows = **control signals** (setup and teardown). Once the circuit is established, the data flows as a **continuous bit stream** with no addressing required at intermediate switches.

---

## 4.2 ­¤ōł Diagram 2: Datagram Packet Switching ŌĆö Independent Routing

This shows three packets of the same message taking **three different paths** through the network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["SOURCE HOST"] --> R1["ROUTER 1"]
    S --> R2["ROUTER 2"]
    R1 --> R3["ROUTER 3"]
    R1 --> R4["ROUTER 4"]
    R2 --> R5["ROUTER 5"]
    R2 --> R6["ROUTER 6"]
    R3 --> D["DESTINATION HOST"]
    R4 --> D
    R5 --> D
    R6 --> D

    P1["PACKET 1 ROUTE A"] -.-> S
    P2["PACKET 2 ROUTE B"] -.-> S
    P3["PACKET 3 ROUTE C"] -.-> S

    P1 -. "TAKES PATH 1" .-> R1
    P2 -. "TAKES PATH 2" .-> R2
    P3 -. "TAKES PATH 3" .-> R6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** In datagram mode, every router consults its **routing table** independently for *each* packet. The decision may differ per packet based on current congestion, link failures, or routing protocol metrics (e.g., OSPF cost, BGP policy).

---

## 4.3 ­¤ōł Diagram 3: Virtual Circuit Packet Switching ŌĆö Logical Path

This shows a **pre-established logical path** (Virtual Circuit Identifier: VCI = 47) used by all packets in the session.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOURCE HOST VCI 47"] --> SW1["SWITCH 1 LOOKUP VCI 47 TO PORT 2"]
    SW1 --> SW2["SWITCH 2 LOOKUP VCI 47 TO PORT 5"]
    SW2 --> SW3["SWITCH 3 LOOKUP VCI 47 TO PORT 1"]
    SW3 --> D["DESTINATION HOST"]

    P1["PACKET 1 HEADER VCI 47"] -.-> S
    P2["PACKET 2 HEADER VCI 47"] -.-> S
    P3["PACKET 3 HEADER VCI 47"] -.-> S

    D -. "VC RELEASE SIGNAL" .-> SW3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Virtual Circuit is a **hybrid** of circuit and packet switching. The *path* is pre-decided (like circuit), but resources are *not* reserved exclusively (like packet). This gives **low overhead per packet** + **predictable in-order delivery**.

---

## 4.4 ­¤ōł Diagram 4: TDM vs FDM Multiplexing in Circuit Switching

A visual comparison of how multiple circuits share a single physical link.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FDM["FREQUENCY DIVISION MULTIPLEXING FDM"]
        F1["FREQUENCY SLOT 1 VOICE CALL A"] --- F2["FREQUENCY SLOT 2 VOICE CALL B"] --- F3["FREQUENCY SLOT 3 VOICE CALL C"] --- F4["FREQUENCY SLOT 4 VOICE CALL D"]
    end

    subgraph TDM["TIME DIVISION MULTIPLEXING TDM"]
        direction LR
        T1["TIME SLOT 1 CALL A"] --> T2["TIME SLOT 2 CALL B"] --> T3["TIME SLOT 3 CALL C"] --> T4["TIME SLOT 4 CALL D"] --> T1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Be ready to **draw the TDM frame structure** showing N time slots per frame, each carrying one circuit's data. FDM is rarely used today; TDM evolved into **Statistical TDM (STDM)** which dynamically allocates slots ŌĆö a conceptual bridge to packet switching.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Circuit Switching. List its three phases.**
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**
Circuit switching is a switching technique in which a **dedicated physical communication path** is established between the source and destination *before* any data transmission begins. This path remains exclusively reserved for the entire duration of the session.

The three phases are:
1. **Circuit Establishment** ŌĆö A setup request propagates from source to destination, with each switch reserving a dedicated channel.
2. **Data Transfer** ŌĆö Data flows through the reserved circuit as a continuous stream, with no addressing overhead at intermediate nodes.
3. **Circuit Teardown** ŌĆö A disconnect signal releases all reserved resources once communication ends.

*Example:* Traditional **PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network)**.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Datagram and Virtual Circuit Packet Switching.**
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Datagram** | **Virtual Circuit** |
|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | Not required | Required (logical) |
| Addressing | Full source/destination per packet | Only VCI per packet |
| Routing decision | Per packet (independent) | Per connection (once) |
| Path | Variable | Fixed (logical) |
| Packet order at receiver | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed |
| Router state | Stateless | Stateful (VCI tables) |
| Example | Internet (IP) | ATM, Frame Relay |

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't confuse** "Packet Switching is faster than Circuit Switching" ŌĆö it depends on the workload. For long, continuous streams, circuit switching can be *more efficient* due to zero per-packet header overhead.
> - **Always** show the **$(N + P - 1)$ pipelining factor** in delay calculations ŌĆö students frequently write $N \cdot L/R$ and miss the remaining packets' pipeline time, losing **3 to 4 marks**.
> - **Don't forget** to include propagation delay in the *total* end-to-end delay ŌĆö this is the single most common mistake in KTU answer sheets.
> - For **comparison questions**, draw a *table*, not a paragraph ŌĆö tabular answers score higher in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question A: Switching Techniques Comparison & Delay Analysis (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compare Circuit Switching and Packet Switching across any SEVEN parameters. Explain the store-and-forward mechanism with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

Circuit switching and packet switching represent the two fundamental paradigms of network data transfer. A detailed comparison follows:

| **S.No** | **Parameter** | **Circuit Switching** | **Packet Switching** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Connection setup | Required (dedicated circuit) | Not required (datagram) or lightweight (VC) |
| 2 | Path | Fixed & reserved | Variable (datagram) or logical (VC) |
| 3 | Resource allocation | Dedicated | Statistical (on-demand) |
| 4 | Bandwidth utilization | Poor (idle if user silent) | Excellent (shared) |
| 5 | Addressing overhead | None (after setup) | Header per packet |
| 6 | Delay type | Constant after setup | Variable (depends on queue) |
| 7 | Suitable traffic type | Continuous, real-time (voice) | Bursty, data (web, email) |
| 8 | Example | PSTN, leased lines | Internet (IP) |

**Store-and-Forward Mechanism:**
In packet switching, each router must **receive the entire packet** into its local buffer (store) before transmitting it on the outgoing link (forward). This ensures that no packet is partially transmitted ŌĆö eliminating the need for reassembly at every hop.

> The diagram of store-and-forward (3 routers, 3 links) is shown below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOURCE SENDS PACKET L BITS"] --> R1["ROUTER 1 RECEIVES FULL PACKET BUFFERS IT"]
    R1 --> R2["ROUTER 2 RECEIVES FULL PACKET BUFFERS IT"]
    R2 --> R3["ROUTER 3 RECEIVES FULL PACKET BUFFERS IT"]
    R3 --> D["DESTINATION DELIVERED"]

    T1["TIME L OVER R FOR LINK 1"] -.-> R1
    T2["TIME L OVER R FOR LINK 2"] -.-> R2
    T3["TIME L OVER R FOR LINK 3"] -.-> R3
```

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A 1.5 Mbps link connects 4 packet switches. A message of 240,000 bits is to be sent from Switch A to Switch D. The message is segmented into packets of 10,000 bits each. Calculate (i) the total delay in packet switching (neglecting propagation) and (ii) the total delay if the entire message is sent as a single packet. Comment on the result.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $R = 1.5$ Mbps $= 1.5 \times 10^{6}$ bps
- Message size $M = 240{,}000$ bits
- Number of hops $N = 3$ (AŌåÆBŌåÆCŌåÆD uses 3 links)
- Number of packets $P = 240{,}000 / 10{,}000 = 24$ packets
- Packet size $L = 10{,}000$ bits

**Step 1:** Transmission time per packet per link:
$$
t_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{10{,}000}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Part (i):** Total delay with 24 packets (using $D = (N + P - 1) \cdot L/R$):
$$
D_{PS} = (3 + 24 - 1) \cdot 6.67 \text{ ms} = 26 \cdot 6.67 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
\boxed{D_{PS} = 173.42 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Part (ii):** Total delay with 1 single packet of 240,000 bits ($P = 1$, $L = 240{,}000$):
$$
t_{trans\_single} = \frac{240{,}000}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.16 \text{ s} = 160 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
D_{single} = (3 + 1 - 1) \cdot 160 \text{ ms} = 3 \cdot 160 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
\boxed{D_{single} = 480 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Comparison and Comment:**

| **Mode** | **Total Delay** |
|---|---|
| Segmented (24 packets) | **173.42 ms** Ō£ģ |
| Single packet (no segmentation) | **480 ms** ŌØī |

**Comment:** Segmentation into smaller packets **reduces the total delay by approximately 64%** (from 480 ms to 173.42 ms). This is because the **pipelining effect** allows multiple packets to be in transit simultaneously across the three links, rather than waiting for the entire large packet to be received at each router before forwarding. This is a fundamental advantage of packet switching for bulk data transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Marks Distribution:** (2 marks) Given data, (2 marks) Part (i) calculation, (2 marks) Part (ii) calculation, (1 mark) Comparison and comment.

---

### **Question B: Virtual Circuit vs Datagram & Numerical Analysis (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain Virtual Circuit packet switching with a suitable diagram. How is it different from datagram switching in terms of routing decisions and packet ordering?**

**Model Answer:**

**Virtual Circuit (VC) Packet Switching:**
In Virtual Circuit switching, a **logical connection** (called a *virtual circuit*) is established between the source and destination *before* data transfer begins. This is purely a **logical path** ŌĆö no physical resources are dedicated exclusively to the connection.

**Process:**
1. **VC Setup Phase:** The source sends a *CALL REQUEST* packet to the destination. Each switch along the path assigns a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** to this connection and records an entry in its VCI table mapping `(incoming_VCI, incoming_port) ŌåÆ (outgoing_VCI, outgoing_port)`.
2. **Data Transfer Phase:** Each data packet carries the **VCI** in its header. Switches look up the VCI in their table and forward the packet on the corresponding outgoing port (after swapping the VCI for the next hop). All packets follow the **same path**.
3. **VC Teardown Phase:** A *CLEAR REQUEST* packet removes all VCI entries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["SOURCE HOST VCI 12"] --> SW1["SWITCH 1 VCI 12 MAPS TO VCI 22 ON PORT 3"]
    SW1 --> SW2["SWITCH 2 VCI 22 MAPS TO VCI 78 ON PORT 5"]
    SW2 --> SW3["SWITCH 3 VCI 78 MAPS TO VCI 45 ON PORT 1"]
    SW3 --> D["DESTINATION HOST"]
```

**Differences from Datagram Switching:**

| **Aspect** | **Datagram** | **Virtual Circuit** |
|---|---|---|
| **Routing decision** | Made **per packet** at every router | Made **once** during setup; thereafter, simple VCI lookup |
| **Routing table** | Large (destination address based) | Small (VCI-based local mapping) |
| **Path** | Can differ per packet | Fixed for entire session |
| **Packet ordering** | **Not guaranteed** at destination; needs reassembly | **Guaranteed** ŌĆö packets arrive in send order |
| **Header overhead** | High (full source + destination) | Low (just VCI) |
| **Router state** | Stateless (simpler) | Stateful (VCI tables) |
| **Connection nature** | Connectionless | Connection-oriented (logically) |
| **Failure handling** | Graceful (reroute per packet) | All packets lost if path fails |
| **Setup delay** | None | Yes (VC setup) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The **Internet Protocol (IP)** is datagram-based. **Asynchronous Transfer Mode (ATM)** and **Frame Relay** historically used virtual circuits.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Two hosts A and B are connected via 3 links of 50 kbps each. They use circuit switching with a setup time of 1.2 seconds. Host A wants to send a 1 MB file to Host B. Calculate the total time for (i) Circuit Switching and (ii) Packet Switching with 10 equal packets. Propagation delay is 30 ms per link. State which is faster and why.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth per link $R = 50$ kbps $= 50 \times 10^3$ bps
- Number of links $N = 3$
- Setup time $T_{setup} = 1.2$ s
- File size $M = 1$ MB $= 8 \times 10^6$ bits
- Number of packets $P = 10$ ŌåÆ Packet size $L = 8 \times 10^5$ bits
- Propagation delay per link = 30 ms ŌåÆ Total propagation = $3 \times 30 = 90$ ms = 0.09 s

**Part (i): Circuit Switching:**

$$
D_{CS} = T_{setup} + \frac{M}{R} + T_{teardown} + t_{prop}
$$

Assuming $T_{teardown}$ is included in the total or negligible here, and including the total propagation:
$$
D_{CS} = 1.2 + \frac{8 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^3} + 0.09 = 1.2 + 160 + 0.09
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{CS} = 161.29 \text{ s}}
$$

**Part (ii): Packet Switching (10 packets, store-and-forward):**

Transmission time per packet per link:
$$
t_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8 \times 10^5}{50 \times 10^3} = 16 \text{ s}
$$

Total transmission delay (pipelined):
$$
D_{trans} = (N + P - 1) \cdot t_{trans} = (3 + 10 - 1) \cdot 16 = 12 \cdot 16 = 192 \text{ s}
$$

Total delay with propagation:
$$
D_{PS} = 192 + 0.09 = 192.09 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{PS} = 192.09 \text{ s}}
$$

**Comparison:**

| **Method** | **Total Time** |
|---|---|
| Circuit Switching | **161.29 s** Ō£ģ |
| Packet Switching | **192.09 s** ŌØī |

**Conclusion & Justification:**
In this scenario, **Circuit Switching is FASTER** (by ~30.8 s). This is because the file is **large and continuous** ŌĆö a single bulk transfer. The pipelining advantage of packet switching is offset by:
1. The large per-packet transmission time (16 s) on slow 50 kbps links.
2. The overhead of 10 separate transmission stages.
3. The absence of setup/teardown cost in circuit switching amortizes over the 160 s data transfer.

> This counter-intuitive result is a classic KTU question highlight: **Packet switching is NOT always faster** ŌĆö its advantage shines for **bursty, intermittent traffic** and **multi-user statistical multiplexing**, not for single-user bulk transfer on slow links.

---

## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **­¤ö┤ MUST REMEMBER for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- Ō£ģ **Circuit Switching** = 3 phases: **Setup ŌåÆ Transfer ŌåÆ Teardown**. Used in **PSTN**.
- Ō£ģ **Packet Switching** = **Store-and-Forward**. Two flavors: **Datagram** (connectionless, e.g., IP) and **Virtual Circuit** (connection-oriented, e.g., ATM).
- Ō£ģ **Master the formula:** $D_{PS} = (N + P - 1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d_{total}}{s}$ ŌĆö used in **almost every numerical** in KTU exams.
- Ō£ģ **Circuit Switching delay:** $D_{CS} = T_{setup} + \dfrac{M}{R} + T_{teardown} + t_{prop}$.
- Ō£ģ **FDM** = divide **frequency**; **TDM** = divide **time**; **WDM** = divide **wavelength** (optical).
- Ō£ģ **Throughput** in packet switching is limited by the **bottleneck link** (slowest link in path).
- Ō£ģ **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $B \cdot D = R \cdot t_{prop}$ tells you how many bits can "fill the pipe" ŌĆö important for understanding latency vs. capacity.
- Ō£ģ **Segmentation helps** by enabling **pipelining** across multiple links ŌĆö but only if packets are small relative to link capacity.
- Ō£ģ Packet switching has **no setup cost** but introduces **variable delay** and **packet reordering complexity**.
- Ō£ģ **Virtual Circuit** uses **VCI (Virtual Circuit Identifier)** for fast per-packet forwarding (no full address lookup).
- Ō£ģ **Datagram** is **stateless and resilient** ŌĆö but routers do heavy work (full address lookup per packet).
- Ō£ģ Always **include propagation delay** in end-to-end calculations ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** if missed.
- Ō£ģ For **comparison answers**, always use a **tabular format** with at least **6 to 8 parameters** ŌĆö KTU valuation rewards structured answers.
- Ō£ģ **Common pitfall:** Do NOT say "packet switching is always faster than circuit switching" ŌĆö it depends on traffic characteristics.
- Ō£ģ **Bandwidth utilization in CS** is poor because reserved slots are wasted during silence (especially in voice).
- Ō£ģ **Statistical multiplexing** in PS achieves near-100% utilization when many users share a link ŌĆö this is the **economic foundation of the Internet**.

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤ōÜ End of Topic: Switching ŌĆö Circuit & Packet. This content fully covers Module 1 of PCCGT302 (Computer Networks & Protocols) under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Refer to CO1 (Understand the layered architecture and fundamental concepts of computer networks) for outcome mapping.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networks: Network Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Networks: Network Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of computer networks, **Network Criteria** refer to the standardized set of qualitative and quantitative measures used to evaluate, classify, and benchmark the operational quality of a communication network. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCGT302 ŌĆō Computer Networks & Protocols**, a network is fundamentally assessed against three principal criteria:

1. **Performance**
2. **Reliability**
3. **Security**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō Module 1):**
> *"A network must be able to meet a certain expected level of performance, reliability, and security. These are the three primary criteria upon which every communication network is evaluated."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the internet as a **massive highway system** connecting cities:

- **Performance** is like the **speed limit and traffic flow** on the highway ŌĆö how fast can a car (data packet) travel from City A to City B, and how many cars can pass through per hour?
- **Reliability** is like the **structural integrity of the road** ŌĆö how often does it break down, and how quickly can it be repaired when it does?
- **Security** is like the **police patrol and toll booths** ŌĆö who is allowed on the road, and how well is the road protected from hijackers?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A network that is **fast but insecure** (like an open road with no police) is dangerous. A network that is **secure but slow** is frustrating. A good network balances **all three criteria** optimally.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars ŌĆö At a Glance

| Criterion | Core Question | Primary Metrics |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Performance** | How fast and efficiently does the network deliver data? | Throughput, Delay (Latency), Jitter, Bandwidth |
| **Reliability** | How consistently and dependably does the network operate? | MTBF, Availability, Failure Rate, Recovery Time |
| **Security** | How well is the network protected from threats? | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to articulate that **Performance** is typically measured transitively, **Reliability** is measured temporally (over time), and **Security** is measured structurally (by architecture and protocol).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Criterion

### 2.1.1 Performance

**Performance** is the measure of how efficiently a network delivers data from source to destination. It is evaluated using two main dimensions:

- **Transit Time (Delay / Latency):** The amount of time required for a message to travel from one point to another.
- **Response Time:** The total elapsed time between an inquiry and a response, especially in client-server architectures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sub-Metrics of Performance:**
> - **Throughput** ŌĆö actual rate of successful data delivery (bits/second)
> - **Bandwidth** ŌĆö theoretical maximum capacity of the link (bits/second)
> - **Latency** ŌĆö time taken for a single bit to traverse the network (seconds)
> - **Jitter** ŌĆö variation in packet delay over time
> - **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** ŌĆö time for a packet to go to destination and acknowledgment to return

### 2.1.2 Reliability

**Reliability** is the measure of how dependably a network performs its intended function over a period of time. It is concerned with:

- **Frequency of Failure** ŌĆö How often does the network fail?
- **Time to Recover** ŌĆö How quickly can the network be restored after a failure?
- **Catastrophic Failure Mode** ŌĆö What happens during a complete system breakdown?

### 2.1.3 Security

**Security** addresses the protection of data from unauthorized access, tampering, and destruction. It encompasses three core pillars of information security:

- **Confidentiality** ŌĆö Ensuring data is accessible only to authorized parties.
- **Integrity** ŌĆö Ensuring data is not altered during transmission.
- **Authentication** ŌĆö Verifying the identity of users and devices.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** and frequently appear in KTU University Exam Part A and Part B questions.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| $\text{Total Delay}$ | $D_{total} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | Sum of all delay components | seconds (s) |
| $d_{trans}$ | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay (L = packet length, R = link bandwidth) | seconds |
| $d_{prop}$ | $d_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | Propagation delay (d = distance, s = signal speed) | seconds |
| $\text{Throughput}$ | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$ | Bottleneck link rate | bits/second |
| $\text{End-to-End Delay}$ | $D_{e2e} = N \cdot d_{trans} + (N-1) \cdot d_{prop} + d_{proc}$ | Delay across N links | seconds |
| $\text{Bandwidth-Delay Product}$ | $\text{BDP} = R \times d_{prop}$ | Volume of data "in flight" | bits |
| $\text{Utilization}$ | $U = \dfrac{\text{Throughput}}{\text{Bandwidth}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of capacity used | percent |
| $\text{Availability}$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction of time system is operational | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering delay types:** **"P-Q-T-P"** ŌåÆ **P**rocessing, **Q**ueuing, **T**ransmission, **P**ropagation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, these criteria directly influence:
- **Network Service Providers (NSPs):** Use performance metrics in **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** with customers.
- **Data Center Design:** Reliability criteria dictate **redundancy levels** (N+1, 2N configurations).
- **Cybersecurity Firms:** Security criteria drive the implementation of **firewalls, IDS/IPS, and encryption protocols (TLS/SSL, IPsec)**.
- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** SLAs are typically defined as **99.9% availability ("three nines")** or higher.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuation, students are expected to connect theoretical criteria to **practical trade-offs** (e.g., higher security often adds overhead and reduces throughput).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Network Delay

The total delay experienced by a packet traversing a network is the sum of four distinct delay components at each node (router/switch).

$$
D_{total} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

### Step-by-Step Explanation of Each Component

**Step 1 ŌĆö Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$):**
The time required for the router to examine the packet's header, determine the outgoing link, and perform error checking.

$$
d_{proc} = \text{time to inspect header} + \text{time to check bit errors} + \text{time to determine output link}
$$

Typically ranges from **microseconds to milliseconds** in modern routers.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$):**
The time a packet waits in the router's buffer (queue) before being transmitted onto the link. This depends on the **traffic intensity** ($I$).

$$
d_{queue} = f(I) \quad \text{where} \quad I = \frac{L \cdot a}{R}
$$

Here, $L$ = packet length, $a$ = average arrival rate, $R$ = link bandwidth.

- If $I \approx 0$ ŌåÆ negligible queuing delay
- If $I \to 1$ ŌåÆ delay becomes very large
- If $I > 1$ ŌåÆ queue grows without bound (system overload)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$):**
The time required to push all of the packet's bits onto the link.

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of the packet in **bits**
- $R$ = transmission rate of the link in **bits/second**

**Step 4 ŌĆö Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$):**
The time required for a bit to travel from one end of the link to the other.

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = length of the physical link in **meters**
- $s$ = propagation speed in **meters/second** (typically $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber)

## 3.2 Worked Example: End-to-End Delay Across 3 Links

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU Part B problem** that frequently appears in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

**Given:**
- A packet of size $L = 1500$ bytes = $12{,}000$ bits
- Number of links: $N = 3$
- Each link has bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps
- Each link is $d = 2000$ km long
- Propagation speed $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Processing delay at each router $d_{proc} = 1$ ms
- Queuing delay $d_{queue} = 2$ ms per router

**Find:** Total end-to-end delay

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate transmission delay per link.

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 0.012 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate propagation delay per link.

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate delay at one router (one node).

$$
d_{node} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} = 1 + 2 + 12 = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate total end-to-end delay for 3 routers and 3 links.

$$
D_{total} = 3 \cdot d_{node} + 3 \cdot d_{prop} = 3(15) + 3(10) = 45 + 30 = 75 \text{ ms}
$$

### Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Delay Distribution

| Component | Symbol | Value (per hop) | Total (3 hops) | Percentage |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|----------------|------------|
| Processing | $d_{proc}$ | 1 ms | 3 ms | 4.0% |
| Queuing | $d_{queue}$ | 2 ms | 6 ms | 8.0% |
| Transmission | $d_{trans}$ | 12 ms | 36 ms | 48.0% |
| Propagation | $d_{prop}$ | 10 ms | 30 ms | 40.0% |
| **Total** | $D_{total}$ | **29 ms** | **75 ms** | **100%** |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** In this example, **transmission delay dominates** (48%). In contrast, for long-distance satellite links, **propagation delay dominates** (can be 250+ ms for geostationary orbits).

## 3.3 Derivation: Bottleneck Throughput

When a file of size $F$ is transmitted across a path with $N$ links of rates $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N$, the **end-to-end throughput** is limited by the **slowest link** (bottleneck).

$$
\text{Throughput}_{e2e} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_N)
$$

**Derivation logic:**
The throughput is constrained by whichever link delivers bits at the lowest rate, because all bits must traverse every link sequentially. Even if one link is gigabit, the path's effective rate is throttled by the slowest member.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Adding bandwidth to non-bottleneck links does **NOT** increase end-to-end throughput. This is a classic KTU exam trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Network Criteria (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["NETWORK CRITERIA"] --> nodeB["PERFORMANCE"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["RELIABILITY"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["SECURITY"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Throughput"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Latency"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Jitter"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Bandwidth"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["MTBF"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["MTTR"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Availability"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Confidentiality"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Integrity"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Authentication"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Non-Repudiation"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeE["d_proc"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeF["d_queue"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeG["d_trans"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeH["d_prop"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three primary criteria branch into measurable sub-metrics. Performance breaks down into throughput, latency, jitter, and bandwidth, with latency further decomposed into the four delay types.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["SOURCE NODE"] --> trans1["TRANSMITTER"]
    trans1 --> link1["LINK 1: R1"]
    link1 --> router1["ROUTER 1"]
    router1 --> link2["LINK 2: R2"]
    link2 --> router2["ROUTER 2"]
    router2 --> link3["LINK 3: R3"]
    link3 --> rec["RECEIVER NODE"]
    router1 --> q1["QUEUE BUFFER"]
    router2 --> q2["QUEUE BUFFER"]
    rec --> metric1["THROUGHPUT: min R"]
    rec --> metric2["END-TO-END DELAY: d_total"]
    rec --> metric3["RELIABILITY: MTBF"]
    rec --> metric4["SECURITY: CIA TRIAD"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** Every intermediate node (router/switch) introduces additional **processing, queuing, transmission, and propagation** delay. End-to-end metrics are **accumulated** across all hops, while throughput is **constrained by the minimum** link rate.

## 4.3 The CIA Triad (Security Sub-Model)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS["SECURITY"] --> nodeC["CONFIDENTIALITY"]
    nodeS --> nodeI["INTEGRITY"]
    nodeS --> nodeA2["AUTHENTICATION"]
    nodeC --> exC["Example: Encryption TLS AES"]
    nodeI --> exI["Example: Hashing SHA-256"]
    nodeA2 --> exA["Example: Digital Certificates PKI"]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication) is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams. Always remember the order: **C-I-A**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the three network criteria. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three primary network criteria are:

1. **Performance:** Refers to the measure of how efficiently a network delivers data. It is evaluated through metrics like **transit time, response time, throughput, and delay**. A high-performance network minimizes delay and maximizes throughput.

2. **Reliability:** Refers to the measure of how dependably a network operates over time. It is evaluated through metrics like **frequency of failure, time to recover from failure, and catastrophic failure mode**. A reliable network has high availability (e.g., 99.999% "five nines").

3. **Security:** Refers to the protection of data from unauthorized access, modification, or destruction. It encompasses **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation**. A secure network prevents data breaches and ensures privacy.

### Question 2: Differentiate between throughput and bandwidth. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Throughput | Bandwidth |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| Definition | Actual rate of successful data delivery | Theoretical maximum capacity of a link |
| Nature | Practical, measured | Theoretical, designed |
| Unit | bits/second (bps) | bits/second (bps) |
| Relation | Always $\leq$ bandwidth | Upper bound on throughput |
| Affected by | Congestion, errors, protocol overhead | Physical medium properties |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** *Bandwidth is the size of the pipe; throughput is how much water actually flows through it.*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Delay Analysis [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the four types of delay encountered in packet-switched networks with relevant formulas. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

In a packet-switched network, a packet experiences four types of delay at each node:

**1. Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$):**
The time taken by the router to examine the packet header, check for bit errors, and determine the appropriate output link. Modern routers use hardware-based forwarding to minimize this delay to **microseconds**.

$$
d_{proc} = \text{Header inspection time} + \text{Error check time} + \text{Routing lookup time}
$$

**2. Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$):**
The time a packet waits in the router's output buffer before being transmitted. It depends on the **traffic intensity** $I = \frac{La}{R}$.

- If $I \approx 0$: negligible delay
- If $I \to 1$: large delay
- If $I > 1$: infinite delay (system overloaded)

**3. Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$):**
The time required to push all bits of the packet onto the transmission link.

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

Where $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps).

**4. Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$):**
The time for a bit to travel from the sender to the receiver across the physical medium.

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s}
$$

Where $d$ = link length (m), $s$ = signal propagation speed (m/s).

**Total nodal delay:**

$$
D_{total} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}
$$

---

**(b)** A packet of size 4000 bytes traverses 4 links, each of length 1000 km with bandwidth 2 Mbps. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, processing delay is 0.5 ms per router, and queuing delay is 1 ms per router. Calculate the total end-to-end delay. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 4000$ bytes $= 32{,}000$ bits
- $N = 4$ links
- $R = 2$ Mbps $= 2 \times 10^6$ bps
- $d = 1000$ km $= 10^6$ m per link
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $d_{proc} = 0.5$ ms per router
- $d_{queue} = 1$ ms per router

**Step 1: Transmission delay**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{32{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Propagation delay**

$$
d_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Delay at each router (3 routers for 4 links)**

$$
d_{node} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} = 0.5 + 1 + 16 = 17.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Total end-to-end delay**

$$
D_{e2e} = 3 \times d_{node} + 4 \times d_{prop} = 3(17.5) + 4(5) = 52.5 + 20 = 72.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $D_{e2e} = 72.5$ ms

---

### Question B: Network Criteria and Trade-offs [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three network criteria in detail with suitable examples. How are they interrelated? Discuss the trade-offs among them. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**The Three Network Criteria:**

**1. Performance:**
Performance measures the speed and efficiency of data delivery. Key metrics include:
- **Throughput** (e.g., 100 Mbps effective rate)
- **Latency** (e.g., 20 ms ping time)
- **Jitter** (variation in delay, critical for VoIP)
- **Bandwidth** (e.g., 1 Gbps link capacity)

*Example:* A fiber-optic backbone has higher throughput than a dial-up modem.

**2. Reliability:**
Reliability measures consistency of operation over time. Key metrics include:
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operational time before failure
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average recovery time
- **Availability:** Fraction of time system is operational

*Example:* Enterprise servers use redundant power supplies to improve MTBF.

**3. Security:**
Security protects data from unauthorized access. Encompasses:
- **Confidentiality:** Encryption (e.g., AES-256)
- **Integrity:** Hashing (e.g., SHA-256)
- **Authentication:** Digital certificates, passwords, biometrics

*Example:* HTTPS uses TLS to ensure secure web communication.

**Interrelation and Trade-offs:**

| Trade-off | Effect |
|-----------|--------|
| **Security vs Performance** | Encryption adds computational overhead, reducing throughput. |
| **Reliability vs Cost** | Higher redundancy (e.g., 2N power) increases cost. |
| **Performance vs Reliability** | Aggressive caching improves speed but may serve stale data. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe the criteria as **independent**. In reality, they are deeply **interdependent** ŌĆö improving one may degrade another. Always discuss trade-offs to earn full marks.

---

**(b)** Consider a file of 4 MB being transferred over a network path consisting of 3 links with rates 200 Mbps, 100 Mbps, and 50 Mbps. Calculate: (i) End-to-end throughput, (ii) End-to-end delay, (iii) Bandwidth-delay product for each link. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- File size $F = 4$ MB $= 4 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 32 \times 10^6$ bits
- Link rates: $R_1 = 200$ Mbps, $R_2 = 100$ Mbps, $R_3 = 50$ Mbps

**(i) End-to-End Throughput:**

The throughput is limited by the **slowest link** (bottleneck).

$$
\text{Throughput}_{e2e} = \min(200, 100, 50) \text{ Mbps} = 50 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(ii) End-to-End Delay (using throughput):**

$$
\text{Transfer time} = \frac{F}{\text{Throughput}} = \frac{32 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.64 \text{ s}
$$

If we assume negligible propagation delay:

$$
D_{e2e} \approx 0.64 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Bandwidth-Delay Product (assuming $d_{prop} = 10$ ms for each link):**

For **Link 1** ($R_1 = 200$ Mbps, $d_{prop} = 10$ ms):

$$
BDP_1 = R_1 \times d_{prop} = 200 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ Mb}
$$

For **Link 2** ($R_2 = 100$ Mbps, $d_{prop} = 10$ ms):

$$
BDP_2 = 100 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 1 \text{ Mb}
$$

For **Link 3** ($R_3 = 50$ Mbps, $d_{prop} = 10$ ms):

$$
BDP_3 = 50 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 0.5 \text{ Mb}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Computing throughput as the **average** of link rates (i.e., 116.67 Mbps). It is always the **MINIMUM**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert MB to bits. Always use: $1 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 10^6$ bits.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up bandwidth and throughput in the BDP formula. BDP uses **link bandwidth**, not throughput.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting units in final answer. Always specify **seconds, bits, or Mb** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Network Criteria**

### Core Concepts
- ŌśÉ A network is judged on **3 criteria**: Performance, Reliability, Security
- ŌśÉ **Performance** = how fast and efficient the network is
- ŌśÉ **Reliability** = how dependable the network is over time
- ŌśÉ **Security** = how well data is protected (CIA Triad)

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ŌśÉ $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ (packet length / link rate)
- ŌśÉ $d_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ (distance / propagation speed)
- ŌśÉ $D_{total} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$
- ŌśÉ $\text{Throughput}_{e2e} = \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$
- ŌśÉ $BDP = R \times d_{prop}$
- ŌśÉ $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$

### Performance Metrics
- ŌśÉ **Throughput** = actual data delivery rate
- ŌśÉ **Bandwidth** = theoretical maximum capacity
- ŌśÉ **Latency** = delay experienced by a packet
- ŌśÉ **Jitter** = variation in delay
- ŌśÉ **RTT** = round-trip time (request + response)

### Reliability Metrics
- ŌśÉ **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures
- ŌśÉ **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair
- ŌśÉ **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$

### Security Pillars (CIA + N)
- ŌśÉ **C**onfidentiality (Encryption: AES, RSA)
- ŌśÉ **I**ntegrity (Hashing: SHA-256, MD5)
- ŌśÉ **A**uthentication (Certificates, Biometrics, Passwords)
- ŌśÉ **N**on-Repudiation (Digital Signatures)

### Exam-Ready Statements
- ŌśÉ The end-to-end throughput is **always** the minimum of all link rates (bottleneck).
- ŌśÉ The total delay is the **sum** of all four delay components at each node.
- ŌśÉ For long-distance satellite links, **propagation delay** dominates.
- ŌśÉ For short, high-speed LANs, **transmission delay** typically dominates.
- ŌśÉ Queuing delay is the only delay that is **variable and unbounded**.

### Trade-offs to Remember
- ŌśÉ **Security Ōåö Performance:** More security = more processing = lower throughput
- ŌśÉ **Reliability Ōåö Cost:** More redundancy = higher MTBF = higher cost
- ŌśÉ **Performance Ōåö Reliability:** Aggressive timeouts reduce delay but increase false failures

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, always show **unit conversions explicitly** (e.g., MB to bits, km to meters, ms to seconds). Examiners deduct marks for missing units. Also, draw **clear, labeled diagrams** wherever the question says "explain with a diagram" ŌĆö a well-drawn diagram alone can fetch 2-3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Physical Structures" in Computer Networks?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCGT302 ŌĆō Computer Networks & Protocols**, the term **"Physical Structures"** refers to the *tangible arrangement* of devices, links, and nodes that collectively form a computer network. It is a foundational concept from **Module 1** that establishes how computing devices are physically (or logically at layer 1) connected to one another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Exam Approved):**
> A *physical structure* of a network describes **the way in which computing devices (nodes/hosts) are interconnected using transmission media (cables/wireless channels) and the geometric arrangement (topology) of those interconnections.** It encompasses three pillars: (1) **Type of Connection**, (2) **Physical Topology**, and (3) **Network Scale (Geographic Span)**.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Physical Structure

| Pillar | Focus | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Connection** | How two devices are linked | Point-to-Point, Multipoint |
| **Physical Topology** | Geometric layout of the entire network | Star, Bus, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid |
| **Network Scale / Span** | Geographic coverage area | PAN, LAN, CAN, MAN, WAN |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Conceptual Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The "Road Network of a City"**
>
> Think of a computer network as the **road system of a city**:
>
> - **Devices (nodes)** = Houses, offices, and shops.
> - **Links (cables/wireless)** = Roads, streets, and highways.
> - **Type of Connection** = Whether a road is a *private driveway* (point-to-point) or a *shared lane* (multipoint).
> - **Physical Topology** = The *map layout* of the roads ŌĆö a star-shaped city center, a ring-shaped bypass, or a fully meshed grid.
> - **Network Scale** = Whether we are talking about a *single building* (LAN) or the *entire country* (WAN).
>
> Just as a city's traffic flow depends on how roads are laid out, a computer network's performance, cost, and reliability depend on its physical structure.

---

## 1.4 Why KTU Boards Test This Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This is a **high-weightage** Module-1 topic. KTU examiners expect students to (a) draw and label topologies, (b) compute the number of cables/channels required for a given topology, and (c) compare topologies using parameters like *cost*, *flexibility*, *reliability*, and *ease of installation*.

### 1.5 Key Terminology to Memorize

- **Node / Host / Device:** Any computing entity that communicates over the network (computer, router, switch, printer).
- **Link:** The physical medium connecting two nodes. A link is a **dedicated path** for one connection.
- **Point-to-Point (P2P) Connection:** A link connecting exactly **two** devices with the full capacity of the channel reserved.
- **Multipoint Connection:** A link shared by **three or more** devices; capacity is shared (multiplexed) in time or frequency.
- **Topology:** The arrangement of nodes and links in a network ŌĆö either physical (actual cabling) or logical (data flow).
- **PAN / LAN / CAN / MAN / WAN:** Categories classified by *geographic coverage* ŌĆö **P**ersonal, **L**ocal, **C**ampus, **M**etropolitan, **W**ide Area Network.
- **Internetwork (Internet):** A *network of networks* ŌĆö multiple distinct networks connected via routers/gateways.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 ŌĆö Type of Connection (Detailed Theory)

### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (P2P) Connection
- A dedicated link connects **exactly two** devices.
- The **entire bandwidth** of the link is reserved for the two endpoints.
- Common examples: a computer-to-printer USB cable, a leased line between two offices, a dial-up modem link.
- **Advantages:** Simple, private, predictable performance, easy error isolation.
- **Disadvantages:** Costly when many devices must communicate (needs $N$ separate links for $N$ devices ŌĆö see Formula Sheet below).

### 2.1.2 Multipoint (Multi-Drop) Connection
- A **single shared link** connects **three or more** devices.
- The channel capacity is **shared** ŌĆö typically via **TDM** (Time Division Multiplexing) or contention-based access (e.g., Ethernet bus).
- Common examples: traditional 10BASE5 Ethernet bus, Wi-Fi (shared radio channel).
- **Advantages:** Economical on cabling; one cable serves many devices.
- **Disadvantages:** Collisions, contention, lower per-device throughput as more devices join.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A 3-mark KTU question frequently asks, *"Differentiate between point-to-point and multipoint connections."* Always mention **dedicated bandwidth vs. shared bandwidth** ŌĆö that single phrase carries 2 marks.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 ŌĆö Physical Topology (Detailed Theory)

A **physical topology** describes *the actual cabling layout*. There are **five canonical** topologies and one *hybrid* combination.

### 2.2.1 Mesh Topology
- **Every node is connected to every other node** via dedicated point-to-point links.
- **Two variants:**
  - **Full Mesh** ŌĆö every pair of nodes has a direct link.
  - **Partial Mesh** ŌĆö only some pairs are directly linked.
- **Why "Mesh"?** The structure resembles a *woven net* where every strand touches every other.
- **Real-world example:** The Internet backbone (partial mesh), military command networks.

### 2.2.2 Star Topology
- All nodes connect to a **central hub/switch**.
- The central device is the *single point of communication*; all data passes through it.
- **Real-world example:** Modern Ethernet LANs, Wi-Fi networks (AP is the center).

### 2.2.3 Bus (or Backbone) Topology
- All nodes tap onto a **single shared backbone cable** (the "bus" or "trunk").
- Signals travel in **both directions** along the bus; terminators (resistors) prevent signal reflection at the ends.
- **Real-world example:** Legacy 10BASE2 / 10BASE5 Ethernet, cable TV distribution.

### 2.2.4 Ring Topology
- Each node connects to **exactly two other nodes**, forming a closed loop.
- Data travels in **one direction** (single ring) or **both directions** (dual ring ŌĆö used in SONET/FDDI for fault tolerance).
- **Real-world example:** Older Token Ring (IEEE 802.5), SONET rings in telecom.

### 2.2.5 Tree (or Hierarchical) Topology
- A *combination of star and bus*: groups of star-configured nodes are connected to a central backbone.
- Also called **hierarchical** or **star-bus hybrid**.
- **Real-world example:** Corporate networks where departmental stars connect to a building backbone.

### 2.2.6 Hybrid Topology
- A **deliberate combination** of two or more different topologies to leverage the strengths of each.
- **Real-world example:** A university campus (mesh backbone between buildings + star within each building).

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 ŌĆö Categories of Networks (Scale-Based Classification)

Networks are classified by **geographic coverage** and **ownership**. KTU boards frequently test the differences between LAN and WAN.

| Category | Full Name | Typical Span | Example | Typical Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
