| **PAN** | Personal Area Network | < 10 m | Bluetooth headset Ōåö phone | kbpsŌĆōMbps |
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | Building / campus (Ōēż few km) | Office Ethernet, Wi-Fi | 100 Mbps ŌĆō 10 Gbps |
| **CAN** | Campus Area Network | Multiple buildings in a campus | University campus | 1ŌĆō10 Gbps |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | City (Ōēż ~100 km) | Cable TV network, Metro Ethernet | 100 Mbps ŌĆō 1 Gbps |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Country / continent / globe | Internet, leased-line networks | kbps ŌĆō Gbps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Internet is NOT a single network.** It is the world's largest **internetwork** ŌĆö a *network of networks* connected by the **TCP/IP protocol suite**. Always use the term **internetwork** (or *internet*, lowercase) when referring to any generic network-of-networks, and **Internet** (capitalised) when referring to the global public internetwork.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these formulas. Every KTU university exam from 2018 onwards has had at least one numeric on topologies. The two recurring formulas are the number of links and the number of ports required.**

| # | Topology | Number of Cables / Links | Number of I/O Ports per Node | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mesh** (Full) | $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $(n-1)$ ports per node | Each link is a dedicated P2P; no sharing. |
| 2 | **Star** | $L = n$ | $1$ port per node + $n$ ports at hub | All traffic passes through the central hub. |
| 3 | **Bus** | $1$ backbone + $n$ drop cables | $1$ tap interface per node | All nodes share the single backbone. |
| 4 | **Ring** | $L = n$ | $2$ ports per node | Forms a closed loop. |
| 5 | **Tree** (k-level binary) | $L = n - 1$ (for $k$ levels) | varies by level | Hierarchical; failure at root = total loss. |

Where:
- $n$ = total number of nodes (devices) in the network.
- $L$ = total number of physical links/cables required.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters beyond the exam:**
> - **Network Engineers** choose topology based on *redundancy requirements*. A full mesh gives $n-1$ fault tolerance but is expensive; a ring gives 1-fault tolerance cheaply.
> - **Data Center Design** uses a *fat-tree* or *leaf-spine* topology (a tree variant) to minimize hop count and maximize bisection bandwidth.
> - **Telecom Backbones** use *dual-ring* topologies (e.g., SONET, MPLS rings) so that a single fiber cut does not interrupt service.
> - **The Internet's physical structure** is a *partial mesh* ŌĆö chosen because it balances redundancy and cost at planetary scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Links in a Full Mesh Topology

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Suppose we have a network with $n$ devices (nodes), and we want every device to be directly connected to every other device using a *dedicated point-to-point link*. How many physical links $L$ are required?

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the combinatorial question.**
A link is an *unordered pair* of distinct nodes. We must count the number of unique pairs that can be formed from $n$ nodes.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the combination formula.**
The number of ways to choose 2 distinct nodes from $n$ nodes is:

$$
L = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n!}{2!\,(n-2)!}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the factorial expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times 1 \times (n-2)!} \\
  &= \dfrac{n \times (n-1)}{2} \\
  &= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sanity checks (boundary conditions).**
- If $n = 1$: $L = \dfrac{1 \times 0}{2} = 0$ links Ō£ō (a single node has no link).
- If $n = 2$: $L = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{2} = 1$ link Ō£ō (one cable between two devices).
- If $n = 5$: $L = \dfrac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$ links Ō£ō (matches the full-mesh count of 5 nodes).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Result.**

$$
\boxed{\,L_{\text{mesh}} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}\,}
$$

### 3.1.3 Number of I/O Ports per Node in Full Mesh
Each node must have a physical port for **every link that touches it**. Since every node in a full mesh touches **every other node**, the number of ports per node is:

$$
P_{\text{node}} = n - 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A 7-mark KTU problem often gives $n$ and asks for *both* the number of links and the number of NIC/ports needed. Always compute **both** to capture full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö KTU-Style Numeric Problem

### Problem
A company has **6 departments**, each in a separate building. For maximum reliability, the company wants a **full-mesh** topology. Compute:
- (i) The number of physical links.
- (ii) The number of I/O ports required at *each* node.
- (iii) The total number of ports across *all* nodes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:** $n = 6$ nodes.

**Part (i) ŌĆö Number of links:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} \\
  &= \dfrac{6 \times (6-1)}{2} \\
  &= \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} \\
  &= \dfrac{30}{2} \\
  &= 15 \text{ links}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) ŌĆö Ports per node:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{node}} &= n - 1 \\
                &= 6 - 1 \\
                &= 5 \text{ ports per node}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) ŌĆö Total ports:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= n \times P_{\text{node}} \\
                 &= 6 \times 5 \\
                 &= 30 \text{ ports}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Total ports = $2L$ for any full mesh because *every link has two endpoints*. Indeed, $2 \times 15 = 30$ Ō£ō. This identity is useful for cross-verification in exams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö All Five Topologies (KTU's Most-Asked 14-Mark Format)

This is the comparison table KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark question. Memorize the entries under each column.

| Parameter | Mesh (Full) | Star | Bus | Ring | Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of links** | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n$ | $1$ backbone $+ n$ taps | $n$ | $n - 1$ |
| **Cabling cost** | Very High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Reliability** | Very High (no single point of failure) | Low (hub failure = total failure) | Low (cable break = total failure) | Moderate (one break may isolate a node) | Moderate |
| **Ease of expansion** | Difficult (add $n$ new links for one new node) | Easy (just add a cable to hub) | Moderate (must tap onto the bus) | Moderate (must break ring to add) | Moderate |
| **Ease of troubleshooting** | Difficult (many paths) | Easy (centralized at hub) | Difficult (entire bus affected) | Easy (token-based) | Moderate |
| **Performance under load** | Excellent (dedicated links) | Good (centralized) | Degrades quickly (collisions) | Predictable (token-based) | Good |
| **Real-world example** | Internet backbone | Office Ethernet | Legacy 10BASE2 | SONET / Token Ring | Corporate hierarchical LAN |
| **Best suited for** | Mission-critical networks (military, banks) | Small to medium LANs | Small, temporary LANs | Telecom backbones | Multi-building campuses |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: When Does Mesh Become "Worse" than Star?

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup
Find the smallest $n$ for which a *full-mesh* topology requires **more links** than a *star* topology.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Analysis

We need:

$$
L_{\text{mesh}} > L_{\text{star}}
$$

Substituting the formulas:

$$
\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} > n
$$

Multiplying both sides by $2$:

$$
n(n-1) > 2n
$$

Dividing both sides by $n$ (valid for $n > 0$):

$$
n - 1 > 2
$$

$$
n > 3
$$

### 3.4.3 Verification with Concrete Numbers
- $n = 3$: Mesh needs $\dfrac{3 \times 2}{2} = 3$ links; Star needs $3$ links ŌåÆ **Tied**.
- $n = 4$: Mesh needs $\dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ links; Star needs $4$ links ŌåÆ **Mesh > Star**.
- $n = 5$: Mesh needs $10$; Star needs $5$ ŌåÆ **Mesh requires $2 \times$ Star**.

### 3.4.4 Final Result

$$
\boxed{\,\text{For } n \geq 4, \; L_{\text{mesh}} > L_{\text{star}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is *why* full mesh is reserved for small, mission-critical networks (e.g., 4-node server clusters) and *why* the Internet uses a *partial mesh* ŌĆö full mesh does not scale. This single fact, when stated in a KTU exam, demonstrates "design awareness" and earns bonus marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram ŌĆö Five Canonical Physical Topologies (Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below uses **Mermaid** syntax with strictly alphanumeric node IDs. Labels contain **only clean uppercase alphanumeric text** (no bold or markdown inside the labels) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TOPO_MESH["FULL MESH TOPOLOGY n equals 5"]
        M1[NODE 1]
        M2[NODE 2]
        M3[NODE 3]
        M4[NODE 4]
        M5[NODE 5]
        M1 --- M2
        M1 --- M3
        M1 --- M4
        M1 --- M5
        M2 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
        M2 --- M5
        M3 --- M4
        M3 --- M5
        M4 --- M5
    end

    subgraph TOPO_STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
        S1[NODE 1]
        S2[NODE 2]
        S3[NODE 3]
        S4[NODE 4]
        S5[NODE 5]
        SH[HUB OR SWITCH]
        S1 --- SH
        S2 --- SH
        S3 --- SH
        S4 --- SH
        S5 --- SH
    end

    subgraph TOPO_BUS["BUS TOPOLOGY"]
        B1[NODE 1]
        B2[NODE 2]
        B3[NODE 3]
        B4[NODE 4]
        BB[BACKBONE BUS]
        B1 --- BB
        B2 --- BB
        B3 --- BB
        B4 --- BB
    end

    subgraph TOPO_RING["RING TOPOLOGY"]
        R1[NODE 1]
        R2[NODE 2]
        R3[NODE 3]
        R4[NODE 4]
        R5[NODE 5]
        R1 --- R2
        R2 --- R3
        R3 --- R4
        R4 --- R5
        R5 --- R1
    end

    subgraph TOPO_TREE["TREE TOPOLOGY"]
        TR[ROOT NODE]
        T1[CHILD 1]
        T2[CHILD 2]
        T3[CHILD 3]
        T4[CHILD 4]
        TR --- T1
        TR --- T2
        T1 --- T3
        T2 --- T4
    end
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram ŌĆö Network Classification by Scale (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SCALE[GEOGRAPHIC SCALE]
    SCALE --> PAN[PAN  LESS THAN 10 M]
    SCALE --> LAN[LAN  BUILDING]
    SCALE --> CAN[CAN  CAMPUS]
    SCALE --> MAN[MAN  CITY]
    SCALE --> WAN[WAN  COUNTRY OR GLOBE]

    PAN --> PANEX[BLUETOOTH USB]
    LAN --> LANEX[OFFICE ETHERNET WIFI]
    CAN --> CANEX[UNIVERSITY CAMPUS]
    MAN --> MANEX[METRO ETHERNET CABLE TV]
    WAN --> WANEX[INTERNET LEASED LINES]

    WAN --> INTERNET[INTERNETWORK OF NETWORKS]
    INTERNET --> GLOBAL[THE GLOBAL INTERNET]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram ŌĆö Connection Type (Point-to-Point vs. Multipoint)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph P2P["POINT TO POINT  DEDICATED LINK"]
        DA[DEVICE A]
        DB[DEVICE B]
        LK1[FULL BANDWIDTH CHANNEL]
        DA === LK1
        LK1 === DB
    end

    subgraph MP["MULTIPOINT  SHARED LINK"]
        MC[DEVICE C]
        MD[DEVICE D]
        ME[DEVICE E]
        MF[DEVICE F]
        MLK[SHARED CHANNEL BANDWIDTH DIVIDED]
        MC --- MLK
        MD --- MLK
        ME --- MLK
        MF --- MLK
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue for Exams:** In your answer sheet, *always* use `=====` (thick line) for a *point-to-point* link and `---` (thin line) for a *multipoint shared* link. Examiners in Kerala have rewarded this micro-convention with bonus marks for clarity.

---

## 4.4 Diagram ŌĆö Hybrid Topology: A University Campus Network

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CORE["CORE BACKBONE PARTIAL MESH"]
        CR1[CORE ROUTER A]
        CR2[CORE ROUTER B]
        CR3[CORE ROUTER C]
        CR1 --- CR2
        CR2 --- CR3
        CR1 --- CR3
    end

    subgraph BLDG1["BUILDING 1  STAR"]
        SW1[SWITCH]
        C11[LAB 1]
        C12[LAB 2]
        C13[OFFICE]
        SW1 --- C11
        SW1 --- C12
        SW1 --- C13
    end

    subgraph BLDG2["BUILDING 2  BUS"]
        BB2[BACKBONE]
        C21[ROOM 1]
        C22[ROOM 2]
        C23[ROOM 3]
        BB2 --- C21
        BB2 --- C22
        BB2 --- C23
    end

    subgraph BLDG3["BUILDING 3  RING"]
        C31[NODE 1]
        C32[NODE 2]
        C33[NODE 3]
        C31 --- C32
        C32 --- C33
        C33 --- C31
    end

    CR1 --- SW1
    CR2 --- BB2
    CR3 --- C31
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This figure is the **gold-standard KTU answer** for the question *"Explain hybrid topology with a real-world example."* Always label (a) the inner topologies, (b) the outer mesh, and (c) state explicitly *why* it is hybrid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between Point-to-Point and Multipoint connections.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Point-to-Point | Multipoint |
|---|---|---|
| **Devices linked** | Exactly 2 | Three or more |
| **Bandwidth** | Fully dedicated | Shared among devices |
| **Collision** | Impossible | Possible (needs MAC arbitration) |
| **Example** | Telephone landline, USB | Wi-Fi, legacy Ethernet bus |

> **Examiner's note (1 mark for the table, 1 mark for an example, 1 mark for the conclusion):** Always end with a one-line conclusion such as, *"Thus, P2P gives dedicated performance while multipoint is cost-efficient for shared media."*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three differences between LAN and WAN.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Parameter | LAN | WAN |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographic scope** | Building / campus (Ōēż few km) | Country / continent |
| **Ownership** | Private (single organization) | Often public / multi-org / leased |
| **Typical speed** | 100 Mbps ŌĆō 10 Gbps | kbps ŌĆō Gbps (lower per-link) |
| **Example** | Office Ethernet | Internet, MPLS backbone |

> **Examiner's note:** Three crisp differences = 3 marks. Adding *one example* per type gives you a safe 1-mark buffer.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]
**(a) With neat diagrams, explain the following physical topologies:**
**(i) Mesh (ii) Star (iii) Bus (iv) Ring (v) Tree**

**(b) Compare the above topologies with respect to: number of links, reliability, cost, ease of troubleshooting, and scalability.**

#### Model Answer ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]:

1. **Mesh Topology:** A full-mesh has $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ dedicated links, every node connected to every other. *High reliability* (no single point of failure); *high cost*. Diagram: see Section 4.1.

2. **Star Topology:** All $n$ nodes connect to a central hub/switch via $n$ dedicated links. *Failure of hub = total network failure*. Diagram: see Section 4.1.

3. **Bus Topology:** All nodes tap onto a single backbone cable ($1$ backbone $+ n$ drop cables). *Single cable break affects all*. Diagram: see Section 4.1.

4. **Ring Topology:** Each node connects to exactly two neighbours, forming a closed loop ($L = n$). Data flows unidirectionally (or bidirectionally in dual rings). Diagram: see Section 4.1.

5. **Tree Topology:** Hierarchical structure with a root and child nodes, like an inverted tree. Failure at the root disconnects the entire subtree beneath it. Diagram: see Section 4.1.

> **Marking key (a):** 5 topologies ├Ś 1 mark each for diagram + 0.5 mark each for description = **7 marks**.

#### Model Answer ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]:

Refer to the **Analytical Comparison Matrix** in Section 3.3. Cover:

- **Number of links:** Mesh (highest, $n(n-1)/2$) > Star = Ring ($n$) > Tree ($n-1$) > Bus ($1$ backbone).
- **Reliability:** Mesh > Tree Ōēł Star > Ring > Bus.
- **Cost:** Mesh > Star > Ring > Tree > Bus.
- **Ease of troubleshooting:** Star > Ring > Tree > Bus > Mesh.
- **Scalability:** Bus Ōēł Star (easy to add one node) < Tree < Ring < Mesh (adding a node requires many new links).

> **Marking key (b):** Five parameters ├Ś 1.4 marks each = 7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model ŌĆö Alternative Choice]
**(a) Explain the categories of networks: PAN, LAN, CAN, MAN, WAN. Give one example and the typical range of each.**

**(b) What is an internetwork? How is the global Internet structured? Explain with a diagram showing LANŌĆōMANŌĆōWAN interconnection.**

#### Model Answer ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks]:

| Category | Span | Example | Speed | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN** | < 10 m | Bluetooth headset | kbpsŌĆōMbps | Very low |
| **LAN** | Building | Office Ethernet | 100 Mbps ŌĆō 10 Gbps | Low |
| **CAN** | Campus | University | 1ŌĆō10 Gbps | Moderate |
| **MAN** | City | Metro Ethernet | 100 Mbps ŌĆō 1 Gbps | High |
| **WAN** | Country/Globe | Internet | kbps ŌĆō Gbps | Very high |

> **Marking key (a):** 5 categories ├Ś 1.4 marks = 7 marks.

#### Model Answer ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks]:

An **internetwork** is a *network of networks* ŌĆö multiple distinct networks (LAN, MAN, WAN) connected by **routers** running the **TCP/IP** protocol suite. The **Internet** (capital I) is the global public internetwork.

**Structural layering** (using the diagram in Section 4.2 as a base):

- **LANs** in homes, offices, and campuses connect users locally.
- **MANs** aggregate multiple LANs within a city.
- **WANs** interconnect MANs and LANs across countries and continents.
- **Routers** operate at the network layer to forward packets between these heterogeneous networks.
- The global Internet is therefore a **federation** of independently operated networks (ISPs, telcos, content providers) that agree to exchange traffic using BGP (Border Gateway Protocol).

> **Marking key (b):** Definition (2) + structure explanation (3) + diagram (2) = 7 marks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Physical Structures Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "number of links" with "number of cables"** in mesh. A *full mesh* has $n(n-1)/2$ *links*, but each link is a *single cable*. Examiners will mark you correct only if you state the formula and substitute the value.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the central device's ports** in star topology. A star with $n$ nodes needs $n$ ports on the hub AND 1 port on each node. Students often forget the hub's port count.
>
> 3. **Mixing up "Internet" and "internetwork".** Internet = the global public network. Internetwork = a generic *network of networks*. KTU paper-setters consider this a 1-mark distinction.
>
> 4. **Missing diagrams.** For every 7-mark topology question, *at least 3 marks* are reserved for the diagram. A textual answer without a labelled drawing will lose those marks.
>
> 5. **Not specifying "full" vs "partial" mesh.** Always clarify: *"Assuming full mesh..."* ŌĆö otherwise the examiner cannot award the formula marks.
>
> 6. **Writing $n(n-1)$ instead of $n(n-1)/2$** in mesh. The $/2$ is the most-skipped factor in KTU answer sheets.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision block ŌĆö read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- ŌśÉ **Three pillars of physical structure:** Type of Connection, Physical Topology, Network Scale.
- ŌśÉ **Two connection types:** Point-to-Point (dedicated, 2 devices) vs. Multipoint (shared, Ōēź 3 devices).
- ŌśÉ **Five canonical topologies:** Mesh, Star, Bus, Ring, Tree (+ Hybrid as a combination).
- ŌśÉ **Mesh link formula:** $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ (full mesh).
- ŌśÉ **Star link formula:** $L = n$.
- ŌśÉ **Bus:** 1 backbone cable shared by $n$ devices via drop cables.
- ŌśÉ **Ring link formula:** $L = n$ (each node has 2 ports).
- ŌśÉ **Tree link formula:** $L = n - 1$ (for a tree with $n$ nodes and no cycles).
- ŌśÉ **Mesh becomes worse than Star** for $n \geq 4$ (link count crossover).
- ŌśÉ **Five scale categories (in increasing range):** PAN ŌåÆ LAN ŌåÆ CAN ŌåÆ MAN ŌåÆ WAN.
- ŌśÉ **LAN = private + high speed + small area; WAN = public/slow/large area.**
- ŌśÉ **Internetwork ŌēĀ Internet.** Use lowercase "internetwork" for the generic concept; capital "Internet" for the global public network.
- ŌśÉ **Hybrid topology** = combination of two or more canonical topologies (e.g., mesh backbone + star LANs).
- ŌśÉ **Real-world mapping:** Office LAN ŌåÆ Star; Campus ŌåÆ Tree/Hybrid; Internet backbone ŌåÆ Partial Mesh; Legacy Ethernet ŌåÆ Bus; SONET ŌåÆ Ring.
- ŌśÉ **Reliability ranking (high ŌåÆ low):** Mesh > Tree Ōēł Star > Ring > Bus.
- ŌśÉ **Cost ranking (high ŌåÆ low):** Mesh > Star > Ring > Tree > Bus.
- ŌśÉ **Always draw diagrams** in topology questions ŌĆö 3 of 7 marks are visual.
- ŌśÉ **For full-mesh sanity check:** Total ports across all nodes = $2L = n(n-1)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## ­¤öĘ 1.1 What is a Network Model?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Network Model** (also called a **Network Architecture** or **Protocol Stack**) is a structured, layered framework that defines how data is transmitted, received, and processed across a communication network. It standardizes the rules, functions, and interfaces between heterogeneous systems to ensure reliable end-to-end communication.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term "Network Models" primarily refers to two reference architectures:

1. **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** ŌĆö defined by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** as ISO/IEC 7498.
2. **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol) Model** ŌĆö the practical model that powers the modern Internet, originally designed by **DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)**.

---

## ­¤öĘ 1.2 Why Do We Need Layered Models?

A network communication task is extremely complex. Imagine trying to build a single monolithic program that handles electrical signaling, error detection, routing, congestion control, encryption, and application logic **simultaneously**. This would be unmanageable, unscalable, and impossible to debug.

**Layered models** solve this by:
- **Decomposition**: Breaking a complex task into smaller, manageable sub-tasks.
- **Abstraction**: Each layer only interacts with the layers directly above and below it (a concept called **Service Abstraction**).
- **Modularity**: A change in one layer's internal technology does not affect the other layers (e.g., switching from copper to fiber optics only affects the Physical layer).
- **Standardization**: Vendors across the world can build interoperable products.

---

## ­¤öĘ 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Postal System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: Sending a Parcel via International Post**

Think of sending a letter from your home in **Kerala** to a friend in **Germany**.

| Network Concept | Postal Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | You writing the letter in English (your data/message) |
| **Transport Layer** | Putting the letter in an envelope with tracking number (TCP = registered post, UDP = ordinary post) |
| **Network Layer** | Writing the destination address (IP address) on the envelope |
| **Data Link Layer** | Sorting the letter at the local post office using postal codes |
| **Physical Layer** | The actual truck/train/plane carrying the physical letter |

Just as the postal system has different departments handling different jobs, a network model has layers handling specific responsibilities. The letter goes through **encapsulation** (adding envelope ŌåÆ address ŌåÆ tracking) on the sender's side and **decapsulation** (removing them one by one) on the receiver's side.

---

## ­¤öĘ 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** The formatted unit of data at any given layer. It is the "envelope" at that layer's level.
> - **SDU (Service Data Unit):** The actual data payload passed from the layer above.
> - **PCI (Protocol Control Information):** The header/trailer added by a layer.
> - **Encapsulation:** The process of adding headers (and sometimes trailers) as data moves **down** the stack.
> - **Decapsulation:** The reverse process as data moves **up** the stack on the receiver side.
> - **SAP (Service Access Point):** The logical interface through which a layer requests services from the layer below it.

The relationship is governed by the equation:

$$ PDU = SDU + PCI $$

This single equation is **extremely high-yield** for KTU exams ŌĆö expect at least one sub-part asking you to apply it.

---

## ­¤öĘ 1.5 Overview of the Two Models

The **OSI Model** is a **7-layer theoretical model**, while the **TCP/IP Model** is a **4-layer practical model** that was actually implemented and is the backbone of the Internet.

The TCP/IP model can be considered a condensed, practical simplification of the OSI model. KTU frequently asks for **layer-to-layer mapping** between these two models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ­¤öČ 2.1 The OSI Reference Model (7 Layers)

The OSI model has **7 distinct layers**, typically remembered using the mnemonic:

> **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**
> (Top ŌåÆ Bottom: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical)

### Layer 7 ŌĆö Application Layer
- **Role:** Closest to the end-user. Provides network services directly to user applications.
- **Examples:** HTTP (web), SMTP (email), FTP (file transfer), DNS (name resolution), SNMP.
- **PDU Name:** **Data** (or Message).
- **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse HTTP/FTP/SMTP as "Transport layer" ŌĆö they are NOT. They are **Application layer protocols**.

### Layer 6 ŌĆö Presentation Layer
- **Role:** Handles **data translation, encryption/decryption, and compression**.
- **Examples:** SSL/TLS (encryption), JPEG, MPEG, ASCII Ōåö EBCDIC conversion.
- **PDU Name:** **Data**.
- **Mnemonic:** "Presentation makes data look pretty (encrypted, compressed, formatted)."

### Layer 5 ŌĆö Session Layer
- **Role:** Manages and controls **connections (sessions)** between two communicating hosts. Handles **dialogue control** (half-duplex/full-duplex) and **synchronization** (checkpoints).
- **Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP.
- **PDU Name:** **Data**.

### Layer 4 ŌĆö Transport Layer
- **Role:** Provides **end-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control, and error recovery**. This is the heart of reliability.
- **Core Protocols:** **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) and **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless).
- **PDU Name:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP).
- **Key Functions:** Port addressing, multiplexing/demultiplexing, sequencing, acknowledgments.
- **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Most KTU questions on Module 1 focus on this layer's protocol mechanisms.**

### Layer 3 ŌĆö Network Layer
- **Role:** Provides **logical addressing (IP addresses)** and **routing** of packets across multiple networks.
- **Core Protocols:** **IP (IPv4/IPv6)**, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, ARP.
- **PDU Name:** **Packet**.
- **Key Concept:** Routers operate at this layer.

### Layer 2 ŌĆö Data Link Layer
- **Role:** Provides **node-to-node data transfer** on the same network segment. Handles **MAC addressing**, **framing**, and **error detection/correction** (but not recovery).
- **Sub-layers:** **LLC (Logical Link Control)** ŌĆö upper sublayer, and **MAC (Media Access Control)** ŌĆö lower sublayer.
- **Core Protocols:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC.
- **PDU Name:** **Frame**.
- **Key Concept:** Switches and bridges operate at this layer.

### Layer 1 ŌĆö Physical Layer
- **Role:** Transmits **raw bits** over a physical medium. Defines **electrical, mechanical, and procedural** specifications.
- **Examples:** Cables (Cat6, fiber), connectors (RJ45), hubs, repeaters, bit synchronization.
- **PDU Name:** **Bit**.
- **Key Metrics:** **Bandwidth (Hz or bps)**, **Bit rate (bps)**, **Attenuation (dB)**, **Propagation delay (sec)**, **Throughput**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Devices per layer (MEMORIZE):**
> - Layer 1: Hub, Repeater, Cable
> - Layer 2: Bridge, Switch, NIC
> - Layer 3: Router, Layer-3 Switch
> - Layer 4: Gateway (also at Layer 7)
> - Layer 7: Application Gateway, Proxy Server

---

## ­¤öČ 2.2 The TCP/IP Model (4 Layers)

The TCP/IP model (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) was developed by DARPA for the ARPANET. It has **4 layers**:

| Layer | Name | OSI Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Application | Application + Presentation + Session (OSI 5ŌĆō7) |
| 3 | Transport | Transport (OSI 4) |
| 2 | Internet | Network (OSI 3) |
| 1 | Network Access (Link/Physical) | Data Link + Physical (OSI 1ŌĆō2) |

### Layer 4 ŌĆö Application Layer
- Combines OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers.
- **Protocols:** HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet.

### Layer 3 ŌĆö Transport Layer
- Identical in function to OSI Layer 4.
- **Protocols:** TCP, UDP.

### Layer 2 ŌĆö Internet Layer
- Handles logical addressing and routing.
- **Protocols:** IP, ICMP, IGMP, ARP.

### Layer 1 ŌĆö Network Access (Link) Layer
- Combines OSI's Data Link and Physical layers.
- **Protocols/Standards:** Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Frame Relay, ATM.

---

## ­¤öČ 2.3 Encapsulation & Decapsulation Flow

Data flows down the stack on the sender's side (encapsulation) and up the stack on the receiver's side (decapsulation).

**Encapsulation Order:**

$$ \text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{App}} \text{Data} \xrightarrow{\text{Transport}} \text{Segment} \xrightarrow{\text{Network}} \text{Packet} \xrightarrow{\text{Data Link}} \text{Frame} \xrightarrow{\text{Physical}} \text{Bits} $$

Each layer (except Physical) **adds a header** to the PDU it receives. The Data Link layer may also add a **trailer** (for error detection like CRC).

---

## ­¤öČ 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all critical formulas for this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| PDU Composition | $PDU = SDU + PCI$ | ŌĆö | Identify headers/payloads |
| Bit Rate vs Bandwidth | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ (Nyquist for noiseless) | bps | Capacity calculation |
| Nyquist (Noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2 L$ | bps | Max data rate |
| Shannon (Noisy) | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Capacity with noise |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | dB | Convert SNR to dB |
| Propagation Delay | $t_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$ | sec | $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable |
| Transmission Delay | $t_t = \frac{\text{Packet Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | sec | Time to push packet onto link |
| End-to-End Delay | $t_{end} = t_p + t_t + t_{queue} + t_{proc}$ | sec | Total delay |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Data Delivered}}{\text{Time Taken}}$ | bps | Effective rate |
| Delay-Bandwidth Product | $DBP = B \times t_p$ | bits | Amount of data "in flight" |

---

## ­¤öČ 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineers** use the OSI model as a universal troubleshooting framework ("**OSI bottom-up troubleshooting**" ŌĆö start at Physical layer, work up).
- **Cybersecurity Analysts** use it to pinpoint attack surfaces (e.g., DDoS attacks at Network layer, ARP spoofing at Data Link layer, SQL injection at Application layer).
- **Protocol Designers** follow the layered approach to ensure backward compatibility (e.g., IPv6 encapsulates IPv4 in transition tunnels).
- **Cloud Architects** map services to layers ŌĆö e.g., AWS VPC Peering operates at Layer 3, while AWS Direct Connect is a Layer 1/2 service.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ­¤ōÖ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## ­¤¤ó 3.1 Analytical Matrix: OSI vs TCP/IP Comparison

This is a **guaranteed KTU question**. Memorize the differences:

| Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| **Developed By** | ISO (International Organization for Standardization) | DARPA (US Dept. of Defense) |
| **Number of Layers** | 7 | 4 |
| **Nature** | Theoretical / Conceptual Reference Model | Practical / Implementation Model |
| **Development Era** | 1984 (formal standard) | 1970s (ARPANET) |
| **Session & Presentation** | Has dedicated layers | Merged into Application layer |
| **Physical & Data Link** | Separate layers | Merged into Network Access layer |
| **Protocol Dependency** | Protocol-independent generic model | Built around specific protocols (TCP, IP, UDP) |
| **Adoption** | Rarely implemented, used for teaching | Universally implemented (powers the Internet) |
| **Strict Layer Boundaries** | Yes ŌĆö strict, clear interfaces | Loose ŌĆö protocols can span multiple conceptual roles |
| **Header Size** | Variable (defined by protocols used) | TCP: 20ŌĆō60 bytes, IP: 20ŌĆō60 bytes (IPv4) |
| **Reliability Philosophy** | Reliability built into the model design | Reliability pushed to the Transport layer (TCP) |
| **Best Use** | Teaching, design, troubleshooting | Real-world networking and the Internet |

---

## ­¤¤ó 3.2 Step-by-Step PDU Derivation at Each Layer

Let us trace a single email message from sender to receiver and derive what the PDU looks like at **each layer**. Assume an HTTP request of **1500 bytes** of actual application data.

### Step 1: Application Layer (OSI Layer 7 / TCP-IP Layer 4)

The application produces the raw user data. No header is added at this conceptual layer.

$$ PDU_{\text{App}} = \text{Data} = 1500 \text{ bytes} $$

### Step 2: Transport Layer (OSI Layer 4 / TCP-IP Layer 3)

The transport layer (using **TCP**) adds a **20-byte header** (minimum, no options). This header contains:
- Source/Destination **Port numbers** (16 bits each)
- **Sequence number** (32 bits)
- **Acknowledgment number** (32 bits)
- **Flags** (SYN, ACK, FIN, etc.)
- **Checksum** (16 bits)
- Other control fields

$$ PDU_{\text{Transport}} = SDU_{\text{App}} + PCI_{\text{Transport}} $$

$$ PDU_{\text{Transport}} = 1500 \text{ bytes} + 20 \text{ bytes} = 1520 \text{ bytes} $$

This PDU is called a **TCP Segment**.

### Step 3: Network Layer (OSI Layer 3 / TCP-IP Layer 2)

The network layer (using **IPv4**) adds a **20-byte IP header** containing:
- Source/Destination **IP addresses** (32 bits each in IPv4)
- **TTL (Time To Live)**
- **Protocol** field (e.g., 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP)
- Header checksum

$$ PDU_{\text{Network}} = SDU_{\text{Transport}} + PCI_{\text{Network}} $$

$$ PDU_{\text{Network}} = 1520 \text{ bytes} + 20 \text{ bytes} = 1540 \text{ bytes} $$

This PDU is called an **IP Packet** (or Datagram).

### Step 4: Data Link Layer (OSI Layer 2 / TCP-IP Layer 1)

The data link layer (using **Ethernet**) adds:
- **Ethernet Header**: 14 bytes (6-byte source MAC + 6-byte destination MAC + 2-byte EtherType)
- **Ethernet Trailer**: 4 bytes (FCS ŌĆö Frame Check Sequence using CRC-32)

$$ PDU_{\text{DataLink}} = SDU_{\text{Network}} + PCI_{\text{Header}} + PCI_{\text{Trailer}} $$

$$ PDU_{\text{DataLink}} = 1540 \text{ bytes} + 14 \text{ bytes} + 4 \text{ bytes} = 1558 \text{ bytes} $$

This PDU is called an **Ethernet Frame**.

### Step 5: Physical Layer (OSI Layer 1)

The physical layer does NOT add headers. It simply **converts the frame into a stream of bits** and transmits them as electrical, optical, or radio signals over the medium.

$$ PDU_{\text{Physical}} = 1558 \text{ bytes} = 12{,}464 \text{ bits} $$

---

## ­¤¤ó 3.3 Final Encapsulation Summary Table

| Layer | Header Added | Trailer Added | PDU Name | Total Size (from example) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application | 0 | 0 | Data / Message | 1500 bytes |
| Transport (TCP) | 20 bytes | 0 | Segment | 1520 bytes |
| Network (IPv4) | 20 bytes | 0 | Packet | 1540 bytes |
| Data Link (Ethernet) | 14 bytes | 4 bytes (FCS) | Frame | 1558 bytes |
| Physical | 0 | 0 | Bits | 12,464 bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On the receiver's side, the process is reversed (Decapsulation):**
> - Physical layer receives bits ŌåÆ assembles into a Frame.
> - Data Link layer strips its header + trailer ŌåÆ passes Packet up.
> - Network layer strips IP header ŌåÆ passes Segment up.
> - Transport layer strips TCP header ŌåÆ reassembles Data.
> - Application layer delivers the original message to the user.

---

## ­¤¤ó 3.4 Worked Example: Applying the PDU Formula

**Problem:** A user sends 8000 bytes of application data over TCP/IP using Ethernet.
- TCP header: 20 bytes
- IP header: 20 bytes
- Ethernet header: 18 bytes (using 802.1Q VLAN-tagged Ethernet for example)
- Ethernet trailer: 4 bytes

**Question:** What is the total number of bits transmitted on the physical medium?

**Step 1:** Application data

$$ SDU_{\text{App}} = 8000 \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 2:** Add TCP header at Transport layer

$$ PDU_{\text{Transport}} = 8000 + 20 = 8020 \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 3:** Add IP header at Network layer

$$ PDU_{\text{Network}} = 8020 + 20 = 8040 \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 4:** Add Ethernet header and trailer at Data Link layer

$$ PDU_{\text{DataLink}} = 8040 + 18 + 4 = 8062 \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 5:** Convert bytes to bits at Physical layer

$$ \text{Bits on wire} = 8062 \times 8 = 64{,}496 \text{ bits} $$

**Answer:** **64,496 bits** are transmitted on the physical medium.

---

## ­¤¤ó 3.5 Numerical Example: Propagation & Transmission Delay

**Given:**
- Link length = 5,000 km
- Propagation speed in fiber = $2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Bandwidth = 1 Gbps
- Packet size = 1,500 bytes

**Step 1: Propagation Delay**

$$ t_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}} $$

$$ t_p = \frac{5{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 2: Transmission Delay**

$$ t_t = \frac{\text{Packet Size (bits)}}{\text{Bandwidth (bps)}} $$

$$ t_t = \frac{1500 \times 8}{10^9} = \frac{12{,}000}{10^9} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.012 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 3: Total Delay (ignoring queueing and processing)**

$$ t_{total} = t_p + t_t = 25 \text{ ms} + 0.012 \text{ ms} = 25.012 \text{ ms} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** In long-distance links, **propagation delay dominates** over transmission delay. This is a common KTU conceptual question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## ­¤¤Ż 4.1 OSI 7-Layer Reference Model (Top-Down Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION LAYER\nHTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP\nPDU: Data / Message"]
    PRES["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION LAYER\nSSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII\nPDU: Data"]
    SESS["LAYER 5 - SESSION LAYER\nNetBIOS, RPC, PPTP\nPDU: Data"]
    TRANS["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP, UDP\nPDU: Segment or Datagram"]
    NET["LAYER 3 - NETWORK LAYER\nIP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP\nPDU: Packet"]
    DL["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK LAYER\nEthernet 802.3, Wi-Fi 802.11\nPDU: Frame"]
    PHY["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL LAYER\nCables, Connectors, Hubs\nPDU: Bits"]

    APP --> PRES
    PRES --> SESS
    SESS --> TRANS
    TRANS --> NET
    NET --> DL
    DL --> PHY

    PHY -.->|Decapsulation Reverse| DL
    DL -.->|Decapsulation Reverse| NET
    NET -.->|Decapsulation Reverse| TRANS
    TRANS -.->|Decapsulation Reverse| SESS
    SESS -.->|Decapsulation Reverse| PRES
    PRES -.->|Decapsulation Reverse| APP
```

---

## ­¤¤Ż 4.2 TCP/IP 4-Layer Model (The Internet Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TCPA["APPLICATION LAYER\nHTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet\nCombines OSI Layers 5 6 7"]
    TCPT["TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP - Reliable Connection Oriented\nUDP - Unreliable Connectionless\nSame as OSI Layer 4"]
    TCPI["INTERNET LAYER\nIP - IPv4 and IPv6\nICMP, ARP, IGMP\nSame as OSI Layer 3"]
    TCPN["NETWORK ACCESS LAYER\nEthernet, Wi-Fi, Frame Relay\nCombines OSI Layers 1 and 2"]

    TCPA --> TCPT
    TCPT --> TCPI
    TCPI --> TCPN
```

---

## ­¤¤Ż 4.3 OSI vs TCP/IP Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSI7["OSI 7 - APPLICATION"] -.-> TCPA["TCP/IP APPLICATION"]
    OSI6["OSI 6 - PRESENTATION"] -.-> TCPA
    OSI5["OSI 5 - SESSION"] -.-> TCPA
    OSI4["OSI 4 - TRANSPORT"] ==> TCPT["TCP/IP TRANSPORT"]
    OSI3["OSI 3 - NETWORK"] ==> TCPI["TCP/IP INTERNET"]
    OSI2["OSI 2 - DATA LINK"] -.-> TCPN["TCP/IP NETWORK ACCESS"]
    OSI1["OSI 1 - PHYSICAL"] -.-> TCPN
```

---

## ­¤¤Ż 4.4 Encapsulation and Decapsulation End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S_USER["User Data\nApplication Layer"] --> S_TRANS["Transport Header Added\nSEGMENT FORMED"]
    S_TRANS --> S_NET["Network Header Added\nPACKET FORMED"]
    S_NET --> S_DL["Data Link Header and Trailer Added\nFRAME FORMED"]
    S_DL --> S_PHY["Bits Encoded and Transmitted\nPHYSICAL LAYER"]
    S_PHY -->|Physical Medium| R_PHY["Bits Received\nPHYSICAL LAYER"]
    R_PHY --> R_DL["Header and Trailer Stripped\nPACKET EXTRACTED"]
    R_DL --> R_NET["Network Header Stripped\nSEGMENT EXTRACTED"]
    R_NET --> R_TRANS["Transport Header Stripped\nDATA EXTRACTED"]
    R_TRANS --> R_USER["Original Data Delivered to User\nApplication Layer"]
```

---

## ­¤¤Ż 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PDU at Each Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENCAPSULATION["ENCAPSULATION - SENDER SIDE"]
        A1["Application Data\nVariable Length"] --> A2["+ Transport Header\nTCP or UDP 20 bytes"]
        A2 --> A3["+ IP Header\nIPv4 20 bytes"]
        A3 --> A4["+ Ethernet Header 14 bytes\n+ Ethernet Trailer 4 bytes"]
        A4 --> A5["Physical Encoding\nBits on the Wire"]
    end

    subgraph DECAPSULATION["DECAPSULATION - RECEIVER SIDE"]
        B1["Bits Received from Wire"] --> B2["Strip Ethernet Header and Trailer\nExtract IP Packet"]
        B2 --> B3["Strip IP Header\nExtract TCP Segment"]
        B3 --> B4["Strip TCP Header\nExtract Application Data"]
        B4 --> B5["Deliver Data to Application"]
    end

    A5 -->|Transmit over medium| B1
```

---

## ­¤¤Ż 4.6 Device-to-Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION\nGateway, Proxy Server"]
    L6["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION\nEncryption Devices"]
    L5["LAYER 5 - SESSION\nSession Border Controller"]
    L4["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT\nLayer 4 Load Balancer"]
    L3["LAYER 3 - NETWORK\nRouter, Layer 3 Switch"]
    L2["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK\nSwitch, Bridge, NIC"]
    L1["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL\nHub, Repeater, Cable"]

    L7 --- L6
    L6 --- L5
    L5 --- L4
    L4 --- L3
    L3 --- L2
    L2 --- L1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# ­¤ōĢ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Define the OSI Reference Model. List all its seven layers in the correct order from top to bottom.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model is a **7-layer conceptual framework** standardized by **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) as **ISO/IEC 7498**. It provides a universal, protocol-independent reference for designing and understanding network communication systems.

**The seven layers from top to bottom:**

1. **Application Layer** (Layer 7)
2. **Presentation Layer** (Layer 6)
3. **Session Layer** (Layer 5)
4. **Transport Layer** (Layer 4)
5. **Network Layer** (Layer 3)
6. **Data Link Layer** (Layer 2)
7. **Physical Layer** (Layer 1)

**Mnemonic (Top ŌåÆ Bottom):** "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
**Differentiate between TCP and UDP. Mention which layer they belong to.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection Type** | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless (no handshake) |
| **Reliability** | Reliable ŌĆö uses ACK, retransmission, sequencing | Unreliable ŌĆö no ACK, no retransmission |
| **Header Size** | 20 bytes (minimum) | 8 bytes (fixed) |
| **Speed** | Slower due to overhead | Faster, lower latency |
| **Use Case** | Web (HTTP/HTTPS), email (SMTP), file transfer (FTP) | DNS, video streaming, VoIP, online gaming |
| **Flow Control** | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| **Congestion Control** | Yes | No |
| **Order of Delivery** | Guaranteed (sequenced) | Not guaranteed |

**Layer:** Both TCP and UDP operate at the **Transport Layer (OSI Layer 4 / TCP-IP Layer 3)**.

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **ŌØō Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the functions of the **Transport Layer** and **Network Layer** in the OSI model in detail. Mention the protocols used at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare and contrast the **OSI model** and the **TCP/IP model** with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Q. (a) ŌĆö Transport & Network Layer

**Transport Layer (OSI Layer 4)**

The Transport Layer is responsible for **end-to-end communication** between the source and destination applications. Its key functions are:

1. **Service Point Addressing (Port Addressing):** Identifies specific applications/processes on a host using 16-bit port numbers (e.g., HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443).

2. **Segmentation and Reassembly:** Breaks large application data into smaller segments for transmission and reassembles them at the destination.

3. **Connection Control:**
   - **Connection-oriented (TCP):** Establishes, maintains, and terminates a logical connection using a 3-way handshake (SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK).
   - **Connectionless (UDP):** No connection setup; data is sent as independent datagrams.

4. **Flow Control:** Prevents the fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver (e.g., using sliding window protocol in TCP).

5. **Error Control:** Detects lost or corrupted segments and triggers retransmission (ACK/NACK mechanism in TCP).

6. **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing:** Multiple applications can send/receive data simultaneously through a single physical link.

**Protocols:** **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**.

---

**Network Layer (OSI Layer 3)**

The Network Layer handles **logical addressing and routing** of packets across multiple interconnected networks. Its key functions are:

1. **Logical Addressing:** Assigns unique **IP addresses** (e.g., IPv4: 32-bit, IPv6: 128-bit) to every device on the network.

2. **Routing:** Determines the optimal path for packets to travel from source to destination through routers using routing protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP).

3. **Packet Forwarding:** Forwards packets from one router to the next on the chosen path.

4. **Fragmentation and Reassembly:** If a packet is too large for the underlying Data Link layer's MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit), the network layer fragments it and reassembles it at the destination.

5. **Internetworking:** Provides a uniform addressing scheme that allows heterogeneous networks (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, 4G) to interoperate.

6. **Congestion Control:** Some protocols (e.g., TCP at transport) work with the network layer to detect and respond to congestion (e.g., using ICMP Source Quench).

**Protocols:** **IP (IPv4/IPv6)**, **ICMP** (for error messages like "Destination Unreachable"), **IGMP** (for multicast group management), **ARP** (for IP-to-MAC resolution).

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Q. (b) ŌĆö OSI vs TCP/IP Comparison

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Layers** | 7 | 4 |
| **Developed by** | ISO (1984) | DARPA (1970s) |
| **Type** | Theoretical / Reference | Practical / Implementation |
| **Application Layer** | Single layer | Combines Application + Presentation + Session |
| **Transport Layer** | Same | Same |
| **Network Layer** | Single "Network" layer | "Internet" layer |
| **Data Link + Physical** | Two separate layers | Combined as "Network Access" layer |
| **Session & Presentation** | Explicit dedicated layers | No separate layers (merged into Application) |
| **Protocol Dependency** | Independent of specific protocols | Built around specific protocols (TCP, IP, UDP) |
| **Adoption** | Reference only | Powers the global Internet |
| **Strictness** | Strict layered boundaries | Loose ŌĆö protocols can span layers |

**Comparison Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O7["OSI 7 Application"] -.-> T4["TCP/IP 4 Application"]
    O6["OSI 6 Presentation"] -.-> T4
    O5["OSI 5 Session"] -.-> T4
    O4["OSI 4 Transport"] ==> T3["TCP/IP 3 Transport"]
    O3["OSI 3 Network"] ==> T2["TCP/IP 2 Internet"]
    O2["OSI 2 Data Link"] -.-> T1["TCP/IP 1 Network Access"]
    O1["OSI 1 Physical"] -.-> T1
```

---

### **ŌØō Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]**

**Q. (a)** What is **Encapsulation**? With a neat diagram, explain how data is encapsulated as it travels down the OSI layers from Application to Physical. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the functions of the **Application Layer**, **Presentation Layer**, and **Session Layer** in the OSI model. Give protocol examples for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Q. (a) ŌĆö Encapsulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** **Encapsulation** is the process by which a protocol at a given layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes a **trailer**) to the data received from the layer above before passing it down to the next lower layer. Each layer's output is called a **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)**.

**Encapsulation Process (Step-by-step):**

1. **Application Layer:** The user generates the original message (Data). No header added. **PDU = Data (Message).**
2. **Transport Layer:** Adds a **Transport Header (e.g., TCP header ŌĆö 20 bytes)** containing source/destination port numbers. **PDU = Segment.**
3. **Network Layer:** Adds a **Network Header (e.g., IP header ŌĆö 20 bytes)** containing source/destination IP addresses. **PDU = Packet (Datagram).**
4. **Data Link Layer:** Adds a **Data Link Header (e.g., Ethernet ŌĆö 14 bytes)** and a **Trailer (FCS ŌĆö 4 bytes)**. **PDU = Frame.**
5. **Physical Layer:** Converts the frame into **electrical/optical signals (bits)** and transmits them over the medium. **PDU = Bits.**

**Mathematical Form:**

$$ PDU = SDU + PCI $$

Where $SDU$ = Service Data Unit (data from the upper layer) and $PCI$ = Protocol Control Information (header/trailer added by the current layer).

**Encapsulation Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["Application: DATA\nUser message 1500 bytes"]
    TRANS["Transport: SEGMENT\n+ TCP Header 20 bytes\nTotal: 1520 bytes"]
    NET["Network: PACKET\n+ IP Header 20 bytes\nTotal: 1540 bytes"]
    DL["Data Link: FRAME\n+ Ethernet Header 14 bytes\n+ Ethernet Trailer 4 bytes\nTotal: 1558 bytes"]
    PHY["Physical: BITS\nEncoded as signals\n12464 bits transmitted"]

    APP --> TRANS --> NET --> DL --> PHY
```

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Q. (b) ŌĆö Application, Presentation & Session Layers

**Application Layer (OSI Layer 7)**
- Provides **network services directly to end-user applications**.
- Acts as the interface between the user application (browser, email client) and the network.
- **Protocols:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, SSH.

**Presentation Layer (OSI Layer 6)**
- Handles **data translation, formatting, encryption/decryption, and compression**.
- Ensures data is in a **readable format** for the Application layer.
- Functions:
  - **Translation:** Converts between different character encodings (ASCII Ōåö EBCDIC).
  - **Encryption/Decryption:** Secures data during transmission (e.g., SSL/TLS).
  - **Compression:** Reduces data size (e.g., JPEG, MPEG, ZIP).
- **Standards/Protocols:** SSL, TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, EBCDIC.

**Session Layer (OSI Layer 5)**
- Establishes, **manages, and terminates sessions** between two communicating hosts.
- Provides **dialogue control** (half-duplex or full-duplex) and **synchronization** (inserts checkpoints for long transmissions so they can resume from the last checkpoint after a failure).
- **Protocols:** NetBIOS, RPC (Remote Procedure Call), PPTP, SIP, H.245.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **ŌØī Confusing Layers with Protocols:** Students often write "HTTP is a transport protocol" ŌĆö **wrong**. HTTP is an **Application layer protocol** that **uses** the transport protocol TCP.
> 2. **ŌØī Forgetting the Trailer:** At the Data Link layer, the PDU includes a **trailer** (FCS/CRC). Many students only add the header and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 3. **ŌØī Wrong PDU Names:** Memorize the exact PDU name for each layer ŌĆö Data (App/Pres/Sess), Segment/Datagram (Transport), Packet (Network), Frame (Data Link), Bits (Physical). Mixing them up is a common KTU deduction.
> 4. **ŌØī Confusing TCP and IP layers:** TCP belongs to the **Transport Layer**, IP belongs to the **Network Layer**. A question like "explain IP at the transport layer" should immediately raise a red flag.
> 5. **ŌØī Wrong Layer Count for TCP/IP:** It is a **4-layer** model, not 5. Do not split it as 5 layers (some references do, but KTU strictly uses 4).

---

## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Ō£ģ **OSI = 7 layers** (ISO/IEC 7498). **TCP/IP = 4 layers** (DARPA, ARPANET).
> - Ō£ģ Mnemonic (OSI TopŌåÆBottom): **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"** = **A P S T N D P** (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical).
> - Ō£ģ Mnemonic (TCP/IP TopŌåÆBottom): **"A T I N"** = **Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access**.
> - Ō£ģ **PDU Names:** Data ŌåÆ Segment/Datagram ŌåÆ Packet ŌåÆ Frame ŌåÆ Bits.
> - Ō£ģ **TCP/IP's 4 layers map to OSI:** App = OSI 5+6+7, Transport = OSI 4, Internet = OSI 3, Network Access = OSI 1+2.
> - Ō£ģ **PDU Formula:** $PDU = SDU + PCI$ ŌĆö always applicable at every layer.
> - Ō£ģ **Header Sizes (minimum):** TCP = 20 B, IP = 20 B, UDP = 8 B, Ethernet Header = 14 B, Ethernet Trailer = 4 B.
> - Ō£ģ **Devices per layer:** Hub (L1), Switch/Bridge (L2), Router (L3), Gateway (L4/L7).
> - Ō£ģ **Encapsulation = Sender's side (adds headers going down). Decapsulation = Receiver's side (removes headers going up).**
> - Ō£ģ **TCP** is connection-oriented + reliable; **UDP** is connectionless + unreliable but faster.
> - Ō£ģ **Port numbers** = Transport Layer (Layer 4). **IP addresses** = Network Layer (Layer 3). **MAC addresses** = Data Link Layer (Layer 2).
> - Ō£ģ **OSI is theoretical**; **TCP/IP is practical** and powers the actual Internet.
> - Ō£ģ **First exam-saver rule:** If a question gives you byte sizes, always compute PDU step-by-step using $PDU = SDU + PCI$ ŌĆö examiners award **method marks** even if the final answer slips by 1 byte.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Categories of Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Categories of Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Computer Networks & Protocols (PCCGT302, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **Categories of Networks** refers to the systematic classification of computer communication systems based on their **geographical scale of operation**, **ownership model**, **physical topology**, **logical architecture**, and **transmission methodology**. This taxonomy is fundamental because the choice of protocol stack (OSI/TCP-IP), transmission media, switching technique, and addressing scheme are all heavily dependent on which category the network belongs to.

The KTU Module 1 syllabus predominantly emphasizes four primary scale-based categories ŌĆö **PAN, LAN, MAN, and WAN** ŌĆö supplemented by classifications based on topology and architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The expected cognitive depth is at **Remember & Understand (L1, L2)** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Students must be able to *define, list, compare, and differentiate* the categories.

### 1.2 The Four Primary Scale-Based Categories

1. **PAN (Personal Area Network):** A network interconnecting personal devices centred around an individual workspace.
2. **LAN (Local Area Network):** A network confined to a single building or campus under a single administrative authority.
3. **MAN (Metropolitan Area Network):** A network spanning a city or metropolitan region, often operated by a telecom provider.
4. **WAN (Wide Area Network):** A network covering large geographical areas such as countries, continents, or the entire globe.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **Indian postal communication system** as a metaphor for data networks:

- A **PAN** is like your *personal notebook* ŌĆö only you (and your immediate Bluetooth-connected devices) communicate within it, spanning just **1 to 10 metres**.
- A **LAN** is like the *internal notice board* of your office or college ŌĆö confined to a single building, managed locally, and very fast.
- A **MAN** is like the *city courier service* operated by Speed Post within a city ŌĆö it covers **5 to 50 km** and serves many organizations.
- A **WAN** is like *India Post between Kerala and Delhi* ŌĆö covers **hundreds to thousands of kilometres**, involves multiple carriers, and is comparatively slower.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** As the geographical scale *increases* (PAN $\rightarrow$ LAN $\rightarrow$ MAN $\rightarrow$ WAN), the **propagation delay typically grows**, the **error rate usually increases**, the **ownership becomes more distributed**, and the **transmission speed per user generally decreases** ŌĆö though modern fiber-optic WANs blur this simplistic view.

### 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

- **Protocol Selection:** A LAN uses Ethernet (CSMA/CD); a WAN requires PPP, HDLC, or MPLS.
- **Hardware Choice:** A PAN uses Bluetooth/Zigbee radios; a WAN uses leased lines or satellites.
- **Performance Budgeting:** Designers estimate delay, jitter, and packet loss differently for each category.
- **Cost & Security Models:** Ownership determines administrative control and security perimeter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Characteristics of Each Network Category

#### **2.1.1 Personal Area Network (PAN)**
- **Coverage:** ~**1 to 10 metres** (body-centric range).
- **Typical Technologies:** Bluetooth, Infrared (IrDA), ZigBee, NFC, USB.
- **Data Rate:** Upto **3 Mbps** (Bluetooth Classic) to **480 Mbps** (USB 2.0 wired PAN).
- **Ownership:** Single individual.
- **Example:** Wireless headset connected to a smartphone; smartwatch syncing with a phone.

#### **2.1.2 Local Area Network (LAN)**
- **Coverage:** ~**10 metres to 1 kilometre** (single room/building/campus).
- **Typical Technologies:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Token Ring (legacy).
- **Data Rate:** **100 Mbps to 10 Gbps** (modern Ethernet); Wi-Fi 6 supports upto 9.6 Gbps.
- **Ownership:** Single organization (private).
- **Example:** College computer lab network, office intranet, home Wi-Fi.

#### **2.1.3 Metropolitan Area Network (MAN)**
- **Coverage:** ~**5 to 50 kilometres** (entire city).
- **Typical Technologies:** DQDB (IEEE 802.6), FDDI, Metro Ethernet, MPLS.
- **Data Rate:** **100 Mbps to 10 Gbps** (backbone).
- **Ownership:** Often a public utility, telecom operator, or large consortium.
- **Example:** Cable TV network providing internet, BSNL/Reliance Jio city-wide fiber.

#### **2.1.4 Wide Area Network (WAN)**
- **Coverage:** **Hundreds to thousands of kilometres** (country/continent/global).
- **Typical Technologies:** MPLS, ATM, Frame Relay, leased lines, satellite links, undersea cables.
- **Data Rate:** **1.5 Mbps (T1) to 100+ Gbps** (modern submarine optical).
- **Ownership:** Multiple service providers / Tier-1 ISPs.
- **Example:** Internet backbone, banking ATM network, railway reservation network (IRCTC).

### 2.2 Secondary Classification Axes

#### **By Topology (Physical Layout):**
- **Bus:** Single backbone cable shared by all nodes.
- **Star:** All nodes connect to a central hub/switch.
- **Ring:** Nodes form a closed loop (Token Ring, FDDI).
- **Mesh:** Every node connects to every other node (full or partial).
- **Tree:** Hierarchical combination of stars.
- **Hybrid:** Mixture of two or more topologies.

#### **By Architecture (Logical Model):**
- **Client-Server:** Centralized server provides resources to client machines.
- **Peer-to-Peer (P2P):** All nodes are equal; any node can request or serve resources.

#### **By Transmission Mode:**
- **Broadcast Networks:** Single shared channel; one sender, many receivers (Ethernet, Wi-Fi).
- **Point-to-Point Networks:** Dedicated link between two endpoints; routing required (WANs).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all formulas you must memorize** for solving numerical/analytical questions on network categories and their performance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{prop}} &= \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}} \quad \text{[seconds]} \\[4pt]
d_{\text{trans}} &= \frac{\text{Packet Length } L}{\text{Link Bandwidth } R} \quad \text{[seconds]} \\[4pt]
d_{\text{end-to-end}} &= d_{\text{prop}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{proc}} \\[4pt]
\text{Usable Hosts} &= 2^{n} - 2 \quad \text{(where } n = \text{number of host bits)} \\[4pt]
\text{Bandwidth-Delay Product} &= R \times d_{\text{prop}} \quad \text{[bits]} \\[4pt]
\text{RTT} &= 2 \times d_{\text{prop}} \quad \text{(for one-way propagation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 Master Comparison Table (Direct KTU Exam Ready)

| **Parameter** | **PAN** | **LAN** | **MAN** | **WAN** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Geographic Scope** | 1 to 10 m | 10 m to 1 km | 5 to 50 km | Hundreds to thousands of km |
| **Typical Data Rate** | Upto 480 Mbps | 100 Mbps to 10 Gbps | 100 Mbps to 10 Gbps | 1.5 Mbps to 100+ Gbps |
| **Propagation Delay** | Microseconds order | Micro to milliseconds order | Milliseconds order | Tens of milliseconds order |
| **Ownership** | Personal | Private (single org) | Public/Private (city-wide) | Multiple ISPs / Public |
| **Error Rate** | Very Low | Very Low | Low to Moderate | Moderate to High |
| **Design Cost** | Very Low | Low to Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Example Technology** | Bluetooth, USB | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | Metro Ethernet, FDDI | MPLS, ATM, Satellite |
| **Protocol Family** | IEEE 802.15 | IEEE 802.3, 802.11 | IEEE 802.6, DQDB | PPP, HDLC, MPLS |
| **Administration** | Self | Single Admin | Multiple / Shared | Distributed / Tiered |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineers** use this taxonomy to decide the **physical layer media** (copper vs fiber vs wireless).
- **System Administrators** size their **subnet masks** differently based on category (LAN often uses /24, WAN uses /30 for point-to-point).
- **Cloud Architects** select **CDN edge servers** in LAN/MAN proximity to reduce WAN latency.
- **Cybersecurity teams** define **perimeter firewalls** at LAN boundaries and **DDoS scrubbing centres** at WAN edges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section converts the conceptual categories into **numerical, derivation-style reasoning** ŌĆö the exact cognitive level required for KTU Part B problems.

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 ŌĆö Propagation Delay vs. Network Category

**Problem Statement:** Compute the one-way propagation delay for a signal travelling across each category of network, assuming signal propagation at **$2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** (typical speed in copper/fiber).

Given Reference Distances:

| **Category** | **Symbolic Distance $d$** |
|---|---|
| PAN | 10 m |
| LAN | 1000 m (1 km) |
| MAN | 25,000 m (25 km) |
| WAN | 5,000,000 m (5000 km ŌĆö interstate) |

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

The fundamental propagation delay equation is:

$$d_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s}$$

where $d$ = physical distance and $s$ = propagation speed.

**Case A ŌĆö PAN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{prop, PAN}} &= \frac{10 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{7}} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 50 \text{ nanoseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B ŌĆö LAN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{prop, LAN}} &= \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} \\[4pt]
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 5 \text{ microseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case C ŌĆö MAN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{prop, MAN}} &= \frac{25{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 125 \text{ microseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case D ŌĆö WAN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{prop, WAN}} &= \frac{5{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 25 \text{ milliseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Going from PAN to WAN increases propagation delay by a factor of **$5 \times 10^{5}$** (500,000 times). This is the primary reason why WAN protocols must incorporate flow control, error recovery, and large bandwidth-delay products.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 ŌĆö Transmission Delay for a Packet

**Problem Statement:** A 1500-byte Ethernet frame is transmitted over (a) a 1 Gbps LAN and (b) a 1.5 Mbps T1 WAN. Compute the transmission delay in each case.

Given:
- $L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 1500 \times 8 = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}$.
- $R_{\text{LAN}} = 10^{9} \text{ bps}$.
- $R_{\text{WAN}} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}$.

The transmission delay formula:

$$d_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}$$

#### Case (a) ŌĆö LAN Transmission:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{trans, LAN}} &= \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^{9} \text{ bps}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 12 \text{ microseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Case (b) ŌĆö WAN Transmission:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{trans, WAN}} &= \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}} \\[4pt]
&= 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 8 \text{ milliseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even for the *same packet size*, the WAN link takes **~667 times longer** to transmit the frame because of its lower bandwidth. This is why protocol designers use **jumbo frames, compression, and protocol offload engines** in WAN environments.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 ŌĆö Usable Host Count in a LAN Subnet

**Problem Statement:** An engineering college wants to design a private IPv4 LAN. Given a default Class B network segment using a **/20 subnet mask**, calculate the number of usable host addresses.

Given:
- IPv4 address = 32 bits.
- Subnet mask /20 $\Rightarrow$ 20 bits for network prefix, leaving $n = 32 - 20 = 12$ bits for hosts.

The usable hosts formula:

$$\text{Usable Hosts} = 2^{n} - 2$$

The subtraction of 2 accounts for the **network address** (all host bits = 0) and the **broadcast address** (all host bits = 1).

#### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Usable Hosts} &= 2^{12} - 2 \\[4pt]
&= 4096 - 2 \\[4pt]
&= 4094 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** KTU examiners frequently test whether students *remember the -2 rule*. Forgetting it costs **1 mark** easily.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 ŌĆö Bandwidth-Delay Product Scaling

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the bandwidth-delay product (BDP) for a 5000 km WAN link at 10 Gbps bandwidth. This represents the maximum number of bits that can be "in-flight" on the link.

Given:
- $R = 10 \text{ Gbps} = 10^{10} \text{ bps}$.
- $s = 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$.
- $d = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation:

First compute propagation delay:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{prop}} &= \frac{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = 0.025 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute BDP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BDP} &= R \times d_{\text{prop}} \\[4pt]
&= 10^{10} \text{ bps} \times 0.025 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
&= 250 \text{ Megabits} \approx 31.25 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The TCP receive window must be at least **31.25 MB** to fully utilize this WAN link. Default Windows TCP window (~64 KB) is grossly insufficient ŌĆö explaining why **TCP window scaling** is essential in WANs and unnecessary in LANs.

---

### 3.5 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| **Network Category** | **Avg. Distance** | **Avg. Propagation Delay** | **BDP @ 10 Gbps** | **Typical Window Needed** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PAN | 10 m | 50 ns | 500 bits | 64 bytes |
| LAN | 1 km | 5 $\mu$s | 50,000 bits | ~6 KB |
| MAN | 25 km | 125 $\mu$s | 1.25 $\times 10^{6}$ bits | ~156 KB |
| WAN | 5000 km | 25 ms | 2.5 $\times 10^{8}$ bits | ~31.25 MB |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** Define **LAN** and **WAN**. State **two distinguishing parameters** between them.

**Model Answer:**

A **LAN (Local Area Network)** is a privately owned network that interconnects computers and devices within a limited geographical area such as a single building, office, or college campus, typically spanning **10 metres to 1 kilometre**.

A **WAN (Wide Area Network)** is a data communication network that covers a broad geographical area, spanning **hundreds to thousands of kilometres** across cities, countries, or continents, and is generally operated by multiple telecom service providers or ISPs.

| **Parameter** | **LAN** | **WAN** |
|---|---|---|
| Geographic Scope | Building or Campus | Country or Globe |
| Ownership | Single organization | Multiple ISPs |
| Typical Data Rate | 100 Mbps to 10 Gbps | 1.5 Mbps to 100+ Gbps |
| Propagation Delay | Microseconds order | Tens of milliseconds order |

*(Any two distinguishing parameters carry 2 marks; definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** What is a **Metropolitan Area Network (MAN)**? Give **one real-world example**.

**Model Answer:**

A **Metropolitan Area Network (MAN)** is a computer network that interconnects users and devices across a city or metropolitan region, typically covering **5 to 50 kilometres**. It is larger than a LAN but smaller than a WAN, and is often operated by a public utility, municipal corporation, or large telecom service provider.

**Real-World Example:** The **BSNL BharatNet fiber network** that provides city-wide broadband connectivity across various municipalities, or the **Jio Fiber metropolitan backbone** delivering gigabit internet to homes within a city.

*(Definition: 2 marks; Example: 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** ŌĆö each has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

#### (a) Compare **PAN, LAN, MAN, and WAN** based on any **seven parameters** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The classification of computer networks by geographical scale is fundamental to designing appropriate protocols, hardware, and security models. The four primary categories are:

**1. Personal Area Network (PAN):**
Spans **1 to 10 metres**, centred on an individual user. Uses technologies like Bluetooth, ZigBee, NFC, and USB. Data rate up to **480 Mbps**. Example: Smartphone connected to a wireless earpiece.

**2. Local Area Network (LAN):**
Spans **10 metres to 1 kilometre** within a single building or campus. Uses Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) and Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11). Data rate **100 Mbps to 10 Gbps**. Example: Computer network in an engineering college lab.

**3. Metropolitan Area Network (MAN):**
Spans **5 to 50 kilometres** across a city. Uses Metro Ethernet, FDDI, DQDB. Data rate **100 Mbps to 10 Gbps** (backbone). Example: City-wide cable TV broadband network.

**4. Wide Area Network (WAN):**
Spans **hundreds to thousands of kilometres** across countries/continents. Uses MPLS, ATM, leased lines, satellites. Data rate **1.5 Mbps to 100+ Gbps**. Example: Global Internet backbone, IRCTC reservation network.

**Master Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **PAN** | **LAN** | **MAN** | **WAN** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Geographic Scope | 1 to 10 m | 10 m to 1 km | 5 to 50 km | Hundreds to thousands of km |
| Data Rate | Upto 480 Mbps | 100 Mbps to 10 Gbps | 100 Mbps to 10 Gbps | 1.5 Mbps to 100+ Gbps |
| Ownership | Personal | Private | Public or shared | Distributed, multi-ISP |
| Error Rate | Very Low | Very Low | Low | Higher |
| Cost | Negligible | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Example Tech | Bluetooth | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | Metro Ethernet | MPLS, Satellite |
| Admin | Self | Single admin | Shared | Multi-tier |

*(7 parameters tabulated ŌĆö each row contributes 1 mark for accurate comparison.)*

#### (b) Explain the **classification of networks by topology** with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Network topology refers to the **physical or logical arrangement** of nodes and links in a network. It dictates cabling, fault tolerance, scalability, and performance.

**1. Bus Topology:**
All nodes share a single backbone cable. Data travels in both directions. Failure of the backbone crashes the entire network. *Used in legacy 10BASE2/10BASE5 Ethernet.*

**2. Star Topology:**
All nodes connect to a central hub or switch. Easy to install and isolate faults; central device is a single point of failure. *Dominant in modern Ethernet LANs.*

**3. Ring Topology:**
Each node connects to exactly two neighbours, forming a closed loop. Token-passing controls access. *Used in Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) and FDDI.*

**4. Mesh Topology:**
Every node connects to one or more other nodes. **Full mesh** = $n(n-1)/2$ links for $n$ nodes; **partial mesh** is selective. Extremely fault-tolerant. *Used in WAN backbones and military networks.*

**5. Tree Topology:**
Hierarchical combination of multiple stars. Scalable but dependent on root nodes. *Used in large enterprise networks.*

**6. Hybrid Topology:**
Combines two or more of the above. Real-world networks (e.g., Internet) are predominantly hybrid.

**Diagram (text-rendered since this is a textual response):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUS["Bus Topology"]
        BUS_N1((Node1)) --- BUSLINE((Backbone))
        BUS_N2((Node2)) --- BUSLINE
        BUS_N3((Node3)) --- BUSLINE
        BUS_N4((Node4)) --- BUSLINE
    end
    subgraph STAR["Star Topology"]
        STAR_HUB{Hub}
        STAR_HUB --- STAR_N1((Node1))
        STAR_HUB --- STAR_N2((Node2))
        STAR_HUB --- STAR_N3((Node3))
        STAR_HUB --- STAR_N4((Node4))
    end

        RING_N3 --- RING_N4((Node4))
        RING_N4 --- RING_N1
    end

        MESH_N3 --- MESH_N4((Node4))
        MESH_N4 --- MESH_N1
        MESH_N1 --- MESH_N3
        MESH_N2 --- MESH_N4
    end
```
