### **Question B** (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Differentiate between **Client-Server architecture** and **Peer-to-Peer architecture** with **suitable examples and a neat diagram**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Client-Server Architecture:**
A centralized model where dedicated **servers** provide resources, services, or data, and **clients** request and consume those services. The server is powerful, always-on, and manages access control, security, and resource allocation. Clients are relatively thin and depend on the server.

*Examples:* Web browsing (browser client Ōåö web server), email (Outlook client Ōåö Gmail server), database access (application Ōåö MySQL server).

**Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture:**
A decentralized model where **every node (peer)** acts as both a client and a server. Peers share resources (files, bandwidth, storage) directly with each other without a central coordinator. The network is highly scalable and resilient but harder to secure and manage.

*Examples:* BitTorrent file sharing, blockchain networks, Skype (early versions), LAN file sharing in Windows.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Client-Server** | **Peer-to-Peer** |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Centralized | Decentralized |
| Server Required | Yes | No dedicated server |
| Resource Sharing | Server controls | All peers share equally |
| Scalability | Limited by server capacity | Highly scalable |
| Security | Centralized, easy to enforce | Distributed, harder to enforce |
| Cost | High (server hardware) | Low |
| Example | Web, Email, Banking | BitTorrent, Blockchain |
| Failure Impact | Server crash stops service | Single peer failure is tolerable |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS["Client Server Model"]
        CS_SRV[Server]
        CS_C1((Client 1)) --> CS_SRV
        CS_C2((Client 2)) --> CS_SRV
        CS_C3((Client 3)) --> CS_SRV
        CS_C4((Client 4)) --> CS_SRV
    end
    subgraph P2P["Peer to Peer Model"]
        P2P_A((Peer A)) <--> P2P_B((Peer B))
        P2P_B <--> P2P_C((Peer C))
        P2P_C <--> P2P_D((Peer D))
        P2P_D <--> P2P_A
        P2P_A <--> P2P_C
        P2P_B <--> P2P_D
    end
    style CS_SRV fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style CS fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style P2P fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

*(3 marks explanation + 2 marks table + 2 marks diagram = 7 marks.)*

#### (b) With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the concept of an **Internetwork**. Why is it essential in modern communication? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An **Internetwork** (often shortened to "Internet") is a collection of two or more distinct networks (LANs, MANs, or WANs) interconnected via **routers** or **gateways** that operate at the network layer (Layer 3 of the OSI model). The constituent networks may use **different protocols, addressing schemes, and physical media**, but they communicate through a common internetworking protocol suite (typically **TCP/IP**).

**Key Components of an Internetwork:**
1. **Multiple Heterogeneous Networks:** Each subnet (LAN, MAN, WAN) has its own internal topology and protocol.
2. **Routers:** Forward packets between networks based on logical IP addresses.
3. **Gateways:** Translate between different protocol families (e.g., TCP/IP to SNA).
4. **Common Protocol Suite:** TCP/IP provides universal interoperability.
5. **Unique Addressing:** Each host has a globally unique IP address.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NET_A["Network A LAN"]
        A1((Host A1)) --- A_SW[Switch A]
        A2((Host A2)) --- A_SW
        A3((Host A3)) --- A_SW
        A_SW --- A_RTR[Router A]
    end
    subgraph NET_B["Network B MAN"]
        B1((Host B1)) --- B_SW[Switch B]
        B2((Host B2)) --- B_SW
        B_SW --- B_RTR[Router B]
    end
    subgraph NET_C["Network C WAN"]
        C1((Host C1)) --- C_RTR[Router C]
        C2((Host C2)) --- C_RTR
        C3((Host C3)) --- C_RTR
    end
    A_RTR ---|WAN Link| B_RTR
    B_RTR ---|WAN Link| C_RTR
    C_RTR ---|Submarine Link| GLOBAL((Global Internet Backbone))
    style NET_A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style NET_B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style NET_C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style A_RTR fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style B_RTR fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style C_RTR fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style GLOBAL fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff
```

**Why Internetworks are Essential:**
1. **Scalability:** No single network can scale infinitely; internetworks allow modular growth.
2. **Heterogeneity:** Different organizations use different hardware; internetworks bridge them.
3. **Redundancy:** Multiple paths between networks improve fault tolerance.
4. **Global Reach:** Enables worldwide communication (e-commerce, banking, social media).
5. **Resource Sharing:** Distributed databases, cloud services, and CDNs rely on internetworks.
6. **Economic Efficiency:** Leverages existing network infrastructure rather than rebuilding.

*(3 marks diagram + 2 marks explanation + 2 marks significance = 7 marks.)*

> 5. **Topology classification** must mention both **physical and logical** aspects for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Pin this in your notebook!**

- **Four scale-based categories:** PAN $\rightarrow$ LAN $\rightarrow$ MAN $\rightarrow$ WAN.
- **PAN range:** 1 to 10 metres; **LAN:** 10 m to 1 km; **MAN:** 5 to 50 km; **WAN:** Hundreds to thousands of km.
- **PAN examples:** Bluetooth, USB, ZigBee, NFC.
- **LAN examples:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11).
- **MAN examples:** BSNL BharatNet, Jio Fiber metro, DQDB.
- **WAN examples:** Internet backbone, submarine cables, satellite links, MPLS.
- **Six topologies:** Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree, Hybrid.
- **Full mesh links** for $n$ nodes = $n(n-1)/2$.
- **Two architectures:** Client-Server (centralized) vs Peer-to-Peer (distributed).
- **Broadcast networks** use a shared channel (Ethernet, Wi-Fi); **point-to-point networks** use dedicated links (WAN).
- **Propagation delay formula:** $d_{\text{prop}} = d / s$, where $s \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber/copper.
- **Transmission delay formula:** $d_{\text{trans}} = L / R$.
- **Usable hosts in subnet:** $2^{n} - 2$ (subtract network and broadcast addresses).
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $R \times d_{\text{prop}}$ ŌĆö indicates TCP window size required for full link utilization.
- **As scale increases:** Delay increases, ownership becomes distributed, error rate grows, cost rises sharply.
- **As scale decreases:** Speed increases, security is easier, administration is simpler, hardware cost drops.
- **Internetwork** = interconnection of networks via routers using a common protocol suite (TCP/IP).
- **KTU 2024 exam weightage:** 2-mark definitions, 3-mark comparisons, 7+7 mark detailed Part B questions.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing MAN coverage with WAN; always verify range values.
- **Most asked question pattern:** "Compare LAN/MAN/WAN" ŌĆö memorize the master table above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare," always present a **tabulated answer** with at least 5ŌĆō7 parameters. Tabular answers fetch significantly more marks than paragraph-style comparisons because examiners can quickly verify each parameter for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Models: OSI Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Network Models: OSI Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model:** A **7-layer abstract reference model** defined by the **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in the standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1** (published in 1984, revised 1994). It standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstraction layers, ensuring **interoperability** between diverse vendor products and technologies. The OSI model partitions the **flow of data** through a network into **seven distinct hierarchical layers**, where each layer communicates only with its **adjacent layers** (above and below) and with the **peer layer** on the remote system through a well-defined **Service Access Point (SAP)** and **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Postal Service Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Check ŌĆö "The 7-Step Letter Delivery System":** > > Imagine two friends, **Alice** (in Delhi) and **Bob** (in London), exchanging a letter. The OSI model works exactly like this: > > 1. Alice **writes** the letter content (Application Layer). > 2. She **chooses the language** (English/Hindi) ŌĆö Presentation Layer handles language/format. > 3. The letter is **sealed in an envelope** with addresses ŌĆö Session Layer. > 4. Bob's address is **translated and added** with postal codes ŌĆö Transport Layer. > 5. A **courier company** (DHL/India Post) is chosen and tracking added ŌĆö Network Layer. > 6. The letter is **sorted at the post office** into bins and trucks ŌĆö Data Link Layer. > 7. Finally, the **truck drives on the highway** ŌĆö Physical Layer. > > Bob receives the letter and **peels off the layers in reverse order**. The "envelopes" (PDUs) are removed one by one until the original message is revealed. ### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Standards Vocabulary (Board-Favorite Keywords):** > > - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** The formatted unit of data at any given layer. Each layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes **trailer**) called **encapsulation**. > - **SAP (Service Access Point):** The logical interface through which one layer provides services to the layer above it. > - **Peer-to-Peer Process:** Communication between the same layer on two different machines. > - **Encapsulation:** The process of adding control information (headers) as data moves **down** the layers at the sender. > - **Decapsulation:** The reverse process at the receiver as data moves **up** the layers. **Standard PDU Names per Layer (MEMORIZE THIS):** | Layer | PDU Name | Memorization Hint | |---|---|---| | Application | **Data** | "The user's raw data" | | Presentation | **Data** | "Formatted data" | | Session | **Data** | "Session-tagged data" | | Transport | **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP) | "S for TCP, D for UDP" | | Network | **Packet** | "P for Network Path" | | Data Link | **Frame** | "F for Frame" | | Physical | **Bits** | "1s and 0s on the wire" | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Layers ŌĆö Structured Layer-by-Layer Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Mnemonic (Top-Down):** "**A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing" > > **Bottom-Up Mnemonic:** "**P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way" #### **Layer 7: Application Layer** - **Role:** Closest to the end user. Provides **network services** directly to user applications (email, file transfer, web browsing). - **Examples of Protocols:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, SSH. - **KTU Note:** This is **NOT** the application itself (e.g., Chrome browser); it is the **protocol** the application uses to communicate. #### **Layer 6: Presentation Layer** - **Role:** **Data translation, encryption/decryption, and compression**. Ensures data is in a usable format. - **Functions:** Character encoding (ASCII ŌåÆ EBCDIC), data format translation (`.docx` ŌåÆ `.pdf`), SSL/TLS encryption, JPEG/MPEG compression. - **Key Point:** Handles syntax and semantics of data exchange. #### **Layer 5: Session Layer** - **Role:** Establishes, **manages, and terminates sessions** between two communicating hosts. - **Functions:** Authentication, reconnection after interruption, dialog control (half-duplex/full-duplex), checkpointing. - **Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP. - **KTU Highlight:** Distinguish from Transport Layer ŌĆö Session handles **dialog coordination**, Transport handles **reliable end-to-end delivery**. #### **Layer 4: Transport Layer** - **Role:** Provides **end-to-end communication**, error recovery, flow control, and segmentation/reassembly. - **Two Main Protocols:** - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** Connection-oriented, reliable, uses **3-way handshake**, segments, sequence numbers. - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** Connectionless, unreliable, low overhead, used for streaming/DNS. - **PDU:** Segment (TCP) or Datagram (UDP). - **Critical:** This is the **first end-to-end layer**; lower layers are hop-by-hop. #### **Layer 3: Network Layer** - **Role:** **Logical addressing (IP)** and **routing** of packets across multiple networks. - **Key Protocols:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP, ARP. - **Devices:** **Routers** operate primarily at this layer. - **PDU:** Packet. #### **Layer 2: Data Link Layer** - **Role:** **Node-to-node** data transfer on the same network segment. Handles **framing**, **MAC addressing**, and error detection/correction on the physical link. - **Two Sublayers:** - **LLC (Logical Link Control):** Upper sublayer, interfaces with Network Layer (IEEE 802.2). - **MAC (Media Access Control):** Lower sublayer, controls access to the shared medium (IEEE 802.3 Ethernet, 802.11 Wi-Fi). - **Devices:** **Switches, Bridges, NICs**. - **PDU:** Frame. - **Error Detection:** CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check). #### **Layer 1: Physical Layer** - **Role:** Transmits **raw bits** over a physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless). Defines **electrical, mechanical, and procedural** specifications. - **Specifications:** Voltage levels, bit duration, connector types (RJ45, LC), cable specs (Cat 6, OS2 fiber), modulation (NRZ, Manchester). - **Devices:** Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Network Interface Cards (electrical specs). - **PDU:** Bits. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Layer Characteristics Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU Board):** | Layer # | Layer Name | PDU | Address Type | Key Device | Protocol Example | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 7 | Application | Data | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | HTTP, FTP | | 6 | Presentation | Data | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | SSL/TLS, JPEG | | 5 | Session | Data | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | NetBIOS, RPC | | 4 | Transport | Segment/Datagram | **Port Number** | ŌĆö | TCP, UDP | | 3 | Network | Packet | **Logical IP** | **Router** | IP, ICMP | | 2 | Data Link | Frame | **MAC Address** | **Switch** | Ethernet, PPP | | 1 | Physical | Bits | ŌĆö | **Hub** | RS-232, 1000BASE-T | ### 2.3 Encapsulation & Decapsulation Process > [!IMPORTANT] > **Encapsulation Flow (Sender):** Data flows **DOWN** from Application ŌåÆ Physical. Each layer **adds a header** (Layer 2 also adds a trailer for CRC). > > **Decapsulation Flow (Receiver):** Data flows **UP** from Physical ŌåÆ Application. Each layer **removes its corresponding header** and processes it. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why the OSI Model Matters in Engineering:** > > 1. **Vendor Independence:** Any combination of hardware/software from different vendors can interoperate. > 2. **Modular Troubleshooting:** The layered approach simplifies network debugging (e.g., "ping works ŌåÆ Layers 1-3 OK; HTTPS fails ŌåÆ Layers 4-7 issue"). > 3. **Protocol Design:** New protocols can be designed for a specific layer without redesigning the entire stack. > 4. **Education & Standardization:** Provides a common **vocabulary** for engineers worldwide. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Header Addition Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Representation of Encapsulation:** > > Let $D_7$ be the original **Application Data**. > As data moves down each layer $i$, a header $H_i$ (and trailer $T_2$ at Layer 2) is appended. > > The total bit stream on the physical medium is: $$ B_{transmitted} = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + H_7 + D_7 + T_2 $$ > Where each $H_i$ represents the control overhead added at layer $i$. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step PDU Transformation Walkthrough > **Step 1 ŌĆö Application Layer (L7):** > > User data (e.g., HTTP GET request) is generated. No header is added. > > $$PDU_7 = D_7$$ > **Step 2 ŌĆö Presentation Layer (L6):** > > Data is encrypted (e.g., TLS) and formatted. Header $H_6$ added. > > $$PDU_6 = H_6 + D_7$$ > **Step 3 ŌĆö Session Layer (L5):** > > Session ID and dialog control info added. > > $$PDU_5 = H_5 + H_6 + D_7$$ > **Step 4 ŌĆö Transport Layer (L4):** > > **TCP** segment is formed. Source/Destination **port numbers** and sequence numbers added in $H_4$. > > $$PDU_4 = H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + D_7$$ > **Step 5 ŌĆö Network Layer (L3):** > > **IP packet** is formed. Source/Destination **IP addresses** added in $H_3$. > > $$PDU_3 = H_3 + H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + D_7$$ > **Step 6 ŌĆö Data Link Layer (L2):** > > **Frame** is formed. Source/Destination **MAC addresses** added in $H_2$, and a **CRC trailer** $T_2$ is appended for error detection. > > $$PDU_2 = H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + D_7 + T_2$$ > **Step 7 ŌĆö Physical Layer (L1):** > > The entire frame is converted into a stream of **bits** and transmitted over the medium. > > $$PDU_1 = \text{bits}(H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + D_7 + T_2)$$ ### 3.3 Numerical Example: Header Overhead Calculation > **Problem (KTU Style):** > > An application generates **1200 bytes** of data. The headers added at Layers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 are **18, 20, 20, 5, 5 bytes** respectively. Layer 2 also adds a **4-byte trailer**. Calculate: > > (a) Total bytes transmitted at the physical layer. > (b) Header overhead percentage. > **Solution:** > **Step (a) ŌĆö Total Transmitted Bytes:** > > Sum all headers + data + trailer: > > $$\text{Total} = H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + D_7 + T_2$$ > > $$\text{Total} = 18 + 20 + 20 + 5 + 5 + 1200 + 4$$ > > $$\boxed{\text{Total} = 1272 \text{ bytes}}$$ > **Step (b) ŌĆö Overhead Percentage:** > > Overhead = Total ŌłÆ Original Data > > $$\text{Overhead} = 1272 - 1200 = 72 \text{ bytes}$$ > > $$\text{Overhead \%} = \left( \frac{72}{1272} \right) \times 100$$ > > $$\boxed{\text{Overhead \%} \approx 5.66\%}$$ ### 3.4 Address Mapping Logic (Inter-Layer Translation) > [!NOTE] > **Address Translation Across Layers (Logical Flow):** > > $$\text{Application uses: } \text{URL/Domain Name} \xrightarrow{\text{DNS resolves}} \text{IP Address (L3)} \xrightarrow{\text{ARP resolves}} \text{MAC Address (L2)}$$ > > - **Layer 7** ŌåÆ Symbolic name (`www.ktu.edu.in`) > - **Layer 4** ŌåÆ Port number (`80` for HTTP, `443` for HTTPS) > - **Layer 3** ŌåÆ Logical IP (`192.168.1.10`) > - **Layer 2** ŌåÆ Physical MAC (`AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`) > - **Layer 1** ŌåÆ No address; only bit transmission ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The OSI 7-Layer Reference Model (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7: APPLICATION
HTTP FTP SMTP DNS"] L6["LAYER 6: PRESENTATION
SSL TLS JPEG MPEG"] L5["LAYER 5: SESSION
NetBIOS RPC SIP"] L4["LAYER 4: TRANSPORT
TCP UDP"] L3["LAYER 3: NETWORK
IP ICMP OSPF"] L2["LAYER 2: DATA LINK
Ethernet WiFi PPP"] L1["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL
Cables Hubs Bits"] L7 --> L6 L6 --> L5 L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 DEV1["Routers: L3"] DEV2["Switches: L2"] DEV3["Hubs: L1"] ``` ### 4.2 Encapsulation & Decapsulation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENDER["SENDER SIDE - ENCAPSULATION"] A1["Application Data"] --> A2["L7 Header + Data"] A2 --> A3["L6 Header + L7 PDU"] A3 --> A4["L5 Header + L6 PDU"] A4 --> A5["L4 Segment: TCP Header + L5 PDU"] A5 --> A6["L3 Packet: IP Header + L4 Segment"] A6 --> A7["L2 Frame: MAC Header + L3 Packet + CRC Trailer"] A7 --> A8["L1: Bits on Physical Medium"] end A8 -->|Transmission| B1 subgraph RECEIVER["RECEIVER SIDE - DECAPSULATION"] B1["L1: Bits Received"] --> B2["L2: Remove MAC Header, Check CRC, Extract L3 Packet"] B2 --> B3["L3: Remove IP Header, Route Up to L4"] B3 --> B4["L4: Reassemble Segment, Remove TCP Header"] B4 --> B5["L5: Close/Manage Session"] B5 --> B6["L6: Decrypt, Decompress"] B6 --> B7["L7: Deliver Data to Application"] end ``` ### 4.3 Layer-to-Address Mapping Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD APP["User Types: https://ktu.edu.in"] --> L7MAP["L7: Domain Name: ktu.edu.in"] L7MAP --> DNSQUERY["DNS Query Resolves"] DNSQUERY --> L3MAP["L3: IP Address: 103.21.58.118"] L3MAP --> ARPQ["ARP Query Resolves"] ARPQ --> L2MAP["L2: MAC Address: 00-1A-2B-3C-4D-5E"] L2MAP --> L4MAP["L4: Port 443 for HTTPS"] L4MAP --> L1MAP["L1: Electrical Signals on Copper/Fiber"] ``` ### 4.4 OSI vs TCP/IP Model Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OSI7["OSI MODEL 7 LAYERS"] OSI7L["7 Application"] OSI6L["6 Presentation"] OSI5L["5 Session"] OSI4L["4 Transport"] OSI3L["3 Network"] OSI2L["2 Data Link"] OSI1L["1 Physical"] end subgraph TCPIP["TCP/IP MODEL 4 LAYERS"] TCPAPP["Application"] TCPTRANS["Transport"] TCPIPNET["Internet"] TCPACC["Network Access"] end OSI7L --- TCPAPP OSI6L --- TCPAPP OSI5L --- TCPAPP OSI4L --- TCPTRANS OSI3L --- TCPIPNET OSI2L --- TCPACC OSI1L --- TCPACC ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define the OSI Reference Model. List its seven layers in the correct order from top to bottom. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 Mark):** > The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model** is a 7-layer conceptual framework defined by ISO in standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It standardizes the functions of a communication system into abstraction layers, enabling interoperability between heterogeneous systems. > > **Seven Layers Top-Down (2 Marks):** > > 1. **Application Layer** > 2. **Presentation Layer** > 3. **Session Layer** > 4. **Transport Layer** > 5. **Network Layer** > 6. **Data Link Layer** > 7. **Physical Layer --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q2. What is a PDU? Name the PDU at the Network, Data Link, and Transport layers. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **PDU Definition (1 Mark):** > A **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)** is the unit of data that consists of **control information (header/trailer)** and **user data**, formatted at a specific layer of the OSI model for transmission. > > **PDU Names (2 Marks):** > > - **Network Layer (L3):** **Packet** > - **Data Link Layer (L2):** **Frame** > - **Transport Layer (L4):** **Segment** (for TCP) or **Datagram** (for UDP) --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ŌĆö Question Choice A (14 Marks)]** ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a) Explain the functions of the OSI model in detail. (7 Marks)** **(b) Describe the Data Link Layer and the Physical Layer with their key functions and associated protocols/devices. (7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks** > **Step 1 ŌĆö Introduction (1 Mark):** > > The OSI model was developed by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** as a part of the **OSI initiative** to establish a common reference for data communication. It divides the communication process into **seven layers**, each performing a well-defined set of functions and communicating with adjacent layers only. > **Step 2 ŌĆö Layer-Wise Functions (5 Marks):** > > | Layer | Primary Functions | > |---|---| > | **Application (L7)** | Provides network services to end-user applications (HTTP, FTP, SMTP) | > | **Presentation (L6)** | Data translation, encryption/decryption, compression (TLS, JPEG) | > | **Session (L5)** | Establishes, manages, terminates sessions; dialog control (NetBIOS, RPC) | > | **Transport (L4)** | End-to-end connection, segmentation, flow control, error recovery (TCP, UDP) | > | **Network (L3)** | Logical addressing, routing, packet forwarding (IP, ICMP) | > | **Data Link (L2)** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (CRC), media access (Ethernet) | > | **Physical (L1)** | Bit transmission over physical medium; defines voltage, connectors | > **Step 3 ŌĆö Importance (1 Mark):** > > The OSI model enables **vendor independence**, **modular troubleshooting**, and **standardized protocol design**. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks** > **Data Link Layer (L2) ŌĆö 3.5 Marks:** > > The Data Link Layer is responsible for **node-to-node** data transfer across a single physical link. Its key functions include: > > 1. **Framing:** Divides the bit stream from the network layer into manageable **frames**. > 2. **Physical Addressing:** Adds **MAC (Media Access Control) addresses** (48-bit) in the header to identify source and destination on the local network. > 3. **Error Detection/Correction:** Uses **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)** in the trailer. > 4. **Flow Control:** Prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. > 5. **Media Access Control:** Determines **when** a device can transmit on a shared medium (CSMA/CD for Ethernet, CSMA/CA for Wi-Fi). > > It has **two sublayers**: **LLC (Logical Link Control)** ŌĆö interfaces with Network Layer; and **MAC (Media Access Control)** ŌĆö controls hardware access. > > **Devices/Protocols:** **Switches, Bridges, NICs** operate here. Protocols include **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **PPP**. > **Physical Layer (L1) ŌĆö 3.5 Marks:** > > The Physical Layer is the lowest layer and deals with the **physical transmission of raw bits** as electrical, optical, or radio signals. Its key responsibilities are: > > 1. **Bit Representation:** Defines voltage levels, current, or light pulses to represent binary 0s and 1s. > 2. **Bit Rate Control:** Defines the **bit duration** (transmission rate). > 3. **Physical Topology:** Defines how devices are connected (star, bus, mesh, ring). > 4. **Transmission Mode:** Defines **simplex, half-duplex, or full-duplex** communication. > 5. **Mechanical Specifications:** Defines connector types (RJ45, LC, SC), cable types (Cat 6, OS2), and pin layouts. > > **Devices/Protocols:** **Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Network Interface Cards (physical interface)**. Standards include **RS-232, 1000BASE-T, 10GBASE-LR**. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ŌĆö Question Choice B (14 Marks)]** ### **Question B (14 Marks):** **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the OSI 7-layer model. List the PDU at each layer. (7 Marks)** **(b) Compare the OSI model with the TCP/IP model. Which model is used in practice and why? (7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks** > **Step 1 ŌĆö Diagram (3 Marks):** > > (Draw the 7-layer stack with proper labeling; the Mermaid diagram from SECTION_4.1 can be used as a reference for redrawing on paper.) > **Step 2 ŌĆö Explanation (2 Marks):** > > Each layer performs a specific function. Upper layers (7-5) deal with **application-level concerns**. Middle layers (4-3) handle **end-to-end transport and routing**. Lower layers (2-1) handle **physical delivery** of bits. > **Step 3 ŌĆö PDU List (2 Marks):** > > - **L7, L6, L5:** Data > - **L4:** Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) > - **L3:** Packet > - **L2:** Frame > - **L1:** Bits --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks** > **Comparison Table (5 Marks):** > > | Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model | > |---|---|---| > | **Layers** | 7 Layers | 4 Layers | > | **Developed By** | ISO | DARPA (US Dept. of Defense) | > | **Approach** | Protocol-independent generic standard | Protocol-centric (TCP, IP) | > | **Adoption** | Reference / Theoretical model | **Practically used on the Internet** | > | **Session & Presentation Layers** | Explicitly defined | Merged into Application Layer | > | **Physical + Data Link** | Separate layers | Combined as "Network Access" | > | **Transport Layer** | Same in both (TCP/UDP) | Same in both | > | **Rigor** | Strictly layered design | More flexible, less rigid boundaries | > **Practical Usage (2 Marks):** > > The **TCP/IP model** is used in practice because: > > 1. The actual Internet protocol suite (TCP, UDP, IP, HTTP, FTP) was built around the TCP/IP architecture. > 2. The OSI model is **conceptual and reference-based** ŌĆö it is used as a teaching tool and for vendor-neutral protocol design, but no real network uses the full OSI protocol stack. > 3. The TCP/IP protocols are **battle-tested** and standardized as **RFCs**, making them the de-facto standard for global networking. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > > 1. **PDU Mistake:** Writing "Data" for the Network or Data Link layer PDU. **Mark deduction: 1 mark.** PDUs at L3, L2, L1 are **Packet, Frame, Bits** respectively. > > 2. **OSI vs TCP/IP Confusion:** Saying the OSI model is "used on the Internet." **Wrong.** The **TCP/IP** model is used; OSI is a **reference model**. > > 3. **Skipping Encapsulation/Decapsulation:** In questions on data flow, simply listing the layers is **not enough**. You **must** describe how headers are added/removed. > > 4. **Device-Layer Mismatch:** Writing that a **Router works at Layer 2** or a **Switch works at Layer 3** is incorrect. Routers = L3, Switches = L2, Hubs = L1. > > 5. **Application vs Application Layer:** Students often confuse the actual application (e.g., Chrome) with the Application Layer. The Application Layer is the **protocol** (HTTP), not the software. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **OSI Model ŌĆö KTU Quick-Fire Revision Points:** > > - **Creator:** **ISO** (not IEEE, not IETF); Standard: **ISO/IEC 7498-1** > - **Layers:** **7** ŌĆö App, Pres, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical > - **Mnemonic Top-Down:** "**A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing" > - **Mnemonic Bottom-Up:** "**P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way" > - **PDUs:** L1 = **Bits**, L2 = **Frame**, L3 = **Packet**, L4 = **Segment/Datagram**, L5-L7 = **Data** > - **Devices:** L1 = **Hub**, L2 = **Switch**, L3 = **Router** > - **Addresses:** L2 = **MAC** (48-bit, hardware), L3 = **IP** (32-bit IPv4 / 128-bit IPv6, logical), L4 = **Port** (16-bit) > - **Protocols:** L4 = TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) / UDP (fast, connectionless); L3 = IP, ICMP; L2 = Ethernet, Wi-Fi; L7 = HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS > - **Encapsulation** = Add headers going **down**; **Decapsulation** = Remove headers going **up** > - **TCP/IP model** is used in practice (4 layers); **OSI** is a **reference/teaching** model > - **Layer 4 (Transport) is the FIRST end-to-end layer**; Layers 1-3 are hop-by-hop > - **Layer 2 adds BOTH a header AND a trailer** (CRC); other layers add only headers

topics.title: Layers in OSI Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Layers in OSI Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition ŌĆö OSI Reference Model**
> The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model** is a **7-layer**, *vendor-neutral*, *protocol-agnostic* conceptual framework defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** in the standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstraction layers, where each layer communicates only with its **direct upper** and **direct lower** layer, and **peer layers** on remote systems via well-defined **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the OSI model is treated as the **canonical reference architecture** against which all real-world protocol suites (notably the **TCP/IP** model) are compared. Each layer encapsulates a specific *functionality*, exposes a *service* to the layer above, and consumes a *service* from the layer below.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Postal Service" View ­¤Åż

Imagine you are sending a **handwritten letter** to a friend in another city:

| Real World Analogy | OSI Layer |
|---|---|
| The actual content of your letter (message) | Application Layer |
| The language/translation of the letter | Presentation Layer |
| The opening/closing conversation etiquette | Session Layer |
| Choosing a registered/courier service with tracking | Transport Layer |
| Writing the destination address & postal code | Network Layer |
| The local post-office sorting process | Data Link Layer |
| The actual truck/courier moving on the road | Physical Layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The postal system works because every step is **independent**. You don't need to know the truck's route to write the address, and the postman doesn't need to know your letter's content to deliver it. The OSI model follows the exact same **"separation of concerns"** principle ŌĆö a fundamental pillar of computer network design.

---

## 1.3 The Seven Layers ŌĆö At-a-Glance Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (Top-Down):** **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**
> **Mnemonic (Bottom-Up):** **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**

The **7 Layers** from top to bottom are:

1. **Application Layer (L7)**
2. **Presentation Layer (L6)**
3. **Session Layer (L5)**
4. **Transport Layer (L4)**
5. **Network Layer (L3)**
6. **Data Link Layer (L2)**
7. **Physical Layer (L1)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Total number of layers in the OSI Reference Model = **7** (fixed by ISO/IEC 7498-1). This is a **board-favorite 1-mark/2-mark question** in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.4 The Three Logical Sub-Groups (KTU High-Yield Point)

The seven layers are conventionally grouped into three functional groups ŌĆö a frequently asked KTU question:

| Sub-Group | Layers Included | Primary Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Application-Oriented (Upper) Layers** | 7, 6, 5 | User-facing services, data representation, dialog control |
| **Transport-Oriented (Middle) Layer** | 4 | End-to-end delivery, reliability, flow control |
| **Network-Oriented (Lower) Layers** | 3, 2, 1 | Path determination, framing, bit transmission over medium |

---

## 1.5 Inter-Layer Communication ŌĆö Two Key Concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Access Point (SAP):** A logical interface through which one layer requests services from the layer directly below it. Each layer's SAP is identified by a unique address.
> - **Examples:** Port numbers (L4 SAPs), MAC addresses (L2 SAPs), IP addresses (L3 SAPs).

- **Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Layers at the **same level** on two different hosts communicate logically using **protocols**. This logical communication is *virtual*; in reality, data flows *vertically* down one host and *vertically* up the other (encapsulation/decapsulation).
- **Interface Communication:** Adjacent layers on the *same* host communicate physically via **Service Primitives** (`REQUEST`, `INDICATION`, `RESPONSE`, `CONFIRM`).

---

## 1.6 Why OSI? (Engineering Justification)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Modularity:** Each layer can be designed, upgraded, and replaced independently.
> - **Interoperability:** Any vendor's product conforming to a layer's standard can interoperate with another vendor's product.
> - **Ease of Learning & Troubleshooting:** Network faults can be isolated to a specific layer (e.g., the *Layered Troubleshooting Model* used by network engineers).
> - **Protocol Standardization:** Provides a common vocabulary for protocol designers.

# ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept ŌĆö Layered Architecture Principle

The OSI model operates on **two fundamental principles** that govern all KTU board questions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Encapsulation / Decapsulation:** As data moves down the sender's stack, each layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes **trailer**) ŌĆö this is **encapsulation**. The reverse process on the receiver's stack is **decapsulation**.
> 2. **Peer-Layer Protocol Exchange:** A layer on the sender communicates *logically* with the same-numbered layer on the receiver, even though the actual bits travel via the physical medium.

---

## 2.2 The 7 Layers ŌĆö Exhaustive Functional Breakdown

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 7 ŌĆö Application Layer

- **Purpose:** Provides **network services** *directly* to end-user applications (browsers, email clients, FTP tools). It is the **closest layer to the user**.
- **Protocols / Standards:** **HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, SSH**.
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** **Data** (sometimes called a *message*).
- **Service Provided:** Interface to user processes; resource sharing; file transfer; directory services.
- **Example:** Chrome browser invoking `GET /index.html` over HTTP.
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** Web servers, REST APIs, IoT telemetry services.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 6 ŌĆö Presentation Layer

- **Purpose:** Handles **data representation, encoding, and encryption/decryption**. It acts as the *translator* of the network.
- **Functions:**
  - **Data Translation** (e.g., EBCDIC Ōåö ASCII)
  - **Data Compression** (e.g., LZ77, Huffman)
  - **Data Encryption/Decryption** (e.g., **TLS, SSL** ŌĆö though these are formally considered to straddle layers 5ŌĆō7)
  - **Data Formatting / Serialization** (e.g., **JPEG, MPEG, GIF, PNG, ASCII, EBCDIC**)
- **PDU:** **Data** (same as L7).
- **Service Provided:** A common data format between two systems.
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** TLS handshake in HTTPS, JPEG compression in video streaming.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 5 ŌĆö Session Layer

- **Purpose:** Manages and controls **connections (sessions)** between two communicating hosts. It is the *dialogue manager*.
- **Functions:**
  - **Session Establishment, Maintenance, and Termination.**
  - **Dialog Control:** Monologue, half-duplex, or full-duplex communication.
  - **Synchronization:** Inserts *checkpoints* in long data streams to allow recovery from interruption.
  - **Token Management** (in older protocols).
- **Protocols / Standards:** **NetBIOS, RPC (Remote Procedure Call), PPTP, SIP**.
- **PDU:** **Data**.
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** WebSocket session management, VoIP call setup.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** In modern TCP/IP implementations, the Session Layer's responsibilities have largely been **absorbed into the Application Layer** (e.g., TLS handles encryption which is officially a Presentation function). This is a frequently-asked **compare/contrast** question.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 4 ŌĆö Transport Layer

- **Purpose:** Provides **end-to-end communication services** between processes on different hosts. The *most critical layer* ŌĆö the boundary between *host-to-host* and *process-to-process* delivery.
- **Functions:**
  - **Service-Point (Port) Addressing** (e.g., TCP port 80, UDP port 53).
  - **Segmentation and Reassembly.**
  - **Connection Control** (connection-oriented vs. connectionless).
  - **Flow Control** (windowing, rate limiting).
  - **Error Control** (acknowledgment, retransmission).
  - **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing** of multiple application sessions over a single network connection.
- **Protocols:** **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** ŌĆö connection-oriented, reliable; **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** ŌĆö connectionless, best-effort.
- **PDU:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP).
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** Web (TCP/443), DNS (UDP/53), video streaming (RTP/UDP).

---

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 3 ŌĆö Network Layer

- **Purpose:** Provides **logical addressing** and **best-path determination (routing)** for packet delivery across *multiple* networks (inter-networks).
- **Functions:**
  - **Logical Addressing** (e.g., **IPv4, IPv6** addresses).
  - **Routing** (selection of path via routing tables and protocols).
  - **Packet Forwarding.**
  - **Fragmentation and Reassembly** (of oversized segments).
  - **Congestion Control** (basic).
- **Protocols:** **IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, IGMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP, ARP (technically L2/L3 hybrid)**.
- **PDU:** **Packet** (or *datagram* in the Internet context).
- **Devices:** **Router, Layer 3 Switch**.
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** Internet backbone routing via BGP; intranet OSPF.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 2 ŌĆö Data Link Layer

- **Purpose:** Provides **node-to-node** (hop-to-hop) data transfer across a **single link**. Detects and (optionally) corrects errors introduced by the physical layer.
- **Sub-Layers (IEEE 802 framework):**
  - **LLC (Logical Link Control) ŌĆö IEEE 802.2:** multiplexes upper-layer protocols, handles error notification.
  - **MAC (Media Access Control) ŌĆö IEEE 802.3/802.11:** controls *how* devices access the shared medium.
- **Functions:**
  - **Framing** (adding header + trailer around payload).
  - **Physical (MAC) Addressing** (e.g., 48-bit **MAC address** like `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`).
  - **Error Detection** (CRC ŌĆö Cyclic Redundancy Check).
  - **Error Correction** (in some protocols, e.g., FEC).
  - **Flow Control** (per-link).
  - **Medium Access Control** (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA, Token Passing).
- **Protocols / Standards:** **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay**.
- **PDU:** **Frame**.
- **Devices:** **Switch, Bridge, NIC (Network Interface Card)**.
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** Every LAN uses L2 framing; switches build MAC address tables.

---

### ­¤ö╣ Layer 1 ŌĆö Physical Layer

- **Purpose:** Defines the **electrical, mechanical, procedural, and functional** specifications for activating, maintaining, and deactivating the physical link. Deals with **raw bits**.
- **Functions:**
  - **Bit Representation** (voltages, light pulses, RF signals).
  - **Bit Rate / Baud Rate** definition.
  - **Topology** (bus, star, mesh, ring).
  - **Transmission Mode** (simplex, half-duplex, full-duplex).
  - **Physical Media Specification** (cables, connectors, fiber).
  - **Synchronization of bits**, **line configuration**.
- **Protocols / Standards:** **IEEE 802.3 (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T), RS-232, RJ-45, V.35, SONET/SDH, DSL, ISDN**.
- **PDU:** **Bit**.
- **Devices:** **Hub, Repeater, Cable, Connector, Network Interface Card (physical portion), Modem**.
- **Real-World Engineering Use:** Fiber optic backbone deployment, 5G NR physical channels.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the "Layers in OSI Model" topic in KTU exams. Memorize it verbatim.

| Layer # | Layer Name | PDU (Data Unit) | Address Type | Key Protocols | Key Devices | Header Adds | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **7** | Application | Data / Message | ŌĆö | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS | Gateway (App) | App-specific | Web browser |
| **6** | Presentation | Data | ŌĆö | TLS, SSL, JPEG, MPEG | Gateway (App) | Encrypted/Encoded | HTTPS |
| **5** | Session | Data | ŌĆö | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP | Gateway (App) | Session token | Video call |
| **4** | Transport | **Segment / Datagram** | **Port Number** | TCP, UDP | Gateway (Transport), Firewall (stateful) | TCP/UDP header | TCP port 80 |
| **3** | Network | **Packet** | **IP Address (Logical)** | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP | **Router, L3 Switch** | IP header | 192.168.1.1 |
| **2** | Data Link | **Frame** | **MAC Address (Physical)** | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, HDLC | **Switch, Bridge, NIC** | MAC header + Trailer | 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E |
| **1** | Physical | **Bit** | ŌĆö | RS-232, 802.3 PHY | **Hub, Repeater, Cable** | (no header ŌĆö raw bits) | Electrical signal |

---

## 2.4 PDU Naming Convention ŌĆö Engineering Convention

> [!NOTE]
> The PDU name changes at each layer because the *encapsulation context* changes. For example, the same data entity is called a **"Data"** at L7, a **"Segment"** at L4, a **"Packet"** at L3, and a **"Frame"** at L2. By L1, it is reduced to **"Bits"** on the wire.

$$\text{App Data} \xrightarrow{\text{+App Hdr}} \text{App PDU (Message)} \xrightarrow{\text{+Pres Hdr}} \text{Trans PDU} \xrightarrow{\text{+Sess Hdr}} \text{Sess PDU}$$
$$\xrightarrow{\text{+TCP/UDP Hdr}} \text{Segment / Datagram} \xrightarrow{\text{+IP Hdr}} \text{Packet}$$
$$\xrightarrow{\text{+MAC Hdr + Trailer}} \text{Frame} \xrightarrow{\text{encoding}} \text{Bits}$$

---

## 2.5 Critical Distinctions Frequently Tested by KTU

| Concept | Layer 2 | Layer 3 |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Type** | **MAC (48-bit physical, hardware-burned)** | **IP (32-bit or 128-bit logical, software-assigned)** |
| **Scope** | **Local network (same broadcast domain)** | **Inter-network (across routers)** |
| **PDU Name** | **Frame** | **Packet** |
| **Device** | **Switch** | **Router** |
| **Header Fields** | Source/Dest MAC, EtherType, CRC | Source/Dest IP, TTL, Protocol, Checksum |

| Concept | TCP (Layer 4) | UDP (Layer 4) |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection** | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| **Reliability** | Reliable (ACK, retransmit) | Unreliable (best-effort) |
| **Speed** | Slower (overhead) | Faster (low overhead) |
| **Use Cases** | Web, email, file transfer | DNS, video streaming, VoIP |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications (5G/6G):** The OSI layers map onto the **3GPP protocol stack**: NAS (Application/Session) ŌåÆ IP (Network) ŌåÆ PDCP/RLC/MAC (L2) ŌåÆ PHY (L1).
- **Cybersecurity:** Layered defenses (Defense in Depth) directly mirror OSI layers ŌĆö firewalls at L3/L4, IDS at L7.
- **Industrial Automation:** PROFINET and EtherCAT use OSI layers with real-time extensions.
- **Cloud Networking (AWS/Azure):** VPC subnets operate at L3, security groups at L4, application load balancers at L7.

# ­¤ōÖ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: The Journey of a Single Data Packet

To understand the OSI layers thoroughly, we will trace a **single HTTP GET request** sent from **Host A** (IP `192.168.1.10`, MAC `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`, Port `49152`) to **Host B** (IP `93.184.216.34`, MAC `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`, Port `80`).

### ­¤öĖ Sender Side (Encapsulation ŌĆö Top-Down)

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Application Layer (L7)

The user's browser constructs the HTTP message.

$$\text{HTTP Message} = \text{``GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: example.com\r\n\r\n''}$$

This is the **Application-Layer PDU** (Message).

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Presentation Layer (L6)

If HTTPS is used, TLS encrypts the HTTP message:

$$\text{TLS Ciphertext} = \text{Encrypt}_{K_{\text{session}}}(\text{HTTP Message})$$

If compression is negotiated, it is applied first:

$$\text{Compressed} = \text{Compress}(\text{HTTP Message}), \quad \text{then} \quad \text{TLS Ciphertext} = \text{Encrypt}_{K}(\text{Compressed})$$

This is still called **Data** (PDU name unchanged at L6).

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Session Layer (L5)

The session layer (e.g., TLS record layer) wraps the ciphertext, attaches a **session ID** for tracking, and may add a sequence number for resumption.

$$\text{Session PDU} = \text{Session Hdr} \;\Vert\; \text{TLS Ciphertext} \;\Vert\; \text{Session Trailer}$$

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Transport Layer (L4) ŌĆö Segment Formation

TCP adds an 8-byte **source port** and **destination port** plus control fields.

$$\text{TCP Segment} = \underbrace{\text{Src Port} \;\Vert\; \text{Dst Port} \;\Vert\; \text{Seq} \;\Vert\; \text{Ack} \;\Vert\; \text{Flags}}_{\text{20 bytes (TCP Header)}} \;\Vert\; \text{Encrypted HTTP Message}$$

| TCP Header Field | Value (Hex / Decimal) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Source Port | `0xC000` (49152) | Client's ephemeral port |
| Dest Port | `0x0050` (80) | HTTP server well-known port |
| Sequence Number | e.g., `0x0000 0001` | Initial byte count |
| Flags | `0x018` (PSH+ACK) | "Push & acknowledge" |

**PDU at L4 = Segment** (Ōēł 20 bytes header + payload).

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Network Layer (L3) ŌĆö Packet Formation

IP wraps the TCP segment, adding **logical addresses**.

$$\text{IP Packet} = \underbrace{\text{Ver} \;\Vert\; \text{IHL} \;\Vert\; \text{TTL} \;\Vert\; \text{Protocol} \;\Vert\; \text{Hdr Checksum}}_{\text{20 bytes (IPv4 Header)}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{192.168.1.10}_{\text{Source IP}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{93.184.216.34}_{\text{Dest IP}} \;\Vert\; \text{TCP Segment}$$

**PDU at L3 = Packet** (20-byte IP header + TCP segment).

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Data Link Layer (L2) ŌĆö Frame Formation

Ethernet adds a **14-byte header** and a **4-byte trailer (CRC)**.

$$\text{Ethernet Frame} = \underbrace{\text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA}}_{\text{Dest MAC}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB}}_{\text{Src MAC}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{0x0800}_{\text{EtherType (IPv4)}}}_{\text{14-byte header}} \;\Vert\; \text{IP Packet} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{CRC-32}_{\text{4 bytes}}}_{\text{Trailer}}$$

**PDU at L2 = Frame** (14 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 Ōēł 72 bytes minimum for a tiny HTTP request).

#### Step 7 ŌĆö Physical Layer (L1) ŌĆö Bit Transmission

The frame is converted to a stream of bits and encoded onto the physical medium (e.g., 4B/5B encoding on 100BASE-TX, Manchester encoding on 10BASE-T).

$$\text{Bits} = \text{Encode}_{\text{scheme}}(\text{Ethernet Frame})$$

For example, for the byte `0xAA` (`10101010`) using **Manchester encoding**, every bit `0` becomes `01` and every bit `1` becomes `10`:

$$\text{`10101010'} \xrightarrow{\text{Manchester}} \text{`10 01 10 01 10 01 10 01'}$$

The doubled bit stream is then modulated onto the copper pair as voltage transitions.

**PDU at L1 = Bit** (sequence of `0`s and `1`s on the wire).

---

### ­¤öĖ Receiver Side (Decapsulation ŌĆö Bottom-Up)

The receiver reverses the process **exactly**:

1. **L1** receives bits ŌåÆ decodes to a frame.
2. **L2** strips MAC header/trailer ŌåÆ delivers a packet to L3.
3. **L3** strips IP header ŌåÆ delivers a segment to L4.
4. **L4** strips TCP header ŌåÆ reassembles data stream.
5. **L5/L6** decrypt, decompress, deserialize ŌåÆ plaintext.
6. **L7** hands plaintext to the browser which renders the HTML.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Headers and Their Sizes

| Layer | Header / Trailer | Size (bytes) | Critical Fields Added |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 7 ŌĆö Application | None (raw app data) | Variable | HTTP method, URL, headers |
| 6 ŌĆö Presentation | TLS record header | 5 | Content type, version, length |
| 5 ŌĆö Session | Session token | Variable | Session ID |
| 4 ŌĆö Transport | TCP header | 20 (min) | Src/Dst port, seq, ack, flags |
| 3 ŌĆö Network | IPv4 header | 20 (min) | Src/Dst IP, TTL, protocol, checksum |
| 2 ŌĆö Data Link | Ethernet header + CRC trailer | 14 + 4 = 18 | Src/Dst MAC, EtherType, FCS |
| 1 ŌĆö Physical | None (encoding only) | 0 | Bit timing, voltage levels |
| **Total Overhead (min)** | ŌĆö | **Ōēł 67 bytes** | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For a single-byte payload (e.g., a TCP ACK), the **header-to-payload ratio** is 67:1 ŌĆö illustrating why protocols like **Nagle's algorithm** exist to coalesce small segments.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Effective Bandwidth Calculation

> **Question:** A 1500-byte Ethernet frame carries a 1460-byte TCP segment (MSS) which carries a 1440-byte HTTP payload. If the link is **100 Mbps**, calculate the **maximum application-layer throughput** in Mbps, ignoring ACKs.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify overhead.**

$$\text{Overhead} = \text{Frame Size} - \text{HTTP Payload} = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Payload}}{\text{Frame Size}} = \frac{1440}{1500} = 0.96 \quad \text{or} \quad 96\%$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute effective throughput.**

$$T_{\text{eff}} = \eta \times \text{Link Rate} = 0.96 \times 100 = 96 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Generalize the formula.**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\text{Payload Size (bytes)}}{\text{Frame Size (bytes)}} \times \text{Link Rate (Mbps)}}$$

This formula is used in **network capacity planning** for enterprise WAN links.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Routing Determination Using Logical Address

Given a packet with **Destination IP = `10.20.30.40`** and a router's routing table:

| Network | Subnet Mask | Next Hop | Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| `10.20.0.0` | `255.255.0.0` | `10.0.0.2` | eth0 |
| `10.20.30.0` | `255.255.255.0` | `10.0.0.5` | eth0 |
| `0.0.0.0` | `0.0.0.0` | `10.0.0.1` | eth0 (default) |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Longest Prefix Match (LPM).**

$$\text{Row 1 match: } 10.20.30.40 \;\text{AND}\; 255.255.0.0 = 10.20.0.0 \quad \checkmark$$
$$\text{Row 2 match: } 10.20.30.40 \;\text{AND}\; 255.255.255.0 = 10.20.30.0 \quad \checkmark$$
$$\text{Row 3 match: } \text{default} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose longest prefix (Row 2, /24 = 24 bits match > 16 bits).**

$$\boxed{\text{Next Hop} = 10.0.0.5, \quad \text{Outgoing Interface} = \text{eth0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This demonstrates the **Network Layer's** fundamental decision-making process ŌĆö *path determination using logical addressing and longest-prefix match*. This is a common **Part B sub-question** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.5 Encapsulation vs. Decapsulation ŌĆö Comparative Matrix

| Property | Encapsulation (Sender) | Decapsulation (Receiver) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Top ŌåÆ Down (L7 ŌåÆ L1) | Bottom ŌåÆ Up (L1 ŌåÆ L7) |
| **Action** | Add header/trailer | Remove header/trailer |
| **Address added** | Source address (of this host) | Read destination address |
| **PDU changes name** | Data ŌåÆ Segment ŌåÆ Packet ŌåÆ Frame ŌåÆ Bits | Bits ŌåÆ Frame ŌåÆ Packet ŌåÆ Segment ŌåÆ Data |
| **Error check added** | CRC at L2, checksum at L3/L4 | Verification of CRC, checksums |
| **Triggered by** | Application initiating a send | Incoming signal on the medium |

## 4.3 Diagram 2 ŌĆö End-to-End Encapsulation & Decapsulation Flow (Host A ŌåÆ Host B)

# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Ō£Å’ĖÅ Question 1 (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom: Remember/Understand]`

**Q: List the seven layers of the OSI Reference Model in the correct order (top to bottom). Briefly explain the function of the Network Layer in 2ŌĆō3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **seven layers of the OSI Reference Model** in order (top to bottom) are:

1. Application Layer
2. Presentation Layer
3. Session Layer
4. Transport Layer
5. Network Layer
6. Data Link Layer
7. Physical Layer

**Function of the Network Layer (1 mark):**

The **Network Layer** (Layer 3) is responsible for **logical addressing** and **routing of packets** from a source host to a destination host, possibly across multiple intermediate networks (routers). It provides *best-effort*, *connectionless* packet delivery using protocols such as **IP**, and handles **fragmentation and reassembly** of packets that exceed the maximum transmission unit (MTU) of intermediate links.

*Key terms to mention for full marks: logical addressing, IP, routing, packet, inter-network.*

---

### Ō£Å’ĖÅ Question 2 (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom: Understand]`

**Q: Differentiate between the TCP and UDP protocols at the Transport Layer with respect to (i) connection type, (ii) reliability, and (iii) typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Connection Type** | Connection-oriented (uses 3-way handshake: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | Connectionless (no handshake; data sent immediately) |
| (ii) **Reliability** | Reliable ŌĆö provides ACK, retransmission, sequencing, flow & congestion control | Unreliable ŌĆö best-effort, no ACK, no retransmission |
| (iii) **Typical Applications** | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SSH, SMTP, Email | DNS, DHCP, TFTP, SNMP, VoIP, live video streaming, online gaming |

> **Mnemonic:** **T**CP = **T**rustworthy, **U**DP = **U**nreliable (but **U**nbeatably fast).

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** "Module-level internal choice" ŌĆö candidates answer **ONE** of two alternatives from each module.

---

### ­¤¤ó Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Analyze]`

#### **Q: With a neat diagram, describe the OSI Reference Model. Explain in detail the functions, services, and protocols of any FOUR layers of your choice.**

#### (a) Describe the OSI Model with diagram ŌĆö 7 Marks

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model**, defined by **ISO in ISO/IEC 7498-1**, is a **7-layer conceptual framework** that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into distinct abstraction layers. It enables **interoperability between heterogeneous systems** by enforcing a common layered architecture.

**Architectural Principles:**
- Each layer provides **services** to the layer *above* it.
- Each layer **requests services** from the layer *below* it.
- Layers communicate *logically* with **peer layers** on remote systems using **protocols**.
- Adjacent layers communicate *physically* through **Service Access Points (SAPs)** using **service primitives** (`REQUEST`, `INDICATION`, `RESPONSE`, `CONFIRM`).

**The Seven Layers (Top to Bottom):**

1. **Layer 7 ŌĆö Application:** Interface to end-user processes (HTTP, FTP, DNS).
2. **Layer 6 ŌĆö Presentation:** Data translation, encryption, compression (TLS, JPEG).
3. **Layer 5 ŌĆö Session:** Establishes, maintains, terminates sessions (NetBIOS, RPC).
4. **Layer 4 ŌĆö Transport:** End-to-end delivery, multiplexing (TCP, UDP).
5. **Layer 3 ŌĆö Network:** Logical addressing, routing (IP, OSPF, BGP).
6. **Layer 2 ŌĆö Data Link:** Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (Ethernet, Wi-Fi).
7. **Layer 1 ŌĆö Physical:** Bit transmission over medium (cables, signals, hubs).

**Protocol Data Units (PDUs):**

| Layer | PDU |
|---|---|
| 7, 6, 5 | Data / Message |
| 4 | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) |
| 3 | Packet |
| 2 | Frame |
| 1 | Bit |

*(Reference Diagram 1 in Section 4 above for the layered stack.)*

#### (b) Explain any FOUR layers in detail ŌĆö 7 Marks (Ōēł 1.75 marks each)

**1. Transport Layer (L4) ŌĆö 1.75 marks**

The Transport Layer provides **end-to-end communication** between *processes* (not just hosts) on different systems. Its key services are:

- **Service-point addressing** using **port numbers** (e.g., HTTP ŌåÆ 80, HTTPS ŌåÆ 443).
- **Segmentation and Reassembly:** The Application Layer data is broken into manageable segments.
- **Connection control:** TCP establishes a connection via the **3-way handshake** (`SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK`).
- **Flow control** using sliding-window mechanisms.
- **Error control** using checksums, ACKs, and retransmission timeouts.
- **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing:** Multiple application sessions share one IP connection.

**Primary Protocols:** **TCP (reliable, connection-oriented)** and **UDP (unreliable, connectionless)**.

**2. Network Layer (L3) ŌĆö 1.75 marks**

The Network Layer delivers packets from a **source host to a destination host** *across multiple networks*. It introduces the concept of **logical addressing** using the **Internet Protocol (IP)**.

Functions:
- **Logical addressing** (IPv4 ŌĆö 32-bit, IPv6 ŌĆö 128-bit).
- **Routing** of packets based on routing tables and protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP).
- **Fragmentation and Reassembly** when packets exceed the link's **MTU**.
- **TTL (Time to Live)** handling to prevent infinite loops.
- **ICMP** for error reporting (e.g., "Destination Unreachable").

Devices: **Routers** and **Layer-3 Switches**.

**3. Data Link Layer (L2) ŌĆö 1.75 marks**

The Data Link Layer provides **node-to-node** (hop-to-hop) transfer on a single link. It is divided into two sub-layers:
- **LLC (Logical Link Control) ŌĆö IEEE 802.2**
- **MAC (Media Access Control) ŌĆö IEEE 802.3 / 802.11**

Functions:
- **Framing** (organizes bits into frames with a header and CRC trailer).
- **MAC Addressing** (48-bit hardware address, e.g., `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`).
- **Error Detection** using **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC-32)**.
- **Medium Access Control:** CSMA/CD (Ethernet), CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi), Token Passing (Token Ring).
- **Per-link flow control.**

Devices: **Switches, Bridges, Network Interface Cards (NICs)**.

**4. Physical Layer (L1) ŌĆö 1.75 marks**

The Physical Layer is concerned with the **raw transmission of bits** as signals over a physical medium. It defines:

- **Mechanical specifications:** connector types (RJ-45, LC, SC), cable pinouts.
- **Electrical specifications:** voltage levels, current, impedance.
- **Functional specifications:** bit timing, synchronization.
- **Procedural specifications:** how a connection is established/maintained/torn down.

Examples of standards: **RS-232, V.35, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-SX, SONET/SDH, DSL, IEEE 802.11 PHY**.

Devices: **Hubs, Repeaters, Network Cables, Fiber, Antennas**.

---

### ­¤öĄ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Sample) | Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Understand/Apply]`

#### **Q: Explain in detail the concepts of (a) Encapsulation and Decapsulation with a neat diagram showing the journey of data through all 7 layers, and (b) compare and contrast the OSI Model with the TCP/IP Model across all relevant dimensions.**

#### (a) Encapsulation and Decapsulation ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
> **Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer in the OSI model adds its own control information (header and/or trailer) to the data received from the layer above, before passing it to the layer below. The reverse process at the receiver is **Decapsulation**.

**Step-by-Step Encapsulation on Sender (3.5 marks):**

| Step | Layer | Action | Resulting PDU |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | 7 (App) | User message generated | **Data** (e.g., HTTP GET) |
| 2 | 6 (Pres) | Encrypt/Compress | Encrypted **Data** |
| 3 | 5 (Sess) | Add session ID | Session-tagged **Data** |
| 4 | 4 (Trans) | Add TCP/UDP header (ports, seq, ack) | **Segment / Datagram** |
| 5 | 3 (Net) | Add IP header (src/dst IP, TTL) | **Packet** |
| 6 | 2 (DLL) | Add MAC header + CRC trailer | **Frame** |
| 7 | 1 (Phy) | Encode as bits, transmit | **Bits on wire** |

**Step-by-Step Decapsulation on Receiver (1.5 marks):**

1. **L1** receives bits ŌåÆ reconstructs frame.
2. **L2** verifies CRC, strips MAC header/trailer ŌåÆ delivers packet to L3.
3. **L3** checks IP header, decrements TTL, strips it ŌåÆ delivers segment to L4.
4. **L4** reassembles stream, strips TCP header ŌåÆ delivers data to L5/L6.
5. **L6** decrypts/decompresses ŌåÆ delivers plaintext to L7.
6. **L7** hands plaintext to the application (e.g., browser).

**Key Conceptual Point (1 mark):** Each layer only **adds its own header** ŌĆö it does not inspect the inner payload. This is what makes the model **modular** and **replaceable**.

#### (b) Comparison: OSI vs. TCP/IP Model ŌĆö 7 Marks

| Parameter | **OSI Model** | **TCP/IP Model** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin / Author** | **ISO (ISO/IEC 7498-1)** | **DARPA / IETF (RFC series)** |
| **Number of Layers** | **7** | **4 (or 5 with Session)** |
| **Layer Names (top-down)** | Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical | Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access |
| **Design Philosophy** | Conceptual reference model | Practical protocol model (implementation-first) |
| **Adoption** | Primarily a **teaching/reference** framework | The **actual Internet** is built on it |
| **Session & Presentation** | **Explicit layers (5 & 6)** | **Not separate layers** ŌĆö folded into Application |
| **Transport Protocols** | Not specified explicitly | **Explicitly TCP and UDP** |
| **Network Layer Protocol** | Generic (could be IP, CLNP, etc.) | **IP (IPv4 / IPv6) is mandatory** |
| **Data Link / Physical** | Two separate layers | Combined as **Network Access / Link layer** |
| **Development Order** | Specified first, protocols developed later | Protocols developed first (from ARPANET), model derived later |
| **Rigidity** | Strict layered separation | More flexible (e.g., ICMP spans layers) |
| **Example Protocols** | Generic | HTTP, TCP, UDP, IP, Ethernet, Wi-Fi |

> **Note for full marks (1 mark):** A student should also explicitly state that the **TCP/IP Application layer** *combines* the **OSI Application, Presentation, and Session** layers into one, and the **TCP/IP Network Access layer** *combines* the **OSI Data Link and Physical** layers.

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in the "OSI Layers" Question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing L2 and L3 PDUs** ŌĆö Students often call a Packet a "Frame" or vice-versa. *Frame = L2, Packet = L3 ŌĆö these are NOT interchangeable.*
> 2. **Mixing up TCP/UDP layers** ŌĆö Both are **Layer 4 (Transport)**, NOT Layer 3. Writing "TCP works at the network layer" ŌåÆ loses 1 mark minimum.
> 3. **Forgetting the Trailer at L2** ŌĆö The Data Link Layer adds a **trailer (CRC/FCS)**, not just a header. Mentioning only "header" is incomplete.
> 4. **Calling the Hub a Layer-3 device** ŌĆö Hub = **Layer 1**; Switch = **Layer 2**; Router = **Layer 3**. This is a board-favorite 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Forgetting the Service Access Point (SAP) concept** ŌĆö Whenever a question asks "how do adjacent layers communicate?", answer with **"via SAPs using service primitives"**, not just "through interfaces".
> 6. **Wrong direction of encapsulation** ŌĆö Encapsulation is **TopŌåÆDown** (sender); Decapsulation is **BottomŌåÆUp** (receiver). Reversing this is a common error.
> 7. **Treating "OSI = TCP/IP"** ŌĆö They are **NOT** the same. OSI is 7 layers; TCP/IP is 4. A direct equation will attract 0 marks in the comparison question.
> 8. **In the framing calculation question**, students often forget to include **all 7 layers' overhead** in the efficiency formula.

---

## 5.4 Ō£ģ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision ŌĆö Read this 1 hour before the KTU exam.**

- Ō£ģ **OSI has exactly 7 layers** (ISO/IEC 7498-1). Mnemonics: *"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"* (top-down) or *"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"* (bottom-up).
- Ō£ģ **PDU Mapping (MEMORIZE):** L7/6/5 ŌåÆ **Data**; L4 ŌåÆ **Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)**; L3 ŌåÆ **Packet**; L2 ŌåÆ **Frame**; L1 ŌåÆ **Bit**.
- Ō£ģ **Address Mapping:** L4 ŌåÆ **Port**; L3 ŌåÆ **IP (logical)**; L2 ŌåÆ **MAC (physical, 48-bit)**; L1 ŌåÆ **No address**.
- Ō£ģ **Device Mapping:** Hub & Repeater = **L1**; Bridge & Switch = **L2**; Router & L3-Switch = **L3**; Gateway = **Multi-layer (often L4-L7)**.
- Ō£ģ **Protocol Mapping:** L4 ŌåÆ **TCP (reliable) / UDP (unreliable)**; L3 ŌåÆ **IP / ICMP / OSPF / BGP**; L2 ŌåÆ **Ethernet (802.3) / Wi-Fi (802.11) / PPP / HDLC**; L1 ŌåÆ **RS-232 / 100BASE-T / Fiber**.
- Ō£ģ **Encapsulation = TopŌåÆDown (Sender)**; **Decapsulation = BottomŌåÆUp (Receiver)**.
- Ō£ģ **Peer-layer communication is logical (virtual)**, not physical. Actual data flows vertically through the stack.
- Ō£ģ **SAPs (Service Access Points)** are the *interfaces* between adjacent layers; the *primitives* are `REQUEST`, `INDICATION`, `RESPONSE`, `CONFIRM`.
- Ō£ģ **Data Link Layer has TWO sub-layers:** **LLC (802.2)** for logical multiplexing, and **MAC (802.3/802.11)** for medium access.
- Ō£ģ **OSI vs TCP/IP:** TCP/IP has 4 layers and is the *de facto* Internet model; OSI has 7 layers and is the *theoretical reference* model.
- Ō£ģ **TCP 3-way Handshake:** `SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK` ŌĆö establishes a connection.
- Ō£ģ **CRC-32** is used at L2 for error *detection*; full error *correction* is typically handled at L4 (TCP).
- Ō£ģ **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit) governs **fragmentation** decisions at L3.
- Ō£ģ In KTU 2024 scheme, **Module 1** questions are tagged to **CO1 (Remember/Understand)** and sometimes **CO2 (Apply/Analyze)**.
- Ō£ģ Always cite **specific layer numbers** in your answers (e.g., "MAC address is at **Layer 2**"), not just the layer name.

---

> **End of Notes ŌĆö Layers in OSI Model (Module 1, PCCGT302 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme)**
