topics.title: TCP/IP Protocol Suite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** TCP/IP Protocol Suite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **TCP/IP Protocol Suite** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) is a comprehensive, layered framework of communication protocols designed for interconnecting network devices across the internet. It was originally developed under the sponsorship of the **Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA)** in the 1970s as part of the **ARPANET** project and later became the foundational architecture of the global Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard Examiner Phrasing):**
> "TCP/IP is a four-layer (or five-layer) conceptual model that standardizes the transmission of data over heterogeneous networks by providing end-to-end communication services through a stack of interoperable protocols ŌĆö namely, the **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** and the **Internet Protocol (IP)** which give the suite its name."

### Layered Architecture Hierarchy

The TCP/IP suite organizes network communication into **4 layers** (as per the original DARPA/Internet standard) ŌĆö sometimes extended to **5 layers** (a hybrid model adopted in the KTU syllabus for clarity with the OSI reference model).

> [!NOTE]
> The **5-layer hybrid TCP/IP model** is the preferred teaching model in the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCGT302)** because it cleanly separates the *Physical* and *Data Link* concerns, making it easier to map to the **OSI 7-layer model** in viva and exam questions.

**The 5 layers (top to bottom) are:**

1. **Application Layer** ŌĆö End-user services (HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, SSH, Telnet)
2. **Transport Layer** ŌĆö Process-to-process delivery (TCP, UDP)
3. **Internet (Network) Layer** ŌĆö Logical addressing & routing (IP, ICMP, ARP, RARP, IGMP)
4. **Data Link Layer** ŌĆö Framing & MAC addressing (Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11, PPP, HDLC)
5. **Physical Layer** ŌĆö Raw bit transmission (Cables, Fiber, Radio Waves, Connectors)

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Global Postal System" ­¤ō¼

Imagine you are sending a **gift** to a friend in another country:

| Real-World Element | TCP/IP Layer |
|---|---|
| The **gift itself** (your actual message) | Application Layer (Data/Message) |
| The **box and address labels** with sender/receiver info | Transport Layer (TCP/UDP Header + Segment) |
| The **country, city, and postal code** (logical routing info) | Internet Layer (IP Header + Datagram) |
| The **delivery truck and route map** (local transport) | Data Link Layer (MAC Address + Frame) |
| The **actual road, wheels, and engine** | Physical Layer (Electrical/Optical/RF Signals) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** "**A**ll **T**ea **I**n **D**eep **P**ots" ŌåÆ **A**pplication ŌåÆ **T**ransport ŌåÆ **I**nternet ŌåÆ **D**ata Link ŌåÆ **P**hysical (top to bottom).

### Origin & Historical Context

- **1973:** Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn publish the foundational TCP design paper.
- **1974:** TCP/IP protocols formally specified in **RFC 675**.
- **1983:** ARPANET switches from NCP to TCP/IP on **January 1** ŌĆö the symbolic "birth of the Internet."
- **1989:** Tim Berners-Lee invents **HTTP** and the **World Wide Web** at CERN, riding on top of TCP/IP.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö Layer by Layer

#### ­¤¤” Layer 5: Application Layer (User-Facing Services)

- **Function:** Provides network services directly to end-user applications (web browsers, email clients, file transfer tools).
- **Operates on:** *Data* (raw message from the application).
- **Key Protocols:**
  - **HTTP / HTTPS** ŌåÆ Web browsing (Port 80 / 443)
  - **FTP / SFTP** ŌåÆ File transfer (Port 21 / 22)
  - **SMTP / POP3 / IMAP** ŌåÆ Email (Port 25 / 110 / 143)
  - **DNS** ŌåÆ Domain name resolution (Port 53)
  - **Telnet / SSH** ŌåÆ Remote login (Port 23 / 22)
  - **SNMP** ŌåÆ Network management (Port 161)

> [!NOTE]
> The application layer does **not** add a header. It simply passes the user data down to the transport layer.

#### ­¤¤” Layer 4: Transport Layer (End-to-End Delivery)

- **Function:** Provides *process-to-process* delivery using **port numbers** (16-bit, range **0ŌĆō65535**).
- **Operates on:** *Segments* (TCP) or *Datagrams* (UDP).
- **Key Protocols:**

| Protocol | Type | Reliability | Overhead | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP** | Connection-oriented | Ō£ģ Guaranteed delivery, ordering, error recovery | High (20+ bytes header) | Web, Email, File transfer |
| **UDP** | Connectionless | ŌØī Best-effort, no ACK | Low (8 bytes header) | Video streaming, DNS, VoIP, Gaming |

**TCP's 3-way Handshake (MUST KNOW FOR EXAMS):**

$$\text{SYN} \rightarrow \quad \leftarrow \text{SYN-ACK} \quad \text{ACK} \rightarrow$$

#### ­¤¤” Layer 3: Internet Layer (Logical Addressing & Routing)

- **Function:** Routes packets across networks using **logical IP addresses** (32-bit IPv4, 128-bit IPv6).
- **Operates on:** *Datagrams* (or *Packets*).
- **Key Protocols:**
  - **IP (IPv4 / IPv6)** ŌåÆ Addressing and routing
  - **ICMP** ŌåÆ Error reporting & diagnostics (used by `ping` and `traceroute`)
  - **ARP** ŌåÆ Resolves IP address ŌåÆ MAC address
  - **RARP** ŌåÆ Resolves MAC address ŌåÆ IP address (legacy)
  - **IGMP** ŌåÆ Multicast group management

#### ­¤¤” Layer 2: Data Link Layer (Node-to-Node Delivery)

- **Function:** Frames the IP datagram and adds **MAC addresses** (48-bit hardware address) for local network delivery.
- **Operates on:** *Frames*.
- **Sub-layers:**
  - **LLC (Logical Link Control)** ŌĆö IEEE 802.2
  - **MAC (Media Access Control)** ŌĆö IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet), 802.11 (Wi-Fi)
- **Key Protocols:** Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay.

#### ­¤¤” Layer 1: Physical Layer (Raw Bit Transmission)

- **Function:** Converts frames into electrical, optical, or radio signals.
- **Key Metrics:** Bandwidth (Hz), Throughput (bps), Latency (ms), Bit Error Rate (BER).
- **Examples:** Cat6 cables, Single-mode Fiber, Microwave, Satellite, 4G/5G radio waves.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Encapsulation & Header Overhead

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks "calculate the total overhead/total bits transmitted" type questions.** Memorize the table below.

| Layer | Protocol | Header Size (min) | Unit/PDU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | HTTP/FTP/DNS | 0 (no header added) | **Data / Message** |
| Transport | TCP | **20 bytes** (60 with options) | **Segment** |
| Transport | UDP | **8 bytes** | **Datagram** |
| Internet | IP | **20 bytes** (IPv4 standard) | **Datagram / Packet** |
| Data Link | Ethernet | **14 bytes** (header) + **4 bytes** (FCS trailer) | **Frame** |
| Physical | ŌĆö | Preamble + SFD (8 bytes) for Ethernet | **Bits** |

**Canonical KTU Formulas:**

$$\text{Total Frame Size} = \text{Application Data} + H_{TCP} + H_{IP} + H_{Ethernet} + \text{Trailer}$$

$$\text{Ethernet: } T_{frame} = D + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = D + 58 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Overhead Percentage} = \frac{H_{total}}{T_{frame}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{D}{D + 58} \times 100\%$$

**Standard Port Number Categories (Memorize for MCQs & Short Answers):**

| Range | Category |
|---|---|
| 0 ŌĆō 1023 | **Well-known ports** (HTTP 80, FTP 21, SSH 22, SMTP 25, DNS 53) |
| 1024 ŌĆō 49151 | **Registered ports** (assigned by IANA) |
| 49152 ŌĆō 65535 | **Dynamic / Ephemeral ports** (used by clients) |

### 2.3 TCP/IP vs. OSI ŌĆö KTU Comparison Table

> [!TIP]
> This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in every KTU exam. The two sub-questions "Explain TCP/IP" and "Compare with OSI" are almost always paired.

| Feature | **OSI Model** (7-layer) | **TCP/IP Model** (4/5-layer) |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application) | 4 or 5 |
| Origin | **ISO** (International Standards Org) ŌĆö theoretical | **DARPA** ŌĆö practical, implementation-first |
| Development | Standards-first, then protocols | Protocols-first (TCP, IP existed before the model) |
| Usage | Reference / teaching model | Real-world Internet model |
| Session & Presentation | Separate dedicated layers | Merged into the **Application Layer** |
| Physical & Data Link | Separate | Separate (in 5-layer model) or merged (in 4-layer) |
| Strictness | Strict layering | "Flexible" ŌĆö protocols may bypass layers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Frame Size & Efficiency (Classic KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A user sends an HTTP message of **1500 bytes** of application data over an Ethernet network using TCP/IP. Assuming standard minimum headers (no options), calculate:
> (i) The total size of the frame on the wire.
> (ii) The total overhead in bytes and as a percentage.
> (iii) The transmission efficiency.

**Given:**
- Application Data $(D) = 1500$ bytes
- TCP Header $(H_{TCP}) = 20$ bytes
- IP Header $(H_{IP}) = 20$ bytes
- Ethernet Header $(H_{Eth}) = 14$ bytes
- Ethernet Trailer $(T_{FCS}) = 4$ bytes

---

**Step 1: Calculate the Transport Layer Segment (PDU at L4)**

$$S_{TCP} = D + H_{TCP}$$

$$S_{TCP} = 1500 + 20 = 1520 \text{ bytes}$$

**Logic:** The Application Layer hands 1500 bytes of data to TCP. TCP appends its own 20-byte header to form a *Segment*. The Segment's payload (Data) remains 1500 bytes because **TCP does not alter the data**.

---

**Step 2: Calculate the Internet Layer Datagram (PDU at L3)**

$$P_{IP} = S_{TCP} + H_{IP}$$

$$P_{IP} = 1520 + 20 = 1540 \text{ bytes}$$

**Logic:** TCP hands the Segment to IP. IP treats the entire Segment as its **payload** and adds its own 20-byte header, creating a *Datagram* (or Packet). The IP datagram's payload is 1520 bytes.

---

**Step 3: Calculate the Data Link Layer Frame (PDU at L2)**

$$F_{Eth} = P_{IP} + H_{Eth} + T_{FCS}$$

$$F_{Eth} = 1540 + 14 + 4 = 1558 \text{ bytes}$$

**Logic:** IP hands the Datagram to the Data Link Layer (Ethernet). Ethernet adds a 14-byte header (containing source & destination MAC addresses, EtherType) and a 4-byte **Frame Check Sequence (FCS)** trailer (CRC-32 error detection code). The resulting *Frame* is what gets transmitted.

---

**Step 4: Calculate the Physical Layer Transmission**

The frame is encoded into bits and sent on the wire:

$$F_{bits} = F_{Eth} \times 8 = 1558 \times 8 = 12{,}464 \text{ bits}$$

**Logic:** The Physical Layer does **not** add bytes (in basic calculations); it simply converts each byte into 8 electrical/optical pulses.

---

**Step 5: Total Overhead in Bytes**

$$H_{total} = H_{TCP} + H_{IP} + H_{Eth} + T_{FCS}$$

$$H_{total} = 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 58 \text{ bytes}$$

**Logic:** Overhead is the sum of ALL headers and trailers added across layers ŌĆö it represents "non-user" data that consumes bandwidth.

---

**Step 6: Overhead Percentage**

$$\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{H_{total}}{F_{Eth}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{58}{1558} \times 100\% \approx 3.72\%$$

**Logic:** Out of every 100 bytes transmitted, ~3.72 bytes are non-user overhead (control information). For a 1500-byte payload, this is remarkably efficient ŌĆö the "sweet spot" of Ethernet's **MTU of 1500 bytes**.

---

**Step 7: Transmission Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{D}{F_{Eth}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{1500}{1558} \times 100\% \approx 96.28\%$$

**Logic:** Efficiency is the fraction of the on-wire bytes that carry *actual user data*. A higher efficiency means better bandwidth utilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that 1500 is the **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** of standard Ethernet. This is *intentional* ŌĆö Ethernet was designed so that a single IP packet (with default headers) fits exactly into one frame with **minimum overhead**. This is why 1500 bytes is the de facto standard MTU across the internet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency Curve for Variable Data Sizes

Let us derive efficiency $\eta$ as a function of data size $D$:

$$\eta(D) = \frac{D}{D + 58} \times 100\%$$

| Data Size (bytes) | Total Frame | Overhead % | Efficiency % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 68 | 85.29% | 14.71% |
| 100 | 158 | 36.71% | 63.29% |
| 500 | 558 | 10.39% | 89.61% |
| 1500 | 1558 | 3.72% | 96.28% |
| 5000 | 5058 | 1.15% | 98.85% |

**Interpretation:** As $D \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{D \to \infty} \eta(D) = \lim_{D \to \infty} \frac{D}{D+58} = 1 = 100\%$$

**Logic:** For very large messages, the **fixed** 58-byte overhead becomes negligible. This is why large file transfers (FTP, video streaming) are bandwidth-efficient, while small messages (e.g., DNS queries, ~50 bytes) suffer from poor efficiency.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Protocol-to-Layer Mapping

| Protocol | Layer | Type | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP | Application | Request-Response | RFC 2616 / 9110 |
| DNS | Application | Query-Response | RFC 1034 / 1035 |
| TCP | Transport | Connection-oriented | RFC 793 |
| UDP | Transport | Connectionless | RFC 768 |
| IP (IPv4) | Internet | Connectionless datagram | RFC 791 |
| ICMP | Internet | Error & diagnostic | RFC 792 |
| ARP | Internet | Address resolution | RFC 826 |
| Ethernet | Data Link | LAN framing | IEEE 802.3 |
| Wi-Fi | Data Link / Physical | Wireless LAN | IEEE 802.11 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TCP/IP 5-Layer Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["LAYER 5 APPLICATION LAYER - HTTP FTP DNS SMTP SSH SNMP"]
    B["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP UDP"]
    C["LAYER 3 INTERNET LAYER - IP ICMP ARP IGMP"]
    D["LAYER 4 DATA LINK LAYER - Ethernet Wi-Fi PPP HDLC"]
    E["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL LAYER - Cables Fiber Radio Waves"]

    A -->|Data| B
    B -->|Segment or Datagram| C
    C -->|IP Datagram| D
    D -->|Frame| E
    E -->|Bits| NET[("Transmission Medium")]

    NET -->|Bits| E
    E -->|Frame| D
    D -->|IP Datagram| C
    C -->|Segment or Datagram| B
    B -->|Data| A
```

### 4.2 Encapsulation Process ŌĆö "Wrapping a Gift Step-by-Step"

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP["User Data: 1500 bytes Application Layer"]:::app
    TCP["TCP Header: 20 bytes Segment: 1520 bytes"]:::trans
    IP["IP Header: 20 bytes Datagram: 1540 bytes"]:::net
    ETH["Ethernet Header 14 bytes + FCS 4 bytes Frame: 1558 bytes"]:::link
    BIT["Bits on the wire 12464 bits"]:::phys

    APP -->|adds TCP header| TCP
    TCP -->|adds IP header| IP
    IP -->|adds Ethernet header and trailer| ETH
    ETH -->|converts to electrical or optical signals| BIT

    classDef app fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef trans fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef net fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    classDef link fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef phys fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 TCP/IP vs. OSI Layer Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OSI["OSI 7-Layer Model"]
        O7["7. Application"]:::osi
        O6["6. Presentation"]:::osi
        O5["5. Session"]:::osi
        O4["4. Transport"]:::osi
        O3["3. Network"]:::osi
        O2["2. Data Link"]:::osi
        O1["1. Physical"]:::osi
    end

    subgraph TCP["TCP/IP 5-Layer Model"]
        T5["5. Application - HTTP FTP DNS SMTP"]:::tcp
        T4["4. Transport - TCP UDP"]:::tcp
        T3["3. Internet - IP ICMP ARP"]:::tcp
        T2["2. Data Link - Ethernet Wi-Fi"]:::tcp
        T1["1. Physical - Cables Fiber Radio"]:::tcp
    end

    O7 --- T5
    O6 --- T5
    O5 --- T5
    O4 --- T4
    O3 --- T3
    O2 --- T2
    O1 --- T1

    classDef osi fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tcp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 TCP 3-Way Handshake ŌĆö Connection Establishment Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C: CLOSED
    Note over S: LISTEN

    C->>S: SYN seq=x
    Note over C: SYN_SENT
    Note over S: SYN_RCVD

    S->>C: SYN seq=y ACK=x+1
    Note over S: SYN_RCVD

    C->>S: ACK=y+1
    Note over C: ESTABLISHED
    Note over S: ESTABLISHED

    Note over C,S: Connection Ready for Data Transfer
```

### 4.5 Detailed Frame Format (Ethernet + IP + TCP + Data)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Frame["Ethernet Frame - 1558 bytes total"]
        PRE["Preamble 7 bytes"]:::pre
        SFD["SFD 1 byte"]:::pre
        DA["Destination MAC 6 bytes"]:::eth
        SA["Source MAC 6 bytes"]:::eth
        ET["EtherType 2 bytes"]:::eth
        subgraph IP["IP Datagram 1540 bytes"]
            subgraph TCP["TCP Segment 1520 bytes"]
                TCPH["TCP Header 20 bytes"]:::tcp
                DATA["Application Data 1500 bytes"]:::data
            end
            IPH["IP Header 20 bytes"]:::ip
        end
        FCS["FCS CRC-32 4 bytes"]:::fcs
    end

    classDef pre fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969
    classDef eth fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    classDef ip fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22
    classDef tcp fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    classDef data fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520
    classDef fcs fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Compare TCP and UDP. List any 6 differences.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **TCP** | **UDP** |
|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Transmission Control Protocol | User Datagram Protocol |
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery + retransmission | Best-effort, no guarantee |
| Ordering | In-order delivery via sequence numbers | No ordering guarantee |
| Header Size | 20 bytes minimum | 8 bytes fixed |
| Speed | Slower (overhead of acknowledgments) | Faster (minimal overhead) |
| Flow/Congestion Control | Built-in (sliding window) | None |
| Use Cases | HTTP, FTP, Email, SSH | DNS, Video streaming, VoIP, Gaming |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A 6-point table is enough for 3 marks. Use crisp, one-line contrasts ŌĆö examiners reward clarity, not essays.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the role of the IP protocol in the TCP/IP suite? Why is it called "connectionless"?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Internet Protocol (IP)** operates at the **Internet Layer (Layer 3)** of the TCP/IP model. Its primary roles are:

1. **Logical Addressing:** Assigns unique 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) addresses to every device on a network.
2. **Routing & Forwarding:** Determines the best path from source to destination through intermediate routers using routing tables.
3. **Fragmentation & Reassembly:** Breaks large packets into smaller fragments when traversing networks with smaller MTUs, and reassembles them at the destination.
4. **Best-Effort Delivery:** Provides no guarantee of packet delivery, ordering, or error recovery.

**Why "Connectionless"?**
IP is called *connectionless* because **it does not establish a dedicated session or handshake before sending data**. Each IP datagram is treated as an **independent unit** ŌĆö routers forward them based solely on the destination address in the header, with no prior "call setup." This is why IP is described as providing **"unreliable, connectionless, best-effort delivery."**

---

### ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

#### **Question A: (14 Marks) ŌĆö Comprehensive TCP/IP Treatment** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the TCP/IP protocol suite with a neat diagram. List the protocols at each layer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **TCP/IP protocol suite** is a layered architecture designed by DARPA in the 1970s for interconnecting heterogeneous networks. It consists of **5 layers** (in the KTU 2024 syllabus model):

**Layer-wise protocol breakdown:**

| Layer | Key Protocols | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **5. Application** | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SFTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, Telnet, SSH, SNMP | End-user services & data representation |
| **4. Transport** | **TCP**, **UDP** | Process-to-process delivery using port numbers; TCP = reliable, UDP = fast |
| **3. Internet** | **IP** (IPv4/IPv6), **ICMP**, **ARP**, **RARP**, **IGMP** | Logical addressing, routing, error reporting |
| **2. Data Link** | **Ethernet** (802.3), **Wi-Fi** (802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection |
| **1. Physical** | Coaxial, Twisted pair, Fiber optics, Radio waves | Raw bit transmission as signals |

*(Include the layer diagram from Section 4.1 here ŌĆö examiners expect a labeled stack diagram.)*

**Key features:**
- **Modular:** Each layer is independent and can evolve separately.
- **Protocol-agnostic at lower layers:** Can run over any physical medium.
- **End-to-end principle:** Intelligence resides at the edges (hosts), not in the network core.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain encapsulation and decapsulation in the TCP/IP model. How does data flow from sender to receiver? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Encapsulation** (Sender side): Each layer adds its own **header (and sometimes trailer)** to the PDU received from the layer above. This is the "wrapping" process.

| Layer | Action | Resulting PDU |
|---|---|---|
| Application | No header added | **Data** (e.g., 1500 bytes) |
| Transport | Adds 20-byte TCP header | **Segment** (1520 bytes) |
| Internet | Adds 20-byte IP header | **Datagram** (1540 bytes) |
| Data Link | Adds 14-byte Ethernet header + 4-byte FCS trailer | **Frame** (1558 bytes) |
| Physical | Converts to bits | **Bits on wire** (12,464 bits) |

*(Include the encapsulation diagram from Section 4.2.)*

**Decapsulation** (Receiver side): The reverse process. The Physical Layer receives bits, reconstructs the Frame, the Data Link layer **strips** the Ethernet header/trailer and passes the IP Datagram up. The Internet Layer strips the IP header, passing the TCP Segment to the Transport Layer. The Transport Layer strips the TCP header, delivering the original **Data** to the Application Layer.

**Data Flow Summary:**

$$\text{Sender: App} \xrightarrow{+H_{TCP}} \text{Transport} \xrightarrow{+H_{IP}} \text{Internet} \xrightarrow{+H_{Eth}} \text{DataLink} \xrightarrow{\to \text{bits}} \text{Physical} \xrightarrow{\text{medium}} \text{Physical} \xrightarrow{\text{bits} \to \text{Frame}} \text{DataLink} \xrightarrow{-H_{Eth}} \text{Internet} \xrightarrow{-H_{IP}} \text{Transport} \xrightarrow{-H_{TCP}} \text{App: Receiver}$$

---

#### **Question B: (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Compare the TCP/IP model with the OSI model. Highlight the key differences and similarities. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **OSI Model** | **TCP/IP Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Open Systems Interconnection | Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol |
| Number of Layers | 7 | 4 (or 5 in KTU variant) |
| Developed by | **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) | **DARPA** (US Department of Defense) |
| Approach | **Theoretical / Reference** model ŌĆö standards first | **Practical / Implementation** model ŌĆö protocols first |
| Layer Independence | Strict layering; no cross-layer access | Flexible; protocols may interact across layers |
| Session & Presentation Layers | **Separate dedicated layers** | **Merged into the Application Layer** |
| Physical & Data Link | Two separate layers | Separate in 5-layer model; merged in 4-layer model |
| Usage Today | Primarily a teaching/conceptual model | The actual model running the Internet |
| Bottom 3 layers | Network, Data Link, Physical are distinct | Internet, Data Link, Physical (in 5-layer) |
| Top layers | Application, Presentation, Session | Single unified Application layer |

**Similarities:**
- Both are **layered architectures** with well-defined interfaces between layers.
- Both support **packet switching** and **connection-oriented/connectionless** services.
- Both have **transport** and **network** layers performing similar functions.
- Both use **protocol stacks** with headers added at each layer.

**Key Difference:** OSI is a **reference model** (rarely implemented as-is), while TCP/IP is a **practical model** (the actual protocol suite of the Internet).

---

**(b) Explain the functions of the Transport Layer and the Internet Layer in detail. Discuss TCP and IP with their key features. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Transport Layer (Layer 4):**
The Transport Layer is responsible for **process-to-process delivery** ŌĆö ensuring data reaches the correct application (identified by port number) on the correct host.

**Key Protocols:**

**­¤ö╣ TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) ŌĆö Connection-Oriented:**
- **Features:** Reliable, ordered, error-checked, flow-controlled delivery.
- **3-Way Handshake:** SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK to establish a connection.
- **Sliding Window Protocol:** Manages flow control between sender and receiver.
- **Sequence Numbers & ACKs:** Ensure in-order delivery and trigger retransmission on loss.
- **Header size:** 20 bytes minimum.
- **Use cases:** HTTP, FTP, SSH, SMTP.

**­¤ö╣ UDP (User Datagram Protocol) ŌĆö Connectionless:**
- **Features:** Unreliable, no ordering, no flow control ŌĆö simply "fire and forget."
- **8-byte fixed header** with source port, destination port, length, checksum.
- **Use cases:** DNS, live video streaming, VoIP, online gaming.

**Internet Layer (Layer 3):**
The Internet Layer is responsible for **host-to-host delivery** across multiple networks using **logical IP addressing** and **routing**.

**Key Protocol: IP (Internet Protocol)**
- **IPv4:** 32-bit addresses (~4.3 billion unique), written as four octets (e.g., 192.168.1.1).
- **IPv6:** 128-bit addresses (~3.4├Ś10┬│ŌüĖ unique), written in hexadecimal (e.g., 2001:0db8::1).
- **Connectionless & Unreliable:** No guarantee of delivery, ordering, or duplication protection ŌĆö handled by upper layers (TCP).
- **Fragmentation:** IP packets can be split into smaller fragments when traversing networks with smaller MTUs.

**Other Internet Layer Protocols:**
- **ICMP** (Internet Control Message Protocol): Used by `ping` and `traceroute` for diagnostics and error reporting (e.g., "Destination Unreachable").
- **ARP** (Address Resolution Protocol): Maps an IP address ŌåÆ physical MAC address on a LAN.
- **RARP** (Reverse ARP): Maps MAC ŌåÆ IP (legacy, replaced by BOOTP/DHCP).
- **IGMP** (Internet Group Management Protocol): Manages multicast group memberships.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks
>
> 1. **Confusing PDU Names:** Examiners **deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks** if you call a TCP unit a "packet" or an IP unit a "frame." Remember:
>    - TCP ŌåÆ **Segment**; UDP ŌåÆ **Datagram**; IP ŌåÆ **Datagram/Packet**; Ethernet ŌåÆ **Frame**; Physical ŌåÆ **Bits**.
> 2. **Forgetting the FCS Trailer:** Many students calculate overhead as $20+20+14 = 54$ bytes and forget the **4-byte FCS**, giving 58 total. This loses a mark.
> 3. **Saying IP is "Unreliable = Bad":** IP is unreliable by **design**. It is the **TCP's job** to add reliability. Examiners want to see this understanding.
> 4. **Mixing up TCP/IP and OSI layers:** Saying "TCP is a Layer 3 protocol" or "IP is a Layer 4 protocol" is a common but **costly mistake**.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** A **neat labeled stack diagram** is **expected** in every Part B question. Without it, expect 1ŌĆō2 marks deducted.
> 6. **Not mentioning encapsulation with numbers:** A bare diagram is worth 2 marks; a diagram **with a numerical example** (as in Derivation 1) is worth 5ŌĆō6 marks.

---

### ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- Ō£ģ TCP/IP has **4 layers** (original) or **5 layers** (KTU teaching model): **A**pplication, **T**ransport, **I**nternet, **D**ata Link, **P**hysical.
- Ō£ģ **TCP** = connection-oriented, reliable, 20-byte header. **UDP** = connectionless, unreliable, 8-byte header.
- Ō£ģ **IP** = Layer 3, 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6), best-effort delivery, logical addressing.
- Ō£ģ **Port numbers:** 0ŌĆō1023 (well-known), 1024ŌĆō49151 (registered), 49152ŌĆō65535 (dynamic).
- Ō£ģ **Encapsulation** = Sender adds headers; **Decapsulation** = Receiver strips them.
- Ō£ģ **PDUs:** Data ŌåÆ Segment ŌåÆ Datagram ŌåÆ Frame ŌåÆ Bits.
- Ō£ģ **Standard header sizes:** TCP = 20 B, IP = 20 B, Ethernet = 14 B header + 4 B trailer = 18 B.
- Ō£ģ **Total overhead for one Ethernet frame carrying one TCP/IP packet = 58 bytes.**
- Ō£ģ **MTU of Ethernet = 1500 bytes** ŌåÆ maximum unfragmented IP payload.
- Ō£ģ **OSI = 7 layers (theoretical, ISO)**; **TCP/IP = 4/5 layers (practical, DARPA)**.
- Ō£ģ OSI's **Session + Presentation + Application** = TCP/IP's **Application Layer**.
- Ō£ģ **3-way TCP handshake:** **SYN ŌåÆ SYN-ACK ŌåÆ ACK** (memorize the sequence).
- Ō£ģ **ARP = IP ŌåÆ MAC**; **RARP = MAC ŌåÆ IP**; **ICMP = error & diagnostic**; **IGMP = multicast**.
- Ō£ģ TCP/IP was developed under **DARPA**; first deployed on ARPANET on **January 1, 1983**.
- Ō£ģ **Key RFCs:** IPv4 = **RFC 791**; TCP = **RFC 793**; UDP = **RFC 768**; ICMP = **RFC 792**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Draw the layered stack, label each layer with 2ŌĆō3 protocols, walk through encapsulation with a numerical example, and conclude with a TCP/UDP or TCP/IP/OSI comparison table."* ŌĆö This single template will fetch you **12ŌĆō14 out of 14 marks** in any Part B question on this topic. ­¤Ä»
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer: Guided and Unguided Transmission Media (Co-axial Cable, Fiber Optic Cable)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer: Guided and Unguided Transmission Media (Co-axial Cable, Fiber Optic Cable)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transmission Media ŌĆö The Syllabus Definition

A **Transmission Medium** is the physical path (or the wireless channel) that connects the **sender** and the **receiver** in a data communication system. It is the lowest layer of the **OSI Reference Model** (Layer 1) and is responsible for carrying raw electrical, light, or radio-frequency signals from one node to another.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, transmission media are broadly divided into two super-classes:

| Class | Definition | Examples (KTU Syllabus) |
|---|---|---|
| **Guided Media** | Signals are confined and propagate *inside* a solid physical conductor/waveguide. | Twisted Pair, **Co-axial Cable**, **Fiber Optic Cable** |
| **Unguided Media** | Signals propagate *through* free space (air/vacuum) in the form of electromagnetic waves. | Radio Waves, Microwave, Satellite, Infrared |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A guided transmission medium provides a closed physical path between the transmitter and receiver, whereas an unguided medium uses the atmosphere (or space) as the propagation medium. Co-axial and Fiber Optic cables are the *two* guided media mandated for Module 1 in PCCGT302.

---

## 1.2 Co-axial Cable ŌĆö Definition

A **Co-axial Cable (Coax)** is a cylindrical, shielded guided transmission medium consisting of a central copper conductor surrounded by a tubular insulating dielectric, which in turn is wrapped by a braided metallic shield and finally an outer plastic jacket. The name "co-axial" derives from the fact that **all four layers share a common geometric axis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The concentric construction gives coaxial cable excellent **EMI/RFI immunity** and a controlled characteristic impedance, making it suitable for broadband analog signalling, cable TV, and legacy Ethernet (10BASE-2 / 10BASE-5).

### Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Garden Hose
Think of a coaxial cable as a **garden hose for electrons**. The inner rubber tube (the conductor) is surrounded by foam padding (the dielectric), which is then wrapped in a stiff wire mesh (the shield) and finally a green outer skin (the jacket). Water (the signal) flows cleanly down the centre, while the mesh stops the water from leaking sideways and also blocks outside dirt (interference) from entering. The shielding is *co-axial* ŌĆö i.e. it shares the same centre line as the inner tube.

---

## 1.3 Fiber Optic Cable ŌĆö Definition

A **Fiber Optic Cable** is a guided, dielectric transmission medium that carries information in the form of **pulses of light** (typically infrared at 850 nm, 1300 nm, or 1550 nm wavelengths) through a hair-thin strand of ultra-pure glass (silica) or plastic. It is composed of three concentric regions: an inner **Core**, an outer **Cladding** with a slightly lower refractive index, and a protective **buffer/jacket** system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the medium is *dielectric* (no metal), fiber is **immune to electromagnetic interference**, has **near-zero crosstalk**, and offers the **lowest attenuation** of any guided medium (Ōēł 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm). It forms the backbone of every modern ISP, undersea cable, and data centre.

### Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Light Pipe
Imagine looking down a long glass tube filled with water. If you shine a torch at one end, you can see the light spot at the other end, even if the tube is bent ŌĆö this is the principle of an optical fiber. The light *cannot escape* the glass walls because it keeps bouncing off them. The "walls" are actually two different glasses: a dense inner core and a less dense outer cladding. The boundary between them acts like a one-way mirror for light, trapping it inside. We then bundle thousands of such hair-thin glass pipes inside a tough plastic jacket, and we get a fiber optic cable.

---

| Fiber Optic Cable | Section 1.3 above |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Co-axial Cable ŌĆö Engineering Anatomy

### 2.1.1 Layered Structure (from centre outward)

1. **Central Conductor** ŌĆö Solid or stranded copper; carries the signal current.
2. **Dielectric Insulator** ŌĆö Foam polyethylene or solid plastic; maintains a constant spacing between the inner conductor and the outer shield, thereby fixing the **characteristic impedance** of the cable.
3. **Metallic Shield (Braid/Foil)** ŌĆö A woven copper braid (sometimes combined with aluminium foil); serves a **dual purpose**: (a) acts as the return path for the signal (since coax is unbalanced), and (b) shields the inner conductor from external EMI.
4. **Outer Jacket** ŌĆö PVC or PE sheath; provides mechanical and environmental protection.

> [!NOTE]
> The two standard characteristic impedances used in practice are **50 ╬®** (used in Ethernet / RF transmission) and **75 ╬®** (used in cable TV / broadband). The impedance is fixed by the ratio of outer-to-inner conductor diameter.

### 2.1.2 Standard Co-axial Cable Categories

| Type | Impedance | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **RG-6** | 75 ╬® | Cable TV, satellite, broadband internet |
| **RG-8 / RG-11** | 50 ╬® | Thick Ethernet (10BASE-5), amateur radio |
| **RG-58** | 50 ╬® | Thin Ethernet (10BASE-2), lab instrumentation |
| **RG-59** | 75 ╬® | CCTV, short video runs |

### 2.1.3 Performance Characteristics of Co-axial Cable

- **Bandwidth:** Up to several hundred MHz for short runs (cable TV uses up to 1 GHz).
- **Attenuation:** Increases with the square root of frequency (╬▒ ŌłØ ŌłÜf), due to the **skin effect**.
- **Maximum practical run:** Ōēł 500 m for 10BASE-2 Ethernet; Ōēł 100 m for modern broadband coax.
- **Crosstalk:** Very low (the shield blocks inter-pair coupling).
- **EMI immunity:** High but not perfect ŌĆö still susceptible to strong RF sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Co-axial cable supports two signalling modes: **Baseband** (single channel, digital, e.g. 10BASE-2) and **Broadband** (multiple channels via FDM, e.g. cable TV). The textbook (Forouzan) emphasises this distinction in Module 1.

### 2.1.4 Advantages & Disadvantages of Co-axial Cable

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Higher bandwidth than twisted pair | More expensive than twisted pair |
| Excellent EMI shielding | Bulky and rigid ŌĆö difficult to install in ducts |
| Lower error rate | Single point of failure (cut cable brings down the segment) |
| Can be used for long distances | Still uses metal ŌåÆ still some EMI pickup |
| Supports both baseband & broadband | Skin-effect losses grow rapidly with frequency |

---

## 2.2 Fiber Optic Cable ŌĆö Engineering Anatomy

### 2.2.1 Layered Structure (from centre outward)

1. **Core** ŌĆö Ultra-pure silica glass (SiOŌéé), diameter **8ŌĆō10 ┬Ąm** (single-mode) or **50ŌĆō62.5 ┬Ąm** (multi-mode). Carries the light signal.
2. **Cladding** ŌĆö Silica glass with refractive index Ōēł 1 % lower than the core. Causes **total internal reflection** that traps the light inside the core.
3. **Buffer Coating** ŌĆö Acrylate polymer; absorbs shocks and prevents micro-bending losses.
4. **Strength Members** ŌĆö Kevlar (Aramid) yarn; provides tensile strength.
5. **Outer Jacket** ŌĆö PVC or LSZH polyethylene; environmental protection.

> [!NOTE]
> The diameter of the **cladding is standardised** at **125 ┬Ąm** for almost all telecom fibers, regardless of whether the core is 8 ┬Ąm or 62.5 ┬Ąm. This standardisation allows connectors to be universal.

### 2.2.2 The Physics of Light Propagation ŌĆö Why It Works

The guiding principle is **Snell's Law**:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

where $n_1$ is the refractive index of the **core** and $n_2$ is the refractive index of the **cladding**. For light to be *trapped* inside the core, we require $n_1 > n_2$.

The **critical angle** $\theta_c$ (measured from the normal at the core-cladding interface) is the smallest angle for which total internal reflection just begins:

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

If the light strikes the boundary at an angle *greater* than $\theta_c$ (from the normal), it is **100 % reflected** back into the core, with no leakage into the cladding.

### 2.2.3 The Two Families of Fiber

| Property | **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** | **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** |
|---|---|---|
| Core diameter | **50 ┬Ąm or 62.5 ┬Ąm** | **8 to 10 ┬Ąm** |
| Cladding diameter | 125 ┬Ąm | 125 ┬Ąm |
| Light source | LED (incoherent) | Laser (coherent) |
| Propagation | Many modes (paths) bounce down the core | One mode, straight line down the core |
| Modal dispersion | High ŌåÆ limits bandwidth | Negligible |
| Bandwidth-distance | 500 MHz┬Ękm (OM1) up to 4700 MHz┬Ękm (OM4) | Effectively unlimited (limited by chromatic dispersion) |
| Maximum distance | Up to Ōēł 2 km (LAN) | Up to Ōēł 100 km+ (telecom) |
| Cost | Cheap (LED source) | Expensive (laser source, precise connectors) |
| Colour jacket (convention) | Orange / Aqua | Yellow |
| Typical use | LANs, data centres, campus backbones | Undersea cables, ISP backbones, long-haul telecom |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinguishing Question:** "Distinguish between single-mode and multi-mode fiber." ŌĆö The **core diameter**, **light source (LED vs Laser)**, and **modal dispersion behaviour** are the three mandatory points in the model answer.

### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages of Fiber Optic Cable

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| **Lowest attenuation** (Ōēł 0.2 dB/km) of any guided medium | High installation cost (precision connectors, splicing equipment) |
| **Highest bandwidth** (Tbps in lab demos) | Fragile ŌĆö glass can crack under excessive bend |
| **Immune to EMI** (purely dielectric medium) | Difficult to tap ŌĆö *and* difficult to splice |
| **No crosstalk** between fibers in a bundle | Requires skilled installers and OTDR-based test equipment |
| Lightweight, non-corrosive, secure | One-way only ŌĆö requires a second fiber (or wavelength) for full-duplex |
| No ground-loop problems | Termination is more expensive than copper |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$ | $n$ = refractive index, $c$ = speed of light in vacuum, $v$ = speed in medium | Defines how much light *slows down* in glass |
| 2 | $\sin \theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | $\theta_c$ = critical angle, $n_1$, $n_2$ = refractive indices of core and cladding | Threshold for total internal reflection |
| 3 | $NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | $NA$ = Numerical Aperture, $n_0$ = refractive index of surrounding medium (Ōēł 1 for air), $\theta_a$ = acceptance angle | How much light the fiber *can capture* |
| 4 | $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | $A_{dB}$ = attenuation in decibels, $P_{in}$, $P_{out}$ = input / output power | Loss of signal power over a cable run |
| 5 | $\alpha = \dfrac{A_{dB}}{L}$ | $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (dB/km), $L$ = length of cable | Quality metric of the medium |
| 6 | $\Delta \tau_{modal} = \dfrac{n_1 \, \Delta n \, L}{c}$ | $\Delta \tau$ = pulse broadening, $\Delta n$ = refractive index difference, $L$ = length | Inter-symbol interference in MMF |
| 7 | $B \cdot L = \dfrac{0.44}{\Delta \tau}$ | $B \cdot L$ = bandwidth-distance product (Hz┬Ękm) | Maximum data rate achievable over a length |
| 8 | $v_{prop} = \dfrac{c}{n_1}$ | $v_{prop}$ = propagation speed in the core | Used for end-to-end delay calculations |
| 9 | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{L}{v_{prop}} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}$ | $t_{prop}$ = propagation delay | Latency in optical links |
| 10 | $N_{modes} \approx \dfrac{2 \pi^2 a^2}{\lambda^2}\,(NA)^2$ | $a$ = core radius, $\lambda$ = wavelength | Number of guided modes in MMF |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** formula is the single most-tested equation for the fiber optic topic. Memorise the *three equivalent forms* ŌĆö $NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a$, $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$, and $NA = n_1 \sqrt{2 \Delta}$ where $\Delta$ is the *relative refractive index difference*.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Medium | Where You Will Find It Today |
|---|---|
| **Co-axial Cable** | Last-mile cable internet (DOCSIS 3.1), CCTV cameras, GPS antenna feeds, RF test equipment, satellite TV distribution. |
| **Multi-Mode Fiber** | Inside data centres (40G/100G SR4 optics), campus LANs, FTTD (Fiber-to-the-Desk), storage-area networks. |
| **Single-Mode Fiber** | Undersea cables (Ōēł 99 % of intercontinental internet), 5G fronthaul, FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home) using GPON/EPON, long-haul telecom backbones. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Numerical Aperture (NA) ŌĆö The Heart of Fiber Optics

The **Numerical Aperture** is a dimensionless number that quantifies the *light-gathering ability* of an optical fiber. The following is the exact KTU-style derivation.

### 3.1.1 Setting up the geometry

Let a ray of light enter the fiber from a medium of refractive index $n_0$ (typically **air**, so $n_0 \approx 1$) at an angle $\theta_a$ with respect to the fiber axis. The ray refracts into the core, where it now makes an angle $\theta_1$ with the axis, before striking the coreŌĆōcladding interface at an angle of incidence $\phi_1$.

From the geometry of the ray:

$$\theta_1 + \phi_1 = 90^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_1 = 90^\circ - \theta_1$$

### 3.1.2 Apply Snell's Law at the airŌĆōcore boundary

By Snell's law at the entrance face:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

Rearranging:

$$\sin \theta_1 = \frac{n_0 \sin \theta_a}{n_1}$$

### 3.1.3 Apply the total-internal-reflection condition at the coreŌĆōcladding boundary

For the ray to be guided, it must strike the cladding interface at an angle $\phi_1$ that is **at least** equal to the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$\phi_1 \geq \theta_c \quad \text{where} \quad \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Substituting $\phi_1 = 90^\circ - \theta_1$:

$$\sin(90^\circ - \theta_1) \geq \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

$$\cos \theta_1 \geq \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

### 3.1.4 Convert to a condition on the acceptance angle

Using the identity $\cos \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_1}$ and substituting $\sin \theta_1$ from step 3.1.2:

$$\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_0 \sin \theta_a}{n_1}\right)^2} \geq \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$1 - \frac{n_0^2 \sin^2 \theta_a}{n_1^2} \geq \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{n_0^2 \sin^2 \theta_a}{n_1^2} \leq 1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2} = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

Multiplying by $n_1^2$:

$$n_0^2 \sin^2 \theta_a \leq n_1^2 - n_2^2$$

Taking the square root:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a \leq \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

### 3.1.5 The Final Acceptance-Angle Equation

The *maximum* value of $\theta_a$ for which the ray is still guided is the **acceptance angle**. By definition, the **Numerical Aperture** is the sine of this angle (multiplied by $n_0$):

$$\boxed{\,NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **acceptance angle** itself is therefore $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA / n_0)$. A high NA means a "wide" light-cone can enter the fiber, which is desirable for cheap LED sources in LANs (MMF). A low NA (Ōēł 0.1 for SMF) means a very narrow cone ŌĆö requiring the beam to be a collimated laser.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem ŌĆö Attenuation & Power Budget

### Problem
A single-mode fiber link is **40 km** long. The input power launched by the laser is $P_{in} = 1$ mW. The attenuation coefficient at the operating wavelength is $\alpha = 0.25$ dB/km. There are **two connectors** in the link, each with a loss of **0.5 dB**. **One fusion splice** introduces an additional loss of **0.1 dB**. Find (a) the total end-to-end loss, and (b) the received optical power in dBm and in mW.

### Solution ŌĆö Step by Step

**Step 1: Compute the loss of the 40 km fiber run.**

$$A_{fiber} = \alpha \cdot L = 0.25 \, \frac{dB}{km} \times 40 \, km = 10 \, dB$$

*Logic:* The attenuation coefficient tells us how many dB are lost per km. Multiplying by length gives the *total* loss.

**Step 2: Compute the total connector + splice loss.**

$$A_{connectors} = 2 \times 0.5 \, dB = 1.0 \, dB$$
$$A_{splice} = 0.1 \, dB$$
$$A_{passive} = A_{connectors} + A_{splice} = 1.0 + 0.1 = 1.1 \, dB$$

*Logic:* Splice/connector losses are *additive* to each other but still in dB ŌĆö they are not converted to linear mW until the very end.

**Step 3: Compute the total end-to-end loss.**

$$A_{total} = A_{fiber} + A_{passive} = 10 + 1.1 = 11.1 \, dB$$

*Logic:* In a linear optical link, dB losses simply add (because dB is a logarithmic unit).

**Step 4: Convert the input power to dBm for the budget calculation.**

$$P_{in, dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1 \, mW}{1 \, mW}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1) = 0 \, dBm$$

*Logic:* By definition, **0 dBm = 1 mW**. This is a useful starting point for any power-budget problem.

**Step 5: Subtract the total loss to get the received power (in dBm).**

$$P_{out, dBm} = P_{in, dBm} - A_{total} = 0 - 11.1 = -11.1 \, dBm$$

*Logic:* A loss in dB is *subtracted* from the power level in dBm.

**Step 6: Convert the received power back to mW for the final answer.**

$$P_{out, mW} = 1 \, mW \times 10^{\,-11.1/10} = 1 \, mW \times 10^{-1.11}$$

$$P_{out, mW} = 1 \times 0.0776 \approx 0.078 \, mW = 78 \, \mu W$$

### Final Answers
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Total end-to-end loss | **11.1 dB** |
| Received power (dBm) | **ŌłÆ11.1 dBm** |
| Received power (linear) | **Ōēł 78 ┬ĄW** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** A 7-mark question often gives you the attenuation coefficient, fiber length, and connector losses, then asks for the **received power**. Always show all six steps above ŌĆö partial credit is given per step.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Co-axial vs Fiber Optic

| Parameter | Co-axial Cable | Fiber Optic Cable |
|---|---|---|
| Signal carrier | Electrical (electrons in copper) | Optical (photons in glass) |
| Operating frequency | Up to Ōēł 1 GHz | Up to Ōēł 100 THz (light) |
| Typical bandwidth | 100 MHz ŌĆō 1 GHz | 100s of GHz per wavelength |
| Attenuation at operating ╬╗ | Ōēł 10 ŌĆō 30 dB/100 m | Ōēł 0.2 dB/km |
| Maximum practical distance | 100 m ŌĆō 500 m | 100 km+ (no repeater) |
| EMI immunity | High (shielded) | Total (dielectric) |
| Crosstalk | Low | Effectively zero |
| Security | Easy to tap | Very difficult to tap |
| Installation cost | Low | High (specialist tools) |
| Weight & size | Heavy, bulky | Light, thin |
| Powering of devices | Carries DC power easily | Cannot carry DC |
| Typical connectors | BNC, F-type, N-type | SC, LC, ST, MPO/MTP |

---

## 3.4 Why These Two Media Co-exist in Practice

Even though fiber is *superior* in almost every measurable metric, coaxial cable is still in use because:

1. **Existing infrastructure** ŌĆö billions of dollars of coax plant already exist for cable TV and last-mile broadband.
2. **Power-over-coax** ŌĆö coax can carry DC power to remote amplifiers and antenna units, which fiber *cannot*.
3. **Mechanical robustness** ŌĆö coax tolerates tighter bends, crushing, and rough handling better than glass.
4. **Cost sensitivity** ŌĆö for short indoor runs (under 10 m), coax is *cheaper* than fiber + transceivers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A correct KTU answer **does not declare fiber as "always better"**. It explains that the *engineering trade-off* is: **distance ├Ś bandwidth ├Ś immunity ├Ś cost** ŌĆö and coax wins on cost/ruggedness, fiber wins on distance/bandwidth/immunity.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Co-axial Cable ŌĆö Cross-Sectional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CENTRAL CONDUCTOR Copper Solid or Stranded] --> B[DIELECTRIC INSULATOR Foam Polyethylene]
    B --> C[METALLIC SHIELD Braided Copper or Aluminium Foil]
    C --> D[OUTER JACKET PVC or PE Sheath]
    E[EMI SOURCE] -. SHIELD BLOCKS .-> C
    F[SIGNAL CURRENT] --> A
    A --> G[RETURN CURRENT ON SHIELD]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The signal current flows on the inner conductor (F ŌåÆ A), while the braided shield carries the return current (A ŌåÆ G). The metallic shield (C) intercepts any external electromagnetic interference (E), preventing it from coupling into the signal path.

---

## 4.2 Fiber Optic Cable ŌĆö Cross-Sectional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CORE Silica Glass Diameter 8 to 62.5 micrometres] --> B[CLADDING Silica Glass Lower Refractive Index 125 micrometres]
    B --> C[BUFFER COATING Acrylate Polymer]
    C --> D[STRENGTH MEMBERS Kevlar Aramid Yarn]
    D --> E[OUTER JACKET PVC or LSZH]
    F[INJECTED LIGHT PULSE] --> A
    A --> G[TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION AT CORE CLADDING BOUNDARY]
    G --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Light (F) is injected into the core (A). The refractive index step at the coreŌĆōcladding boundary (G) causes total internal reflection, so the light zig-zags down the core without escaping. The buffer (C), Kevlar (D), and jacket (E) are purely *mechanical* ŌĆö they do not carry the signal.

---

## 4.3 Light Propagation ŌĆö Acceptance & Critical Angle Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Light Source LED or Laser] --> B[Strikes Core Face]
    B --> C{Is Angle within Acceptance Cone}
    C -->|YES Greater than Critical Angle| D[TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION Light Trapped in Core]
    C -->|NO Less than Critical Angle| E[REFRACTION INTO CLADDING Light Lost]
    D --> F[PROPAGATES DOWN FIBER TO RECEIVER]
    E --> G[ATTENUATION LOSS Recorded in dB]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Not every ray entering the fiber face is guided. Only those whose angle falls **inside the acceptance cone** (defined by the Numerical Aperture) are totally internally reflected. Stray rays refract into the cladding and are lost as heat ŌĆö this is a key loss mechanism.

---

## 4.4 Multi-Mode vs Single-Mode ŌĆö Propagation Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1[Multi Mode Fiber 50 to 62.5 micrometres Core] --> M2[Many Modes Bounce Different Paths]
    M2 --> M3[Modal Dispersion Pulse Broadening]
    M3 --> M4[Limits Bandwidth Distance]
    S1[Single Mode Fiber 8 to 10 micrometres Core] --> S2[One Mode Straight Line]
    S2 --> S3[No Modal Dispersion]
    S3 --> S4[Very High Bandwidth Long Distance]
```

**Reading the diagram:** In MMF, many light paths (modes) travel simultaneously, but they take different geometric paths and therefore arrive at different times ŌĆö this is **modal dispersion**, the dominant bandwidth limit. In SMF, the core is so small that only one mode can propagate; the path is essentially a straight line, and modal dispersion vanishes.

---

## 4.5 Decision Flow ŌĆö Which Guided Medium To Pick?

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[New Link Requirement] --> Q1{Distance over 100 m}
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{EMI Environment}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q3{Data Rate over 1 Gbps}
    Q2 -->|Mild| C1[Use Twisted Pair or Coax]
    Q2 -->|Severe| F1[Use Fiber Optic]
    Q3 -->|NO| C2[Use Co axial Cable]
    Q3 -->|YES| F2[Use Single Mode Fiber]
    C1 --> Done
    F1 --> Done
    C2 --> Done
    F2 --> Done
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the KTU-recommended decision logic. Use this exact decision tree in a viva when asked: *"Given a campus link of 600 m, which medium would you choose?"* ŌĆö answer: **Single-mode fiber**, because the distance exceeds 100 m *and* modern campus backbones easily exceed 1 Gbps.
