# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part A carries **2 marks for correctness + 1 mark for diagram / neatness**. Use a labelled cross-section wherever the question mentions a *cable* ŌĆö students who skip the diagram forfeit the third mark.

### Q1. Define transmission media. Differentiate between guided and unguided media with two examples each. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **transmission medium** is the physical path over which information signals travel from a source to a destination in a data communication system. The **OSI Physical Layer** uses the medium to convert a frame into electromagnetic or optical energy.
>
> - **Guided Media** ŌĆö signals are confined *within* a solid physical channel. Examples: **Co-axial Cable**, **Fiber Optic Cable**, Twisted Pair.
> - **Unguided Media** ŌĆö signals propagate *through* free space (air/vacuum) as electromagnetic waves. Examples: **Radio waves**, **Microwave**, Satellite communication.

**[3/3]**

---

### Q2. List any three advantages of fiber optic cable over co-axial cable. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lower attenuation** (Ōēł 0.2 dB/km vs 10 ŌĆō 30 dB/100 m for coax) ŌåÆ enables runs of 100 km+ without repeaters.
2. **Higher bandwidth** ŌĆö Tbps capability vs Ōēł 1 GHz for coax.
3. **Complete immunity to EMI** ŌĆö fiber is dielectric (no metal) so it neither radiates nor picks up interference.

*Acceptable substitute points:* no crosstalk, lighter weight, secure against tapping.

**[3/3]**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B sub-questions are typically **7 + 7 marks**. Each sub-part must contain at least **one labelled diagram** to score full marks, and every formula must be substituted with values before solving (no naked symbols).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q A (a) ŌĆö Explain the construction and working of a co-axial cable. List its advantages, disadvantages, and applications. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):** A co-axial cable is a cylindrical, four-layer guided medium. From the centre outward:

1. **Inner conductor** ŌĆö solid or stranded copper, carries the signal.
2. **Dielectric insulator** ŌĆö foam polyethylene; maintains geometric spacing.
3. **Metallic shield** ŌĆö woven copper braid, often combined with aluminium foil; provides the return-current path *and* blocks EMI.
4. **Outer jacket** ŌĆö PVC sheath; provides mechanical and environmental protection.

All four layers share a common **geometric axis** ŌĆö hence the name "co-axial".

**Working (2 marks):** The cable is used in an **unbalanced** configuration. The signal current flows on the inner conductor, while the return current flows on the *inner surface* of the outer shield. Because of the skin effect at high frequencies, the two currents are physically separated by the dielectric, which gives the cable its well-defined **characteristic impedance** (50 ╬® or 75 ╬®). The shield also intercepts any external electromagnetic field, preventing it from coupling into the signal.

**Advantages (1 mark):** Excellent EMI shielding, high bandwidth (up to Ōēł 1 GHz), low crosstalk, supports both baseband and broadband signalling, robust mechanical design.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):** Bulkier and more expensive than twisted pair, skin-effect losses grow with ŌłÜf, single point of failure per segment, requires proper grounding of the shield.

**Applications:** Cable TV distribution, broadband internet (DOCSIS), CCTV, GPS antenna feeds, legacy 10BASE-2 / 10BASE-5 Ethernet, RF test equipment.

**[7/7]**

#### Q A (b) ŌĆö Explain the construction and working of a fiber optic cable. State and derive the expression for Numerical Aperture (NA). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):** A fiber optic cable consists of three concentric optical regions surrounded by mechanical protection:

1. **Core** ŌĆö ultra-pure silica glass, diameter 8ŌĆō10 ┬Ąm (SMF) or 50ŌĆō62.5 ┬Ąm (MMF).
2. **Cladding** ŌĆö silica with refractive index Ōēł 1 % lower than the core; diameter 125 ┬Ąm (standardised).
3. **Buffer coating** ŌĆö acrylate polymer; absorbs shocks.
4. **Strength members** ŌĆö Kevlar yarn; provides tensile strength.
5. **Outer jacket** ŌĆö PVC.

**Working (1 mark):** Light pulses from an LED or laser are injected into the core. Because $n_1 > n_2$, rays striking the coreŌĆōcladding interface at an angle Ōēź the critical angle undergo **total internal reflection** and are trapped, zig-zagging down the core to the receiver.

**Derivation of NA (4 marks):** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full six-step derivation. Key results to reproduce in the exam:

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$
$$NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

The acceptance angle is $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA / n_0)$. A high NA means a wider light-cone can be captured (suitable for LED + MMF in LANs). A low NA means a narrow, collimated beam (suitable for laser + SMF in long-haul links).

**[7/7]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Alternative Choice

#### Q B (a) ŌĆö With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a co-axial cable. Compare baseband and broadband coaxial transmission. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (2 marks):** A neat cross-section with four labelled layers ŌĆö *Inner Conductor*, *Dielectric*, *Shield*, *Outer Jacket*. Use a proper Mermaid-style block or hand-drawn diagram. (See Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Construction (2 marks):** As described in Q A(a) above. The key engineering point to mention is that the **ratio of outer-to-inner conductor diameter** fixes the characteristic impedance (50 ╬® for digital Ethernet, 75 ╬® for CATV).

**Baseband vs Broadband Co-axial Transmission (3 marks):**

| Feature | Baseband Co-ax | Broadband Co-ax |
|---|---|---|
| Signalling | Digital, single channel | Analog, multi-channel (FDM) |
| Modulation | None ŌĆö direct digital | RF carriers at different frequencies |
| Direction | Half-duplex (e.g. 10BASE-2 Ethernet) | Full-duplex possible via sub-split / mid-split |
| Distance | Shorter (Ōēż 500 m for 10BASE-2) | Longer (cable TV runs Ōēź 10 km with amplifiers) |
| Topology | Bus (single shared medium) | Tree-and-branch |
| Example | 10BASE-2 Ethernet | Cable TV (DOCSIS) |

**[7/7]**

#### Q B (b) ŌĆö Differentiate between single-mode and multi-mode fiber. A fiber has core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.48$. Find (i) the numerical aperture, (ii) the acceptance angle, and (iii) the critical angle. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison (3 marks):** See the table in Section 2.2.3 of these notes. Mandatory points: core diameter (8ŌĆō10 ┬Ąm vs 50ŌĆō62.5 ┬Ąm), light source (Laser vs LED), modal dispersion (negligible vs high), bandwidth-distance product, maximum distance, and typical cost/application.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.48$, surrounding medium is air so $n_0 = 1$.

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.48)^2}$$

Computing:

$$(1.50)^2 = 2.2500$$
$$(1.48)^2 = 2.1904$$
$$n_1^2 - n_2^2 = 2.2500 - 2.1904 = 0.0596$$

$$NA = \sqrt{0.0596} = 0.2441$$

**Answer (i): NA Ōēł 0.244**

**(ii) Acceptance Angle:**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA / n_0) = \sin^{-1}(0.2441 / 1) = \sin^{-1}(0.2441)$$

$$\theta_a = 14.13^\circ \approx 14.1^\circ$$

**Answer (ii): $\theta_a \approx 14.1^\circ$**

**(iii) Critical Angle:**

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.48}{1.50} = 0.9867$$

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9867) = 80.63^\circ \approx 80.6^\circ$$

**Answer (iii): $\theta_c \approx 80.6^\circ$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A high critical angle (Ōēł 80┬░) means most rays strike the boundary *grazing* the cladding ŌĆö exactly what we want for total internal reflection. A small acceptance angle (Ōēł 14┬░) means the fiber is "narrow-cone" ŌĆö consistent with a low NA.

**[7/7]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Skipping the labelled diagram** in a 7-mark sub-part costs **1 ŌĆō 2 marks outright**, even if the textual answer is perfect. *Always* draw a cross-section of coax and a longitudinal view of fiber.
> 2. **Confusing core with cladding diameter** ŌĆö students frequently write "core = 125 ┬Ąm" which is the *cladding*. The cladding is *always* 125 ┬Ąm; the *core* is 8ŌĆō62.5 ┬Ąm.
> 3. **Mixing up the symbol conventions** ŌĆö writing $\theta_a$ where the question asks for $\theta_c$, or using the formula $NA = \sqrt{n_2^2 - n_1^2}$ (subtraction in the wrong order). This single sign error fails the derivation entirely.
> 4. **Forgetting the *relative* refractive index difference** $\Delta$ in NA problems ŌĆö some KTU problems state $\Delta$ directly and expect you to use $NA = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$.
> 5. **Failing to state the working principle** before plugging in numbers ŌĆö e.g. directly substituting into $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ without mentioning *total internal reflection* loses 1 mark for missing the concept.
> 6. **Unit errors in the power-budget** ŌĆö the output of $\alpha L$ is in dB, the input power in dBm; the *subtraction* gives the output in dBm. Students often incorrectly add.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Co-axial Cable
- Ōśæ Four-layer concentric construction: **Inner Conductor ŌåÆ Dielectric ŌåÆ Shield ŌåÆ Jacket**
- Ōśæ Two standard impedances: **50 ╬® (Ethernet/RF)** and **75 ╬® (CATV)**
- Ōśæ Attenuation grows with ŌłÜf due to the **skin effect**
- Ōśæ Supports **Baseband (digital)** and **Broadband (analog, FDM)** signalling
- Ōśæ Standardised cable types: **RG-6, RG-58, RG-59, RG-8**
- Ōśæ Can carry DC power along with the signal (advantage over fiber)
- Ōśæ Maximum Ōēł 500 m for 10BASE-2 Ethernet

### Fiber Optic Cable
- Ōśæ **Core** (light carrier) + **Cladding** (TIR) + **Buffer** + **Kevlar** + **Jacket**
- Ōśæ Cladding is always **125 ┬Ąm**; core is **8ŌĆō10 ┬Ąm (SMF)** or **50ŌĆō62.5 ┬Ąm (MMF)**
- Ōśæ Working principle = **Total Internal Reflection** at the coreŌĆōcladding interface
- Ōśæ **SNR Ōēł n1 > n2** (mandatory) for TIR
- Ōśæ **NA = nŌéĆ sin ╬Ėa = ŌłÜ(n1┬▓ ŌłÆ n2┬▓)** ŌĆö the "light-gathering power" of the fiber
- Ōśæ **╬Ėc = sinŌü╗┬╣(n2 / n1)** ŌĆö the boundary between guided and refracted rays
- Ōśæ MMF ŌåÆ LED, large core, modal dispersion limits distance (Ōēż 2 km)
- Ōśæ SMF ŌåÆ Laser, tiny core, negligible modal dispersion, runs of 100+ km
- Ōśæ Lowest attenuation of any guided medium: **Ōēł 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm**
- Ōśæ Three operating windows: **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm** (WDM operates in the 1550 nm band)
- Ōśæ **Purely dielectric** ŌåÆ total EMI immunity, no crosstalk, no ground loops

### Universal Must-Know Formulae
- Ōśæ $NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$
- Ōśæ $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$
- Ōśæ $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$
- Ōśæ $\alpha = A_{dB}/L$ (dB/km)
- Ōśæ $P_{out, dBm} = P_{in, dBm} - A_{total}$ (linear dB losses *add*; dBm powers *subtract*)
- Ōśæ $\Delta\tau_{modal} = n_1 \Delta n L / c$

### Quick Decision Heuristic
- Ōśæ **Twisted pair** ŌĆö short runs, low cost, low EMI (e.g. office Ethernet)
- Ōśæ **Co-axial** ŌĆö medium distance, broadband, needs to carry DC power
- Ōśæ **MMF** ŌĆö data-centre / campus backbone, 1ŌĆō10 Gbps, up to 2 km
- Ōśæ **SMF** ŌĆö long-haul, undersea, FTTH, 10 Gbps ŌĆō Tbps, up to 100+ km
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Framing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Framing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Framing** is the **Data Link Layer** function responsible for partitioning a stream of bits received from the **Network Layer** into manageable, discrete units called **frames**, prior to their transmission over the physical medium. It involves attaching structured **headers** and **trailers** (containing source/destination addresses, control information, and error-detection codes) to the payload, and implementing mechanisms such as **character stuffing** or **bit stuffing** to ensure unambiguous frame boundary identification at the receiver.

In the **OSI Reference Model**, framing resides in the **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)**, and is the first critical step before **Medium Access Control (MAC)**, **error detection**, and **physical transmission**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Postal Parcel" Model

Imagine you are sending a long letter to a friend, but the post office has a rule: **"No envelope may exceed 20 pages."** Before mailing, you must:

1. **Split** the letter into chunks of 20 pages or fewer.
2. **Number** each chunk (e.g., "Page 1 of 5", "Page 2 of 5") so your friend knows the order.
3. **Mark** the start and end of each envelope with a recognizable sticker.
4. **Seal** each envelope with a tamper-evident tape (a "checksum").
5. **Avoid** using tape that looks identical to your content (a "delimiter collision").

**Framing is precisely this process for bits.** The Data Link Layer chops the bit-stream from upper layers into **frames** (the envelopes), adds a **header** (sender address, frame number, type) and a **trailer** (CRC, end marker), and inserts special **delimiter patterns** that the receiver uses to recognize where one frame ends and the next begins.

---

## 1.3 Why Framing is Needed ŌĆö The Bit-Stream Problem

The physical layer delivers to the Data Link Layer a raw, synchronous **bit-stream**. Without framing:

- The receiver has **no way to know** where one PDU (Protocol Data Unit) ends and the next begins.
- Errors in transmission cannot be isolated to a specific unit; the **entire** stream would have to be retransmitted.
- Addressing would be impossible because there is no fixed unit to which an address can be attached.
- Synchronization between sender and receiver would drift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The physical layer is concerned with *raw symbols on the wire*; the data link layer is concerned with *meaningful groups of those symbols*. **Framing is the bridge.**

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard MTU (Ethernet v2)** | **1500 bytes** | Maximum payload per frame |
| **Ethernet Frame Max Size** | **1518 bytes** | With header + trailer |
| **Ethernet Frame Min Size** | **64 bytes** | For CSMA/CD collision detection |
| **Standard Flag Byte (HDLC/PPP)** | `0x7E` = `01111110` | Frame delimiter |
| **HDLC Address Field** | **1 byte** (default `11111111` = broadcast) | Link addressing |
| **HDLC Control Field** | **1 byte** (or 2 in extended) | Frame type / sequence numbering |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Frame Structure

A frame is logically divided into three regions:

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{\text{Header}}_{\text{source/dest addr, type, seq\#}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Payload}}_{\text{data from network layer}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Trailer}}_{\text{CRC, FCS, end flag}}$$

| Field | Function | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|
| **Flag / Delimiter** | Marks the start (and often end) of the frame | 1 byte (or 8 bits) |
| **Address Field** | Source / Destination MAC address | 6 bytes (Ethernet) / 1 byte (HDLC) |
| **Control Field** | Frame type (data/control), sequence numbers | 1ŌĆō2 bytes |
| **Length / Type** | Payload length or upper-layer protocol | 2 bytes |
| **Payload (Data)** | Network Layer PDU | 0 ŌĆō MTU bytes |
| **FCS (CRC-32)** | Error-detection code | 4 bytes |
| **Trailing Flag** | Marks end of frame (optional in some schemes) | 1 byte |

---

## 2.2 The Three Principal Framing Methods

### Method 1: Character-Oriented Framing (Byte Stuffing)

Used in older protocols like **IBM's BSC (Binary Synchronous Communication)** and the older versions of **PPP**.

**Mechanism:**

1. Each frame begins and ends with a special **sentinel byte** (e.g., `DLE STX` = `0x10 0x02` to start, `DLE ETX` = `0x10 0x03` to end).
2. If the payload itself contains the byte `DLE` (`0x10`), the sender inserts an **extra** `DLE` byte immediately after it. This is called **byte stuffing**.
3. The receiver, upon seeing two consecutive `DLE` bytes, removes the second one to recover the original payload.

**Example:**

```
Original payload (in hex):  A B 10 C D
After stuffing at sender:   A B 10 10 C D
After de-stuffing at recv:  A B 10 C D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback:** Character-oriented framing is **byte-bound** and depends on an 8-bit character set (e.g., ASCII). It is inefficient for binary data and is largely obsolete in modern LANs.

---

### Method 2: Bit-Oriented Framing (Bit Stuffing)

Used in **HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control)**, **SDLC**, and **PPP** (in its bit-oriented mode). This is the **most exam-relevant** method for KTU.

**Mechanism:**

1. Each frame begins and ends with the **flag** `0x7E` = `01111110` (binary).
2. The sender **counts the consecutive 1s** in the bit-stream. After every sequence of **five** consecutive `1`s, the sender automatically inserts a single `0` bit. This is **bit stuffing**.
3. The receiver, after detecting a flag, scans the incoming bit-stream. If it sees five consecutive `1`s followed by a `0`, it **removes** that `0`. If it sees five consecutive `1`s followed by another `1`, it has detected the closing flag.

**Example:**

```
Original bit-stream:   ...01101111111011111...
                      5 ones detected ŌåÆ stuff a 0
After bit stuffing:    ...0110111110110111110...
After de-stuffing:     ...01101111111011111...
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "five 1s"?** Because the flag itself is six `1`s (`01111110`). By stuffing a `0` after every five `1`s, the protocol guarantees that the flag pattern `01111110` can never accidentally appear inside the payload.

---

### Method 3: Physical Layer Coding Violations

Used in protocols like **IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring)** and **100BASE-TX Ethernet**.

- The physical layer's encoding scheme (e.g., Manchester) reserves certain symbols as **illegal** (e.g., `HIGH-HIGH` or `LOW-LOW`).
- These reserved symbols are used exclusively to mark **frame boundaries**.
- No overhead is added to the payload, but it requires special physical-layer support.

> [!NOTE]
> This method is **not bit/byte transparent** in the conventional sense; it exploits redundancy in the line coding. It is rarely asked in KTU exams but is a valid conceptual answer.

---

## 2.3 HDLC Frame Format (High-Yield)

```
   | Flag (8) | Address (8) | Control (8/16) | Information (variable) | FCS (16/32) | Flag (8) |
   | 01111110 |  11111111   |     N(S),N(R)  |        Payload         |   CRC       | 01111110 |
```

| Field | Bits | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Flag** | 8 | `0x7E`, may be shared as both closing of one frame and opening of next |
| **Address** | 8 (extended: 16+) | For point-to-multipoint; all-1s means broadcast |
| **Control** | 8 (or 16) | Defines frame type: I-Frame, S-Frame, U-Frame |
| **Information** | Variable (0ŌĆōN) | Data from higher layer; not present in some control frames |
| **FCS** | 16 (CRC-CCITT) or 32 | Frame Check Sequence (error detection) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stuffing Rule (Bit)** | After **5 consecutive 1s**, insert a `0` | Applied to data between flags only |
| 2 | **De-stuffing Rule (Bit)** | After **5 consecutive 1s**, discard the next `0` | If next bit is `1`, it is the flag |
| 3 | **Stuffing Rule (Byte)** | If payload byte = `DLE` (`0x10`), insert extra `DLE` after it | Applies in DLE-based protocols |
| 4 | **Frame Overhead (HDLC)** | $H_{HDLC} = 8 + 8 + 8 + 16 + 8 = 48$ bits | Excluding payload |
| 5 | **Frame Overhead (Ethernet)** | $H_{Eth} = 14 + 4 + 12 = 30$ bytes (with preamble+SFD) | 8-byte preamble + 14 hdr + 4 CRC |
| 6 | **Maximum Frame Size (Ethernet)** | $L_{max} = 1518$ bytes | Header + Payload + Trailer |
| 7 | **Minimum Frame Size (Ethernet)** | $L_{min} = 64$ bytes | For collision detection |
| 8 | **Stuffing Overhead (Worst Case, Bit)** | Every 5 bits ŌåÆ 1 stuffed bit ŌåÆ **20% overhead** | If payload is all `1`s |
| 9 | **Stuffing Overhead (Worst Case, Byte)** | Every `DLE` byte doubles to 2 ŌåÆ **100%** | Pathological case |
| 10 | **CRC Polynomial (Ethernet)** | $G(x) = x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + x^{22} + x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{11} + x^{10} + x^8 + x^7 + x^5 + x^4 + x^2 + x + 1$ | Used in Ethernet FCS |
| 11 | **HDLC CRC-CCITT** | $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{12} + x^5 + 1$ | Used in HDLC FCS-16 |
| 12 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{overhead}}$ | Always $\eta < 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Bit stuffing is the foundation of **HDLC**, which in turn underpins **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)**, used by virtually every dial-up, DSL, and serial-line internet connection. Mastering bit stuffing is essential for understanding **frame synchronization in synchronous serial communication**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example 1: Bit Stuffing

**Problem (Modeled on KTU pattern):**
A bit-oriented protocol uses the flag `01111110`. Apply **bit stuffing** to the following data bit-stream (the flag is NOT to be stuffed; stuffing applies only to data between flags):

$$\text{Data} = \texttt{011011111110011111101011111101}$$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

We scan left-to-right, counting consecutive `1`s. After every block of **five** consecutive `1`s, we insert a `0`.

| Step | Bit(s) Read | Action | Running Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `0` | None | `0` |
| 2 | `1` | Count = 1 | `01` |
| 3 | `1` | Count = 2 | `011` |
| 4 | `0` | Reset count = 0 | `0110` |
| 5 | `1` | Count = 1 | `01101` |
| 6 | `1` | Count = 2 | `011011` |
| 7 | `1` | Count = 3 | `0110111` |
| 8 | `1` | Count = 4 | `01101111` |
| 9 | `1` | Count = 5 | `011011111` |
| 10 | `1` | Count would become 6 ŌåÆ **stuff a 0** | `011011111 0 1` |
| 11 | `1` | Count = 1 (after reset) | `011011111 0 11` |
| 12 | `0` | Count = 0 | `011011111 0 110` |
| ... (continues) | ... | ... | ... |

**Final Stuffed Bit-Stream (between flags):**

$$\text{Stuffed Data} = \underbrace{011011111}_{\text{original 9 bits}}\;\texttt{0}\;1\;\underbrace{0011111}_{\text{next 7}}\;\texttt{0}\;1\;\underbrace{0111111}_{\text{next 7}}\;\texttt{0}\;\underbrace{01}_{\text{remainder}}$$

**Concatenated Result:**

$$\text{Stuffed Data} = \texttt{01101111101100111110101111110101}$$

**Verification at Receiver:**

When the receiver encounters `11111 0` in the bit-stream, it removes the trailing `0` and continues scanning. The original flag `01111110` can therefore never be confused with payload data, because a `0` is always guaranteed to follow any run of five `1`s inside the payload.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 2: Byte Stuffing

**Problem:**
A character-oriented protocol uses `STX = 0x02` to mark the start of payload, `ETX = 0x03` to mark the end, and `DLE = 0x10` as the escape character. Apply **byte stuffing** to the following data:

$$\text{Data} = \texttt{0x41, 0x10, 0x02, 0x55, 0x10, 0x10, 0x09}$$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

| Step | Byte Seen | Action | Stuffed Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `0x41` (`A`) | Data byte, copy as-is | `0x41` |
| 2 | `0x10` (DLE) | **Stuff:** insert an extra `DLE` after it | `0x41, 0x10, 0x10` |
| 3 | `0x02` (STX) | This would prematurely end payload ŌåÆ **Stuff:** insert `DLE` before it | `0x41, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x02` |
| 4 | `0x55` (`U`) | Data byte, copy as-is | `0x41, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x02, 0x55` |
| 5 | `0x10` (DLE) | **Stuff:** insert extra `DLE` | `0x41, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x02, 0x55, 0x10, 0x10` |
| 6 | `0x10` (DLE) | **Stuff:** insert extra `DLE` | `0x41, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x02, 0x55, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10` |
| 7 | `0x09` (TAB) | Data byte, copy as-is | `0x41, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x02, 0x55, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x10, 0x09` |

**Final Stuffed Payload (between STX and ETX):**

$$\text{Stuffed} = \underbrace{\texttt{41}}_{\text{A}}\; \underbrace{\texttt{10 10}}_{\text{DLE escaped}}\; \underbrace{\texttt{10 02}}_{\text{STX escaped}}\; \underbrace{\texttt{55}}_{\text{U}}\; \underbrace{\texttt{10 10}}_{\text{DLE}}\; \underbrace{\texttt{10 10}}_{\text{DLE}}\; \underbrace{\texttt{09}}_{\text{TAB}}$$

**Complete Frame Transmitted:**

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{\texttt{DLE, STX}}_{\text{Start}} \; \mid \; \text{Stuffed Payload} \; \mid \; \underbrace{\texttt{DLE, ETX}}_{\text{End}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Some protocols (like PPP in its default mode) only stuff the **flag byte** itself, not arbitrary special bytes. The above is the *general* classical character-stuffing rule.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Framing Methods

| Property | Character-Oriented | Bit-Oriented | Coding Violation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary marker** | Special byte(s) e.g., `DLE STX` | Flag bit pattern `01111110` | Reserved line symbols |
| **Transparency** | Limited to 8-bit alphabets | Full bit transparency | Encoding-dependent |
| **Overhead** | Up to 100% (pathological) | Max ~20% | 0% in payload |
| **Modern use** | Legacy (BSC) | HDLC, PPP, USB | Token Ring |
| **Receiver logic** | Stateful byte-matching | Count `1`s, drop stuffed `0` | Detect illegal symbol |
| **KTU relevance** | Conceptual | **Highest** | Low |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Frame Efficiency Calculation

**Problem:**
An HDLC frame carries a payload of 1000 bytes. Compute the **frame efficiency** if the HDLC overhead is **48 bits** and the flag bytes at start and end are counted separately.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the bits involved.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{payload} &= 1000 \text{ bytes} = 1000 \times 8 = 8000 \text{ bits} \\
L_{overhead} &= 48 \text{ bits (Addr+Ctrl+FCS)} + 2 \times 8 \text{ bits (2 flags)} = 64 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the efficiency formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{overhead}} \\
&= \frac{8000}{8000 + 64} \\
&= \frac{8000}{8064} \\
&\approx 0.9921 \\
&\approx 99.21\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

A higher percentage means the protocol is **efficient**; a lower percentage indicates significant **framing overhead**. HDLC is highly efficient for large payloads but inefficient for very small ones (e.g., a 1-byte payload has only ~11% efficiency with HDLC's 48-bit overhead).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context:** This is why protocols like **802.11 (Wi-Fi)** aggregate multiple small frames into a single transmission, and why **TCP MSS (Maximum Segment Size)** is negotiated to avoid tiny frames that waste capacity on overhead.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Worst-Case Bit Stuffing Overhead

**Problem:**
A payload is a sequence of $N$ bits, all of which are `1`. What is the worst-case **stuffed length** (excluding flags)?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

For every 5 consecutive `1`s, one `0` is inserted. So in any block of 5 input `1`s, the output is 6 bits (5 `1`s + 1 `0`).

For $N$ bits, the number of 5-bit groups is:

$$\text{Groups} = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{5} \right\rfloor$$

Each full group becomes 6 bits. The remaining $N \bmod 5$ bits remain as `1`s.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{stuffed} &= 6 \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{N}{5} \right\rfloor + (N \bmod 5) \\
\text{Overhead factor} &= \frac{L_{stuffed}}{N} = \frac{6 \lfloor N/5 \rfloor + (N \bmod 5)}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Asymptotically** (as $N \to \infty$):

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{L_{stuffed}}{N} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2$$

So in the worst case, **bit stuffing adds 20% overhead**.

$$\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{L_{stuffed} - N}{N} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Frame Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 7
    Flag["FLAG 01111110"]:1
    Addr["ADDRESS 8 bits"]:1
    Ctrl["CONTROL 8/16 bits"]:1
    Type["TYPE 16 bits"]:1
    Payload["PAYLOAD 0 to MTU"]:2
    FCS["FCS CRC 16/32"]:1
    Flag2["FLAG 01111110"]:1
```

---

## 4.2 Bit Stuffing Sender Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Frame to be sent"] --> B["Output flag 01111110"]
    B --> C["Read next bit from buffer"]
    C --> D{"Is bit = 1?"}
    D -- "No, bit = 0" --> E["Reset ones counter to 0"]
    E --> F["Transmit bit as-is"]
    F --> G{"More bits in buffer?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> C
    G -- "No" --> H["Output closing flag 01111110"]
    D -- "Yes, bit = 1" --> I["Increment ones counter"]
    I --> J{"Counter = 5?"}
    J -- "No" --> F
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Transmit a stuffed 0 bit"]
    K --> L["Reset counter to 0"]
    L --> G
    H --> M["End of transmission"]
```

---

## 4.3 Bit Stuffing Receiver Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Bit stream from physical layer"] --> B["Detect opening flag 01111110"]
    B --> C["Reset ones counter to 0"]
    C --> D["Read next bit"]
    D --> E{"Is bit = 1?"}
    E -- "No, bit = 0" --> F{"Is counter = 5?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Discard this stuffed 0 bit. Reset counter to 0."]
    F -- "No" --> H["Pass bit to upper layer. Reset counter to 0."]
    G --> I{"More bits?"}
    H --> I
    E -- "Yes, bit = 1" --> J["Increment counter"]
    J --> K{"Counter = 5?"}
    K -- "No" --> L["Pass bit to upper layer"]
    K -- "Yes" --> M["Read NEXT bit"]
    M --> N{"Is next bit = 0?"}
    N -- "Yes" --> G
    N -- "No" --> O["Closing flag detected. End of frame."]
    L --> I
    I -- "Yes" --> D
    I -- "No" --> O
    O --> P["End: Frame successfully received"]
```

---

## 4.4 Byte Stuffing Sender Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Payload bytes from network layer"] --> B["Emit DLE STX as start sentinel"]
    B --> C["Read next byte"]
    C --> D{"Is byte = DLE?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Emit DLE, then emit DLE again"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"Is byte = STX or ETX?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Emit DLE, then emit the byte"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Emit byte as-is"]
    E --> I{"More bytes?"}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- "Yes" --> C
    I -- "No" --> J["Emit DLE ETX as end sentinel"]
    J --> K["End: Frame transmitted"]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison: Encapsulation of a Network Layer Packet into a Data Link Frame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        A1["HTTP Request data"]
    end
    subgraph TRANS["Transport Layer"]
        B1["TCP Header + Data = Segment"]
    end
    subgraph NET["Network Layer"]
        C1["IP Header + Segment = Packet"]
    end
    subgraph DLL["Data Link Layer"]
        D1["Frame Header + Packet + Frame Trailer = FRAME"]
    end
    subgraph PHY["Physical Layer"]
        E1["Encoded bits on the medium"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Data Link Layer's frame is the **first unit** to be physically delimited on the wire; everything above it is encapsulated inside the **payload** field. The frame's **header and trailer** are the "envelope" added by the DLL.

---

## 4.6 Timing Diagram ŌĆö Frame Transmission Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Receiver
    participant Physical
    Sender->>Physical: Transmit FLAG 01111110
    Sender->>Physical: Transmit ADDRESS field
    Sender->>Physical: Transmit CONTROL field
    Sender->>Physical: Transmit PAYLOAD (with bit/byte stuffing)
    Sender->>Physical: Transmit FCS / CRC
    Sender->>Physical: Transmit FLAG 01111110
    Physical->>Receiver: Synchronize on flag
    Receiver->>Receiver: De-stuff / De-escape payload
    Receiver->>Receiver: Verify CRC against FCS
    Receiver->>Sender: ACK (if no error) or NAK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define framing. Why is it necessary in the data link layer? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Framing is a data link layer function that divides the stream of bits received from the network layer into discrete units called **frames** by appending a **header** and a **trailer**, and by using delimiters to mark frame boundaries.

**Necessity (2 marks):**
1. **Boundary identification:** The receiver needs to know where each frame begins and ends in the continuous bit-stream.
2. **Error isolation:** Errors can be detected and corrected on a per-frame basis instead of for the entire transmission.
3. **Addressing:** Each frame must carry source/destination addresses so it can be routed through the link.
4. **Synchronization:** Framing keeps sender and receiver synchronized at the link layer.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between bit-oriented and character-oriented framing. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Character-Oriented | Bit-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Bytes (8-bit characters) | Individual bits |
| **Delimiter** | Special bytes (e.g., `DLE STX`) | Flag bit pattern `01111110` |
| **Transparency method** | Byte stuffing (insert extra `DLE`) | Bit stuffing (insert `0` after 5 ones) |
| **Use case** | Legacy text-based protocols (BSC) | Modern protocols (HDLC, PPP) |
| **Data efficiency** | Lower (byte-aligned) | Higher (any bit pattern) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

#### (a) Explain the bit stuffing concept in HDLC with a suitable example. Show how a flag byte `01111110` is guaranteed not to appear in the stuffed data. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept (2 marks):**
In bit-oriented protocols like HDLC, frames are delimited by a unique 8-bit **flag** pattern: `0x7E` = `01111110`. To prevent the flag from appearing inside the payload, the sender applies **bit stuffing**: after every sequence of **five** consecutive `1` bits in the data, a single `0` bit is **automatically inserted**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Example (3 marks):**

Consider the following data to be transmitted:

```
Data:  0110 1111 1110 0111 1110 1
```

Applying bit stuffing:

| Original chunk | After stuffing |
|---|---|
| `0110` | `0110` (only two `1`s, no change) |
| `11111` | `11111 0` (five `1`s ŌåÆ insert `0`) |
| `10` | `10` |
| `0111` | `0111` |
| `11101` | (count again ŌĆö five `1`s would be reached, etc.) |

Final stuffed bit-stream (between flags):

```
0110 11111 0 10 0111 111 0 1
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Why the flag never appears (2 marks):**
The flag is the bit pattern `01111110` (six `1`s surrounded by zeros). Inside the payload, after every sequence of five `1`s, the stuffer **forces a `0` to follow**. This means it is **mathematically impossible** for six consecutive `1`s to occur inside the data. Hence, the flag pattern can occur only at the legitimate start and end positions.

**Step 4 ŌĆö De-stuffing at receiver (1 mark):**
The receiver, after detecting a flag, scans incoming bits. If it sees `11111 0`, it **removes** the `0`. If it sees `11111 1` (i.e., six `1`s followed by a `0`), it has detected the closing flag.

---

#### (b) Given a data bit-stream `011111100111110111110111110`, apply bit stuffing. What is the percentage overhead? Assume the original data is exactly the given bits (excluding the surrounding flags). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify flag and data (1 mark):**

The data (excluding flags) is:
$$D = \texttt{011111100111110111110111110}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply bit stuffing (4 marks):**

We scan and count `1`s. After 5 consecutive `1`s, insert a `0`.

| Position | Bits | Action | Stuffed Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1ŌĆō4 | `0111` | Count = 3, then `0` resets | `0111` |
| 5ŌĆō9 | `1111 1` | 5 consecutive ŌåÆ insert `0` | `0111 1111 1 0` |
| 10ŌĆō14 | `0 011 1` | Count drops to 1 | `... 0 011 1` |
| 15ŌĆō19 | `111 0 1` | 5 ones again ŌåÆ insert `0` | `... 11 0` |
| ... | ... | continues | ... |

Following the rule strictly across the entire stream:

**Original:** `0 11111 1 0 0 11111 0 11111 0 11111 0`

Note: After every run of 5 `1`s followed by another `1`, a `0` is inserted. Let's count `1` runs carefully:

- `011111` ŌåÆ 5 ones at positions 2-6 ŌåÆ output `0111110 1` (stuff 0, then continue)
- `0011111` ŌåÆ 5 ones ŌåÆ stuff 0
- `0111110` ŌåÆ 5 ones ŌåÆ stuff 0
- `111110` ŌåÆ 5 ones ŌåÆ stuff 0

**Final Stuffed Bit-Stream:**

$$D_{stuffed} = \texttt{0111110\;1\;0011111\;0\;011111\;0\;011111\;0\;1111}$$

Total length = 30 bits (vs. original 27 bits).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Overhead calculation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Original length} L &= 27 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Stuffed length} L_s &= 30 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Overhead} &= L_s - L = 30 - 27 = 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{3}{27} \times 100\% = 11.11\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the HDLC frame format. List the three types of HDLC frames and their functions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö HDLC Frame Format Diagram (2 marks):**

```
|<-- 1B -->|<-- 1B -->|<-- 1B -->|<- Variable ->|<- 2B/4B ->|<-- 1B -->|
|  Flag    | Address  | Control  | Information  |   FCS      |  Flag    |
| 01111110 |  8 bits  | 8/16 bits|   Payload    |  CRC-16/32 | 01111110 |
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Field Description (2 marks):**

- **Flag:** `01111110`, marks start/end of frame. Adjacent frames may share a single flag.
- **Address:** Identifies the secondary station; `11111111` indicates broadcast.
- **Control:** Specifies frame type and contains sequence numbers `N(S)` and `N(R)`.
- **Information:** Variable-length payload from higher layer (absent in some control frames).
- **FCS:** Frame Check Sequence, computed over Address + Control + Information fields using CRC.
- **Trailing Flag:** Marks the end of frame.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Three HDLC Frame Types (3 marks):**

| Frame Type | Purpose | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **I-Frame (Information)** | Carries user data; includes `N(S)` (send seq#) and `N(R)` (next expected receive seq#) | Data transfer |
| **S-Frame (Supervisory)** | Performs flow and error control (RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ); no payload | ACK/NAK, flow control |
| **U-Frame (Unnumbered)** | Link establishment, disconnection, mode setting (SABM, DISC, UA, FRMR) | Link management |

---

#### (b) Explain character-oriented framing with byte stuffing. Given the data `A B DLE C ETX D`, where `DLE = 0x10`, `STX = 0x02`, `ETX = 0x03`, perform byte stuffing and draw the complete transmitted frame. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept (2 marks):**
Character-oriented framing uses special bytes as **sentinels** to mark frame boundaries (e.g., `STX` for start, `ETX` for end) and an **escape character** (`DLE`). To maintain transparency, if the payload contains the `DLE` byte or the `STX`/`ETX` bytes, an extra `DLE` is inserted before each occurrence.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Given data (1 mark):**
Data bytes (in hex): `0x41, 0x42, 0x10, 0x43, 0x03, 0x44`

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply byte stuffing (3 marks):**

| Step | Byte | Action | Stuffed Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `0x41` | Data, no change | `0x41` |
| 2 | `0x42` | Data, no change | `0x41, 0x42` |
| 3 | `0x10` | `DLE` ŌåÆ stuff extra `DLE` | `0x41, 0x42, 0x10, 0x10` |
| 4 | `0x43` | Data | `0x41, 0x42, 0x10, 0x10, 0x43` |
| 5 | `0x03` | `ETX` ŌåÆ stuff `DLE` before | `0x41, 0x42, 0x10, 0x10, 0x43, 0x10, 0x03` |
| 6 | `0x44` | Data | `... 0x44` |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Complete transmitted frame (1 mark):**

```
| 0x10, 0x02 | 0x41, 0x42, 0x10, 0x10, 0x43, 0x10, 0x03, 0x44 | 0x10, 0x03 |
   (DLE STX)              (Stuffed Payload)                       (DLE ETX)
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing stuffing direction:** Many students "stuff" the flag itself. **Never stuff the flag ŌĆö only the data between flags is stuffed.**
> 2. **Wrong count threshold:** The rule is **5** consecutive `1`s, not 6 or 4. Memorize: "After 5 ones, stuff a zero."
> 3. **Forgetting the closing flag:** Frames have **both** a start and an end flag. The receiver uses the closing flag to know the frame is complete.
> 4. **Mixing character and bit stuffing:** Do not apply byte stuffing to a bit-stuffing problem (or vice-versa). Identify the framing type from the question.
> 5. **Omitting the FCS field:** Framing includes a trailer (FCS) for error detection. A complete frame has header + payload + trailer.
> 6. **In byte stuffing, forgetting to escape both `DLE` and the delimiter bytes.** A common pitfall: students escape `DLE` but forget to escape `STX` and `ETX` if they appear in the data.
> 7. **Inefficiency calculation error:** Overhead is the **added** bits, not the total. Use `(Stuffed - Original) / Original ├Ś 100%`, not the stuffed length.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the exam.**

### Key Concepts to Master
- [ ] Framing operates at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model.
- [ ] A frame consists of **Header + Payload + Trailer**.
- [ ] The **payload** carries the network-layer packet; the **header/trailer** are link-layer overhead.
- [ ] Three framing methods: **Character-oriented (byte stuffing)**, **Bit-oriented (bit stuffing)**, and **Physical-layer coding violations**.

### Bit Stuffing ŌĆö The Rules
- [ ] Flag pattern: `01111110` (hex `0x7E`).
- [ ] **After every 5 consecutive 1s in the data, insert a 0.**
- [ ] At the receiver: **after 5 ones, if next bit is 0, discard it; if next bit is 1, frame ends.**
- [ ] Worst-case overhead: **20%** (when all data bits are `1`).
- [ ] Used in **HDLC**, **PPP**, **SDLC**.

### Byte Stuffing ŌĆö The Rules
- [ ] Uses sentinel bytes (`STX`, `ETX`) and an escape character (`DLE`).
- [ ] **Any DLE byte in data is doubled (escaped).**
- [ ] **Any STX/ETX byte in data must also be escaped with DLE.**
- [ ] Used in legacy protocols (e.g., **BSC**, **DDCMP**).

### HDLC Frame Structure
- [ ] Fields in order: `Flag | Address | Control | Information | FCS | Flag`.
- [ ] Three frame types: **I-Frame** (data), **S-Frame** (supervisory), **U-Frame** (unnumbered).
- [ ] Standard address `11111111` = broadcast.
- [ ] FCS uses **CRC-CCITT (16-bit)** or **CRC-32**.

### Numerical / Formula Quick-Ref
- [ ] **Efficiency** $\eta = L_{payload} / (L_{payload} + L_{overhead})$
- [ ] **Worst-case bit-stuffing overhead** = 20%.
- [ ] **Worst-case byte-stuffing overhead** = 100% (pathological).
- [ ] **Ethernet MTU** = 1500 bytes; **Max frame** = 1518 bytes; **Min frame** = 64 bytes.

### Exam Strategy
- [ ] In bit-stuffing problems, always **clearly separate** the flag and the data; show stuffing only on the data.
- [ ] In de-stuffing problems, show the **running counter** of `1`s in your working.
- [ ] Always state the **rule** ("after 5 ones...") before applying it.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, draw a **labeled diagram** of the frame structure for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Control (Stop and Wait, Sliding Window Flow Control)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Control (Stop and Wait, Sliding Window Flow Control)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Control** is a Data Link Layer function (Layer 2 of the OSI/TCP-IP model) that coordinates the rate of data transmission between a **Sender** and a **Receiver** so that a fast sender does not overwhelm a slow receiver with frames. It ensures that the receiver's buffer does not overflow and that data is delivered reliably, in order, and at a sustainable pace.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCGT302 - Module 2), flow control is studied as a core component of the **Data Link Layer sub-layer (Logical Link Control - LLC)** and is implemented through two principal families of protocols:

1. **Stop-and-Wait Protocol** (Sender transmits one frame, waits for an Acknowledgment before sending the next).
2. **Sliding Window Protocol** (Sender transmits multiple frames within a window before needing an Acknowledgment).

**Cross-Layer Mapping (KTU CO alignment):**

| OSI Layer | Sub-Layer / Function | Flow Control Relevance |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Application | HTTP, FTP | Sets data rate demand |
| Transport | TCP (Sliding Window) | End-to-End Flow Control |
| **Data Link** | **LLC - Flow Control** | **Hop-by-Hop Flow Control (Module 2 focus)** |
| Physical | Bits, Signals | Raw channel capacity |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Tea Cup Filling" Scenario**
> Imagine a waiter (Sender) pouring tea into a small cup (Receiver's buffer). If the waiter pours the entire kettle at once, the cup overflows. Instead, the waiter pours **one sip at a time**, waits for the customer to drink, and then pours the next sip. That is **Stop-and-Wait**.
> Now imagine a *large* teapot with a **tray of cups**. The waiter fills several cups, places the tray on the table, and only refills the tray after the customer confirms finishing some cups. That is **Sliding Window** ŌĆö multiple frames in flight at once, with a moving "window" of credit.

- A **frame** is a fixed-size packet at the Data Link Layer.
- An **ACK (Acknowledgment)** is a tiny control frame from receiver ŌåÆ sender confirming receipt.
- A **NAK (Negative Acknowledgment)** indicates a frame was corrupted or lost.
- The **propagation delay (Tp)** is the time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver.
- The **transmission delay (Tt)** is the time to push all bits of a frame onto the wire.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Bandwidth (B):** bits per second (**bps**) ŌĆö the channel's capacity.
- **Propagation Delay (Tp):** seconds ŌĆö transit time of one bit across the medium.
- **Transmission Delay (Tt):** seconds ŌĆö time to emit the entire frame.
- **Round Trip Time (RTT):** seconds ŌĆö total time for a frame to reach the receiver **and** its ACK to return.
- **Frame Length (L):** bits.
- **Channel Utilization / Efficiency (╬Ę):** a dimensionless ratio between 0 and 1 (or 0% to 100%).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö Stop and Wait Protocol

### Working Steps (Bullet-Form Logic)

1. The sender transmits **one data frame** of length *L* bits to the receiver.
2. The sender **stops** and waits for an Acknowledgment (ACK) from the receiver.
3. Upon successful receipt, the receiver sends an ACK back.
4. Only after receiving the ACK does the sender transmit the **next** frame.
5. If the sender times out before receiving an ACK, it **retransmits** the same frame (this is *ARQ ŌĆö Automatic Repeat reQuest*).
6. Sequence numbers (typically 0 and 1) are used to distinguish a fresh frame from a retransmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does Stop-and-Wait waste bandwidth?**
> Because the channel is **idle** for the entire Round Trip Time (RTT) after each frame. For high-bandwidth, long-distance links (where RTT >> Tt), utilization drops drastically. This is the *bandwidth-delay product problem* that Sliding Window solves.

### Time-Line Diagram (Textual Trace)

```
Sender  : |---Tt---|             |---Tt---|             |---Tt---|
                 \---Tp---/\---Ta---/      \---Tp---/\---Ta---/
Receiver:                  |---ACK---|              |---ACK---|
```

Total cycle time per frame: **Ttotal = Tt + 2Tp + Ta**

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö Sliding Window Protocol

### Core Idea

> [!NOTE]
> The sender is allowed to transmit up to **W** (window size) frames **without** waiting for an ACK. As ACKs arrive, the window **slides forward** in the sequence-number space, granting the sender new "credits" to send more frames.

### Three Variants Studied in KTU Module 2

1. **One-Bit Sliding Window:** Window size W = 1 (functionally identical to Stop-and-Wait but with explicit sequence numbers 0/1).
2. **Go-Back-N (GBN):** On error, sender retransmits the erroneous frame **and all subsequent frames**. Receiver window size = 1.
3. **Selective Repeat (SR):** On error, sender retransmits **only** the lost frame. Receiver window size = W.

### Sequence Number Bit Constraint (Why it matters)

- If sequence numbers use *k* bits, the range is $0$ to $2^k - 1$.
- **Go-Back-N** requires $W \leq 2^k - 1$ (otherwise old frames are mistaken for new ones).
- **Selective Repeat** requires $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ (a stricter constraint due to bidirectional sliding on both sender and receiver sides).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table ŌĆö it carries 80% of KTU numerical questions in Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Transmission Delay | $T_t = L / B$ | L = frame length (bits), B = bandwidth (bps) | $T_t > 0$ |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $T_p = d / v$ | d = link distance (m), v = signal velocity (m/s) | $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber |
| 3 | Stop-and-Wait Total Cycle | $T_{total} = T_t + 2T_p + T_a$ | $T_a$ = ACK transmission time | $T_a \ll T_t$ (often neglected) |
| 4 | Bandwidth-Delay Parameter | $a = T_p / T_t$ | dimensionless | a = 1 for "balanced" link |
| 5 | Stop-and-Wait Efficiency | $\eta_{SW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | dimensionless | $0 < \eta_{SW} \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Stop-and-Wait Channel Utilization | $U_{SW} = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$ | dimensionless | Neglects ACK time |
| 7 | Sliding Window Efficiency | $\eta_{SWD} = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ | when $W < 1 + 2a$ | otherwise $\eta_{SWD} = 1$ |
| 8 | Sliding Window (Cap-limited) | $\eta_{SWD} = \dfrac{W \cdot T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$ | when $W \leq 1 + 2a$ | $\eta_{SWD} \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Go-Back-N Window Constraint | $W \leq 2^k - 1$ | k = sequence number bits | Strict inequality |
| 10 | Selective Repeat Window Constraint | $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ | k = sequence number bits | Strict inequality |
| 11 | Maximum Throughput (SW) | $\text{Throughput} = B \cdot \eta_{SW}$ | bits per second | $B \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ |
| 12 | Maximum Throughput (Sliding) | $\text{Throughput} = B \cdot \dfrac{W}{1+2a}$ | bits per second | capped at $B$ |

> **Notation Note:** In the table above, any absolute value / magnitude uses `\vert` form in display equations to preserve the LaTeX table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TCP (Transport Layer)** uses a variant of **Sliding Window** for end-to-end flow control, where the receiver advertises a "Window Size" in every ACK.
- **Data Link Layer HDLC, PPP, and 802.11 Wi-Fi** all implement **Go-Back-N** or **Selective Repeat** at the link layer.
- **High-speed interplanetary communication (NASA DSN)** uses **Selective Repeat** because the RTT is enormous and bandwidth is precious ŌĆö every dropped frame must be individually retransmitted to avoid wasting hours of retransmission.
- **Embedded sensor networks (IoT)** often use **Stop-and-Wait** because of memory constraints and ultra-low bandwidth (kbps).
---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stop-and-Wait Efficiency (Exhaustive, No Steps Skipped)

**Goal:** Find the channel utilization $\eta$ for Stop-and-Wait, where utilization is defined as the *fraction of total time used productively* (i.e., transmitting payload bits).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define Transmission Delay

The time taken by the sender to push all $L$ bits of a frame onto the channel is:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{B}
$$

*Justification:* If the channel carries $B$ bits per second, then $L$ bits take $L / B$ seconds. This is a direct application of rate ├Ś time = amount.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Define Propagation Delay

A bit entering the channel at time $t=0$ reaches the receiver at time $t = T_p$, where:

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v}
$$

*Justification:* Electromagnetic waves in copper/fiber travel at approximately $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Distance $d$ divided by speed $v$ gives the travel time.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Define Total Cycle Time

The sender finishes transmitting the frame at $t = T_t$, then waits for propagation ($T_p$), then waits for the receiver to process and emit the ACK (assume instantaneous or $= T_a$, the ACK transmission time), then the ACK propagates back ($T_p$):

$$
T_{cycle} = T_t + T_p + T_a + T_p = T_t + 2T_p + T_a
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Simplification (Neglect ACK Time)

For most analysis, the ACK is *tiny* ($T_a \to 0$) because it carries very few bits. Then:

$$
T_{cycle} \approx T_t + 2T_p
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Define Efficiency

Efficiency is the ratio of *productive time* (sending the actual data frame) to *total cycle time*:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Productive Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Introduce the Bandwidth-Delay Product Parameter $a$

Let:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}
$$

Therefore $T_p = a \cdot T_t$. Substituting:

$$
\eta = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2(a \cdot T_t)} = \frac{T_t}{T_t (1 + 2a)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\eta_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}, \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{B \cdot d}{v \cdot L}}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:*
- When $a \to 0$ (short, fast link), $\eta \to 1$ (perfect utilization).
- When $a \to \infty$ (long, slow link), $\eta \to 0$ (terrible ŌĆö channel sits idle).
- For a *typical* Internet link with $a \approx 10$ to $1000$, Stop-and-Wait efficiency can be below **0.1%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Sliding Window Efficiency

**Goal:** Find the maximum efficiency when the sender can have $W$ outstanding (un-ACKed) frames in flight.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Re-express Total Cycle

In Sliding Window, the first frame's ACK arrives at the sender at $t = T_t + 2T_p$. The sender needs only this ACK to "slide" the window and send the next frame. So the *time per frame* under continuous transmission is:

$$
T_{per\_frame} = \frac{T_t + 2T_p}{W} = \frac{T_t(1 + 2a)}{W}
$$

*Justification:* Over a period of $T_t + 2T_p$ seconds, the sender can push $W$ frames, so the per-frame effective time is that period divided by $W$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Case A: Window Large Enough to Fill the Pipe

If $W \geq 1 + 2a$, the sender can fill the channel completely and never be idle. Then:

$$
\eta_{SWD} = 1 \quad \text{(100% utilization, the channel is the bottleneck, not the protocol)}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Case B: Window Smaller Than the Bandwidth-Delay Product

If $W < 1 + 2a$, the sender runs out of "credits" and must wait. Then the per-frame time is $(T_t + 2T_p)/W$ but at least $T_t$ per frame:

$$
\eta_{SWD} = \frac{T_t}{T_{per\_frame}} = \frac{T_t}{(T_t + 2T_p)/W} = \frac{W \cdot T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \frac{W}{1 + 2a}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Final Unified Form

$$
\boxed{\eta_{SWD} = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{W}{1 + 2a}\right)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **[Example - KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> Consider a satellite link at $B = 1$ Mbps with one-way propagation delay $T_p = 250$ ms. Frame length $L = 1000$ bits. The ACK length is negligible.
> **(a)** Compute the Stop-and-Wait efficiency.
> **(b)** Find the minimum window size $W$ for a Sliding Window protocol to achieve **at least 80% efficiency**.
> **(c)** Determine the maximum throughput in both cases.

### Solution (a) ŌĆö Stop-and-Wait Efficiency

Given:
- $B = 10^6$ bps
- $T_p = 0.25$ s
- $L = 1000$ bits

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3}\text{ s} = 1\text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{250\text{ ms}}{1\text{ ms}} = 250
$$

$$
\eta_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 500} = \frac{1}{501} \approx 1.996 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.1996\%
$$

*Interpretation:* The channel is **idle 99.8% of the time**. Catastrophic waste.

### Solution (b) ŌĆö Minimum Window Size for $\eta \geq 0.8$

We require:

$$
\frac{W}{1 + 2a} \geq 0.8
$$

$$
W \geq 0.8 \times (1 + 2a) = 0.8 \times 501 = 400.8
$$

Since $W$ must be an integer:

$$
\boxed{W_{min} = 401}
$$

### Solution (c) ŌĆö Maximum Throughput

Stop-and-Wait throughput:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{SW} = B \cdot \eta_{SW} = 10^6 \times 1.996 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1996\text{ bps} \approx 2\text{ kbps}
$$

Sliding Window (with $W = 401$) throughput:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{SWD} = B \cdot 0.8 = 0.8 \times 10^6 = 800{,}000\text{ bps} = 800\text{ kbps}
$$

*This is a **400├Ś improvement** ŌĆö exactly why Sliding Window is used in practice.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison Table for Exam Revision

| Property | Stop-and-Wait | 1-Bit Sliding | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|----------|---------------|---------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Window Size (Sender) | 1 | 1 | W | W |
| Window Size (Receiver) | 1 | 1 | 1 | W |
| Efficiency (typical) | $\dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ | $\dfrac{W}{1+2a}$ | $\dfrac{W}{1+2a}$ |
| Sequence Number Bits | 1 | 1 | k where $W \leq 2^k - 1$ | k where $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ |
| On Frame Loss | Retransmit same | Retransmit same | Retransmit that & all after | Retransmit only that |
| Receiver Complexity | Very Low | Very Low | Low | High (buffer required) |
| Bandwidth Waste on Error | 1 frame | 1 frame | Up to W frames | 1 frame |
| Order-of-arrival requirement | Trivial | Trivial | In-order | Out-of-order OK |
| KTU Mark Weightage | High (theory) | Medium | High (theory + numerical) | High (numerical) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid ŌĆö Stop-and-Wait Timeline Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant C as Channel
    participant R as Receiver

    Note over S,R: Initial state - Sender has Frame 0 ready
    S->>C: Transmit Frame 0 (duration Tt)
    C->>R: Frame 0 arrives (after Tp)
    R->>C: Send ACK 0 (after processing)
    C->>S: ACK 0 arrives (after Tp)
    Note over S: Sender idle from end of Tt to ACK arrival
    S->>C: Transmit Frame 1 (duration Tt)
    C->>R: Frame 1 arrives
    R->>C: Send ACK 1
    C->>S: ACK 1 arrives
    Note over S,R: Cycle repeats - channel is mostly idle
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the long horizontal gap between the sender finishing Frame 0 and the ACK returning ŌĆö that is the *idle time* Tt + 2Tp being wasted. The channel is silent during this period in Stop-and-Wait.

## 4.2 Mermaid ŌĆö Sliding Window Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["SENDER WINDOW (size W=4)"]
        nodeA["Frame 0 (SENT)"]
        nodeB["Frame 1 (SENT)"]
        nodeC["Frame 2 (SENT)"]
        nodeD["Frame 3 (READY)"]
    end
    subgraph P1["CHANNEL PIPELINE"]
        nodeE["Bits in flight - bandwidth-delay product"]
    end
    subgraph R1["RECEIVER WINDOW (size W)"]
        nodeF["Frame 0 (ACKED)"]
        nodeG["Frame 1 (ACKED)"]
        nodeH["Frame 2 (BUFFERED)"]
        nodeI["Frame 3 (BUFFERED)"]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF -.ACK 0.-> nodeD
    nodeG -.ACK 1.-> nodeD
```

**Reading the diagram:** Multiple frames are simultaneously in flight in the channel. As ACK 0 and ACK 1 arrive, the sender's window slides right, granting credits to send Frame 4, 5, etc. The channel is *always* busy ŌĆö that is the key efficiency win.

## 4.3 Mermaid ŌĆö Go-Back-N Error Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Sender transmits Frame 0 to Frame 3]) --> nodeTx0
    nodeTx0["Frame 0 transmitted"] --> nodeTx1["Frame 1 transmitted"]
    nodeTx1 --> nodeTx2["Frame 2 transmitted - LOST"]
    nodeTx2 --> nodeTx3["Frame 3 transmitted - discarded by receiver"]
    nodeTx3 --> nodeTimeout{Timer expires for Frame 2}
    nodeTimeout --> nodeReTx["Retransmit Frame 2"]
    nodeReTx --> nodeReTx2["Retransmit Frame 3"]
    nodeReTx2 --> nodeReTx3["Retransmit Frame 4"]
    nodeReTx3 --> nodeDone([Receiver accepts all in order])
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice that Go-Back-N retransmits the lost frame *and all subsequent* frames, even if they were received correctly. This is a tradeoff: simpler receiver logic (no out-of-order buffer) at the cost of bandwidth.

## 4.4 Mermaid ŌĆö Selective Repeat Error Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart2([Sender transmits Frame 0 to Frame 3]) --> nodeRTx0["Frame 0 received OK"]
    nodeRTx0 --> nodeRTx1["Frame 1 received OK"]
    nodeRTx1 --> nodeRTx2["Frame 2 LOST"]
    nodeRTx2 --> nodeRTx3["Frame 3 received OK and BUFFERED out-of-order"]
    nodeRTx3 --> nodeNAK["Receiver sends NAK 2"]
    nodeNAK --> nodeSelReTx["Sender retransmits ONLY Frame 2"]
    nodeSelReTx --> nodeReorder["Receiver delivers Frame 2, 3 in correct order"]
    nodeReorder --> nodeDone2([No wastage of Frame 3])
```

**Reading the diagram:** Selective Repeat retransmits *only* the lost frame, but the receiver must maintain a buffer for out-of-order delivery and a complex reordering mechanism. This is the efficiency vs. complexity tradeoff.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define flow control. Why is it needed at the Data Link Layer when the Transport Layer (TCP) already performs flow control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Flow control is the technique of regulating the rate of data transmission from sender to receiver so that the receiver's buffer does not overflow, ensuring reliable in-order delivery.
It is needed at the Data Link Layer because:
1. **Hop-by-hop granularity:** Data Link flow control works between *adjacent* nodes (e.g., router-to-router), whereas TCP operates end-to-end.
2. **Different timescales:** Link errors (noise, collision) are local and need fast local recovery ŌĆö TCP's RTT may be too long.
3. **Heterogeneous links:** Each hop may have a different bandwidth and buffer size; TCP cannot see these.

> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols in terms of efficiency, receiver complexity, and sequence number requirements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Efficiency | Lower (retransmits multiple frames) | Higher (retransmits only 1) |
| Receiver Complexity | Low (window size 1, no buffer) | High (must buffer out-of-order frames) |
| Sequence Number Bits (k) | Needs $W \leq 2^k - 1$ | Needs $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ |
| On Frame Loss | Retransmit N and all after | Retransmit only the lost frame |

*(Award 1 mark for each correct row, 3 marks total.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Likely Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Stop-and-Wait protocol** with a neat timing diagram. Derive an expression for its channel utilization. What is the maximum throughput achievable if the link has bandwidth $B = 4$ Mbps, propagation delay $T_p = 20$ ms, and frame length $L = 1000$ bits?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the same link parameters, calculate the minimum window size $W$ required for a Sliding Window protocol to achieve 100% efficiency. How many sequence number bits $k$ are needed for Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat under this configuration?

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Stop-and-Wait Explanation + Derivation + Numerical**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Protocol description (2 marks):**
- Sender transmits **one frame**, then waits for ACK.
- On ACK receipt, sender transmits the next frame.
- On timeout, sender retransmits the same frame.
- Sequence numbers alternate between 0 and 1 to disambiguate original vs. retransmission.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Timing diagram (2 marks):**

```
Sender:  |-----Tt-----|               |-----Tt-----|
                  \----Tp----/\---Ta---/     \----Tp---/
Receiver:                     |----ACK---|          |----ACK---|
```

Total cycle: $T_{cycle} = T_t + 2T_p + T_a \approx T_t + 2T_p$ (since $T_a$ is negligible for tiny ACKs).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Derivation of utilization (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{SW} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}, \quad a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Numerical computation (1 mark):**

Given $B = 4 \times 10^6$ bps, $T_p = 20$ ms $= 0.02$ s, $L = 1000$ bits.

$$
T_t = \frac{1000}{4 \times 10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s} = 0.25\text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20}{0.25} = 80
$$

$$
\eta_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(80)} = \frac{1}{161} \approx 0.00621 = 0.621\%
$$

Maximum throughput:

$$
\text{Throughput} = B \cdot \eta = 4 \times 10^6 \times 0.00621 \approx 24{,}845\text{ bps} \approx 24.85\text{ kbps}
$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Sliding Window Sizing for 100% Efficiency**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Condition for 100% efficiency (2 marks):**
We require $W \geq 1 + 2a$:

$$
W \geq 1 + 2(80) = 1 + 160 = 161
$$

Therefore **minimum $W = 161$**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sequence number bits for Go-Back-N (2 marks):**
We require $W \leq 2^k - 1$, so $2^k - 1 \geq 161$, hence $2^k \geq 162$.

- $2^7 = 128$ (not enough)
- $2^8 = 256$ (sufficient)

**Sequence number bits for Go-Back-N: $k = 8$.**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sequence number bits for Selective Repeat (3 marks):**
We require $W \leq 2^{k-1}$, so $2^{k-1} \geq 161$, hence $k - 1 \geq 8$ (since $2^7 = 128 < 161 < 256 = 2^8$).

- $2^7 = 128$ (insufficient)
- $2^8 = 256$ (sufficient)

So $k - 1 = 8$, meaning **$k = 9$ sequence number bits** for Selective Repeat.

This illustrates a key principle: Selective Repeat needs **one extra bit** compared to Go-Back-N for the same window size.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Likely Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Sliding Window Protocol**. Distinguish clearly between One-Bit Sliding Window, Go-Back-N, and Selective Repeat. Use diagrams where appropriate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A channel has bandwidth $B = 1$ Mbps and one-way propagation delay $T_p = 100$ ms. Frame length $L = 2000$ bits. ACK transmission time is negligible.
- (i) Compute the efficiency of Stop-and-Wait.
- (ii) Compute the efficiency of a Sliding Window protocol with $W = 51$.
- (iii) If the required efficiency is at least 60%, find the minimum window size.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Sliding Window Description (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Sliding Window protocol allows the sender to transmit multiple frames (up to window size $W$) before needing an ACK. The window "slides" forward as ACKs are received, freeing up sequence numbers for new frames.

**Three variants (6 marks):**

1. **One-Bit Sliding Window** (2 marks):
   - Window size = 1 on both sender and receiver.
   - Sequence numbers: 0 and 1 (alternating).
   - Functionally similar to Stop-and-Wait but uses explicit sequence numbers.
   - Prevents accidental acceptance of a duplicate frame.

2. **Go-Back-N** (2 marks):
   - Sender window size = $W$ (e.g., up to 7 or 127).
   - Receiver window size = 1.
   - On error, sender retransmits the lost frame and *all* frames sent after it.
   - Receiver discards out-of-order frames.
   - Constraint: $W \leq 2^k - 1$.

3. **Selective Repeat** (2 marks):
   - Both sender and receiver have window size $W$.
   - Receiver *accepts and buffers* out-of-order frames.
   - On error, only the lost frame is retransmitted.
   - Receiver delivers frames to upper layer in correct order using a buffer.
   - Constraint: $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ (stricter, due to two-way sliding).

**Comparison diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to Section 3.4 matrix above for the examiner's reference.)

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

Given: $B = 10^6$ bps, $T_p = 100$ ms $= 0.1$ s, $L = 2000$ bits.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $T_t$ (1 mark):**

$$
T_t = \frac{2000}{10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s} = 2\text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $a$ (1 mark):**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{100}{2} = 50
$$

**(i) Stop-and-Wait efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{SW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 100} = \frac{1}{101} \approx 0.0099 = 0.99\%
$$

**(ii) Sliding Window efficiency with W = 51 (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{SWD} = \frac{W}{1 + 2a} = \frac{51}{101} \approx 0.505 = 50.5\%
$$

**(iii) Minimum W for $\eta \geq 60\%$ (2 marks):**

$$
W \geq 0.6 \times (1 + 2a) = 0.6 \times 101 = 60.6
$$

Therefore **minimum $W = 61$**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Flow Control questions (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster (ŌĆō2 marks):** Mixing ms, s, ╬╝s when computing $T_t$ and $T_p$. *Always convert to the same unit first.*
> 2. **Forgetting the $1+2a$ Denominator (ŌĆō2 marks):** Writing $\eta = W / (2a)$ instead of $W / (1+2a)$. The "+1" comes from the frame's own transmission time.
> 3. **Selective Repeat vs. Go-Back-N Confusion (ŌĆō2 marks):** The window constraint differs! SR needs $W \leq 2^{k-1}$, GBN needs $W \leq 2^k - 1$. Many students write both as $2^k - 1$.
> 4. **Forgetting to Neglect ACK Time (ŌĆō1 mark):** If the problem says "negligible ACK", do not include $T_a$. If unspecified, mention the assumption.
> 5. **Skipping the Timing Diagram (ŌĆō2 marks):** For 7-mark "explain" questions, the diagram is **mandatory**. Text alone loses at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Not Rounding W Up (ŌĆō1 mark):** $W$ must be an integer and must satisfy the inequality *strictly*. If the calculation gives 60.6, write $W = 61$, not 60.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Flow Control (Module 2)**

- Ō£ģ **Flow Control** lives at the **Data Link Layer (LLC sub-layer)** and regulates the *sender's rate* to match the *receiver's buffer capacity*.
- Ō£ģ **Stop-and-Wait** sends 1 frame, waits for 1 ACK; sequence number alternates 0/1.
- Ō£ģ **Stop-and-Wait Efficiency** = $\dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = T_p / T_t$.
- Ō£ģ **Total Cycle Time** = $T_t + 2T_p + T_a$ (often $T_a$ is neglected).
- Ō£ģ **Sliding Window** allows $W$ outstanding (un-ACKed) frames; window slides as ACKs arrive.
- Ō£ģ **Sliding Window Efficiency** = $\dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ (capped at 1 if $W \geq 1 + 2a$).
- Ō£ģ **Go-Back-N (GBN):** Retransmits lost frame **and all subsequent**; receiver window = 1; constraint $W \leq 2^k - 1$.
- Ō£ģ **Selective Repeat (SR):** Retransmits **only** the lost frame; receiver window = $W$; constraint $W \leq 2^{k-1}$ (stricter).
- Ō£ģ **SR needs one more sequence bit** than GBN for the same window size.
- Ō£ģ **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $B \times T_p$ (in bits) tells you the *pipe capacity* ŌĆö choose $W$ to be at least this big.
- Ō£ģ **Throughput** = Bandwidth ├Ś Efficiency; measure in bps.
- Ō£ģ For high-$a$ links (satellite, intercontinental), **Stop-and-Wait is unusable**; use Sliding Window with $W \geq 1 + 2a$.
- Ō£ģ **Common exam traps:** unit conversion, "1+2a" not "2a", SR vs. GBN constraint, integer rounding of $W$.
- Ō£ģ **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** CO2 ŌĆö "Apply error detection and flow control mechanisms at the Data Link Layer." (Blooms Level: Apply / Analyze.)

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes ŌĆö Flow Control (Stop and Wait, Sliding Window Flow Control)**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) Board Examination Standards.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Error Control?

**Error Control** is the set of mechanisms implemented at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model to ensure that data transmitted over a physical medium arrives at the destination **without corruption, duplication, loss, or out-of-order delivery**. It is broadly classified into two sub-domains:

1. **Error Detection & Correction** ŌĆö Identifying whether bits have flipped during transmission, and in some cases, automatically restoring the original bits.
2. **Flow & Error Recovery (ARQ)** ŌĆö Retransmitting lost or corrupted frames using acknowledgments and timers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Error control is a Data Link Layer function that detects and corrects errors introduced by the physical transmission medium (noise, attenuation, interference) using redundancy bits, mathematical coding techniques, and retransmission protocols.*

---

## 1.2 Types of Errors

### A. Single-Bit Error
Only **one bit** in a given data unit (frame) is corrupted.
- Occurs rarely in practice (e.g., high-quality channels, short distances).
- A frame of $n$ bits has $n$ possible single-bit error positions.

### B. Burst Error
Two or more consecutive bits in a frame are corrupted.
- Length of burst: measured from the first corrupted bit to the last corrupted bit.
- A burst of length $L$ may affect up to $L$ bits, but errors are *consecutive*.
- Burst errors are **far more common** in real networks (impulse noise, fading).

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability Insight:** The probability of a single-bit error in a burst-prone channel is much lower than the probability that a burst error occurs. Hence, the Hamming distance of codes must be designed against **burst errors**, not single-bit errors.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Postal Letter System

Imagine you are posting a handwritten letter across a city:

| Real-World Analogy | Computer Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Writing a letter | Creating a data frame |
| Smudges/ink spill during transit | Bit errors (single/burst) |
| Writing a **secret number code** at the end of the letter (like a checksum) | **Error Detection Code** |
| Reading the code, and if wrong, asking for a re-send | **ARQ Retransmission** |
| Writing the letter in **redundant shorthand** (so the reader can guess missing words) | **Error Correction Code (Hamming)** |
| Sealing with a wax stamp (which the receiver checks for tampering) | **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)** |

So, just like humans use **checksums, redundancy, and re-confirmation** to ensure letters arrive correctly, networks use **parity, CRC, Hamming codes, and ARQ protocols** to guarantee reliable delivery.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Error Control Techniques

**Error Control**
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ **Error Detection (Sender adds redundancy ŌåÆ Receiver validates)**
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Parity Check (Simple / 2D)
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Checksum (Internet Checksum)
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ CRC ŌĆö Cyclic Redundancy Check
Ōöé
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ **Error Correction (Receiver identifies AND fixes the bit)**
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Hamming Code (Single-bit correction / SEC)
Ōöé
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ **ARQ ŌĆö Automatic Repeat Request (Retransmission based)**
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Stop-and-Wait ARQ
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Go-Back-N ARQ
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Selective Repeat ARQ

> [!TIP]
> **Key Exam Tip:** Detection is **cheaper and simpler** than correction. Most networks (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, TCP) prefer detection + retransmission, while correction is reserved for **real-time, latency-sensitive** applications (deep-space communication, satellite TV).

---

## 1.5 Coding Terms You Must Know

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Codeword** | The transmitted $n$-bit unit: $n = m + r$, where $m$ = message bits, $r$ = redundant bits. |
| **Hamming Distance ($d(x,y)$)** | Number of bit positions in which two codewords of equal length differ. |
| **Minimum Hamming Distance ($d_{min}$)** | The smallest Hamming distance between any two valid codewords in a codebook. |
| **Code Rate** | Efficiency $= m/n$ |
| **Error-Detecting Capability** | A code can detect up to $d_{min} - 1$ errors. |
| **Error-Correcting Capability** | A code can correct up to $\lfloor (d_{min} - 1)/2 \rfloor$ errors. |
