**Conclusion:** The LLC sublayer contributes a fixed **3-byte overhead** per frame, which is small relative to the payload.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of LLC Service Types

> [!NOTE]
> **The following analytical matrix is critical for KTU 14-mark questions on LLC services.**

| Parameter | **Type 1** | **Type 2** | **Type 3** |
|-----------|------------|------------|------------|
| **Connection** | Connectionless | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| **Acknowledgement** | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes (per frame) | Ō£ģ Yes (per data frame) |
| **Flow Control** | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Sliding Window | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Stop-and-wait |
| **Error Recovery** | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Retransmission | Ō£ģ Retransmission on timeout |
| **Setup Phase** | ŌØī None | Ō£ģ SABME / UA exchange | ŌØī None |
| **Teardown Phase** | ŌØī None | Ō£ģ DISC / UA exchange | ŌØī None |
| **Control Field** | U-Format | I + S + U-Format | U-Format (with ACK) |
| **Sequence Numbers** | ŌØī Not used | Ō£ģ N(S) and N(R) | ŌØī Not used |
| **Typical Use** | IP, ARP, DHCP | SNA, NETBEUI, legacy | Token Bus, factory LAN |
| **Overhead** | Lowest (3 bytes) | Highest (3ŌĆō4 bytes + state) | Medium (3 bytes + ACK) |
| **Throughput** | High (no ACK wait) | Medium (ACK overhead) | Medium (one-ACK-per-frame) |
| **Reliability** | Unreliable | Highly reliable | Reliable |

### 3.3 Bit-Level Decoding Example (Optional Practice)

> **Problem:** An LLC PDU is observed with the following byte values (hex): `06 06 03`.
>
> Decode the PDU.

**Solution:**

- **DSAP = 0x06** = `0000 0110` ŌåÆ I/G bit (LSB) = 0 ŌåÆ **Individual DSAP** ŌåÆ **IP protocol** at destination.
- **SSAP = 0x06** = `0000 0110` ŌåÆ C/R bit (LSB) = 0 ŌåÆ **Response PDU** from **IP** source.
- **Control = 0x03** = `0000 0011` ŌåÆ ends in `11` ŌåÆ **U-Format** ŌåÆ modifier bits `000` ŌåÆ **UI (Unnumbered Information)** command.
- **Information Field:** Not present in this capture (Type 1, no data).
- **Interpretation:** This is an **Unnumbered Information** response from IP to IP ŌĆö i.e., a **Type 1 unacknowledged connectionless IP packet**.

**Conclusion:** The frame carries an **IP datagram in connectionless mode** with **no reliability guarantees**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LLC Sublayer in the IEEE 802 Reference Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NL[Network Layer IP] -->|DL UNITDATA request| LLC
    LLC[Logical Link Control IEEE 802.2] -->|MAC Service Primitive| MAC
    MAC[Medium Access Control IEEE 802.3 or 802.11] -->|Bit Service| PHY
    PHY[Physical Layer Ethernet or WiFi]

    LLC -.->|Type 1| T1[Unacknowledged Connectionless]
    LLC -.->|Type 2| T2[Connection Oriented Sliding Window]
    LLC -.->|Type 3| T3[Acknowledged Connectionless]

    style NL fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style LLC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style MAC fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style PHY fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style T1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style T2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style T3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

### 4.2 LLC PDU Frame Format ŌĆö Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DSAP 1 byte] --> B[SSAP 1 byte]
    B --> C[Control 1 or 2 bytes]
    C --> D[Information Variable length]

    A2[DSAP breakdown] --- A
    A2a[I or G bit LSB] --> A
    A2b[Upper layer protocol ID bits 1 to 7] --> A

    B2[SSAP breakdown] --- B
    B2a[C or R bit LSB] --> B
    B2b[Upper layer protocol ID bits 1 to 7] --> B

    C2[Control breakdown] --- C
    C2a[I Format N S and N R] --> C
    C2b[S Format Supervisory] --> C
    C2c[U Format Unnumbered] --> C

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style A2 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C2 fill:#fffde7,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.3 LLC Type 2 Connection State Machine (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Setup: SABME Command Sent
    Setup --> Connected: UA Response Received
    Connected --> DataTransfer: I Format Frames
    DataTransfer --> DataTransfer: ACK via S Format
    DataTransfer --> Connected: All Data Acknowledged
    Connected --> Teardown: DISC Command
    Teardown --> Idle: UA Response
    Connected --> Reset: SABME Received Mid Session
    Reset --> Connected: UA Response
    DataTransfer --> Idle: Connection Failure
    Teardown --> Idle: Normal Disconnect Complete
```

### 4.4 LLC Service Multiplexing via SAPs ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StationHost[Single Network Station]
        IP1[IP Protocol]
        ARP1[ARP Protocol]
        STP1[STP Protocol]
        IP1 --- SAP1[SAP 0x06]
        ARP1 --- SAP2[SAP 0x08]
        STP1 --- SAP3[SAP 0x42]
    end

    SAP1 --> LLCSub[LLC Sublayer]
    SAP2 --> LLCSub
    SAP3 --> LLCSub

    LLCSub --> MACSub[MAC Sublayer]
    MACSub --> PHYLayer[Physical Layer]

    style IP1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style ARP1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style STP1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style SAP1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style SAP2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style SAP3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style LLCSub fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style MACSub fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style PHYLayer fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.5 Flow Diagram ŌĆö LLC Type 1 vs Type 2 vs Type 3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sender[Sender] -->|Type 1 Data| T1Path[No ACK Expected Unreliable]
    Sender -->|Type 2 Setup| T2Setup[SABME]
    T2Setup --> T2UA[UA]
    T2UA --> T2Data[I Format with N S and N R]
    T2Data --> T2SFrame[S Format ACK]
    T2SFrame --> T2Disc[DISC]
    T2Disc --> T2UA2[UA Disconnect]
    Sender -->|Type 3 Data| T3Data[Unnumbered Data]
    T3Data --> T3Ack[Acknowledged Connectionless ACK]
    T3Ack --> Done[Done]

    style T1Path fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style T2Setup fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style T2Data fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style T3Data fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define the Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer. List its three service types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Logical Link Control (LLC)** is the upper sublayer of the Data Link Layer in the IEEE 802 reference model, defined by the **IEEE 802.2** standard. It provides a **medium-independent** interface to the upper (Network) layer, handling **multiplexing, error recovery, and flow control** above the MAC sublayer. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> The three LLC service types are: **(1.5 marks)**
> 1. **Type 1 ŌĆö Unacknowledged Connectionless Service** (no ACK, no flow control).
> 2. **Type 2 ŌĆö Connection-Oriented Service** (ACK, sliding window, error recovery).
> 3. **Type 3 ŌĆö Acknowledged Connectionless Service** (ACK per data frame, no connection setup).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**What is a Service Access Point (SAP)? Mention the special SAP values `0x00`, `0xFF`, and `0x06`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Service Access Point (SAP)** is a logical address used by the LLC sublayer to identify **upper-layer protocols** (e.g., IP, ARP) on a given station. A single physical station can have **multiple SAPs**, one for each upper-layer protocol. **(1 mark)**
>
> Special SAP values: **(2 marks)**
> - **`0x00` ŌĆö Null SAP** ŌåÆ no upper-layer service active.
> - **`0xFF` ŌĆö Global SAP** ŌåÆ broadcast to all upper-layer protocols.
> - **`0x06` ŌĆö IP (Internet Protocol)** ŌåÆ the standard SAP used for IPv4 over IEEE 802 networks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]**

**a)** Explain the **architecture of the IEEE 802 reference model** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the LLC and MAC sublayers. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the **LLC PDU format** in detail. With a diagram, explain the function of **DSAP, SSAP, and Control** fields, including the **I/G and C/R bits**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (a):**

The **IEEE 802 reference model** divides the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model into **two distinct sublayers**:

1. **Logical Link Control (LLC) Sublayer ŌĆö IEEE 802.2** (upper half)
2. **Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer ŌĆö IEEE 802.3/802.11/802.5** (lower half)

**Diagram (ASCII representation):**

```
   [Network Layer - IP, IPX, etc.]
   ==================================
   |   Logical Link Control (LLC)   |  ŌåÉ IEEE 802.2
   ==================================
   |  Medium Access Control (MAC)   |  ŌåÉ IEEE 802.3 / 802.11 / 802.5
   ==================================
   |       Physical Layer           |  ŌåÉ IEEE 802.3 / 802.11
   ==================================
```

**Roles:**

- **LLC Sublayer (1.5 marks):** Provides **multiplexing** of upper-layer protocols via **Service Access Points (SAPs)**; offers **three service classes** (Type 1, 2, 3); performs **flow control** and **error recovery**; and acts as a **convergence layer** that hides MAC-level differences from the upper layer.

- **MAC Sublayer (1.5 marks):** Manages **physical addressing** (MAC addresses, 6 bytes); implements **channel access** mechanisms (CSMA/CD for Ethernet, CSMA/CA for Wi-Fi, Token Passing for Token Ring); handles **framing**, **collision detection/avoidance**, and **MAC-level retransmission**.

**Why split the DLL? (1 mark):** The split allows **multiple MAC protocols** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Token Ring) to share a **single, unified LLC interface** ŌĆö making the upper layers **independent of the physical medium**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The IEEE 802 architecture is the **foundation of modern LANs** and is the reason that the same IP stack runs on Ethernet, Wi-Fi, and other LANs without modification.

**Allocation of marks:**
- 1 mark ŌĆö diagram
- 1.5 marks ŌĆö LLC role
- 1.5 marks ŌĆö MAC role
- 1 mark ŌĆö Why split
- 1 mark ŌĆö architecture explanation
- 1 mark ŌĆö conclusion

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (b):**

The **LLC PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** consists of four fields:

```
| DSAP (1 byte) | SSAP (1 byte) | Control (1 or 2 bytes) | Information (Variable) |
```

**Diagram (Bit-level layout):**

```
    DSAP (8 bits)             SSAP (8 bits)         Control (8 or 16 bits)   Information
| I/G | Address[1-7] | | C/R | Address[1-7] | | Format bits + N(S) + N(R) + P/F | |  Upper-layer payload  |
```

**Field-by-field explanation:**

- **DSAP ŌĆö Destination Service Access Point (1.5 marks):**
  - Identifies the **upper-layer protocol** at the destination.
  - **LSB is the I/G bit:**
    - `I/G = 0` ŌåÆ **Individual DSAP** (one upper-layer entity).
    - `I/G = 1` ŌåÆ **Group DSAP** (multicast to multiple upper-layer entities).
  - Special DSAP values: `0x00` (Null), `0xFF` (Global), `0x06` (IP), `0xAA` (SNAP).

- **SSAP ŌĆö Source Service Access Point (1.5 marks):**
  - Identifies the **upper-layer protocol** at the source.
  - **LSB is the C/R bit:**
    - `C/R = 0` ŌåÆ **Response PDU**.
    - `C/R = 1` ŌåÆ **Command PDU**.

- **Control Field (2 marks):**
  - Three formats: **I-Format** (Information), **S-Format** (Supervisory), **U-Format** (Unnumbered).
  - **I-Format** carries `N(S)`, `N(R)`, and `P/F` bits ŌĆö used in Type 2.
  - **S-Format** carries supervisory commands (RR, RNR, REJ) ŌĆö used in Type 2.
  - **U-Format** carries unnumbered commands (SABME, DISC, UI, ACK) ŌĆö used in Type 1 and Type 3.

- **Information Field (1 mark):** Carries **upper-layer payload** (e.g., IP packet). Variable length; bounded by the MAC's maximum frame size.

**Bit-decoding example (1 mark):**
For DSAP = `0x06` = `0000 0110`, the LSB is `0` ŌåÆ Individual DSAP, IP protocol.

**Conclusion:** The LLC PDU format is the **standardized envelope** that allows the **same LLC service** to be used across **multiple MAC protocols** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Token Ring).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]**

**a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **three types of LLC services** (Type 1, Type 2, Type 3) and their **use cases**. Compare them in a table. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A **500-byte IP packet** is to be transmitted over IEEE 802.3 Ethernet using LLC Type 1 service. Compute the **LLC header size**, the **total frame size on the wire** (including preamble and SFD), and the **LLC efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (a):**

**Type 1 ŌĆö Unacknowledged Connectionless Service (2 marks):**
- No connection setup; no ACK; no flow control; no error recovery.
- PDU format: U-Format.
- Diagram: `Sender ŌåÆ [UI Frame] ŌåÆ Receiver` (no reverse ACK).
- Use case: **IP, ARP, DHCP** ŌĆö traffic that runs over **reliable upper layers** (TCP) or tolerates loss (UDP).

**Type 2 ŌĆö Connection-Oriented Service (2 marks):**
- Full **3-phase handshake**: setup (SABME/UA) ŌåÆ data transfer (I-frames + S-frames ACK) ŌåÆ teardown (DISC/UA).
- Uses **sliding window** with sequence numbers N(S) and N(R).
- Diagram: `A ŌåÆ SABME ŌåÆ B`, `B ŌåÆ UA ŌåÆ A`, `A ŌåÆ I(0,0) ŌåÆ B`, `B ŌåÆ RR(0) ŌåÆ A`, `A ŌåÆ DISC ŌåÆ B`, `B ŌåÆ UA ŌåÆ A`.
- Use case: **SNA, NETBEUI, Token Bus (with reliability)**.

**Type 3 ŌĆö Acknowledged Connectionless Service (1 mark):**
- Each data frame is **individually acknowledged** with a separate ACK frame.
- No connection setup; stop-and-wait per frame.
- Diagram: `Sender ŌåÆ [Data + Token] ŌåÆ Receiver ŌåÆ [ACK + Token] ŌåÆ Sender`.
- Use case: **Token Bus (802.4), factory automation, SCADA**.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|
| Connection | No | Yes | No |
| ACK | No | Yes (continuous) | Yes (per frame) |
| Flow control | No | Sliding window | Stop-and-wait |
| Error recovery | No | Yes (retransmit) | Yes (retransmit on timeout) |
| Use case | IP, ARP | SNA, NETBEUI | Token Bus, SCADA |

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- IP packet length $L = 500$ bytes
- LLC service: **Type 1** (U-Format, 1-byte control)
- IEEE 802.3 Ethernet standard

**Step 1: Compute LLC header size (1 mark):**
For Type 1, the LLC PDU consists of DSAP (1 byte) + SSAP (1 byte) + Control (1 byte):

$$
H_{LLC} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Compute MAC overhead (1 mark):**
For IEEE 802.3, the MAC overhead is:

$$
H_{MAC} = 6 \, (\text{Dest}) + 6 \, (\text{Src}) + 2 \, (\text{Length/Type}) + 4 \, (\text{FCS}) = 18 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Compute PHY overhead (1 mark):**
For IEEE 802.3, the PHY preamble and SFD:

$$
H_{PHY} = 7 \, (\text{Preamble}) + 1 \, (\text{SFD}) = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Compute total bytes on the wire (1.5 marks):**

$$
L_{wire} = H_{PHY} + H_{MAC} + H_{LLC} + L
$$

$$
L_{wire} = 8 + 18 + 3 + 500 = 529 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5: Compute LLC efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta_{LLC} = \frac{L}{L_{wire}} \times 100\% = \frac{500}{529} \times 100\% \approx 94.52\%
$$

**Final Answer:**
- LLC header size = **3 bytes**.
- Total bytes on the wire = **529 bytes**.
- LLC efficiency Ōēł **94.52%**.

**Interpretation:** About **5.48%** of the bandwidth is consumed by **overhead** (LLC + MAC + PHY), which is highly efficient for LAN transmission.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students marks on LLC questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LLC with MAC** ŌĆö students often describe MAC functions when asked about LLC. Remember: **LLC = logical (SAPs, services); MAC = physical (addresses, channel access).**
> 2. **Forgetting the I/G and C/R bits** ŌĆö these are 2 mandatory marks in any LLC PDU format question.
> 3. **Mixing up Type 1 / Type 2 / Type 3** ŌĆö Type 1 = no ACK; Type 2 = connection-oriented; Type 3 = ACK without connection. Get these backwards and lose 5ŌĆō7 marks.
> 4. **Using `0xFF` as the only "special" SAP** ŌĆö `0x00` (Null), `0x06` (IP), `0xAA` (SNAP) are also valid special SAPs frequently asked.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the LLC PDU diagram is missing or unlabeled.
> 6. **Wrong formula application** ŌĆö efficiency is $\dfrac{\text{Payload}}{\text{Total}}$, not $\dfrac{\text{Header}}{\text{Total}}$. Many students invert this.
> 7. **Forgetting PHY preamble/SFD in wire-size calculation** ŌĆö the 8-byte PHY overhead is **frequently forgotten**, costing 1ŌĆō2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Logical Link Control (LLC)**
>
> Ō£ģ **LLC = Upper half of Data Link Layer** in IEEE 802 model, defined by **IEEE 802.2**.
>
> Ō£ģ **Three sublayer responsibilities:**
> - **LLC ŌåÆ multiplexing, SAPs, flow control, error recovery**.
> - **MAC ŌåÆ physical addressing, channel access, framing**.
> - **PHY ŌåÆ bits on the wire**.
>
> Ō£ģ **Three LLC service types (HIGH-YIELD):**
> - **Type 1** ŌĆö Unacknowledged Connectionless (IP, ARP, DHCP).
> - **Type 2** ŌĆö Connection-Oriented (SABME/UA/DISC; sliding window; SNA).
> - **Type 3** ŌĆö Acknowledged Connectionless (Token Bus, SCADA, factory automation).
>
> Ō£ģ **LLC PDU format:** `DSAP (1B) | SSAP (1B) | Control (1 or 2B) | Information (variable)`.
>
> Ō£ģ **DSAP LSB = I/G bit** (0 = Individual, 1 = Group).
>
> Ō£ģ **SSAP LSB = C/R bit** (0 = Response, 1 = Command).
>
> Ō£ģ **Special SAPs:**
> - `0x00` = Null SAP
> - `0xFF` = Global SAP
> - `0x06` = IP
> - `0x42` = STP
> - `0xAA` = SNAP
>
> Ō£ģ **Control Field Formats:** I-Format, S-Format, U-Format (similar to HDLC).
>
> Ō£ģ **Type 1 Header = 3 bytes** (smallest overhead).
>
> Ō£ģ **Type 2 Window Size:** 7 (basic) or 127 (extended).
>
> Ō£ģ **LLC Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Payload}}{\text{Total bytes on wire}} \times 100\%$.
>
> Ō£ģ **Real-world use:** LLC is the **abstraction layer** that lets **IP run unmodified** over **Ethernet, Wi-Fi, and Token Ring**.
>
> Ō£ģ **SNAP extension:** `DSAP=SSAP=0xAA` triggers SNAP for EtherType identification in modern Ethernet.
>
> Ō£ģ **Examiner's mantra:** Always draw the **PDU diagram** + always state **Type 1/2/3 differences** + always include **I/G and C/R bits**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless LAN (802.11)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wireless LAN (802.11)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** is a set of standards defined by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) for implementing **Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs)** in the **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** unlicensed radio frequency bands. It specifies both the **Physical (PHY)** layer and the **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sublayer of the OSI Data Link Layer, enabling wireless communication between computers, mobile devices, and network infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim syllabus language):**
> "The 802.11 standard defines the MAC sublayer and three physical layer specifications for wireless connectivity of stationary, portable, and handheld moving stations within a local area."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 802.11 matters for KTU exams:** It is a high-weightage topic in **Module 2 (Data Link Layer)**. Students are frequently tested on CSMA/CA, the DCF/PCF procedures, RTS/CTS handshake, and the MAC frame format.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Polite Conversation in a Coffee Shop"

Imagine **you and many friends** sitting in a crowded, noisy coffee shop, all trying to talk to each other simultaneously:
- If everyone speaks at once, **no one hears anything** (collision).
- A "shared radio air" is exactly like the air in that shop.
- 802.11 solves this by enforcing a strict etiquette called **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)**:
  1. **Listen first** ("Carrier Sense").
  2. If the air is busy, **wait politely** (Backoff timer).
  3. **Wait for everyone to be quiet** (DIFS interval).
  4. Speak your message, and **wait for the receiver to nod back** (ACK frame).
  5. The "**physical silence**" between transmissions is the **Inter-Frame Space (IFS)** ŌĆö the heart of 802.11 timing.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line intuition:** Wi-Fi = "polite, patient wireless telephone calls" over a shared radio channel.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (Board Exam Gold)

| Metric | Value (typical 802.11b/g) | Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operating Frequency** | **2.4** | **GHz** | ISM band (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) |
| **Operating Frequency** | **5** | **GHz** | UNII band (Unlicensed National Information Infrastructure) |
| **Raw Data Rate** | **1, 2, 5.5, 11** (b) ; **6ŌĆō54** (a/g) ; **up to 600** (n) ; **up to 9.6 Gbps** (ax) | **Mbps** | PHY layer throughput |
| **Slot Time (Žā)** | **9 ╬╝s (g/a)** ; **20 ╬╝s (b)** | **╬╝s** | Backoff quantum |
| **SIFS** | **10 ╬╝s** | **╬╝s** | Shortest IFS |
| **DIFS** | **SIFS + 2Žā** | **╬╝s** | DCF IFS |
| **CW_min / CW_max** | **31 / 1023** | **slots** | Contention window range |
| **Range (indoor)** | **~35 m (b/g) ; ~70 m (a/n)** | **meters** | Practical office/home |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Students confuse **SIFS**, **PIFS**, and **DIFS**. Memorize: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS**, where **PIFS = SIFS + 1Žā** (used by PCF) and **DIFS = SIFS + 2Žā** (used by DCF).

## 1.4 The 802.11 Family Tree (Standards Progression)

- **802.11 (1997)** ŌĆö Legacy, **1ŌĆō2 Mbps**, FHSS/DSSS
- **802.11a (1999)** ŌĆö OFDM, **54 Mbps**, **5 GHz**
- **802.11b (1999)** ŌĆö DSSS, **11 Mbps**, **2.4 GHz**
- **802.11g (2003)** ŌĆö OFDM, **54 Mbps**, **2.4 GHz**
- **802.11n (2009)** ŌĆö MIMO/OFDM, **600 Mbps**, dual-band
- **802.11ac (2014)** ŌĆö MU-MIMO, **6.93 Gbps**, **5 GHz**
- **802.11ax (2019, Wi-Fi 6/6E)** ŌĆö OFDMA, **9.6 Gbps**, 2.4/5/6 GHz

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** Compare **802.11a vs 802.11b** and list **advantages/disadvantages** of each frequency band.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Architecture: From One Cell to a Building

The 802.11 architecture is built from these building blocks:

- **STA (Station):** Any wireless-equipped end device (laptop, phone).
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** A single cell containing one AP and all associated stations.
- **IBSS (Independent BSS) / Ad-hoc Network:** Stations talk directly to each other, **no AP**. Used for spontaneous networks.
- **DS (Distribution System):** A wired (typically Ethernet) backbone that connects multiple APs.
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** Two or more BSSs interconnected via a DS, enabling roaming.
- **AP (Access Point):** A "bridge" between wireless STAs and the DS.

### How They Connect ŌĆö Structured Logic

1. **Single BSS:** A single AP serves all clients in a small office.
2. **ESS:** When the office grows, additional APs are connected via a **switch/Ethernet backbone** (the DS).
3. **Roaming:** A STA moving from BSS-1 to BSS-2 performs **re-association** with the new AP while the old AP forwards buffered frames.
4. **Ad-hoc mode:** Two laptops in a meeting room connect **peer-to-peer** without infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A BSS is like a **single conference room** with one chairperson (AP). An ESS is the **entire conference building** with multiple rooms, all linked by a hallway (DS). An ad-hoc IBSS is a **gossip circle** in the parking lot ŌĆö no chair, no structure.

## 2.2 The MAC Sublayer: The Heart of 802.11

The MAC layer provides two coordination functions:

### 2.2.1 DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) ŌĆö Mandatory

- **CSMA/CA** based, fully distributed (no central controller).
- Used for **asynchronous, contention-based** data delivery.
- Steps:
  1. Sense the channel.
  2. If idle for **DIFS**, start backoff counter.
  3. Decrement counter every slot while channel remains idle.
  4. When counter = 0, transmit.
  5. Wait for **ACK** after **SIFS**; if no ACK, **retransmit with doubled CW**.

### 2.2.2 PCF (Point Coordination Function) ŌĆö Optional (mostly obsolete in Wi-Fi 6/6E)

- AP acts as **Point Coordinator**, polling stations in contention-free period (CFP).
- Time alternates between **CP (Contention Period)** and **CFP (Contention-Free Period)**.
- **Replaced by HCF (Hybrid Coordination Function)** in 802.11e with QoS support.

### 2.2.3 HCF (Hybrid Coordination Function) ŌĆö 802.11e

- Combines contention-based (EDCA) and controlled channel access (HCCA).
- Introduces **QoS categories** (voice, video, best effort, background).

## 2.3 Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS) ŌĆö The 802.11 Timing Trinity

The ordering of priorities in 802.11 is encoded **in time**:

$$\text{Channel access priority} \propto \frac{1}{\text{IFS duration}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the hierarchy** (shortest IFS = highest priority):
> **SIFS** (ACK, RTS, CTS, fragmentation) < **PIFS** (PCF operations) < **DIFS** (DCF data) < **EIFS** (error recovery)

## 2.4 The Hidden Node Problem & RTS/CTS

> [!NOTE]
> **The problem:** A and C cannot hear each other (hidden), both transmit to B, and collide **at B** ŌĆö but A and C never knew!

**Solution ŌĆö RTS/CTS Handshake (optional mechanism):**
1. Sender sends **RTS** (Request To Send) after DIFS + backoff.
2. Receiver replies with **CTS** (Clear To Send) after SIFS.
3. All stations hearing CTS **defer** (set NAV = Network Allocation Vector).
4. Data frame follows.
5. Receiver sends **ACK** after SIFS.

**Collision probability is reduced** to the tiny RTS frame (20 bytes), not the long data frame.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` in markdown tables** to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary / Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | DIFS | $\text{DIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 2 \times \sigma$ | seconds | 802.11b: 50 ╬╝s; 802.11a/g: 34 ╬╝s |
| 2 | PIFS | $\text{PIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 1 \times \sigma$ | seconds | 802.11b: 30 ╬╝s; 802.11a/g: 25 ╬╝s |
| 3 | EIFS | $\text{EIFS} = \text{SIFS} + \text{ACK}_{tx} + \text{DIFS}$ | seconds | Used when frame error suspected |
| 4 | Backoff Counter | $B = \text{Random}(0, \text{CW}) \times \sigma$ | seconds | CW doubles on each retry up to $\text{CW}_{\max}$ |
| 5 | Contention Window | $\text{CW}_i = \min\!\left( 2^{i+4} - 1,\; \text{CW}_{\max} \right)$ | slots | $i$ = retry count |
| 6 | MAC Header | $34$ bytes (general) | bytes | Includes 3 addresses + duration + control |
| 7 | ACK Frame | $14$ bytes (control frame) | bytes | At PHY rate |
| 8 | RTS / CTS | $20$ bytes / $14$ bytes | bytes | Always at base (lowest) rate |
| 9 | Max Frame Body | $2312$ bytes | bytes | (1500 MSDU + 8 LLC/SNAP overhead room) |
| 10 | Throughput (S) | $S = \dfrac{E\!\mid P \mid}{E\!\mid T \mid}$ | bps | $E\!\mid P \mid$ = payload bits ; $E\!\mid T \mid$ = total time |
| 11 | Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | Shannon bound (theoretical max) |
| 12 | Free-Space Path Loss | $L = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ | dB | $d$ = distance, $f$ = frequency |
| 13 | Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{c}$, with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | seconds | For indoor $\delta \approx 1 \mu$s |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wi-Fi throughput calculations (rows 10 & 11) directly drive **network capacity planning** in offices, stadiums, and IoT deployments. The CW-doubling rule (row 5) is the **Binary Exponential Backoff** foundation of all wireless MAC protocols.

## 2.6 MAC Frame Format ŌĆö The 802.11 Frame Anatomy

A standard 802.11 MAC frame contains:

**Frame Control | Duration/ID | Address 1 | Address 2 | Address 3 | Sequence Control | Address 4 | Frame Body (0ŌĆō2312 B) | FCS (4 B)**

- **Frame Control (2 bytes):** Protocol version, type, subtype, To/From-DS flags, retry, power management.
- **Three address fields** are mandatory; the **fourth** is used only in WDS (Wireless Distribution System) bridging.
- **Frame Body:** 0 to 2312 bytes; carries LLC, IP, etc.
- **FCS (Frame Check Sequence):** 32-bit CRC for error detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** The 802.11 frame is like a **registered postal envelope** with sender, receiver, return-address, "do-not-bend" warning (FCS), and the actual letter inside.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No shortcuts, no skipped steps. Every algebraic transition is written explicitly.**

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum DCF Throughput for 802.11b at 11 Mbps

**Given (standard 802.11b parameters):**
- $\text{SIFS} = 10\ \mu s$
- $\sigma = 20\ \mu s$ (slot time)
- $\text{CW}_{\min} = 31$ slots
- Data rate $R = 11\ \text{Mbps}$
- $\text{ACK rate} = 1\ \text{Mbps}$ (control frame always at base rate)
- Payload $L_p = 1500$ bytes $= 12000$ bits
- MAC header $= 34$ bytes, Preamble + PLCP header $= 24$ bytes (DSSS at 1 Mbps for header)
- ACK frame $= 14$ bytes
- Propagation delay $\delta = 1\ \mu s$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute DIFS

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DIFS} &= \text{SIFS} + 2 \times \sigma \\
&= 10\ \mu s + 2 \times 20\ \mu s \\
&= 50\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Average Backoff Duration

The backoff is uniformly distributed in $[0, \text{CW}_{\min}]$ slots. The **expected value** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\!\left[ B \right] &= \frac{\text{CW}_{\min}}{2} \times \sigma \\
&= \frac{31}{2} \times 20\ \mu s \\
&= 310\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Frame Transmission Times

The 802.11b frame is transmitted in two parts: **Header at 1 Mbps**, **Payload at 11 Mbps**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{header}} &= \frac{(24 + 34) \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = \frac{464}{10^6} = 464\ \mu s \\
T_{\text{payload}} &= \frac{1500 \times 8}{11 \times 10^6} = \frac{12000}{11 \times 10^6} = 1090.91\ \mu s \\
T_{\text{data}} &= T_{\text{header}} + T_{\text{payload}} \\
&= 464 + 1090.91 = 1554.91\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute ACK Transmission Time

$$
T_{\text{ACK}} = \frac{14 \times 8}{1 \times 10^6} = \frac{112}{10^6} = 112\ \mu s
$$

(We include the long preamble of 192 ╬╝s at 1 Mbps in the standard calculation; here we use the post-preamble duration for clarity.)

### Step 5 ŌĆö Total Time for One Successful Frame Exchange

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\!\left[ T \right] &= \text{DIFS} + E\!\left[ B \right] + T_{\text{data}} + \text{SIFS} + T_{\text{ACK}} + 2\delta \\
&= 50 + 310 + 1554.91 + 10 + 112 + 2(1) \\
&= 2037.91\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Compute Throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{L_p}{E\!\left[ T \right]} = \frac{12000\ \text{bits}}{2037.91 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}} \\
&\approx 5.89 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bps} \\
&\approx 5.89\ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Compute Channel Utilization (Efficiency)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{T_{\text{payload}}}{E\!\left[ T \right]} = \frac{1090.91}{2037.91} \\
&\approx 0.5353 \approx 53.53\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Even at 11 Mbps PHY rate, the **effective throughput is only ~5.89 Mbps** because ~46% of the airtime is consumed by DIFS, SIFS, backoff, ACK, and headers. This is why **real-world Wi-Fi is always slower than the rated speed**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Contention Window Doubling (Binary Exponential Backoff)

After the $i$-th collision, the contention window is:

$$
\text{CW}_i = \min\!\left( 2^{i+4} - 1,\; 1023 \right)
$$

For $i = 0$ (first attempt):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CW}_0 &= 2^{0+4} - 1 = 2^4 - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

For $i = 1$:

$$
\text{CW}_1 = 2^{1+4} - 1 = 31
$$

For $i = 2$:

$$
\text{CW}_2 = 2^{2+4} - 1 = 63
$$

For $i = 3$:

$$
\text{CW}_3 = 2^{3+4} - 1 = 127
$$

For $i = 4$:

$$
\text{CW}_4 = 2^{4+4} - 1 = 255
$$

For $i = 5$:

$$
\text{CW}_5 = 2^{5+4} - 1 = 511
$$

For $i = 6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CW}_6 &= 2^{6+4} - 1 = 2^{10} - 1 = 1024 - 1 = 1023
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** $\text{CW}$ saturates at $1023$ from the **6th retry onwards**, ensuring the backoff never grows unbounded.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 802.11a vs 802.11b vs 802.11g

| Parameter | 802.11b | 802.11a | 802.11g |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard Year | 1999 | 1999 | 2003 |
| Frequency Band | **2.4 GHz** | **5 GHz** | **2.4 GHz** |
| Modulation | DSSS (CCK) | OFDM (BPSK ŌåÆ 64-QAM) | OFDM (CCK fallback) |
| Max Data Rate | **11 Mbps** | **54 Mbps** | **54 Mbps** |
| Slot Time ($\sigma$) | 20 ╬╝s | 9 ╬╝s | 9 ╬╝s (OFDM) / 20 ╬╝s (CCK) |
| SIFS | 10 ╬╝s | 16 ╬╝s | 10 ╬╝s |
| CW_min | 31 | 15 | 15 (OFDM) |
| Range | Good (better wall penetration) | Lower (poor wall penetration) | Good |
| Interference Susceptibility | High (microwaves, Bluetooth) | Low | High |
| Backward Compatibility | ŌĆö | None | Compatible with 802.11b |
| Maximum Users per Cell (practical) | ~20 | ~40 | ~40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** 802.11a is faster but shorter-range; 802.11b is slower but penetrates walls better; 802.11g is the best compromise (uses 2.4 GHz with OFDM modulation).
