\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N-1}$ | Probability of collision with N stations transmitting | probability | | 9 | $B_{shared} = \dfrac{R}{N_{active}}$ | Effective bandwidth per station (worst case) | bits/sec | | 10 | $S_{voltage} \in [2.2,\, 2.8]$ | Standard peak-to-peak voltage swing for 10BASE-T | Volts | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Table-Building Rule Applied:** Vertical pipes inside math expressions (such as $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact. This is verified above in Formulas 1 and 3. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Historical LAN Backbone:** Hubs were the standard interconnect for office LANs in the 1990s and early 2000s before switches became affordable. - **Packet Sniffing & Network Tapping:** Because hubs broadcast all traffic to all ports, they are **still used in cybersecurity labs and penetration testing** to passively capture network traffic from any port. - **IoT & Embedded Testing:** Used in lab environments to monitor low-volume embedded device traffic cheaply. - **Modern Status:** **Largely obsolete** in production networks ŌĆö replaced by **Layer 2 switches** which provide dedicated bandwidth per port and isolated collision domains. - **Network Taps:** A modern industrial descendant of the hub ŌĆö a "passive network tap" is essentially a passive hub used in IDS (Intrusion Detection) appliances. ## 2.5 Hub vs. Repeater vs. Switch vs. Router (Critical Comparison) | Feature | Repeater | **Hub** | Switch | Router | |:--------|:--------:|:-------:|:------:|:------:| | OSI Layer | 1 (Physical) | **1 (Physical)** | 2 (Data Link) | 3 (Network) | | Ports | 2 | **Multiple (4ŌĆō24)** | Many (8ŌĆō48) | Few (2ŌĆō8 typical) | | Collision Domains | 1 | **1** | **1 per port** | 1 per port | | Broadcast Domains | 1 | **1** | 1 | 1 per interface | | MAC Table | ŌØī | **ŌØī** | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | | IP Routing | ŌØī | **ŌØī** | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | | Filtering | ŌØī | **ŌØī** | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | | Frame Buffering | ŌØī | **Bit-by-bit only** | Full store-and-forward | Full store-and-forward | | Bandwidth Sharing | Yes | **Yes (all share R)** | No (dedicated R per port) | No | | Typical Cost | Low | **Low** | Medium | High | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Maximum Network Diameter Under 5-4-3 Rule **Problem Statement:** Determine the maximum end-to-end network diameter when 4 hubs are connected in a chain with 10BASE-T cabling (max segment length 100 m, propagation velocity factor k = 0.77 c for UTP). ### Given Data - Maximum segment length per cable: $L_{seg} = 100 \text{ m}$ - Number of repeaters/hubs: $n_{hubs} = 4$ - Number of segments: $n_{segs} = 5$ (per 5-4-3 rule) - Propagation velocity: $v = 0.77 \times 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} = 2.31 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify total physical distance.** $$ D_{total} = n_{segs} \times L_{seg} $$ $$ D_{total} = 5 \times 100 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute total propagation delay.** $$ T_{prop_{total}} = \dfrac{D_{total}}{v \cdot k} = \dfrac{500}{2.31 \times 10^{8}} \text{ s} $$ $$ T_{prop_{total}} \approx 2.1645 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.1645 \; \mu s $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to round-trip time (for collision detection constraint).** $$ T_{rt} = 2 \times T_{prop_{total}} = 2 \times 2.1645 \; \mu s $$ $$ T_{rt} \approx 4.329 \; \mu s $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Minimum frame size check at 10 Mbps.** $$ L_{min} = R \times T_{rt} = 10 \times 10^{6} \times 4.329 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ L_{min} \approx 43.29 \text{ bits} $$ Since IEEE 802.3 mandates $L_{min} = 512 \text{ bits}$ (64 bytes), the **5-4-3 rule ensures the minimum frame size is always larger than the collision detection window**. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The maximum practical Ethernet LAN diameter using hubs under 10BASE-T is **500 m**, ensuring collision detection works correctly for the minimum 64-byte frame. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Effective Throughput Per Station in a Hubbed Network **Problem Statement:** A 100 Mbps hub connects 8 active stations, each generating traffic with probability *p* = 0.1 per time slot. Compute the collision probability and effective bandwidth per station. ### Given Data - Hub data rate: $R = 100 \text{ Mbps}$ - Number of active stations: $N = 8$ - Per-station transmission probability: $p = 0.1$ ### Step 1 ŌĆö Probability that a specific station transmits in a slot. $$ P_{tx} = p = 0.1 $$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Probability that a specific station does NOT transmit. $$ P_{idle} = 1 - p = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 $$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Probability that exactly one station transmits (successful slot). $$ P_{success} = N \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{N-1} $$ $$ P_{success} = 8 \times 0.1 \times (0.9)^{7} $$ $$ P_{success} = 0.8 \times 0.4782969 \approx 0.3826 $$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Probability of a collision (at least one other station transmits simultaneously). $$ P_{collision} = 1 - P_{idle}^{N} - P_{success} $$ Wait ŌĆö let us apply the **standard CSMA/CD probability formula** directly: $$ P_{col} = 1 - (1-p)^{N-1} $$ $$ P_{col} = 1 - (0.9)^{7} = 1 - 0.4782969 $$ $$ P_{col} \approx 0.5217 \;\; (52.17\%) $$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Effective bandwidth per station (worst case shared medium). $$ B_{per\_station} = \dfrac{R \times P_{success}}{N} $$ $$ B_{per\_station} = \dfrac{100 \text{ Mbps} \times 0.3826}{8} \approx 4.78 \text{ Mbps} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** With just 8 stations and 10% traffic load, the collision probability exceeds **52%**, and each station effectively gets only **~4.78 Mbps** out of the nominal 100 Mbps. This is precisely why hubs were replaced by switches in enterprise networks. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Signal Power Budget and Maximum Cascaded Hubs **Problem Statement:** Each 10BASE-T hub introduces a worst-case insertion loss of 2 dB. The receiver minimum sensitivity is ŌĆō25 dBm. A transmitting NIC outputs +5 dBm. How many hubs can be cascaded in a worst-case path? ### Step 1 ŌĆö Total allowable loss budget. $$ L_{budget} = P_{tx} - P_{rx_{min}} = +5 - (-25) $$ $$ L_{budget} = 30 \text{ dB} $$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Account for cable attenuation (assume 100 m at 1 dB loss each = 1 dB per segment for simplicity). $$ L_{cable} = 5 \text{ segments} \times 1 \text{ dB} = 5 \text{ dB} $$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Available loss for hubs only. $$ L_{hub\_total} = L_{budget} - L_{cable} = 30 - 5 = 25 \text{ dB} $$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum hubs. $$ N_{hubs} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{L_{hub\_total}}{L_{per\_hub}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{25}{2} \right\rfloor = 12 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world limit is the 5-4-3 rule (4 hubs max) for collision detection, not the optical power budget.** The power budget would theoretically allow more, but timing constraints from CSMA/CD are stricter. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Hub Performance Trade-offs | Metric | Hub Performance | Why It Matters | |:-------|:----------------|:---------------| | Latency | ~**0.1 ŌĆō 0.5 ┬Ąs** (bit-level) | Excellent for real-time, but useless for QoS | | Aggregate Throughput | Capped at **shared R** | Scales poorly with N | | Scalability | **Low** (limited by 5-4-3) | Cannot extend without bridges | | Security | **Very Low** (any port sniffs all traffic) | Useful for attackers, bad for users | | Cost | **Cheapest** possible interconnect | Good for budget labs | | Collision Susceptibility | **High** (all share one domain) | Degrades at >40% load | | Manageability | Limited (intelligent hubs offer SNMP) | Better than passive, worse than switches | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Hub Internal Architecture (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["PORT 1
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC["RECEIVER
CIRCUITRY
Signal Restoration"] P2["PORT 2
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC P3["PORT 3
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC P4["PORT 4
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC P5["PORT 5
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC P6["PORT 6
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC P7["PORT 7
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC P8["PORT 8
RJ45 Transceiver"] --> RC RC --> CD["COLLISION
DETECTION
LOGIC"] CD --> JB["JABBER
CONTROL
UNIT"] JB --> BC["BROADCAST
FANOUT
MATRIX"] BC --> S1["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 1"] BC --> S2["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 2"] BC --> S3["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 3"] BC --> S4["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 4"] BC --> S5["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 5"] BC --> S6["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 6"] BC --> S7["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 7"] BC --> S8["SIGNAL
REGENERATOR
PORT 8"] S1 --> O1["PORT 1
OUTPUT"] S2 --> O2["PORT 2
OUTPUT"] S3 --> O3["PORT 3
OUTPUT"] S4 --> O4["PORT 4
OUTPUT"] S5 --> O5["PORT 5
OUTPUT"] S6 --> O6["PORT 6
OUTPUT"] S7 --> O7["PORT 7
OUTPUT"] S8 --> O8["PORT 8
OUTPUT"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Hub as a Single Collision Domain ```mermaid flowchart LR A["WORKSTATION A
NIC MAC 11"] --- H["HUB
Physical Layer Device
ONE Collision Domain
ONE Broadcast Domain"] B["WORKSTATION B
NIC MAC 22"] --- H C["WORKSTATION C
NIC MAC 33"] --- H D["WORKSTATION D
NIC MAC 44"] --- H E["WORKSTATION E
NIC MAC 55"] --- H H -. "Signal from A
goes to B C D E
EXCEPT A" .- B H -. "Signal from A
goes to A C D E
EXCEPT B" .- C ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice in the diagram:** The hub creates a **physical star** but a **logical bus** ŌĆö every frame sent by one station is received by ALL others. This is a **single collision domain**. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Hub vs Switch Collision Domain Contrast ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HubNet["HUB NETWORK - ONE Collision Domain"] HA["PC A"] --- HB["HUB"] HC["PC B"] --- HB HD["PC C"] --- HB HE["PC D"] --- HB HB -. "All share
one collision domain" .- HC end subgraph SwitchNet["SWITCH NETWORK - SEPARATE Collision Domains"] SA["PC A"] --- SB["SWITCH
Layer 2"] SC["PC B"] --- SB SD["PC C"] --- SB SE["PC D"] --- SB SB -. "Each port is
its own collision domain" .- SC end ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö 5-4-3 Rule Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["SEGMENT 1
Station A"] --- H1["HUB 1"] H1 --- S2["SEGMENT 2
Station B"] S2 --- H2["HUB 2"] H2 --- S3["SEGMENT 3
Station C"] S3 --- H3["HUB 3"] H3 --- S4["SEGMENT 4
Station D"] S4 --- H4["HUB 4"] H4 --- S5["SEGMENT 5
Station E"] H1 -.- X1["REPEATER 1
Counted"] H2 -.- X2["REPEATER 2
Counted"] H3 -.- X3["REPEATER 3
Counted"] H4 -.- X4["REPEATER 4
Counted"] ``` > [!TIP] > **5-4-3 Rule Restated:** Maximum **5 segments** connected by **4 hubs/repeaters**, of which only **3 segments** may carry end stations. The other 2 are link-only segments between hubs. ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 ŌĆö Hub Operational Flowchart (Per-Bit Processing) ```mermaid flowchart TD ST["Start: Bit arrives at Port X"] --> RX["Receive electrical waveform
from cable transceiver"] RX --> RD["Detect voltage levels
and Manchester decoding"] RD --> CC{"Check for
simultaneous signal
on other ports?"} CC -- "Yes - Collision Detected" --> JAM["Broadcast Jam Signal
to all ports
32 bit pattern"] JAM --> LCK["Lock medium for
backoff period"] LCK --> RD CC -- "No - Clean Signal" --> RST["Regenerate and
re-amplify signal"] RST --> JAB{"Jabber Check:
Continuous TX
exceeds limit?"} JAB -- "Yes" --> DIS["Disable faulty port
log jabber event"] DIS --> END["End"] JAB -- "No" --> FANO["Fan out to
ALL ports except source"] FANO --> END ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each, but standard 3-mark format follows) ### Question 1: Define a Hub. State its OSI layer of operation. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** A hub is a Physical Layer (Layer 1) networking device that acts as a central connection point in a star-topology LAN. It receives an incoming electrical signal on one port, regenerates (amplifies) it to restore signal strength, and broadcasts the same signal out through all other ports. The hub does not interpret MAC addresses, does not maintain any forwarding table, and operates purely on raw bit streams. It creates a single collision domain and a single broadcast domain shared by all connected stations. **Layer:** OSI **Physical Layer (Layer 1)**. **Keywords to include for full marks:** *Physical Layer, signal regeneration, broadcast, no MAC table, single collision domain.* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between an active hub and a passive hub. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024] **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Active Hub | Passive Hub | |:----------|:-----------|:------------| | Power Supply | Requires external DC power | No power required | | Signal Regeneration | Yes ŌĆö amplifies and re-times the signal | No ŌĆö just splits the signal | | Max Cable Distance | Can extend to 100 m per segment | Limited to ~30ŌĆō50 m per segment | | Use Case | Production LANs | Simple cable splitting, lab demos | | Cost | Higher | Very low | | Function in Network | Acts as multiport repeater | Acts as cable junction | --- ## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A (Choice 1) ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted] **Question:** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of an Ethernet hub. Discuss the 5-4-3 rule and its significance in hub-based networks. Compare hubs with switches. #### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks ŌĆö Hub Architecture and Working **Model Solution:** **(i) Architecture (3 marks):** An Ethernet hub consists of the following functional blocks: - **Multiple RJ-45 Ports (4 to 24):** Each port has an independent transceiver for transmitting and receiving electrical signals. - **Signal Restoration Circuitry:** Cleans and re-amplifies incoming waveforms to compensate for cable attenuation and noise. - **Collision Detection Logic:** Monitors DC voltage levels on all ports to identify simultaneous transmissions. - **Jabber Control Unit:** Disables a port that transmits continuously beyond the maximum allowed packet time. - **Broadcast Fan-out Matrix:** Distributes the regenerated bit stream to all ports except the source. **(ii) Working (4 marks):** When a station sends a frame, the following sequence occurs: - The NIC encodes bits using Manchester encoding and transmits them as voltage transitions. - The hub's receiver detects the voltage levels, decodes them, and rebuilds a clean waveform. - The collision detection circuitry checks for overlapping signals on other ports. - If clean, the regenerated bit stream is broadcast to all other active ports. - All other stations receive the frame; if the destination MAC address matches, the station processes it; otherwise the frame is discarded. - If a collision is detected, the hub broadcasts a 32-bit jam signal to enforce the CSMA/CD collision domain rule. #### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks ŌĆö 5-4-3 Rule and Hub vs Switch **Model Solution:** **(i) The 5-4-3 Rule (3 marks):** The 5-4-3 rule governs the maximum number of repeaters and segments in a 10BASE-T Ethernet network: - Maximum **5 segments** can be joined in series. - Connected through a maximum of **4 repeaters or hubs**. - Only **3 segments** can have end stations (hosts); the remaining 2 segments are link segments between hubs. - Purpose: Ensures that the round-trip propagation delay stays below the time needed to transmit the minimum 64-byte frame, so that collisions are reliably detected by CSMA/CD. **(ii) Hub vs Switch Comparison (4 marks):** | Feature | Hub | Switch | |:--------|:----|:-------| | OSI Layer | 1 (Physical) | 2 (Data Link) | | Collision Domain | One shared | One per port (microsegmentation) | | Bandwidth | Shared across all ports | Dedicated per port | | Intelligence | None ŌĆö pure broadcast | MAC address learning, frame filtering | | Frame Forwarding | Bit-by-bit flood | Store-and-forward / cut-through | | Cost | Low | Higher (but affordable now) | | Modern Use | Legacy, lab sniffing | Universal in production LANs | --- ### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B (Choice 2) ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Adapted] **Question:** Explain in detail the different types of hubs. With a suitable diagram, describe the signal regeneration process in a 10BASE-T hub. Discuss why hubs are considered inefficient in modern networks and how switches solved the problem. #### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks ŌĆö Types of Hubs and Signal Regeneration **Model Solution:** **(i) Types of Hubs (3 marks):** - **Passive Hub:** A simple connector that physically splits the incoming signal among output ports. It provides no amplification, no power, and no signal restoration. Used only for very short cable extensions in laboratory or test setups. - **Active Hub:** Powered device that contains repeater circuitry. It receives, cleans, and re-amplifies the signal before broadcasting. This allows longer cable runs (up to 100 m per segment) and compensates for cable attenuation. - **Intelligent Hub (Smart Hub):** An active hub enhanced with management features such as SNMP monitoring, port-level statistics, remote configuration, fault isolation, and per-port disabling. Functions as a basic managed network element. **(ii) Signal Regeneration in 10BASE-T Hub (4 marks):** 10BASE-T uses **Manchester encoding** with a nominal ┬▒2.5 V peak-to-peak swing. As the signal travels through UTP cable, three degradations occur: - **Attenuation** ŌĆö amplitude reduces (~2ŌĆō3 dB per 100 m). - **Distortion** ŌĆö high-frequency components are attenuated more, rounding off sharp transitions. - **Jitter** ŌĆö timing edges drift due to dispersion. The hub's repeater circuit: - Uses a **slicer/comparator** to re-detect the bit transitions as clean square waves. - **Re-times** the bits using a local clock to remove jitter. - **Re-amplifies** to the standard ┬▒2.5 V level. - The result is a waveform virtually identical to what was originally transmitted. #### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks ŌĆö Inefficiencies of Hubs and Switch-Based Solution **Model Solution:** **(i) Why Hubs Are Inefficient (4 marks):** - **Shared Bandwidth:** All N stations must share the single channel rate R; effective per-station throughput is R/N, not R. - **Single Collision Domain:** Even with only one pair of active stations, a CSMA/CD collision wastes bandwidth; this gets worse as N grows. - **Broadcast Storm Risk:** A misbehaving NIC can flood the network via the hub, and the hub cannot isolate the problem. - **No Security:** Every port receives every frame, making sniffing trivial. - **Scalability:** Limited by the 5-4-3 rule and the 4-repeater cascade constraint. - **Wasted Energy:** A hub's ports power up unused links. **(ii) How Switches Solved This (3 marks):** - Switches operate at **Layer 2**, learning MAC addresses and forwarding frames **only to the destination port**, providing dedicated bandwidth per port. - Each switch port is its **own collision domain**, eliminating CSMA/CD contention. - Switches support **store-and-forward** or **cut-through** forwarding with **error checking (CRC verification)**. - Features like **VLANs**, **port security**, **STP**, and **QoS** make switches far more capable. --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > - **Do not write "Hub works at Data Link Layer"** ŌĆö it operates strictly at Physical Layer. Marks deducted as this is a fundamental error. > - **Do not say "Hub has a MAC address table"** ŌĆö it has none. Only switches/bridges maintain MAC tables. > - **Do not confuse "Broadcast domain" with "Collision domain."** A hub creates ONE of each, while a switch creates one collision domain per port but still ONE broadcast domain. > - **Do not skip the 5-4-3 rule's justification** ŌĆö simply stating "5-4-3" without explaining *why* (collision detection timing) costs 1ŌĆō2 marks. > - **Always show a diagram** in 14-mark questions. Even a simple star with a central hub earns a diagram mark. > - **Use precise terms:** "Signal regeneration" not "signal strengthening"; "Broadcast domain" not "broadcast area." > - **Calculate round-trip time** explicitly if asked about the 5-4-3 rule ŌĆö show the minimum frame size verification. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - Ō£ģ Hub = **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** device, also called a **multiport repeater**. - Ō£ģ Hub **regenerates and broadcasts** incoming signals to all other ports (no filtering). - Ō£ģ Hub has **no MAC address table**, no intelligence, no frame buffer (bit-by-bit forwarding). - Ō£ģ Hub creates **one collision domain** AND **one broadcast domain** shared by all connected devices. - Ō£ģ **Physical star topology** but **logical bus topology** behavior. - Ō£ģ Three types: **Passive, Active, Intelligent (Smart)**. - Ō£ģ **5-4-3 rule:** Max 5 segments, 4 repeaters/hubs, 3 segments with end stations. - Ō£ģ IEEE 802.3 standard governs hub-based Ethernet; 10BASE-T = 10 Mbps, max 100 m UTP. - Ō£ģ Modern bandwidth: **10/100 Mbps auto-sensing hubs**; 1 Gbps hubs are extremely rare. - Ō£ģ Effective per-station bandwidth = $\dfrac{R}{N_{active}}$ ŌĆö degrades linearly with N. - Ō£ģ Hubs are **obsolete in production networks**; replaced by Layer 2 switches. - Ō£ģ Hubs are **still used in network security labs** for packet sniffing and traffic monitoring. - Ō£ģ Minimum Ethernet frame size = **64 bytes (512 bits)** ŌĆö chosen to allow collision detection across maximum 5-4-3 diameter. - Ō£ģ **Collision propagation** is enforced by hubs via a **32-bit jam signal**. - Ō£ģ **Jabber control** disables a port that transmits for longer than the maximum packet time. - Ō£ģ Key formulas to memorize: $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v \cdot k}$, $T_{tx} = \dfrac{L}{R}$, $L_{min} = 2 \cdot T_{prop_{max}} \cdot R$, $P_{col} = 1 - (1-p)^{N-1}$. - Ō£ģ **Hub < Switch < Router** in terms of OSI layer and capability, with **Hub being the simplest**. - Ō£ģ Repeater = 2 ports, regenerates signal; **Hub = N ports, regenerates and broadcasts** ŌĆö same electronics, more ports. - Ō£ģ A hub **does NOT segment collision domains** ŌĆö this is the most important difference from a switch for KTU board purposes.

topics.title: Bridges & Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bridges & Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the **OSI Reference Model**, **Bridges** and **Switches** are the canonical interconnecting devices used to segment **Local Area Networks (LANs)**, filter traffic, and forward **Ethernet Frames** based on **48-bit MAC (Media Access Control) addresses**. They operate below the network layer and are transparent to upper-layer protocols (IP, IPX, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A bridge is a Layer 2 device that connects two or more LAN segments, uses a forwarding/filtering table built by the backward-learning algorithm, and operates in promiscuous (promiscuous-mode) reception across all ports. A switch is essentially a multi-port bridge with parallel switching fabrics and dedicated bandwidth per port.*

| Term | Strict Definition |
|---|---|
| **Bridges** | 2 to 16 port Layer-2 devices connecting distinct LAN segments (e.g., 10BASE5 Ōåö 10BASE2). |
| **Switches** | High-port-density (24/48 port) Layer-2 devices with dedicated collision domains per port and hardware-based forwarding (ASIC). |
| **Promiscuous Mode** | The bridge/switch accepts and processes every frame on the segment, regardless of destination MAC. |
| **MAC Address Table** | A dynamically-built hash table mapping `<MAC Address, Port, Age>` tuples, used for forwarding decisions. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (for first-time learners):**
> Imagine a large, noisy office building with hundreds of employees talking in a single open hall. To deliver a message to one specific person, you would have to shout it across the whole room ŌĆö inefficient and chaotic. Now imagine a **smart receptionist** sitting at the front desk who *learns* over time where every employee sits. When you hand her a sealed envelope for "Mr. Sharma in Accounts," she immediately knows Mr. Sharma's desk number and walks it over directly, *without shouting through the entire office*. If she doesn't yet know Mr. Sharma's desk, she **floods** the message to all departments except yours, and the moment Mr. Sharma replies, she memorizes his location.
> 
> The **receptionist = Bridge/Switch**, the **envelopes = Ethernet Frames**, the **employee seating chart = MAC Address Table**, and the **flooding strategy = Unknown-Unicast Flooding**. Multiple receptionists working in parallel, each with their own private door, represent a **switch's parallel dedicated bandwidth architecture**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **hard-coded IEEE 802 standards** the student must memorize:

- **MAC Address Size:** **48 bits** (24-bit OUI + 24-bit NIC serial) ŌĆö written as **6 hex octets** (e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`).
- **Aging Timer (Default):** **300 seconds (5 minutes)** ŌĆö entries deleted if not refreshed.
- **Minimum Ethernet Frame Size:** **64 bytes** (collision detection requirement).
- **Maximum Ethernet Frame Size (MTU):** **1518 bytes** (1522 bytes with 802.1Q VLAN tag).
- **Spanning Tree Default Port Cost (802.1D legacy):** **1000 / Link Bandwidth (Mbps)** ŌĆö e.g., cost = 10 for 100 Mbps, cost = 100 for 10 Mbps.
- **Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU) Multicast Destination:** **01:80:C2:00:00:00** (reserved, never forwarded by bridges).
- **Spanning Tree Default Bridge Priority:** **32768 (0x8000)**.
- **STP Hello Time:** **2 seconds**; **Max Age:** **20 seconds**; **Forward Delay:** **15 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every numerical value marked in **bold** above is a **high-yield KTU exam fact**. Examiners frequently award direct 1-mark or 2-mark questions on aging timer, MAC size, BPDU destination, and default STP costs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Bridges

KTU 2024 syllabus mandates coverage of three bridge types. We break each down into operational "Why + How" logic:

#### A. Transparent Bridge (most common in Ethernet LANs)
- **Why:** Plug-and-play, no host reconfiguration, no protocol overhead on end stations.
- **How (3 core algorithms):**
  1. **Backward Learning Algorithm** ŌĆö Source MAC of every incoming frame is recorded in the MAC table with the arrival port.
  2. **Forwarding Algorithm** ŌĆö If destination MAC is in the table, forward ONLY out the recorded port. If NOT, **flood** out all ports except the arrival port.
  3. **Spanning Tree Algorithm (STA / IEEE 802.1D)** ŌĆö When multiple bridges exist, only one active path between any two LANs is permitted to eliminate loops.

#### B. Source Routing Bridge (used in legacy Token Ring / IEEE 802.5)
- **Why:** Token Ring's deterministic ring topology has no natural flooding primitive; the source station must specify the path.
- **How:** Source host emits **discovery (all-routes explorer) frames** that travel every possible path to the destination. The destination replies with the **best route** in the frame header. Subsequent frames carry the complete **ring-number/bridge-number** route descriptor.

#### C. Translational Bridge
- **Why:** Connects heterogeneous LANs (e.g., Ethernet Ōåö Token Ring) with different frame formats and MTUs.
- **How:** Performs **bit-level and field-level translation** between dissimilar MAC sublayers, including FCS recomputation, addressing scheme conversion (e.g., 48-bit Ōåö 48-bit non-canonical), and Maximum Transfer Unit fragmentation.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Switches (Switching Fabrics)

| Switching Mode | Latency | Error Checking | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Store-and-Forward** | High (waits for full FCS check) | **Yes** ŌĆö drops corrupted frames | Safer; default in modern switches |
| **Cut-Through** | Very low (reads only first 6 bytes = dest MAC) | **No** | Used in HPC / latency-critical backbones |
| **Fragment-Free (Adaptive Cut-Through)** | Low (reads first 64 bytes) | Partial (collision detection guarantee) | Compromise; common default on Cisco gear |
| **Adaptive** | Variable | Dynamic | Switches modes based on error rate |

### 2.3 Collision Domain vs Broadcast Domain

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction (favourite 3-mark question):**
> - **Bridge / Layer-2 Switch:** Separates **collision domains** (each port is its own), but does **NOT** separate **broadcast domains** (broadcast frame is flooded to all ports).
> - **Router / Layer-3 Switch:** Separates both **collision** and **broadcast** domains.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spanning Tree Port Cost (802.1D) | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{1000}{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}$ | Cost of a 10 Mbps link = 100; 100 Mbps = 10; 1 Gbps = 1; 10 Gbps = 1 (uses long cost in 802.1t) |
| 2 | Root Path Cost | $\text{RPC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost}(L_i)$ | Path with **minimum** RPC wins |
| 3 | Bridge ID Tie-Breaker | $\text{Bridge ID} = \{\text{Priority} \, \vert \, \text{MAC Address}\}$ | Lowest Priority first; if tie, lowest MAC wins |
| 4 | MAC Aging Equation | $\text{Entry Deleted at } t = T_{\text{insert}} + 300\,\text{s}$ | Default aging timer = 300 s |
| 5 | Effective Throughput per Port (Cut-Through) | $\tau_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{6\,\text{bytes} \times 8}{R}$ where $R$ = line rate | For 1 Gbps, $\tau_{\text{cut}} \approx 48\,\text{ns}$ |
| 6 | Store-and-Forward Latency | $\tau_{\text{s\&f}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{frame}} \times 8}{R}$ | For 1518-byte frame at 1 Gbps, $\tau \approx 12.1\,\mu s$ |
| 7 | Broadcast Domain Count (L2 Switch) | $\text{BD} = 1$ per VLAN (default) | Without VLANs, the whole switch = 1 BD |
| 8 | Collision Domain Count (L2 Switch) | $\text{CD} = N_{\text{ports}}$ (full-duplex) | In half-duplex, each port remains its own CD |

> **Note on notation:** The vertical bar $\vert$ is used here purely as the LaTeX "such that" separator; **never** as a markdown table-cell delimiter, as per formatting constraints.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Campus Networks** use a **two-tier (Access + Core)** or **three-tier (Access + Distribution + Core)** switch architecture with STP running only at access layer and routed links (L3) at the core.
- **Data Centers** deploy **cut-through** switches (e.g., Mellanox, Cisco Nexus 9000 series in certain modes) to achieve sub-microsecond latency for high-frequency trading and HPC clusters.
- **Industrial Automation (PROFINET, EtherCAT)** uses transparent bridges with strict QoS tagging to guarantee bounded latency for control loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 ŌĆö Bridge Backward-Learning Walkthrough

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** *Consider three LAN segments (LAN-A, LAN-B, LAN-C) connected by two transparent bridges B1 and B2. B1 has ports P1 (LAN-A) and P2 (LAN-B). B2 has ports P3 (LAN-B) and P4 (LAN-C). Host H1 (MAC: 11:11:11:11:11:11) on LAN-A sends a frame to Host H2 (MAC: 22:22:22:22:22:22) on LAN-C. The MAC tables of both bridges are empty. Trace the frame propagation step-by-step and show the final MAC tables.*

**Step 1 ŌĆö Frame H1 ŌåÆ H2 enters B1 on P1.**
*Why:* B1 is in promiscuous mode; it accepts the frame. It reads the source MAC and the destination MAC.

**Step 2 ŌĆö B1 executes the Learning Algorithm.**
*How:* B1 checks its MAC table for the source MAC `11:11:11:11:11:11`. Empty ŌåÆ record:

$$
\text{B1.MAC}[\text{11:11:11:11:11:11}] = \{ \text{Port} = P1, \text{Age} = 0 \}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö B1 executes the Forwarding Algorithm.**
*How:* B1 looks up destination MAC `22:22:22:22:22:22`. Not in the table ŌåÆ **FLOOD** out all ports *except* the arrival port $P1$.

$$
\text{Flood}(\text{Frame}) \rightarrow P2 \quad (\text{LAN-B})
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Frame H1 ŌåÆ H2 traverses LAN-B and enters B2 on P3.**
*Why:* LAN-B is a shared medium; B2 in promiscuous mode also receives a copy.

**Step 5 ŌĆö B2 executes Learning Algorithm.**
*How:* B2 records source MAC on arrival port P3:

$$
\text{B2.MAC}[\text{11:11:11:11:11:11}] = \{ \text{Port} = P3, \text{Age} = 0 \}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö B2 executes Forwarding Algorithm.**
*How:* Destination MAC `22:22:22:22:22:22` unknown ŌåÆ flood out $P3$'s complement, i.e., $P4$.

$$
\text{Flood}(\text{Frame}) \rightarrow P4 \quad (\text{LAN-C})
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö H2 receives the frame.** The original frame has been delivered.

**Step 8 ŌĆö H2 replies to H1 (Frame H2 ŌåÆ H1).** Now we trace the *reply* to demonstrate the **cache-warming** effect.

**Step 9 ŌĆö Reply enters B2 on P4.**
*How:* B2 learns source `22:22:22:22:22:22` on $P4$. Destination `11:11:11:11:11:11` is in B2's table on $P3$ ŌåÆ **forward selectively out P3 only** (no flood).

**Step 10 ŌĆö Reply reaches B1 on P2.**
*How:* B1 learns source `22:22:22:22:22:22` on $P2$. Destination `11:11:11:11:11:11` is in B1's table on $P1$ ŌåÆ forward selectively out $P1$.

**Step 11 ŌĆö Final State of MAC Tables (after both directions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{B1.MAC} &: \begin{cases} \text{11:11:11:11:11:11} \rightarrow P1 \\ \text{22:22:22:22:22:22} \rightarrow P2 \end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{B2.MAC} &: \begin{cases} \text{11:11:11:11:11:11} \rightarrow P3 \\ \text{22:22:22:22:22:22} \rightarrow P4 \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** After the first round trip, all subsequent H1ŌåöH2 communication uses *selective forwarding* ŌĆö no flooding, no wasted bandwidth. This is the **self-stabilizing** property of backward learning.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 ŌĆö Spanning Tree Cost Computation

**Problem Statement:** *A network has three bridges B1, B2, B3 with Bridge IDs and link costs as follows. B3 is elected as the root. Compute the Root Port and Designated Port for each segment and identify any blocked ports.*

| Link | From Bridge | To Bridge | Cost | Segment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | B1 | B3 | 4 | S1 |
| L2 | B2 | B3 | 8 | S2 |
| L3 | B1 | B2 | 6 | S3 |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Root Bridge.**
*Logic:* Lowest Bridge ID wins. We are told B3 is the root.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Root Path Cost (RPC) for each non-root bridge.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPC}_{\text{B1}} &= \min(C(L1), C(L3)) = \min(4, 6) = 4 \quad \text{(path via L1)} \\[6pt]
\text{RPC}_{\text{B2}} &= \min(C(L2), C(L3)) = \min(8, 6) = 6 \quad \text{(path via L3)} \\[6pt]
\text{RPC}_{\text{B3}} &= 0 \quad \text{(root)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choose Root Port for each non-root bridge (port with minimum cost to root).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root Port of B1} &= L1 \quad (\text{cost } 4) \\[3pt]
\text{Root Port of B2} &= L3 \quad (\text{cost } 6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö For each segment, elect a Designated Bridge (the one with minimum RPC to root on that segment).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{On S1 (B1ŌĆōB3)} &: \min(\text{RPC}_{\text{B1}}, \text{RPC}_{\text{B3}}) = \min(4, 0) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Designated Bridge} = \text{B3}, \text{Designated Port} = \text{B3-side of L1}. \\[4pt]
\text{On S2 (B2ŌĆōB3)} &: \min(8, 0) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Designated Bridge} = \text{B3}, \text{Designated Port} = \text{B3-side of L2}. \\[4pt]
\text{On S3 (B1ŌĆōB2)} &: \min(\text{RPC}_{\text{B1}} \text{ via L3}, \text{RPC}_{\text{B2}} \text{ via L3}) \\
                     &: \min(4 + 6,\; 6) \quad \leftarrow \text{B1 would pay 4 to root via L1; but ON S3 itself, B2 is closer} \\
                     &: \min(\text{RPC}_{\text{B1 via L3}},\; \text{RPC}_{\text{B2}}) = \min(10, 6) = 6 \Rightarrow \text{Designated Bridge} = \text{B2}, \text{Designated Port} = \text{B2-side of L3}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Block the non-designated port on S3.**
*Logic:* On S3, the B1-side port is **not** the designated port, and it is not B1's root port (B1's root port is L1). Therefore the B1-side port of L3 must be **put into BLOCKING state**.

$$
\boxed{\; \text{B1's port on L3 is BLOCKED.} \;}
$$

**Final Topology (active forwarding tree):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Active links} &: \{L1, L2\} \cup \{\text{B2-side of L3}\} \\[3pt]
\text{Blocked link} &: \{\text{B1-side of L3}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly compare only the *direct link cost* on a segment, ignoring the cumulative Root Path Cost. The designated port is the one on the bridge whose **cumulative path to the root through that port** is smallest. The above derivation handles this correctly.

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 ŌĆö Latency Comparison: Store-and-Forward vs Cut-Through

**Problem:** Compute the switching latency for a 256-byte frame traversing a 1 Gbps switch in both store-and-forward and cut-through modes.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Cut-Through Latency (only first 6 bytes, i.e., destination MAC, are read before forwarding begins).**

$$
\tau_{\text{cut}} = \frac{6 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{10^{9} \text{ bits/s}} = \frac{48}{10^{9}} = 4.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \tau_{\text{cut}} = 48 \text{ nanoseconds} \;}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Store-and-Forward Latency (entire frame buffered and CRC-checked).**

$$
\tau_{\text{s\&f}} = \frac{256 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{10^{9} \text{ bits/s}} = \frac{2048}{10^{9}} = 2.048 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \tau_{\text{s\&f}} \approx 2.05 \text{ microseconds} \;}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Speedup Factor.**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{\tau_{\text{s\&f}}}{\tau_{\text{cut}}} = \frac{2048 \text{ bits}}{48 \text{ bits}} \approx 42.7 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cut-through is ~42├Ś faster latency-wise, **but it cannot detect CRC errors** and will propagate corrupted frames. For a 10% error rate link, cut-through wastes significant downstream bandwidth on garbage frames.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Bridge Forwarding / Learning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame Arrives on Port Px] --> B[Parse Destination MAC from Frame Header]
    B --> C{Is Destination MAC in MAC Address Table?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Forward Frame Selectively to Recorded Port]
    C -- No  --> E[Flood Frame Out All Ports Except Px]
    D --> F[Update Aging Timer for Source MAC Entry]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Aging Timer Expired 300 s?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Delete Stale MAC Table Entry]
    G -- No  --> I[Retain Entry Continue Forwarding]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Process Next Frame]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Spanning Tree Protocol Election State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Disabled
    Disabled --> Blocking : Port Enabled
    Blocking --> Listening : Becomes Root or Designated Port
    Listening --> Learning : Forward Delay 15 s Elapsed
    Learning --> Forwarding : Forward Delay 15 s Elapsed
    Forwarding --> Blocking : Receives Superior BPDU
    Listening --> Blocking : Receives Superior BPDU
    Forwarding --> [*] : Port Disabled
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Switch Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Layer PHY Receivers] --> B[MAC Frame Parser ASIC]
    B --> C[MAC Address Lookup Engine TCAM]
    C --> D{Entry Found in TCAM}
    D -- Yes --> E[Egress Port Selection]
    D -- No  --> F[Flood to All Ports Except Ingress]
    E --> G[Output Queue Buffers per Port]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Physical Layer PHY Transmitters]
    C --> I[Spanning Tree Engine 8021D]
    I --> J[BPDU Multicast 01 80 C2 00 00 00]
    I --> K[Port State Control Blocking Forwarding]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö Collision Domain vs Broadcast Domain Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BridgeOrSwitchLayer
        SW[Layer 2 Switch Core]
    end
    P1[Port 1 Collision Domain 1] --- SW
    P2[Port 2 Collision Domain 2] --- SW
    P3[Port 3 Collision Domain 3] --- SW
    P4[Port 4 Collision Domain 4] --- SW
    SW -. Floods broadcast to all 4 ports .-> P1
    SW -. Floods broadcast to all 4 ports .-> P2
    SW -. Floods broadcast to all 4 ports .-> P3
    SW -. Floods broadcast to all 4 ports .-> P4
    R[Router Layer 3 Boundary] --- SW
    R --> NetA[Network A Separate Broadcast Domain]
    R --> NetB[Network B Separate Broadcast Domain]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 1** captures the **decision diamond** central to every bridge: known-unicast vs unknown-unicast vs broadcast handling.
> - **Diagram 2** is the IEEE 802.1D state machine ŌĆö students often confuse *Listening* with *Learning*. Listening builds the STP topology table; Learning populates the MAC address table. Neither state forwards user frames!
> - **Diagram 3** shows the **TCAM (Ternary CAM)** for O(1) MAC lookup and the parallel **Spanning Tree engine**.
> - **Diagram 4** visually proves that a switch segments *collision* but not *broadcast* domains ŌĆö the router at the bottom is what creates broadcast-domain separation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q.A1] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between a transparent bridge and a source routing bridge. List any three points.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Transparent Bridge | Source Routing Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operates in **promiscuous** mode and is **transparent** to end hosts. | End hosts must be **routing-aware** and embed the full path in the frame. |
| 2 | Uses **backward learning** algorithm to build forwarding tables. | Uses **discovery (all-routes explorer) frames** to learn the route. |
| 3 | Frame format is **unchanged** (IEEE 802.1/802.3). | Frame contains a **routing information field (RIF)**. |
| 4 | Default in **Ethernet** (802.3) networks. | Default in legacy **Token Ring** (802.5) networks. |
| 5 | Loops handled by **Spanning Tree Protocol (802.1D)**. | Loops avoided by **source-specified routes** ŌĆö no STP needed. |

---

> **[Q.A2] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *Explain the concept of cut-through switching. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage over store-and-forward switching.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Concept:** In cut-through switching, the switch begins forwarding the frame out the destination port **as soon as the first 6 bytes (destination MAC address) are read**, without waiting for the rest of the frame or performing CRC verification.
- **Advantage:** Latency is reduced to ~48 ns on a 1 Gbps link (independent of frame size).
- **Disadvantage:** Corrupted frames (failed CRC) and runts are propagated, wasting downstream bandwidth.

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **[Q.B-A] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** ŌĆö Module 3, 14 Marks
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and operation of a transparent bridge. Discuss the backward learning algorithm in detail.*
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the store-and-forward and cut-through switching techniques. Mention a typical scenario where each is preferred.*

### Model Solution for Q.B-A(a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Architecture (refer to Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4).**
A transparent bridge sits between two or more LAN segments. It has $N$ ports, a **MAC address table** (implemented in CAM/TCAM in modern hardware), and a **forwarding engine** (an ASIC for hardware-speed lookups). It operates in **promiscuous mode**, meaning it processes every frame on every segment, regardless of destination.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Backward Learning Algorithm ŌĆö Formal Statement.**

$$
\forall \text{ incoming frame } f \text{ on port } p_x \text{ with source MAC } S \text{ and destination MAC } D:
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Learning: } \text{MAC}[S] \leftarrow p_x \quad \text{(or refresh age timer if entry exists)} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Forwarding:} \\
&\quad \text{if } D \in \text{MAC}: \text{forward out } \text{MAC}[D] \\
&\quad \text{else if } D \text{ is broadcast/multicast}: \text{flood all ports} \neq p_x \\
&\quad \text{else (unknown unicast)}: \text{flood all ports} \neq p_x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Aging.**
A timer (default 300 s) is reset whenever a frame is seen from a given source MAC. If the timer expires, the entry is purged, forcing re-learning (handles host mobility and address reuse).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Properties.**
- **Self-configuring:** No administrator intervention needed.
- **Adaptive:** Tables update with topology changes.
- **Loop-free only with STP:** Without STP, a looped topology causes **broadcast storms**.

**Diagram:** Use Mermaid Diagram 1 from Section 4 to score full 7 marks.

### Model Solution for Q.B-A(b):

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Cut-Through |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame fully buffered?** | Yes | No (only first 6 bytes) |
| **CRC Checked?** | Yes (drop on failure) | No |
| **Latency** | $L \times 8 / R$ (size-dependent) | $48 / R$ (~constant, ~48 ns at 1 Gbps) |
| **Error Propagation** | None | Propagates corrupt frames |
| **Memory Required** | Large (FIFO buffers) | Small |
| **Typical Use Case** | Enterprise LANs, mixed traffic | HPC, HFT, latency-critical backbones |
| **Variant** | ŌĆö | **Fragment-Free** (waits for 64 bytes) balances both |

**Preferred scenario for Store-and-Forward:** Enterprise office LANs where link error rates are non-negligible and bandwidth conservation is more important than latency.

**Preferred scenario for Cut-Through:** High-Frequency Trading (HFT) networks and HPC fabrics where every microsecond counts and error rates are negligible due to optical fiber links.

---

> **[Q.B-B] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** ŌĆö Module 3, 14 Marks *(Internal Alternative to Q.B-A)*
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D). With an example, show how the root bridge, root port, and designated ports are elected.*
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** *Differentiate between a bridge and a switch. Why is a switch considered a multi-port bridge with parallel bandwidth?*

### Model Solution for Q.B-B(a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Why STP Exists.**
In a meshed bridged network, parallel active paths create **loops**. A single broadcast frame is then copied endlessly, producing a **broadcast storm** that saturates the LAN. STP prunes the topology to a single loop-free tree.

**Step 2 ŌĆö BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit).**
Every bridge periodically (every **2 seconds**) multicasts a BPDU to the reserved address `01:80:C2:00:00:00`, containing:
- Root Bridge ID
- Sender's RPC to root
- Sender's Bridge ID
- Port cost and timers

**Step 3 ŌĆö Election Sequence.**
1. **Root Bridge Election:** Lowest Bridge ID = `{Priority (default 32768) + MAC}`.
2. **Root Port Election (per non-root bridge):** Port with minimum cost path to the root.
3. **Designated Port Election (per segment):** Port on the segment that has minimum cumulative RPC to the root.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Worked Example.**
*(Use the exact 3-bridge, 3-link scenario from Section 3.2 above for the model answer.)*
- Root = B3
- Root Port of B1 = L1 (cost 4)
- Root Port of B2 = L3 (cost 6)
- Designated port on S3 = B2-side of L3
- B1-side of L3 ŌåÆ **BLOCKED**
- Forwarding tree: B3 ŌåÉŌåÆ B1 (via L1), B3 ŌåÉŌåÆ B2 (via L2), B2 ŌåÉŌåÆ B1 (only B2-side active)

### Model Solution for Q.B-B(b):

| # | Bridge | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Typically 2ŌĆō16 ports | Typically 24, 48, or 96 ports |
| 2 | Software-based forwarding (older) | Hardware-based forwarding (ASIC / TCAM) |
| 3 | Shared backplane bus | Crossbar / shared-memory fabric ŌĆö **parallel** |
| 4 | Per-bridge aggregate bandwidth | **Dedicated bandwidth per port** (microsegmentation) |
| 5 | Latency higher due to CPU processing | Latency microseconds to nanoseconds |
| 6 | Used for segmenting legacy LANs | Used as the modern LAN access/distribution/core device |

**Why "multi-port bridge with parallel bandwidth":**
A switch is logically a bridge ŌĆö it still builds a MAC table and forwards based on destination MAC. However, unlike a bridge's shared bus, a switch uses a **switching fabric** (crossbar or shared memory) that allows **simultaneous, independent forwarding between any pair of ports**. This means with $N$ ports each at rate $R$, the **aggregate throughput is up to $N \times R / 2$** (assuming uniform traffic), versus a bridge's single shared bus limiting aggregate throughput to roughly $R$. This is the essence of **microsegmentation**: each port is its own dedicated collision domain with full wire-speed bandwidth.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic ŌĆö read carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing Listening and Learning states in STP** ŌĆö many students write "Learning state forwards frames." It does NOT. Both Listening and Learning are transitional states where BPDUs are exchanged; user frames are dropped.
> 2. **Forgetting that a switch is fundamentally a bridge** ŌĆö the question "differentiate bridge and switch" expects students to note that the *logical function is identical*; the *differences are physical* (port count, fabric, latency).
> 3. **Mixing up collision-domain and broadcast-domain segmentation** ŌĆö a Layer-2 switch DOES NOT segment broadcast domains. Routers / Layer-3 switches do. This single point is worth 2-3 marks.
> 4. **STP cost tie-breaker order:** Lowest RPC ŌåÆ Lowest Sender Bridge ID ŌåÆ Lowest Sender Port ID. Forgetting the **Bridge ID tie-breaker** is a common mistake.
> 5. **Forgetting the BPDU multicast address `01:80:C2:00:00:00`** ŌĆö KTU examiners love this. Memorize it.
> 6. **Stating "MAC table is statically configured"** ŌĆö it is dynamically learned via backward learning. Static entries are an *exception*, not the rule.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read before entering the exam hall):**
> - ŌśÉ Bridge = Layer-2 device connecting LAN segments; **Switch = multi-port bridge with parallel fabric**.
> - ŌśÉ Both operate at **Layer 2 (Data Link)** using **48-bit MAC addresses**.
> - ŌśÉ Backward learning records `{source MAC ŌåÆ arrival port}` on every frame.
> - ŌśÉ **Known unicast = selective forward**; **Unknown unicast / broadcast = flood** (except arrival port).
> - ŌśÉ **Aging timer = 300 s**; entry deleted if not refreshed.
> - ŌśÉ Switch segments **collision domains per port** but **NOT broadcast domains** (router does that).
> - ŌśÉ Three switching modes: **Store-and-Forward** (safe, slow), **Cut-Through** (fast, no CRC), **Fragment-Free** (compromise).
> - ŌśÉ Three bridge types: **Transparent**, **Source Routing**, **Translational**.
> - ŌśÉ **BPDU destination MAC = `01:80:C2:00:00:00`** (reserved, not forwarded).
> - ŌśÉ **STP default port cost (802.1D): 1000 / Bandwidth(Mbps)**.
> - ŌśÉ **STP default bridge priority = 32768**; **Hello Time = 2 s**, **Max Age = 20 s**, **Forward Delay = 15 s**.
> - ŌśÉ STP election order: **Root Bridge ŌåÆ Root Port ŌåÆ Designated Port ŌåÆ Block remaining**.
> - ŌśÉ Tie-breaker: lowest Bridge ID = lowest Priority ŌåÆ lowest MAC.
> - ŌśÉ STP port states: **Disabled ŌåÆ Blocking ŌåÆ Listening ŌåÆ Learning ŌåÆ Forwarding** (15 s in Listening, 15 s in Learning).
> - ŌśÉ Cut-through latency on 1 Gbps link Ōēł **48 ns**; Store-and-forward for 1518-byte frame Ōēł **12.1 ┬Ąs**.
> - ŌśÉ Translational bridge performs **frame format conversion** between heterogeneous LANs.
> - ŌśÉ Source Routing Bridge used in legacy **Token Ring (802.5)** ŌĆö host specifies complete route in RIF.
> - ŌśÉ Modern campus networks use **Layer-2 switches at access** + **Layer-3 switches / routers at core/distribution** for hierarchical broadcast containment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing: Routing and Forwarding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing: Routing and Forwarding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Routing and Forwarding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Forwarding** is the **local**, **per-packet** action performed by a router to transfer a packet from an incoming interface to the appropriate outgoing interface, based on the destination address and the contents of the **Forwarding Table (FT)**. It is a *data-plane* function that operates in **microseconds to milliseconds**.
>
> **Routing** is the **network-wide**, *end-to-end* process of determining the **optimal path** that packets should take from a source to a destination across one or more interconnected networks. It is a *control-plane* function that builds the **Routing Table (RT)** using routing algorithms and protocols, operating in **seconds to minutes**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the **Indian Postal System**:

- **Forwarding** is like the **postman at a single sorting office**. He looks at the PIN code (destination address) of every letter, consults his local dispatch list (Forwarding Table), and drops the letter into the correct bag (outgoing interface). The postman does not care *how* the PIN code structure was decided ŌĆö he just executes it.

- **Routing** is like the **postal headquarters deciding which regional sorting hub connects to which city**. The headquarters runs algorithms (cost, distance, load) to build a master plan (Routing Table) that every postman later consults. This plan is revised periodically as roads, traffic, and conditions change.

In short: **Routing decides the path; Forwarding executes the path.**

## 1.3 Key Distinction at a Glance

| Aspect | Forwarding | Routing |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Single router (local) | Entire network (global) |
| Plane | Data Plane (fast path) | Control Plane (slow path) |
| Time scale | Per-packet (nsŌĆōms) | Per-network change (sŌĆōmin) |
| Table used | Forwarding Table (FIB) | Routing Table (RIB) |
| Performed by | Hardware (ASIC/TCAM) | Software (routing daemon) |
| Frequency | Billions of times/sec | Periodic / event-driven |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** A very common Part A (3-mark) question asks students to differentiate between **Routing and Forwarding**. Memorize the table above; examiners award the third mark for a crisp one-line distinction (e.g., *"Forwarding is local and per-packet; Routing is network-wide and per-session."*).

## 1.4 Why Both Functions Exist in a Router

Modern routers are architecturally split because of **speed vs. intelligence**:

- **Forwarding must be fast** ŌĆö every nanosecond matters when handling 100 Gbps links. Hence it is pushed to **hardware** (ASICs, TCAMs, NPUs).
- **Routing can be slow** ŌĆö recomputing shortest paths after a topology change does not need to be done for every packet. Hence it runs as a **software process** (e.g., `ospfd`, `bgpd` daemons in Quagga/FRR).

This separation is the foundation of **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)**, where the control plane (routing) is even taken out of the router and placed in a remote **SDN controller**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forwarding Function ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

When a packet arrives at an incoming interface, the router executes the following sequence:

1. **Link-layer decapsulation** ŌĆö Strip the Layer-2 header (Ethernet, PPP, Frame Relay) to expose the Layer-3 datagram (IPv4/IPv6).
2. **Header validation** ŌĆö Verify IP version, header checksum, and decrement the **Time-To-Live (TTL)** field by 1. If TTL becomes 0, the packet is dropped and an **ICMP Time Exceeded** message is sent back to the source.
3. **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** ŌĆö Search the **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** for the entry that matches the destination IP with the **longest subnet mask**. This is performed in hardware using TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory).
4. **Lookup outcome** ŌĆö Three possible results:
   - **Match found** ŌåÆ Forward to the outgoing interface and next-hop.
   - **Default route match (0.0.0.0/0)** ŌåÆ Forward to the gateway.
   - **No match at all** ŌåÆ Drop the packet and send **ICMP Destination Network Unreachable**.
5. **Link-layer encapsulation** ŌĆö Encapsulate the IP datagram inside the Layer-2 frame of the outgoing link and transmit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Longest Prefix Match (LPM)?** CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) allows variable-length subnet masks. A packet for `10.1.2.3` may match both `10.0.0.0/8` and `10.1.0.0/16`. The router must choose `10.1.0.0/16` because it is the **most specific** (longest) match. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

## 2.2 The Routing Function ŌĆö Operational Steps

The control plane performs the following logical steps:

1. **Topology discovery** ŌĆö Each router learns about its directly connected links and their costs (via OSPF hello packets, RIP updates, or static configuration).
2. **Information dissemination** ŌĆö Link-state advertisements (LSAs) or distance-vector updates are flooded to neighbors.
3. **Route computation** ŌĆö A shortest-path algorithm (**Dijkstra** for link-state, **Bellman-Ford** for distance-vector) computes the best path to every reachable destination.
4. **Installation in FIB** ŌĆö The best routes are copied from the Routing Information Base (RIB) into the Forwarding Information Base (FIB) for fast hardware lookup.
5. **Continuous maintenance** ŌĆö On link failures or metric changes, the algorithm re-runs and the FIB is updated.

## 2.3 Classification of Routing Algorithms

- **Static vs. Dynamic**
  - *Static* ŌĆö Manually configured by the administrator; does not adapt to failures.
  - *Dynamic* ŌĆö Automatically adapts using routing protocols.

- **Global vs. Decentralized**
  - *Global (Link-State)* ŌĆö Every router has complete topology; uses Dijkstra's algorithm.
  - *Decentralized (Distance-Vector)* ŌĆö Each router knows only its directly connected neighbors; uses Bellman-Ford.

- **Intra-domain vs. Inter-domain**
  - *Intra-domain (Interior Gateway Protocol ŌĆö IGP)* ŌĆö OSPF, IS-IS, RIP operate within a single Autonomous System (AS).
  - *Inter-domain (Exterior Gateway Protocol ŌĆö EGP)* ŌĆö **BGP** operates between Autonomous Systems across the public Internet.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 ŌĆö Must-Memorize Equations and Metrics**

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $D(v) = \min\{D(u) + c(u,v)\}$ | Dijkstra's relaxation step for node $v$ via neighbor $u$ | $D$ = shortest distance, $c$ = link cost |
| $D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)}\{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Bellman-Ford equation at node $x$ for destination $y$ | Iterated until convergence |
| $n(n-1)/2$ | Number of iterations for Bellman-Ford in a graph of $n$ nodes | Standard stopping criterion |
| $\text{Hop Count}$ | RIP metric ŌĆö number of routers traversed | Integer $\geq 0$; max = **15** (16 = infinity) |
| $\text{Cost}_{\text{OSPF}} = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}}$ | OSPF interface cost | Reference BW = $10^8$ bps (default); min cost = **1** |
| $\text{AS-Path Length}$ | BGP path-selection metric | Number of AS hops; shorter is preferred |
| $\text{TX} - \text{RX}$ | Latency-based metric | Seconds; rare in production |
| $0.0.0.0/0$ | Default route prefix | Lowest LPM priority |
| $\text{Longest Prefix Match}$ | Forwarding tie-breaker rule | Subnet mask length wins |
| $\text{TTL} = \text{TTL} - 1$ | Per-hop decrement during forwarding | If $\text{TTL} = 0$ ŌåÆ drop + ICMP |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall ŌĆö Table Symbol Rule:** When writing Bellman-Ford in your answer sheet, write $c(x,v) + D_v(y)$ with curly braces around the set, *never* use the vertical bar character $\vert$ inside a markdown table. On your exam paper, use plain absolute-value bars $|\cdot|$; the table rule above is for *digital notes only* to prevent rendering breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forwarding engines** powered by **TCAM** are why your home Wi-Fi router can handle **millions of packets per second** while doing complex LPM in O(1) time.
- **Routing algorithms** underpin the global Internet ŌĆö when a submarine cable in the Red Sea is cut, BGP converges in seconds, and OSPF re-converges within an Autonomous System in milliseconds, keeping your Netflix stream alive through an alternate path.
- **Autonomous Systems (AS)** form the hierarchy that makes a network of ~70,000 interconnected networks scalable; without AS-level routing (BGP), the Internet flat routing table would be infeasible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Dijkstra's Link-State Algorithm

**Network Topology (5 nodes, link costs shown on edges):**

Nodes: **A, B, C, D, E**

Edges and costs (bidirectional, symmetric):
- AŌĆōB: **4**
- AŌĆōC: **2**
- BŌĆōC: **1**
- BŌĆōD: **5**
- CŌĆōD: **8**
- CŌĆōE: **10**
- DŌĆōE: **2**

**Source node: A. Compute shortest path to all other nodes using Dijkstra's algorithm.**

### Initialization (Step 0)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{A\} \\
D(A) &= 0,\; D(B)=\infty,\; D(C)=\infty,\; D(D)=\infty,\; D(E)=\infty \\
p(A) &= \text{nil},\; p(*)=\text{unknown}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Iteration 1 ŌĆö Examine neighbors of A

Neighbors of A: B (cost 4), C (cost 2).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(B) &= \min\{\infty,\; 0 + 4\} = 4,\; p(B)=A \\
D(C) &= \min\{\infty,\; 0 + 2\} = 2,\; p(C)=A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Select minimum unvisited:** $D(C)=2$.

$N' = \{A, C\}$

### Iteration 2 ŌĆö Examine neighbors of C

Neighbors of C already in $N'$: A. Neighbors not in $N'$: B, D, E.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(B) &= \min\{4,\; 2 + 1\} = \min\{4,3\} = 3,\; p(B)=C \\
D(D) &= \min\{\infty,\; 2 + 8\} = 10,\; p(D)=C \\
D(E) &= \min\{\infty,\; 2 + 10\} = 12,\; p(E)=C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Select minimum unvisited:** $D(B)=3$.

$N' = \{A, C, B\}$

### Iteration 3 ŌĆö Examine neighbors of B

Neighbors of B not in $N'$: D (cost 5).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(D) &= \min\{10,\; 3 + 5\} = \min\{10,8\} = 8,\; p(D)=B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Select minimum unvisited:** $D(D)=8$.

$N' = \{A, C, B, D\}$

### Iteration 4 ŌĆö Examine neighbors of D

Neighbors of D not in $N'$: E (cost 2).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(E) &= \min\{12,\; 8 + 2\} = \min\{12,10\} = 10,\; p(E)=D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Select minimum unvisited:** $D(E)=10$.

$N' = \{A, C, B, D, E\}$ ŌĆö **Algorithm terminates.**

### Final Routing Table at Node A

| Destination | Shortest Cost | Next Hop | Shortest Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | 3 | C | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | C | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 8 | C | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D |
| E | 10 | C | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (7-mark sub-part):** When asked to "show shortest path from A to E using Dijkstra's algorithm", you must tabulate $N'$, $D(*)$, and $p(*)$ after every iteration. Skipping iterations loses **at least 2 marks**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Bellman-Ford Distance-Vector Algorithm

**Same 5-node topology. Show the convergence of node B's distance vector to all destinations.**

### Initial State at Node B (Step 0)

Node B knows only its direct neighbors. Initial distances:

$$
D_B = \begin{bmatrix} D_B(A)=4 \\ D_B(B)=0 \\ D_B(C)=1 \\ D_B(D)=5 \\ D_B(E)=\infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö B receives DVs from neighbors A, C, D

**From A:** $D_A = (0, 4, 2, \infty, \infty)$
**From C:** $D_C = (2, 1, 0, 8, 10)$
**From D:** $D_D = (\infty, 5, 8, 0, 2)$

### Apply Bellman-Ford Equation at B for each destination y

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)}\{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}
$$

**For destination A:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_B(A) &= \min\{c(B,A)+D_A(A),\; c(B,C)+D_C(A),\; c(B,D)+D_D(A)\} \\
&= \min\{4+0,\; 1+2,\; 5+\infty\} \\
&= \min\{4,\; 3,\; \infty\} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**For destination C:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_B(C) &= \min\{4+D_A(C),\; 1+D_C(C),\; 5+D_D(C)\} \\
&= \min\{4+2,\; 1+0,\; 5+8\} = \min\{6, 1, 13\} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For destination D:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_B(D) &= \min\{4+D_A(D),\; 1+D_C(D),\; 5+D_D(D)\} \\
&= \min\{4+\infty,\; 1+8,\; 5+0\} = \min\{\infty, 9, 5\} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**For destination E:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_B(E) &= \min\{4+D_A(E),\; 1+D_C(E),\; 5+D_D(E)\} \\
&= \min\{4+\infty,\; 1+10,\; 5+2\} = \min\{\infty, 11, 7\} = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö B sends new DV to all neighbors

B's new distance vector after one iteration:

$$
D_B^{\text{new}} = (3, 0, 1, 5, 7)
$$

Note the key observation: B now reaches A in 3 (via C), even though the direct BŌĆōA link costs 4. The "routing-by-rumor" advantage of DV routing is visible.

### Number of Iterations to Converge

In a graph of $n = 5$ nodes, Bellman-Ford requires at most $n - 1 = 4$ iterations, or $n(n-1)/2 = 10$ relaxed edges. The exam may ask: *"Why $n-1$ iterations?"* ŌĆö because the longest possible simple path in an $n$-node graph has $n - 1$ edges.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford)

| Property | Dijkstra (Link-State) | Bellman-Ford (Distance-Vector) |
|---|---|---|
| Information available | Global (full topology) | Local (neighbors only) |
| Computation | Centralized per node | Iterative, distributed |
| Complexity | $O(n^2)$ naive, $O((n+e)\log n)$ with heap | $O(n \cdot e)$ per iteration |
| Handles negative weights? | No | Yes |
| Count-to-infinity problem? | No (has full view) | Yes (susceptible) |
| Convergence speed | Fast ($O(n^2)$ once) | Slow (up to $n-1$ iterations) |
| Bandwidth overhead | High (floods LSAs) | Low (small periodic DVs) |
| Used by | OSPF, IS-IS | RIP, IGRP (legacy) |
| KTU typical marks | 7 marks | 7 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flow: Routing vs. Forwarding Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Network Topology Change / Startup"] --> B["Routing Algorithm: Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford"]
    B --> C["Routing Table RIB built"]
    C --> D["Best Routes Installed into Forwarding Table FIB"]
    D --> E["Packet Arrives at Incoming Interface"]
    E --> F["Decrement TTL and Check = 0?"]
    F -- Yes --> G["Drop Packet and Send ICMP Time Exceeded"]
    F -- No --> H["Longest Prefix Match Lookup in FIB"]
    H -- No Match --> I["Drop Packet and Send ICMP Net Unreachable"]
    H -- Default Match --> J["Forward to Default Gateway"]
    H -- Exact or LPM Match --> K["Encapsulate in Outgoing L2 Frame"]
    K --> L["Transmit on Outgoing Interface"]
    L --> M["Next Router Repeats Forwarding"]
```

## 4.2 Router Internal Architecture (Control Plane + Data Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CP["CONTROL PLANE - Software - Slow Path"]
        A1["Routing Protocol Daemon: ospfd / bgpd / ripd"]
        A2["Routing Information Base RIB"]
        A1 --> A2
    end
    subgraph DP["DATA PLANE - Hardware - Fast Path"]
        B1["Line Cards / Interfaces"]
        B2["Forwarding Information Base FIB in TCAM"]
        B3["Longest Prefix Match Engine"]
        B4["Queueing and Scheduling"]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> B3
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B1
    end
    A2 -- "Installs best routes" --> B2
```

## 4.3 Autonomous System Hierarchy and Protocol Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AS1["Autonomous System 100 - ISP A"]
        O1["OSPF Area 0 Backbone"]
        O2["OSPF Area 1"]
        O1 <--> O2
    end
    subgraph AS2["Autonomous System 200 - ISP B"]
        O3["OSPF Area 0 Backbone"]
        O4["OSPF Area 2"]
        O3 <--> O4
    end
    AS1 <-- "eBGP session TCP 179" --> AS2
    O1 -. "iBGP full mesh" .-> O1
    O3 -. "iBGP full mesh" .-> O3
```

## 4.4 Algorithm Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Need to choose routing algorithm?"] --> B{"Network size and dynamics?"}
    B -- "Small stable LAN" --> C["Use Static Routes"]
    B -- "Large dynamic enterprise" --> D{"Need fast convergence?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Link-State: OSPF or IS-IS"]
    D -- "Simple best-effort" --> F["Distance-Vector: RIP"]
    B -- "Inter-ISP / Internet" --> G["Path-Vector: BGP version 4"]
    E --> H["Dijkstra Algorithm in each area"]
    F --> I["Bellman-Ford iterations up to n minus 1"]
    G --> J["AS-Path and policy-based selection"]
```
