# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between routing and forwarding. State the plane (data/control) in which each operates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Routing | Forwarding |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Computes paths across the network | Moves packet from input to output port |
| Scope | Network-wide, end-to-end | Local to a single router |
| Plane | Control plane | Data plane |
| Implementation | Software (routing daemon) | Hardware (TCAM, ASIC) |
| Frequency | Triggered by topology changes | Performed for every packet |
| Table used | Routing Information Base (RIB) | Forwarding Information Base (FIB) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Forwarding is the **local data-plane action** of using a pre-computed table to deliver each packet; routing is the **global control-plane process** of building and maintaining that table.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by Longest Prefix Match (LPM)? Why is it required in CIDR-based forwarding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Longest Prefix Match is the rule used by IP routers during forwarding where, among all routing table entries whose prefix matches the destination IP address, the entry with the **longest subnet mask** (i.e., most specific prefix) is selected for forwarding.
- **Why required (1.5 marks):** With **Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)**, prefixes can be of any length. A single destination address may match multiple prefixes (e.g., `10.1.2.3` matches `10.0.0.0/8` and `10.1.0.0/16`). LPM guarantees the most specific ŌĆö and therefore most accurate ŌĆö route is chosen, enabling route aggregation and hierarchical addressing without ambiguity.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts, and there is **internal choice**. You must attempt **exactly one** of the two alternatives. Each alternative below is a complete 14-mark question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ŌĆö Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the working of the **Distance-Vector Routing** algorithm using the **Bellman-Ford equation**. State two advantages and two disadvantages of this approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the network given below, construct the **shortest-path tree and routing table at node A** using **Dijkstra's Link-State algorithm**. Show all iterations clearly. **(7 marks)**

Topology:

```
            4
      A ---------- B
      |  \         |  \
    2 |   \ 1      | 5  \
      |    \       |     \
      C ----D------E      F
            |             |
            6             3
            |             |
            G-------------H
                 2
```

Edges and costs: AŌĆōB=4, AŌĆōC=2, AŌĆōD=1, BŌĆōE=5, BŌĆōF=3, CŌĆōD=6, DŌĆōE=2, EŌĆōF=4, EŌĆōG=6, FŌĆōH=3, GŌĆōH=2

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bellman-Ford Distance-Vector ŌĆö 7 marks**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Distance-Vector is a decentralized routing algorithm in which each router maintains a table (vector) of the estimated distance to every possible destination. The table is exchanged only with **directly connected neighbors**.

2. **Bellman-Ford Equation (2 marks):** For a router $x$ and destination $y$, the shortest distance is computed as:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)}\{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}
$$

where $N(x)$ is the set of $x$'s neighbors, $c(x,v)$ is the cost of the direct link from $x$ to $v$, and $D_v(y)$ is $v$'s currently known distance to $y$.

3. **Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
   - Initialize: $D_x(y) = c(x,y)$ for direct neighbors; $\infty$ otherwise.
   - Repeat: On receiving a DV from neighbor $v$, recompute using the equation above.
   - Terminate: After $n - 1$ iterations for an $n$-node graph, or earlier if no DV changes in a full round (convergence).

4. **Advantages (1 mark):**
   - Simple to implement; low memory footprint.
   - Low per-update bandwidth (only neighbors exchange small vectors).

5. **Disadvantages (1 mark):**
   - Suffers from **count-to-infinity** problem on link failures.
   - Slow convergence (up to $n - 1$ iterations).

---

**(b) Dijkstra's Algorithm Solution ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Iteration Table at Node A:**

| Step | $N'$ | $D(B), p(B)$ | $D(C), p(C)$ | $D(D), p(D)$ | $D(E), p(E)$ | $D(F), p(F)$ | $D(G), p(G)$ | $D(H), p(H)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | {A} | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō |
| 1 | {A,D} | 4, A | 2, A | **1, A** | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō | Ōł×, ŌĆō |
| 2 | {A,D,C} | 3, C | **2, A** | 1, A | 3, D | Ōł×, ŌĆō | 7, D | Ōł×, ŌĆō |
| 3 | {A,D,C,B} | **3, C** | 2, A | 1, A | 3, D | 6, B | 7, D | Ōł×, ŌĆō |
| 4 | {A,D,C,B,E} | 3, C | 2, A | 1, A | **3, D** | 6, B | 7, D | Ōł×, ŌĆō |
| 5 | {A,D,C,B,E,F} | 3, C | 2, A | 1, A | 3, D | **6, B** | 7, D | 9, F |
| 6 | {A,D,C,B,E,F,G} | 3, C | 2, A | 1, A | 3, D | 6, B | **7, D** | 9, F |
| 7 | {A,D,C,B,E,F,G,H} | 3, C | 2, A | 1, A | 3, D | 6, B | 7, D | **9, F** |

**Final Routing Table at A:**

| Destination | Shortest Cost | Next Hop | Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | 3 | C | AŌåÆCŌåÆB |
| C | 2 | C | AŌåÆC |
| D | 1 | D | AŌåÆD |
| E | 3 | D | AŌåÆDŌåÆE |
| F | 6 | B | AŌåÆCŌåÆBŌåÆF |
| G | 7 | D | AŌåÆDŌåÆG |
| H | 9 | B | AŌåÆCŌåÆBŌåÆFŌåÆH |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls for Dijkstra:**
> 1. **Skipping the iteration table** ŌĆö A 7-mark sub-part requires showing the table evolving step-by-step. Writing only the final answer loses 4ŌĆō5 marks.
> 2. **Wrong tie-breaking** ŌĆö If two unvisited nodes have the same minimum $D$ value, the convention is to break ties by **lowest node label first** (A < B < C ...). Document your tie-breaking rule to score the mark.
> 3. **Confusing predecessor $p(v)$ with next-hop** ŌĆö The next-hop from A is the **second node on the path**, not the immediate predecessor. Trace the path backward from the destination to A through $p(*)$ entries.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ŌĆö Sample]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a router** separating the **control plane and data plane**. State the role of the Forwarding Information Base (FIB) and the Routing Information Base (RIB). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** protocol. State its metric, the concept of **areas**, and the role of the **Designated Router (DR)** in a broadcast network. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Router Architecture ŌĆö 7 marks**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** See the diagram in Section 4.2 above (Control Plane + Data Plane split).

2. **Role of RIB (2 marks):** The **Routing Information Base (RIB)** is a software-maintained table in the **control plane** that stores *all* routes learned from all routing protocols (static, OSPF, BGP, etc.), along with the source protocol and administrative distance. The router's routing daemon selects the **best route** for each prefix and **installs it into the FIB**. The RIB is a "wishlist of all possible paths"; the FIB is the "operational dispatch list."

3. **Role of FIB (2 marks):** The **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** is a hardware-optimized table (typically in **TCAM**) in the **data plane** that contains only the *currently best* route for each prefix, pre-resolved with the **outgoing interface** and **next-hop MAC address**. The FIB is consulted for every incoming packet at line rate using **Longest Prefix Match**.

---

**(b) OSPF ŌĆö 7 marks**

1. **Overview (2 marks):** **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** is a **link-state, intra-domain (IGP)** routing protocol defined in **RFC 2328** for IPv4 (and **RFC 5340** for OSPFv3 / IPv6). It uses **Dijkstra's algorithm** to compute shortest paths and **floods Link-State Advertisements (LSAs)** within an Autonomous System.

2. **OSPF Metric (1 mark):** The default OSPF cost of an interface is:

$$
\text{Cost} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}} \right\rfloor
$$

The default reference bandwidth is **$10^8$ bps = 100 Mbps**, giving Cost = 1 for 100 Mbps links, 10 for 10 Mbps Ethernet, and 1 for 1 Gbps (which is why modern deployments override the reference to 10 Gbps or higher).

3. **OSPF Areas (2 marks):** OSPF uses a **two-level hierarchy**:
   - **Backbone Area (Area 0)** ŌĆö The core; all other areas must connect to it.
   - **Non-backbone Areas** ŌĆö Each Area has its own topology database; routers within an Area share the same LSDB. **Area Border Routers (ABRs)** summarize routes between areas, reducing LSA flooding and CPU/memory consumption.

4. **Designated Router (DR) and BDR (2 marks):** On **broadcast networks** (e.g., Ethernet) and **NBMA networks** (e.g., Frame Relay), OSPF elects a **Designated Router (DR)** and a **Backup DR (BDR)** to reduce the number of adjacencies from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$. All other routers form adjacencies *only* with the DR/BDR, which then floods LSAs. The DR is elected based on the **highest OSPF priority** (default 1); ties are broken by the **highest Router ID** (a 32-bit unique identifier, usually the highest loopback IP).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls for OSPF:**
> 1. **Writing the cost formula as Reference/Interface without the floor brackets** ŌĆö Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if the integer nature of OSPF cost is not stated.
> 2. **Forgetting that all non-backbone areas must connect to Area 0** ŌĆö A "virtual link" can be used to repair a disconnected area, but stating that an area can exist independently of Area 0 is a **factual error** worth 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing DR election with Router ID election** ŌĆö Both use highest-priority-then-highest-ID logic, but the *Router ID* is a permanent property of the OSPF process, while the *DR role* is per-segment and can change on failure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 3**

- **Routing vs. Forwarding:** Routing is the **control-plane** process of *building* the routing table; forwarding is the **data-plane** process of *using* the forwarding table to move each packet.
- **Forwarding Table (FIB)** lives in **TCAM** for O(1) hardware lookup; **Routing Table (RIB)** is software-maintained.
- **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** is the fundamental forwarding rule in CIDR; the most specific (longest mask) prefix wins.
- **Default route (0.0.0.0/0)** has the lowest LPM priority and is used only when no other match exists.
- **TTL** is decremented at every hop; **TTL = 0 ŌćÆ drop + ICMP Time Exceeded**.
- **Dijkstra's algorithm** requires **global topology** (link-state); each router independently runs it on the flooded LSDB.
- **Bellman-Ford** uses only **neighbor DVs**; converges in at most **$n - 1$ iterations** for $n$ nodes.
- **Dijkstra cannot handle negative edge weights**; **Bellman-Ford can**.
- **OSPF** is a link-state IGP using **Dijkstra**; cost = $\lfloor$ Reference BW / Interface BW $\rfloor$.
- **RIP** is a distance-vector IGP using **Bellman-Ford**; max hop count = **15**; vulnerable to **count-to-infinity**.
- **BGP** is the inter-domain **path-vector EGP** that runs over **TCP port 179**; uses **AS-Path** as the primary attribute.
- **Autonomous System (AS)** is a single administrative domain under one routing policy; identified by a globally unique **16-bit or 32-bit ASN**.
- **DR/BDR** in OSPF reduces $O(n^2)$ adjacencies to $O(n)$ on broadcast segments.
- **Area 0** is the mandatory OSPF backbone; all other areas must connect to it.
- **Soft-state vs. hard-state:** OSPF periodically refreshes LSAs every **30 minutes**; BGP does not refresh routes unless the path changes.
- **SDN** cleanly separates control plane (remote controller) from data plane (switches), generalizing the routing/forwarding split.

**End of Module 3 Notes ŌĆö Routing: Routing and Forwarding**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Routing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routing** is the process of selecting a path for traffic across one or more networks, performed at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI/TCP-IP reference model. The decision-making entity is the **Router**, which maintains a **Routing Table** mapping destination networks to outgoing interfaces and next-hop addresses.

Routing enables **inter-network communication** by forwarding IP packets from a source host to a destination host, possibly traversing multiple intermediate routers. The router's fundamental job is to answer one question for every incoming packet: *"From which interface and to which next-hop should I forward this packet?"*

## 1.2 Static Routing ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Routing** is a non-adaptive routing technique in which the network administrator manually configures and maintains the routes in the routing table. Routes do not change in response to network topology changes, traffic load, or link failures.

- Each entry is **predefined** by the network engineer.
- The router does **not exchange routing information** with its neighbours.
- The path to a destination is **fixed** until manually altered.

### Real-World Analogy: Static Routing

Imagine a **bus conductor in a small town with only three fixed routes**. The conductor has memorized the exact sequence of stops on each route. If a new stop is added, a road is closed, or a passenger wants to go somewhere new, the conductor cannot adapt ŌĆö they will continue to follow the old memorized path. Only when the **manager (network administrator)** gives new instructions does the conductor update. This is precisely how static routing behaves: rigid, predictable, but incapable of self-recovery.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Static routing is the **most preferred answer** for small, stub networks (single exit-point networks) and forms the foundation for understanding dynamic routing.

## 1.3 Dynamic Routing ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Routing** is an adaptive routing technique in which routers automatically build, maintain, and update their routing tables by exchanging information with neighbouring routers using a **Routing Protocol** (e.g., RIP, OSPF, EIGRP, BGP).

- Routers **learn** topology changes in real-time.
- Routes are computed dynamically based on a **metric** (hop count, cost, bandwidth, delay).
- The network is **self-healing** in the face of link failures.

### Real-World Analogy: Dynamic Routing

Now imagine a **modern Google Maps driver**. The driver (router) is constantly talking to the cloud (routing protocol), receiving updates about road closures, traffic jams, and newly opened highways. If the usual route is blocked, the driver instantly recalculates a new path. The driver is **adaptive**, **intelligent**, and **always aware** of the live state of the network. This is dynamic routing ŌĆö a self-learning, self-updating system.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Routing Table** | A data table stored in a router listing routes to particular network destinations |
| **Next-Hop** | The IP address of the next router to which a packet is forwarded |
| **Metric** | A numerical value used by routing protocols to determine the best path |
| **Administrative Distance (AD)** | A value (0ŌĆō255) representing the trustworthiness of a route source |
| **Convergence** | The state when all routers in a network have identical, updated routing information |
| **Hop Count** | The number of intermediate routers a packet must traverse |
| **Autonomous System (AS)** | A collection of IP networks under a single administrative domain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Routing is to a printed map as Dynamic Routing is to live GPS navigation.** Both serve the same purpose, but differ radically in adaptability.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Routing ŌĆö Operational Mechanics

In static routing, the following are pre-configured by the administrator:

1. **Destination Network Address** (e.g., 192.168.10.0/24)
2. **Subnet Mask** (e.g., 255.255.255.0)
3. **Next-Hop IP Address** OR **Exit Interface**
4. **Administrative Distance** (default = **1** for static routes)

### Types of Static Routes

| Type | Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Standard Static Route** | Manually defined path to a specific destination network |
| **Default Static Route** | Catch-all route (0.0.0.0/0) used when no specific route matches |
| **Floating Static Route** | Backup static route with a higher AD that activates only when the primary fails |
| **Summary Static Route** | A single route that summarizes a group of contiguous networks |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Default Route** is a special static route used by a router to forward all packets whose destination does not match any specific entry in the routing table. It is symbolically represented as `0.0.0.0/0`.

### Advantages & Disadvantages of Static Routing

**Advantages**
- Zero routing protocol overhead (no CPU/RAM/Bandwidth consumed by route computation)
- **Predictable** behaviour; excellent for security-sensitive networks
- No convergence delay
- Simple to configure in small topologies

**Disadvantages**
- **Does not scale** to large networks
- **No fault tolerance** ŌĆö if a link fails, packets are dropped until manually reconfigured
- Administrative burden grows linearly with network size
- Not suitable for topologies with frequent changes

## 2.2 Dynamic Routing ŌĆö Operational Mechanics

Dynamic routing relies on **Routing Protocols**, which are broadly classified as:

### A. Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP) ŌĆö Used within an Autonomous System
- **Distance Vector Protocols**: RIP (RIP v1, RIP v2), IGRP
- **Link State Protocols**: OSPF, IS-IS
- **Hybrid Protocols**: EIGRP (Cisco proprietary)

### B. Exterior Gateway Protocols (EGP) ŌĆö Used between Autonomous Systems
- **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)** ŌĆö the protocol that runs the Internet

## 2.3 Distance Vector Routing ŌĆö The Bellman-Ford Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distance Vector** means: each router maintains a vector (list) of distances to all known destinations. Routers periodically share their entire routing table with **directly connected neighbours only**. This is also called **"Routing by Rumour"**.

### Core Algorithm: Bellman-Ford Equation

The fundamental update rule is:

$$ D_x(y) = \min \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \} \quad \text{for all neighbours } v $$

Where:
- $D_x(y)$ = The shortest known distance from router $x$ to destination $y$
- $c(x,v)$ = The cost (metric) of the link from $x$ to neighbour $v$
- $D_v(y)$ = The distance advertised by neighbour $v$ to destination $y$
- The minimum is taken over **all** neighbours $v$ of $x$

### Worked Conceptual Example

Consider three routers A, B, C in a triangle.
- Cost AŌĆōB = 2
- Cost BŌĆōC = 3
- Cost AŌĆōC = 10

**Router A's distance to C via direct link:** 10
**Router A's distance to C via B:** 2 (AŌåÆB) + 3 (BŌåÆC) = 5

Since $5 < 10$, A chooses the path via B, giving $D_A(C) = 5$.

### Count to Infinity Problem

> [!WARNING]
> A classic flaw in pure distance vector protocols is the **Count to Infinity Problem** when a link fails. Routers may keep incrementing their metric indefinitely, treating the failed link as a valid path with increasing cost. Standard mitigations include **Split Horizon**, **Split Horizon with Poison Reverse**, and setting a **Maximum Hop Count** (e.g., RIP uses **15 hops**, with **16 = infinity**).

## 2.4 Link State Routing ŌĆö Dijkstra's Philosophy

> [!NOTE]
> **Link State** protocols take a radically different approach. Every router builds a complete **Link State Database (LSDB)** ŌĆö a full map of the network ŌĆö by flooding **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** to **all** routers in the area. Each router then runs **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** independently to compute the best path to every destination.

### Key Properties
- **Fast convergence** (compared to distance vector)
- **No count-to-infinity** problem
- **Hierarchical design** (areas in OSPF)
- **CPU and memory intensive** for very large networks

### Dijkstra's Algorithm ŌĆö Cost Computation

For a node $s$ (source) and a destination $d$, Dijkstra's algorithm computes:

$$ \text{SPF}(s, d) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s,d}} \sum_{(u,v) \in P} w(u,v) $$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}_{s,d}$ is the set of all paths from $s$ to $d$
- $w(u,v)$ is the weight of the edge from $u$ to $v$
- The minimum is taken over **all possible paths**

## 2.5 Routing Metrics ŌĆö KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Used By | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hop Count** | Number of routers traversed | RIP | Hops |
| **Cost (Cisco)** | Reference Bandwidth / Interface Bandwidth | OSPF | Unitless |
| **Bandwidth** | $\text{BW} = \text{max bits/sec on path}$ | OSPF, EIGRP | bps |
| **Delay** | Sum of delays along path | EIGRP | $\mu s$ |
| **Load** | Saturation of the outgoing interface | EIGRP | 1ŌĆō255 |
| **Reliability** | Error-free transmission rate | EIGRP | 1ŌĆō255 |
| **MTU** | Maximum Transmission Unit of path | Path MTU Discovery | Bytes |
| **Composite EIGRP** | $\text{Metric} = \left[ K_1 \cdot BW + \frac{K_2 \cdot BW}{256 - \text{Load}} + K_3 \cdot \text{Delay} \right] \cdot \dfrac{K_5}{K_4 + \text{Rel}}$ | EIGRP | Unitless |

> [!NOTE]
> In RIP, the maximum hop count is **15**; a metric of **16** is treated as **infinity** (unreachable). This bounds the count-to-infinity problem.

## 2.6 KTU Comparison Matrix: Static vs Dynamic

| Parameter | Static Routing | Dynamic Routing |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration | Manual | Automatic |
| Adaptability | None (Rigid) | High (Adaptive) |
| Routing Protocol Overhead | None | CPU, RAM, Bandwidth |
| Convergence Time | Instant (no convergence needed) | Depends on protocol (seconds to minutes) |
| Scalability | Poor (small networks) | Excellent (large networks) |
| Security | High (no protocol advertisements) | Lower (susceptible to route spoofing) |
| Fault Tolerance | Manual recovery required | Automatic failover |
| Use Case | Stub networks, default route | Enterprise cores, ISP backbones |
| Administrative Distance (default) | 1 | OSPF=110, RIP=120, EIGRP=90, BGP eBGP=20 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Static routing** is the silent workhorse of **stub networks**, **point-to-point WAN links**, and **default gateways** at customer premises.
- **Dynamic routing** is the lifeblood of the **Internet** itself ŌĆö BGP-4 is the protocol that ties together **70,000+ Autonomous Systems** globally.
- **OSPF** dominates **enterprise campus networks**.
- **EIGRP** was historically Cisco-proprietary, now partly open standard.
- **RIP** is largely obsolete but still studied academically for the count-to-infinity problem.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Distance Vector Table Update ŌĆö Exhaustive Worked Example

Consider the network topology below, where the number on each link is the **cost** (RIP-style hop count or OSPF-style cost).

```
        A ---- 1 ---- B
        |             |
        4             2
        |             |
        C ---- 3 ---- D
              \
               5
                \
                 E
```

**Step 1: Initialize each router's table with directly connected networks.**

- A knows: AŌåÆB (cost 1), AŌåÆC (cost 4)
- B knows: BŌåÆA (cost 1), BŌåÆD (cost 2)
- C knows: CŌåÆA (cost 4), CŌåÆD (cost 3), CŌåÆE (cost 5)
- D knows: DŌåÆB (cost 2), DŌåÆC (cost 3)
- E knows: EŌåÆC (cost 5)

**Step 2: First Iteration ŌĆö Routers exchange tables with neighbours.**

Router **A** receives from **B** the route BŌåÆD (cost 2). A computes: AŌåÆBŌåÆD = 1 + 2 = **3**.
Router A also receives from **C** the route CŌåÆD (cost 3) and CŌåÆE (cost 5).
A computes via C: AŌåÆCŌåÆD = 4 + 3 = **7**; AŌåÆCŌåÆE = 4 + 5 = **9**.
A chooses: AŌåÆD = 3 (via B), AŌåÆE = 9 (via C).

After first iteration, **A's table** becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \rightarrow B &= 1 \quad (\text{direct}) \\
A \rightarrow C &= 4 \quad (\text{direct}) \\
A \rightarrow D &= 3 \quad (\text{via } B, \text{ cost } 1+2) \\
A \rightarrow E &= 9 \quad (\text{via } C, \text{ cost } 4+5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convergence Check.**

After the second iteration, all routers will have stable tables ŌĆö this is the **converged state**.

## 3.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm ŌĆö Exhaustive Step-by-Step on a 5-Node Graph

Consider the following weighted graph (routers A, B, C, D, E):

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| AŌĆōB | 4 |
| AŌĆōC | 2 |
| BŌĆōC | 1 |
| BŌĆōD | 5 |
| CŌĆōD | 8 |
| CŌĆōE | 10 |
| DŌĆōE | 2 |

**Objective:** Compute the shortest path from **A** to **E** using Dijkstra's algorithm.

### Initialization

Create a tentative distance table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(A) &= 0 \quad (\text{source}) \\
D(B) &= \infty \\
D(C) &= \infty \\
D(D) &= \infty \\
D(E) &= \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Visited set $V = \{ \}$, unvisited set $U = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$.

### Iteration 1: Visit A (smallest tentative distance = 0)

Update neighbours of A:
- $D(B) = \min(\infty, 0 + 4) = 4$, predecessor = A
- $D(C) = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2$, predecessor = A

### Iteration 2: Visit C (smallest tentative distance = 2)

Update neighbours of C:
- $D(B) = \min(4, 2 + 1) = 3$, predecessor = C  ŌåÉ **improved**
- $D(D) = \min(\infty, 2 + 8) = 10$, predecessor = C
- $D(E) = \min(\infty, 2 + 10) = 12$, predecessor = C

### Iteration 3: Visit B (smallest tentative distance = 3)

Update neighbours of B:
- $D(D) = \min(10, 3 + 5) = 8$, predecessor = B  ŌåÉ **improved**

### Iteration 4: Visit D (smallest tentative distance = 8)

Update neighbours of D:
- $D(E) = \min(12, 8 + 2) = 10$, predecessor = D  ŌåÉ **improved**

### Iteration 5: Visit E (smallest tentative distance = 10)

All nodes visited. **Shortest path A ŌåÆ E has cost 10.**

### Path Reconstruction (backtracking from E):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\leftarrow D \quad (8+2) \\
D &\leftarrow B \quad (3+5) \\
B &\leftarrow C \quad (2+1) \\
C &\leftarrow A \quad (0+2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final shortest path:** A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E with total cost 10.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the path reconstruction step explicitly. Examiners award full marks only when the path is shown, not just the cost.

## 3.3 Count to Infinity ŌĆö Numerical Walkthrough

Initial state of a 3-router linear chain A ŌĆö B ŌĆö C, all costs = 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_A(C) &= 2 \quad (\text{via B}) \\
D_B(C) &= 1 \quad (\text{direct}) \\
D_B(A) &= 1 \quad (\text{direct})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Event:** Link BŌĆōC fails.

**Tick 1:** B marks C as unreachable (Ōł×). But A still advertises C=2 to B (its last known value).
**Tick 2:** B computes: AŌåÆC = 1 + 2 = **3**. B now believes C is reachable via A.
**Tick 3:** A recomputes: BŌåÆC = 1 + 3 = **4**.
**Tick 4:** B: 1 + 4 = **5**. ... and so on.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_B(C) &= 3 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 7 \rightarrow 9 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow 15 \\
D_A(C) &= 4 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 8 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow 16 \;(\text{INFINITY in RIP})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convergence time** is bounded by the **Infinity Limit** (16 in RIP), after which the route is declared invalid.

## 3.4 Administrative Distance Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a router learns the same destination network from **multiple routing sources** (e.g., a static route AND OSPF), it chooses the route with the **lowest Administrative Distance (AD)**.

| Route Source | Default AD |
|---|---|
| Connected Interface | **0** |
| Static Route | **1** |
| EIGRP (internal) | 90 |
| OSPF | 110 |
| IS-IS | 115 |
| RIP | 120 |
| EIGRP (external) | 170 |
| BGP (external eBGP) | 20 |
| BGP (internal iBGP) | 200 |
| Unknown / Untrusted | 255 |

The selected route's metric is then used only when comparing routes from the **same source** (lowest metric wins).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Routing Decision Flow ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pktA[Incoming IP Packet] --> lookupA{Route Lookup in Routing Table}
    lookupA --> matchA{Destination Matches Entry?}
    matchA -- Yes --> forwardA[Forward via Next-Hop Interface]
    matchA -- No --> defaultA{Default Route 0.0.0.0/0 Exists?}
    defaultA -- Yes --> forwardB[Forward via Default Next-Hop]
    defaultA -- No --> dropA[Drop Packet and Send ICMP Destination Unreachable]
    lookupA --> staticA{Static Route Present?}
    staticA -- Yes --> fwdStatic[Use Static Path - AD 1]
    staticA -- No --> dynamicA{Dynamic Protocol Running?}
    dynamicA -- OSPF --> ospfA[Run SPF on LSDB - AD 110]
    dynamicA -- RIP --> ripA[Apply Bellman-Ford - AD 120]
    dynamicA -- EIGRP --> eigrpA[Apply DUAL Algorithm - AD 90]
    dynamicA -- BGP --> bgpA[Apply Best Path Selection - AD 20 eBGP]
    ospfA --> bestA{Select Lowest Metric}
    ripA --> bestA
    eigrpA --> bestA
    bgpA --> bestA
    bestA --> fwdBest[Install in RIB and Forward]
    fwdStatic --> fwdBest
```

## 4.2 Distance Vector vs Link State ŌĆö Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DV[Distance Vector Protocol e.g. RIP]
        dv1[Each Router Maintains Distance Table] --> dv2[Shares Full Table with Neighbours Only]
        dv2 --> dv3[Bellman-Ford Update]
        dv3 --> dv4[Routing by Rumour]
    end
    subgraph LS[Link State Protocol e.g. OSPF]
        ls1[Each Router Discovers Local Links] --> ls2[Floods LSA to ALL Routers in Area]
        ls2 --> ls3[Build Complete LSDB]
        ls3 --> ls4[Run Dijkstra SPF Locally]
    end
    DV --> comp1[Convergence: Slow]
    DV --> comp2[CPU Memory: Low]
    DV --> comp3[Topology View: Partial]
    LS --> comp4[Convergence: Fast]
    LS --> comp5[CPU Memory: High]
    LS --> comp6[Topology View: Complete]
```

## 4.3 Static Route Installation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Admin Types ip route command] --> s2[Route Added to Routing Information Base RIB]
    s2 --> s3{Another Route Exists for Same Destination?}
    s3 -- No --> s4[Install in FIB and Forwarding Engine]
    s3 -- Yes --> s5{Compare Administrative Distance}
    s5 -- New Route Has Lower AD --> s6[Replace Existing Route]
    s5 -- New Route Has Higher AD --> s7[Keep Existing Route - New Route Backed Up]
    s5 -- Equal AD --> s8{Compare Metric}
    s8 -- New Metric Lower --> s6
    s8 -- New Metric Higher --> s7
    s8 -- Equal Metric --> s9[Load Balance Between Both Routes]
```

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between static routing and dynamic routing. Mention two advantages of dynamic routing over static routing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Static Routing | Dynamic Routing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Routes are manually configured by the administrator. | Routes are automatically learned and updated using routing protocols. |
| 2 | No routing protocol overhead. | Periodic exchange of routing information occurs. |
| 3 | No automatic fault tolerance. | Automatic rerouting on link failure. |
| 4 | Suitable for small, simple, stub networks. | Suitable for large, complex enterprise networks. |

**Two advantages of dynamic routing over static routing:**
1. **Automatic adaptation to topology changes** ŌĆö when a link fails, dynamic protocols (e.g., OSPF, EIGRP) propagate the change and reroute traffic within seconds, while static routing requires manual reconfiguration.
2. **Scalability** ŌĆö dynamic routing scales to thousands of routes with minimal administrative effort, whereas static routing requires O(N) manual entries for N destinations.

*(3 marks: 1 for comparison table summary, 2 for advantages with explanation)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]
**What is the count-to-infinity problem in distance vector routing? Explain how split horizon solves it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **count-to-infinity problem** occurs in distance vector routing protocols (e.g., RIP) when a link fails. Routers incorrectly believe the failed destination is still reachable via their neighbours and keep incrementing the metric in a loop. The metric grows hop-by-hop until it reaches the protocol's **infinity threshold** (16 in RIP), at which point the route is declared invalid. This causes **slow convergence** and temporary black-holing of traffic.

**Split Horizon Solution:**
The **Split Horizon** rule states: **A router must never advertise a route back to the neighbour from which it learned the route.**

In our AŌĆōBŌĆōC example, after BŌĆōC fails, B would no longer advertise C to A (since B learned C from A originally). This breaks the loop immediately, and the count-to-infinity is prevented.

**Enhanced version ŌĆö Split Horizon with Poison Reverse:** The route is advertised back to the originator with metric = 16 (infinity), explicitly marking it as unreachable.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for problem statement, 1.5 for split horizon explanation)*

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **Distance Vector Routing** using the Bellman-Ford algorithm. Apply the algorithm to compute the shortest path from node A to all other nodes for the following network:
AŌĆōB = 4, AŌĆōC = 2, BŌĆōC = 1, BŌĆōD = 5, CŌĆōD = 8, CŌĆōE = 10, DŌĆōE = 2. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **Count to Infinity** problem in detail. Discuss the three techniques used to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Conceptual Explanation:**

In Distance Vector routing, each router maintains a table (vector) containing:
- The **known destinations** in the network
- The **distance** (metric) to each destination
- The **next-hop** router on the path

Routers share this table **periodically** (every 30 seconds in RIP) with **directly connected neighbours only**. Upon receiving a neighbour's table, the router updates its own table using the **Bellman-Ford equation**.

The Bellman-Ford update rule:

$$ D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \} $$

This means: *the shortest distance from x to y is the minimum, over all neighbours v of x, of the link cost (x,v) plus the distance v claims to y.*

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((A)) ---|4| B((B))
    A ---|2| C((C))
    B ---|1| C
    B ---|5| D((D))
    C ---|8| D
    C ---|10| E((E))
    D ---|2| E
```

**Step-by-Step Computation (Initial Tables):**

At $t = 0$, each router knows only its directly connected neighbours.

Router A's initial table:

| Destination | Cost | Next-Hop |
|---|---|---|
| B | 4 | B (direct) |
| C | 2 | C (direct) |
| D | Ōł× | ŌĆö |
| E | Ōł× | ŌĆö |

**Iteration 1 (A learns from C, B):**

- A receives B's table: BŌåÆD = 5
- A receives C's table: CŌåÆD = 8, CŌåÆE = 10

Updates:
- AŌåÆD via B: 4 + 5 = 9
- AŌåÆD via C: 2 + 8 = 10
- AŌåÆE via C: 2 + 10 = 12

| Destination | Cost | Next-Hop |
|---|---|---|
| B | 4 | B |
| C | 2 | C |
| D | 9 | B |
| E | 12 | C |

**Iteration 2 (A's updated table propagates; further refinement):**

- AŌåÆBŌåÆD = 4 + 5 = 9
- AŌåÆCŌåÆD = 2 + 8 = 10  (worse, discarded)
- AŌåÆCŌåÆE = 2 + 10 = 12

After convergence, **A's final routing table** is:

| Destination | Cost | Next-Hop | Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | 4 | B | AŌåÆB |
| C | 2 | C | AŌåÆC |
| D | 9 | B | AŌåÆBŌåÆD |
| E | 10 | C | AŌåÆCŌåÆE (cost 2+10) or AŌåÆBŌåÆDŌåÆE (cost 4+5+2=11) ŌĆö actually AŌåÆCŌåÆE = 12, AŌåÆBŌåÆDŌåÆE = 11 ŌĆö need to recompute |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction on path to E:** AŌåÆCŌåÆE = 2+10 = 12; AŌåÆBŌåÆDŌåÆE = 4+5+2 = 11. So shortest AŌåÆE = **11 via BŌåÆDŌåÆE**. (After full convergence, A would learn this through subsequent iterations.)

**Final stable shortest paths from A:**

| Destination | Shortest Cost | Path |
|---|---|---|
| B | 4 | AŌåÆB |
| C | 2 | AŌåÆC |
| D | 9 | AŌåÆBŌåÆD |
| E | 11 | AŌåÆBŌåÆDŌåÆE |

*(7 marks: 1 for concept, 1 for algorithm, 2 for diagram, 3 for numerical computation)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Count to Infinity ŌĆö Detailed Explanation:**

The count-to-infinity problem arises when routing information about a failed link **propagates slowly** through a distance vector network. Each router, not knowing the link has failed, continues to advertise the route based on **stale information** received from its neighbour. The metrics increase incrementally hop by hop.

**Numerical example (3-router chain AŌĆöBŌĆöC):**

| Step | B's view of C | A's view of C |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 (direct) | 2 (via B) |
| 1 | Ōł× (BŌĆōC fails) | 2 |
| 2 | 3 (A advertises 2; B computes 1+2) | 4 (B now advertises 3) |
| 3 | 5 | 6 |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 15 | 15 | 16 ŌåÆ unreachable |

**Three Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Infinity Threshold (Maximum Hop Count):**
   RIP defines the maximum hop count as **15**; a metric of **16** is treated as **infinity (unreachable)**. This **bounds the count-to-infinity** to a maximum of 16 update cycles.

2. **Split Horizon:**
   A router **never advertises a route back to the neighbour from which it learned it**. In the example, after BŌĆōC fails, B would not advertise a route to C via A back to A, breaking the loop.

3. **Split Horizon with Poison Reverse:**
   The route is advertised back to the originator with metric = 16 (infinity), **explicitly poisoning** the route. A receiving router immediately marks the destination as unreachable, preventing any further increment.

**Bonus (OSPF / Link-State mitigation):** Link-state protocols **do not suffer** from count-to-infinity because they maintain a **complete topology map** and propagate **LSA flooding** instantly with sequence numbers.

*(7 marks: 2 for problem, 1 for example, 3 for three techniques, 1 for concluding remark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**a)** Explain the **Link State Routing** algorithm with a neat diagram. Compare it with **Distance Vector Routing** on at least five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**b)** What is a **routing metric**? Explain the different types of metrics used in routing protocols with suitable examples. How is the **best path** selected when multiple routes to the same destination exist? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Link State Routing ŌĆö Conceptual Explanation:**

Link State Routing is an adaptive, hierarchical approach in which every router:
1. **Discovers** its directly connected neighbours and learns their identities (via Hello packets).
2. **Measures** the cost (metric) of each of its links.
3. **Constructs a Link State Packet (LSP / LSA)** containing the list of its links and costs.
4. **Floods** the LSP to **all** other routers in the area (not just neighbours), using a reliable flooding mechanism.
5. **Stores** all received LSPs in a **Link State Database (LSDB)** ŌĆö a complete map of the network.
6. **Runs Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** locally to compute the shortest path to every destination.
7. **Installs** the computed routes in its routing table.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1: Discover Neighbours via Hello] --> stepB[Step 2: Measure Link Costs]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Build LSA Packet]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Flood LSA to All Routers]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Build LSDB - Complete Map]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Run Dijkstra SPF Locally]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Install Routes in RIB]
```

**Comparison Table ŌĆö Distance Vector vs Link State:**

| Parameter | Distance Vector | Link State |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Shared** | Full routing table to neighbours | LSA about own links to all routers |
| **Topology View** | Partial (routing by rumour) | Complete (full LSDB) |
| **Algorithm** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra SPF |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow | Fast |
| **CPU & Memory** | Low | High |
| **Count to Infinity** | Yes (mitigated by split horizon) | No |
| **Scalability** | Limited (RIP = 15 hops) | High (OSPF hierarchical) |
| **Examples** | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |

*(7 marks: 2 for concept, 1 for diagram, 4 for detailed comparison)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Routing Metric ŌĆö Definition:**

A **routing metric** is a numerical value used by routing protocols to **measure the desirability** of a path. Lower metric = better path. When multiple paths exist, the router selects the one with the lowest metric.

**Types of Metrics:**

1. **Hop Count** ŌĆö Number of routers a packet must pass through. Used by **RIP**. Simple, but ignores link quality.

2. **Cost (OSPF)** ŌĆö Inversely proportional to bandwidth. Formula:

$$ \text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}} $$

   Default reference bandwidth in Cisco OSPF = **100 Mbps**.

3. **Bandwidth** ŌĆö Cumulative bandwidth of the slowest link along the path. Used by **OSPF and EIGRP**.

4. **Delay** ŌĆö Sum of all interface delays along the path (measured in microseconds). Used by **EIGRP**.

5. **Load** ŌĆö Saturation of the outgoing interface (1 to 255; 255 = 100% loaded). Used by **EIGRP**.

6. **Reliability** ŌĆö Error-free transmission rate of the link (1 to 255; 255 = 100% reliable). Used by **EIGRP**.

7. **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** ŌĆö Maximum packet size that can be forwarded without fragmentation. Used by path MTU discovery.

**Best Path Selection ŌĆö Two-Level Tie-Breaking:**

When multiple routes exist to the same destination, the router uses a **two-step decision process:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Administrative Distance (AD) Selection:**
Routes from different sources compete; the **lowest AD wins**.

| Route Source | AD |
|---|---|
| Connected | 0 |
| Static | 1 |
| EIGRP | 90 |
| OSPF | 110 |
| RIP | 120 |
| BGP eBGP | 20 |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Metric Comparison (if same source):**
Among routes from the **same routing protocol**, the **lowest metric wins**. If metrics are equal, **load balancing** occurs.

**Example:** A router has:
- Static route to 10.0.0.0/8 via 192.168.1.1 (AD=1, metric=N/A)
- OSPF route to 10.0.0.0/8 via 192.168.1.2 (AD=110, cost=50)

The router installs the **static route** in the FIB, because **1 < 110**.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 3 for metric types, 1 for AD, 2 for best path selection process)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing metric with Administrative Distance (AD):** AD compares routes from *different* sources; metric compares routes from the *same* source. Many students lose 2ŌĆō3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the Bellman-Ford update for *all* neighbours:** Iteration must consider every directly connected neighbour, not just one.
> 3. **Writing the shortest path cost without reconstructing the path:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the path (AŌåÆCŌåÆBŌåÆDŌåÆE) is not explicitly written.
> 4. **Misremembering RIP's infinity = 16:** It is **16** (not 15); 15 is the maximum usable hop count.
> 5. **Treating OSPF cost as a fixed number:** OSPF cost is computed dynamically as Reference_BW / Interface_BW. A 10 Gbps link has a cost of 1 (with default reference).
> 6. **Stating that "static routing has no overhead" without qualifying:** It has zero *routing protocol* overhead, but the administrative burden is high.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 3 / Static & Dynamic Routing**

- **Routing** is a Layer 3 function; routers use **routing tables** populated by static or dynamic means.
- **Static routing** = manually configured, **non-adaptive**, **no protocol overhead**, **no fault tolerance**, suited to **stub networks** and **default routes**.
- **Dynamic routing** = adaptive, uses **routing protocols**, scales well, **automatic fault recovery**.
- **Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)** operate within an AS: **RIP, OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP**.
- **Exterior Gateway Protocols (EGP)** operate between ASes: **BGP-4**.
- **Distance Vector** = Bellman-Ford, "routing by rumour", periodic full-table exchange, suffers from **count-to-infinity** (mitigated by **split horizon, poison reverse, infinity = 16**).
- **Link State** = Dijkstra SPF, **LSA flooding**, complete topology view, **fast convergence**, **no count-to-infinity**, e.g., **OSPF**.
- **Hybrid** = combines both, e.g., **EIGRP** with DUAL algorithm.
- **Metrics** include **hop count, cost, bandwidth, delay, load, reliability, MTU**.
- **Administrative Distance (AD)** chooses the **most trusted** route source: Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120.
- **Bellman-Ford equation**: $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$.
- **Dijkstra's SPF**: Builds shortest-path tree rooted at the source using a **priority queue**; always picks the unvisited node with the lowest tentative distance.
- **Default route** = `0.0.0.0/0`; **Floating static route** = backup with higher AD.
- **Convergence** = state when all routers have synchronized, accurate routing tables.
- **OSPF** uses **hierarchical areas** with **Area 0 (backbone)** and **ABRs** (Area Border Routers).
- **BGP** is the **path-vector** protocol that runs the global Internet.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Algorithms: Distance Vector Routing Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Algorithms: Distance Vector Routing Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Distance Vector Routing (DVR) Algorithm** is a dynamic, distributed, and iterative **intra-domain (interior) routing algorithm** in which every router maintains a routing table (called a *distance vector*) listing the **shortest known distance** and the **next-hop router** to every reachable destination in the network. Routers exchange these vectors **only with their directly connected neighbours** at periodic intervals (or whenever a topology change is detected), and update their own tables using the **BellmanŌĆōFord Equation** until the network converges to stable shortest paths.

**Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Vocabulary):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Distance Vector** | The collection of (destination, cost, next-hop) tuples stored at a single router. |
| **Neighbour** | A router directly attached via a physical/link-layer interface. |
| **Infinity Metric** | A sentinel value (e.g., **16 in RIP**) representing an unreachable destination. |
| **Convergence** | The state when no router's table changes after an update cycle. |
| **BellmanŌĆōFord Equation** | The recursive formula: $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognise that DVR is also known as **RIPv1/RIPv2** (Routing Information Protocol) in real-world implementations, and that the canonical reference for the update logic is the **BellmanŌĆōFord algorithm** (1958).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Traveller's Note-Book"

Imagine a group of five friends (**A, B, C, D, E**) standing at different towns connected by roads of varying length. Each friend keeps a small note-book listing *"the cheapest cost to reach every other town"*. Critically, **no friend has a map of the whole country** ŌĆö they only know the road leaving their own town.

* Initially, each friend writes only the cost of roads leaving their town (others = infinity, meaning "I have no idea").
* Every 30 seconds, each friend **shouts** their note-book to the *neighbours on adjacent roads*.
* After hearing a neighbour, the friend asks: *"If I take the road to you, and then follow the path you just told me, is the total shorter than what I already have?"* If yes, they update the note-book.
* The note-books are exchanged repeatedly. After a few rounds, **every friend knows the globally shortest route to every town**, even though no one has ever seen the full map.

This is exactly how DVR works. The **note-book = Distance Vector**, the **shouting = Periodic Update**, and the **decision rule = BellmanŌĆōFord Equation**.

### 1.3 Why DVR Matters in Engineering

* **Memory-Efficient**: A router with *N* destinations stores only *N* rows (compared to Link-State which stores the full topology).
* **Computationally Simple**: A single min-search over neighbours per destination per update.
* **Foundation for Historical Internet**: RIP was the de-facto IGP of the early ARPANET/Internet and is still used in small enterprise networks.
* **Pedagogically Critical**: KTU Module-3 explicitly tests convergence behaviour, count-to-infinity, and split-horizon ŌĆö all of which are *only* meaningful in DVR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The BellmanŌĆōFord Equation (Heart of DVR)

For any router *x* trying to find the shortest distance to a destination *y*:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \big\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \big\}
$$

Where:
* $D_x(y)$ = shortest distance from router *x* to destination *y*.
* $N(x)$ = set of *x*'s direct neighbours.
* $c(x,v)$ = link cost (metric) of the direct link between *x* and neighbour *v*.
* $D_v(y)$ = neighbour *v*'s *claimed* distance to *y* (received in the latest update packet).

### 2.2 The Distance-Vector Table Structure

A distance-vector table is a 2-D matrix indexed by destination rows and immediate-neighbour columns. The cell $D_{x}(y, v)$ stores the *hypothetical* distance from *x* to *y* *if* traffic were forced through neighbour *v*. The shortest path is the column-wise minimum:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_v D_x(y, v)
$$

### 2.3 Algorithm Operational Steps (Step-by-Step)

1. **Initialisation (t = 0)**: Each router *x* sets $D_x(x)=0$, $D_x(v)=c(x,v)$ for direct neighbours, and $D_x(y)=\infty$ for all other *y*.
2. **Periodic Sharing**: Every $\Delta t$ seconds (RIP uses 30 s), each router sends a copy of its distance vector to every directly connected neighbour.
3. **Update on Receipt**: When router *x* receives the vector of neighbour *v*, it recomputes for every destination *y*:

   $$
   D_x^{\text{new}}(y) = \min\Big( D_x^{\text{old}}(y),\ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \Big)
   $$

4. **Next-Hop Recording**: If the new minimum uses neighbour *v*, the *next-hop* field for *y* is set to *v*.
5. **Convergence Check**: If **no entry** changes, the router becomes silent (RIP optimisations flag the route as *passive*).
6. **Triggered Update (Optional)**: If a link fails, the cost to that neighbour becomes $\infty$; the router immediately broadcasts its new table rather than waiting for the next periodic timer.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-tested** content on DVR in KTU ESE. Memorise the **Infinity value, Periodicity, and the BellmanŌĆōFord operator**.

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Standard Value (RIP) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| BellmanŌĆōFord Update | $D_x^{\text{new}}(y) = \min_v\{c(x,v)+D_v(y)\}$ | ŌĆö | Core update rule |
| Infinity Metric | $\infty$ | **16** (hop count) | Anything $\ge 16$ = unreachable |
| Maximum Diameter | ŌĆö | **15 hops** | Network limit of RIP |
| Update Period | $T_{upd}$ | **30 s** | RIP default |
| Timeout (Invalid) | $T_{inv}$ | **180 s** | Route marked invalid after this |
| Hold-Down | $T_{hd}$ | **180 s** | Suppress updates after invalidation |
| Flush | $T_{fl}$ | **240 s** | Route finally purged |
| Convergence Time | $O(d)$ rounds | *d* = network diameter | DVR is *iterative* |
| Message Complexity | $O(E \cdot N)$ per round | ŌĆö | Every edge carries a full vector |
| Space Complexity | $O(N)$ per router | ŌĆö | DVR's main advantage |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

* **RIPv2** is mandated in many industrial-control networks where simplicity and predictability outweigh convergence speed.
* DVR variants underpin **BGP path-vector** logic ŌĆö the IGP-of-the-Internet for inter-domain routing.
* **Wireless mesh networks** (802.11s) and **ad-hoc networks (AODV)** use DVR-style distance accumulation.
* **SDN controllers** historically use DVR as a baseline fallback when topology data is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Reference Network Topology (Forouzan Canonical Example)

We will use the following **5-node graph** that is a standard KTU question type. All edges are bi-directional and the number on each edge is the *link cost* (assume RIP hop-count weights for simplicity, but the logic generalises to any additive metric).

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| A Ōåö B | **1** |
| A Ōåö C | **4** |
| B Ōåö C | **2** |
| B Ōåö D | **5** |
| C Ōåö D | **1** |
| C Ōåö E | **3** |
| D Ōåö E | **2** |

### 3.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Initialisation at $t=0$

Each router knows only its **direct** links. All other destinations are $\infty$.

$$
D_A^{0} = \big[ A{=}0,\ B{=}1,\ C{=}4,\ D{=}\infty,\ E{=}\infty \big]
$$

$$
D_B^{0} = \big[ A{=}1,\ B{=}0,\ C{=}2,\ D{=}5,\ E{=}\infty \big]
$$

$$
D_C^{0} = \big[ A{=}4,\ B{=}2,\ C{=}0,\ D{=}1,\ E{=}3 \big]
$$

$$
D_D^{0} = \big[ A{=}\infty,\ B{=}5,\ C{=}1,\ D{=}0,\ E{=}2 \big]
$$

$$
D_E^{0} = \big[ A{=}\infty,\ B{=}\infty,\ C{=}3,\ D{=}2,\ E{=}0 \big]
$$

### 3.3 Step 2 ŌĆö First Synchronous Update at $t=1$

**Router A** receives vectors from neighbours *B* and *C*. For each destination *y*, A computes $c(A,\text{neighbour}) + D_{\text{neighbour}}(y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to A: } & 1 + 1 = 2 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=0, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to C: } & 1 + 2 = 3 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=4, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to D: } & 1 + 5 = 6 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 1 + \infty = \infty \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 4 + 4 = 8 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=0, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 4 + 2 = 6 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 4 + 1 = 5 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 4 + 3 = 7 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_A^{1} = \big[ A{=}0,\ B{=}1,\ C{=}3,\ D{=}5,\ E{=}7 \big]}
$$

**Router B** receives vectors from *A, C, D*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via A to C: } & 1 + 4 = 5 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via A to D: } & 1 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via A to E: } & 1 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 2 + 4 = 6 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 2 + 3 = 5 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 5 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to C: } & 5 + 1 = 6 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to E: } & 5 + 2 = 7 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ no update, kept 5}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_B^{1} = \big[ A{=}1,\ B{=}0,\ C{=}2,\ D{=}3,\ E{=}5 \big]}
$$

**Router C** receives vectors from *A, B, D, E*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via A to B: } & 4 + 1 = 5 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via A to D: } & 4 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via A to E: } & 4 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via B to A: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=4, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to D: } & 2 + 5 = 7 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 1 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to B: } & 1 + 5 = 6 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to E: } & 1 + 2 = 3 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update, tie}) \\
\text{Via E to A: } & 3 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to B: } & 3 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to D: } & 3 + 2 = 5 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_C^{1} = \big[ A{=}3,\ B{=}2,\ C{=}0,\ D{=}1,\ E{=}3 \big]}
$$

**Router D** receives vectors from *B, C, E*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to A: } & 5 + 1 = 6 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to C: } & 5 + 2 = 7 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 5 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 1 + 4 = 5 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE ŌĆö better than 6}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 1 + 2 = 3 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 1 + 3 = 4 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via E to A: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to B: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to C: } & 2 + 3 = 5 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_D^{1} = \big[ A{=}5,\ B{=}3,\ C{=}1,\ D{=}0,\ E{=}2 \big]}
$$

**Router E** receives vectors from *C, D*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via C to A: } & 3 + 4 = 7 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 3 + 2 = 5 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 3 + 1 = 4 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to B: } & 2 + 5 = 7 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ no update ŌĆö kept 5}) \\
\text{Via D to C: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update, tie}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_E^{1} = \big[ A{=}7,\ B{=}5,\ C{=}3,\ D{=}2,\ E{=}0 \big]}
$$

### 3.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Second Update at $t=2$

**Router A** re-evaluates using the *new* vectors it just received:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to D: } & 1 + 3 = 4 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 1 + 5 = 6 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=7, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 4 + 1 = 5 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=4, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 4 + 3 = 7 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=6, \text{ no update}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_A^{2} = \big[ A{=}0,\ B{=}1,\ C{=}3,\ D{=}4,\ E{=}6 \big]}
$$

**Router B** ŌĆö checking all neighbours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via A to C: } & 1 + 3 = 4 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via A to D: } & 1 + 4 = 5 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via A to E: } & 1 + 6 = 7 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 2 + 3 = 5 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 5 + 5 = 10 \\
\text{Via D to C: } & 5 + 1 = 6 \\
\text{Via D to E: } & 5 + 2 = 7 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_B^{2} = \big[ A{=}1,\ B{=}0,\ C{=}2,\ D{=}3,\ E{=}5 \big] \text{ (no change ŌĆö CONVERGED)}}
$$

**Router C** ŌĆö checks all four neighbours:

All computed via-paths exceed or equal current entries (max alternative is via E to D = 3+2=5 > 1). 

$$
\boxed{D_C^{2} = \big[ A{=}3,\ B{=}2,\ C{=}0,\ D{=}1,\ E{=}3 \big] \text{ (CONVERGED)}}
$$

**Router D** ŌĆö checks B, C, E:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to A: } & 5 + 1 = 6 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 5 + 5 = 10 \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 1 + 3 = 4 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ UPDATE ŌĆö better!}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 1 + 2 = 3 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 1 + 3 = 4 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via E to A: } & 2 + 7 = 9 \\
\text{Via E to B: } & 2 + 5 = 7 \\
\text{Via E to C: } & 2 + 3 = 5 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_D^{2} = \big[ A{=}4,\ B{=}3,\ C{=}1,\ D{=}0,\ E{=}2 \big]}
$$

**Router E** ŌĆö checks C, D:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via C to A: } & 3 + 3 = 6 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=7, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 3 + 2 = 5 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 3 + 1 = 4 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 2 + 4 = 6 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=6, \text{ no update, tie}) \\
\text{Via D to B: } & 2 + 3 = 5 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to C: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_E^{2} = \big[ A{=}6,\ B{=}5,\ C{=}3,\ D{=}2,\ E{=}0 \big]}
$$

### 3.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Third Update at $t=3$ (Convergence Verification)

Every router recomputes; **all entries remain identical** to $t=2$. The network is **fully converged**.

### 3.6 Final Stable Distance-Vector Tables

| From \ To | A | B | C | D | E | Next-Hops |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 3 | 4 | 6 | BŌåÆ{B,C,D,E} |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 | AŌåÆ{A}, CŌåÆ{C,D,E} |
| **C** | 3 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 3 | BŌåÆ{A,B}, DŌåÆ{D}, E or DŌåÆ{E} |
| **D** | 4 | 3 | 1 | 0 | 2 | CŌåÆ{A,B,C}, EŌåÆ{E} |
| **E** | 6 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 0 | CŌåÆ{A,B,C}, DŌåÆ{D} |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Time for this network = 3 update rounds** (equal to the network diameter *d* = 3, since longest shortest path AŌĆōE has 4 edges but 3 hops between non-adjacent nodes in a tree sense). In general, for *N* nodes, DVR converges in at most *N ŌłÆ 1* rounds if no link ever changes.

### 3.7 The Count-to-Infinity Problem (Analytical Derivation)

Consider the simpler 3-node line topology: **X ŌĆö Y ŌĆö Z**, with the XŌĆōY link cost = 1, YŌĆōZ link cost = 1.

Suppose the **YŌĆōZ link fails** at $t=0$. Then:

* At $t=0$: Y marks distance to Z as $\infty$ (link down).
* At $t=30s$: Y broadcasts its vector to X: Z=$\infty$. But *before* that, suppose X had earlier learned a path to Z via Y of cost 2. **X still advertises Z=2 to Y** (because X does not know Y's link failed).
* At $t=30s+$: Y receives X's "Z=2" and re-computes: $D_Y(Z) = c(Y,X) + D_X(Z) = 1 + 2 = 3$. Y now *incorrectly* believes Z is reachable via X with cost 3.
* At $t=60s$: Y re-broadcasts Z=3 to X. X recomputes: $D_X(Z) = c(X,Y) + D_Y(Z) = 1 + 3 = 4$. The metric inflates by 1 every cycle.
* The count climbs 3 ŌåÆ 4 ŌåÆ 5 ŌåÆ ... ŌåÆ 16, when finally RIP declares Z **unreachable**.

**Total time to detection** $\approx 16 \times 30s = 480$ seconds Ōēł **8 minutes**. This is the famous *count-to-infinity* pathology that motivated **split horizon** and **poison reverse**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Split Horizon Rule**: "Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from which you learned it." In our example, X would not send Z=2 to Y, breaking the loop immediately.
>
> **Poison Reverse Rule**: "When advertising back to the source, set that destination's metric to $\infty$." X would advertise Z=16 to Y, causing Y to immediately mark Z as unreachable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION_4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Reference Network Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    routerA[Router A]
    routerB[Router B]
    routerC[Router C]
    routerD[Router D]
    routerE[Router E]

    routerA ---|cost 1| routerB
    routerA ---|cost 4| routerC
    routerB ---|cost 2| routerC
    routerB ---|cost 5| routerD
    routerC ---|cost 1| routerD
    routerC ---|cost 3| routerE
    routerD ---|cost 2| routerE
```

### 4.2 Distance-Vector Algorithm ŌĆö Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA([Algorithm Start]) --> stepB[Initialise DV: D_x x = 0, D_x v = c x v, others equal infinity]
    stepB --> stepC{Wait for Trigger Periodic 30s or Incoming Update}
    stepC -->|Tick Fired| stepD[Send Current DV to All Neighbours]
    stepC -->|Packet Received from Neighbour v| stepE[For Every Destination y compute candidate = c x v + D_v y]
    stepD --> stepF{Any Local Table Entry Changed}
    stepE --> stepG{New candidate less than D_x y}
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH[Update D_x y and set Next Hop y = v]
    stepG -->|No| stepI[Keep Old Entry]
    stepH --> stepF
    stepI --> stepF
    stepF -->|Yes| stepJ[Flag Routes Active and Propagate]
    stepF -->|No| stepK([Network Converged Silent])
    stepJ --> stepC
    stepK --> stepC
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a DVR Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputPlane[Input Plane]
        recv[Receive DV Packet from Neighbour v]
        tick[Periodic Timer 30s]
        linkf[Link Failure Detector]
    end

    subgraph controlPlane[Control Plane]
        bf[Bellman-Ford Update Engine]
        table[(Distance Vector Table 2D Matrix)]
        nh[Next Hop Resolver]
    end

    subgraph outputPlane[Output Plane]
        trig[Triggered Update Generator]
        per[Periodic Update Generator]
        send[Send to All Neighbours via UDP Port 520]
    end

    recv --> bf
    tick --> per
    linkf --> trig
    bf --> table
    table --> nh
    nh --> trig
    nh --> per
    trig --> send
    per --> send
```

### 4.4 Count-to-Infinity Timeline Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant X as Router X
    participant Y as Router Y
    participant Z as Router Z

    Note over Y,Z: t=0: Y-Z link FAILS
    Y->>Y: D_Y Z = infinity
    X->>Y: D_X Z = 2 (stale)
    Y->>Y: Recompute: 1 + 2 = 3
    Note over Y: D_Y Z = 3 WRONG
    Y->>X: D_Y Z = 3
    X->>X: Recompute: 1 + 3 = 4
    Note over X: D_X Z = 4
    X->>Y: D_X Z = 4
    Y->>Y: Recompute: 1 + 4 = 5
    Note over Y: Metric inflates 1 per cycle
    Y-->>X: D_Y Z = 5, 6, 7 ... 16
    Note over X,Y: After 16 cycles RIP declares Z UNREACHABLE
```
