## SECTION_5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the **Distance Vector Routing** algorithm. State the **BellmanŌĆōFord equation** and explain each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Distance Vector Routing is a distributed, iterative, and asynchronous intra-domain routing algorithm in which each router maintains a table (the *distance vector*) giving the best known distance and next-hop to every destination, and updates this table based only on information received from directly connected neighbours.

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \big\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \big\}
$$

* $D_x(y)$ ŌĆö shortest distance from router *x* to destination *y*.
* $c(x,v)$ ŌĆö cost of the direct link from *x* to neighbour *v*.
* $D_v(y)$ ŌĆö distance claimed by neighbour *v* to reach *y*.
* The **min** is taken over *x*'s entire neighbour set $N(x)$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** What is the **count-to-infinity problem** in DVR? List **two solutions** with one-line justifications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The count-to-infinity problem occurs when a link fails and routers keep advertising increasingly larger (and incorrect) metrics for the now-unreachable destination in a feedback loop, slowly inflating the cost from 1 up to the **infinity value (16 in RIP)** before the destination is finally declared unreachable.

Two solutions:

1. **Split Horizon** ŌĆö A router never advertises a route back to the neighbour from which the route was learned, breaking the immediate feedback loop.
2. **Poison Reverse** ŌĆö When sending an update back to the source of a route, the destination's metric is explicitly set to **infinity (16)**, immediately propagating the bad news.

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the 5-node network shown below with link costs indicated. Construct the **initial distance-vector table** ($t=0$) for **every router** and explain the significance of the $\infty$ entries.

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| A ŌĆō B | 4 |
| A ŌĆō C | 2 |
| B ŌĆō C | 1 |
| B ŌĆō D | 5 |
| C ŌĆō D | 8 |
| C ŌĆō E | 10 |
| D ŌĆō E | 2 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Perform **two iterations** ($t=1$ and $t=2$) of the distance-vector algorithm. Show all intermediate computations and identify whether the network has converged by the end of $t=2$.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Initial Tables at $t=0$:**

Each router knows only its **direct** neighbours. The cost to non-neighbours is initialised to $\infty$, representing the router's current belief that the destination is **unreachable**. These $\infty$ entries are crucial ŌĆö they will be replaced in subsequent iterations as transitive information propagates.

| Node \ Dest | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 4 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| **B** | 4 | 0 | 1 | 5 | $\infty$ |
| **C** | 2 | 1 | 0 | 8 | 10 |
| **D** | $\infty$ | 5 | 8 | 0 | 2 |
| **E** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 10 | 2 | 0 |

**Significance of $\infty$:** Acts as the *unknown* placeholder. It bounds the metric space (RIP = 16). A router will only update a $\infty$ entry if it finds a finite path via some neighbour. The first iteration is the *only* round where $\infty \to$ finite transitions occur for non-adjacent destinations.

**(b) Iteration $t=1$ ŌĆö Router A updates from B and C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to C: } & 4 + 1 = 5 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to D: } & 4 + 5 = 9 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 4 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 2 + 2 = 4 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=0, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=4, \text{ UPDATE ŌĆö better}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 2 + 8 = 10 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 2 + 10 = 12 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_A^{1} = [A{=}0,\ B{=}3,\ C{=}2,\ D{=}9,\ E{=}12]
$$

**Router B updates from A, C, D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via A to C: } & 4 + 2 = 6 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via A to D: } & 4 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via A to E: } & 4 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 1 + 2 = 3 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=4, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 1 + 8 = 9 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 1 + 10 = 11 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 5 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to C: } & 5 + 8 = 13 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to E: } & 5 + 2 = 7 \quad (B^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_B^{1} = [A{=}3,\ B{=}0,\ C{=}1,\ D{=}5,\ E{=}7]
$$

**Router C updates from A, B, D, E:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via A to B: } & 2 + 4 = 6 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via A to D: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via A to E: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via B to A: } & 1 + 4 = 6 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to D: } & 1 + 5 = 6 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=8, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 1 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 8 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to B: } & 8 + 5 = 13 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=1, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to E: } & 8 + 2 = 10 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=10, \text{ no update, tie}) \\
\text{Via E to A: } & 10 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to B: } & 10 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to D: } & 10 + 2 = 12 \quad (C^{\text{old}}=8, \text{ no update ŌĆö keep 6 via B}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_C^{1} = [A{=}2,\ B{=}1,\ C{=}0,\ D{=}6,\ E{=}10]
$$

**Router D updates from B, C, E:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to A: } & 5 + 4 = 9 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to C: } & 5 + 1 = 6 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=8, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 5 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via C to A: } & 8 + 2 = 10 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ no update ŌĆö keep 9}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 8 + 1 = 9 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=5, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 8 + 10 = 18 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via E to A: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to B: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via E to C: } & 2 + 10 = 12 \quad (D^{\text{old}}=8, \text{ no update ŌĆö keep 6 via B}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_D^{1} = [A{=}9,\ B{=}5,\ C{=}6,\ D{=}0,\ E{=}2]
$$

**Router E updates from C, D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via C to A: } & 10 + 2 = 12 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 10 + 1 = 11 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 10 + 8 = 18 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=2, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via D to A: } & 2 + \infty = \infty \\
\text{Via D to B: } & 2 + 5 = 7 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=\infty, \text{ UPDATE ŌĆö better than 11}) \\
\text{Via D to C: } & 2 + 8 = 10 \quad (E^{\text{old}}=10, \text{ no update, tie}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_E^{1} = [A{=}12,\ B{=}7,\ C{=}10,\ D{=}2,\ E{=}0]
$$

**Iteration $t=2$ ŌĆö checking for further improvement at Router A:**

Using the *new* vectors from B ($D_B^1$) and C ($D_C^1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Via B to D: } & 4 + 5 = 9 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=9, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via B to E: } & 4 + 7 = 11 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=12, \text{ UPDATE}) \\
\text{Via C to D: } & 2 + 6 = 8 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=9, \text{ UPDATE ŌĆö better}) \\
\text{Via C to E: } & 2 + 10 = 12 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=11, \text{ no update}) \\
\text{Via C to B: } & 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (A^{\text{old}}=3, \text{ no update, tie}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_A^{2} = [A{=}0,\ B{=}3,\ C{=}2,\ D{=}8,\ E{=}11]
$$

Performing similar checks at B, C, D, E (only updates with strict improvement are recorded; full enumeration omitted for brevity but follows the same pattern), the network **has not yet fully converged** by $t=2$ because at least one entry in every router's table has been refined (A's D, E entries changed; D's D entry would further change to 4 via C; etc.). **A third iteration is required** to reach steady state.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the **count-to-infinity problem** in distance-vector routing. Use a 3-router linear topology (XŌĆōYŌĆōZ) where the YŌĆōZ link fails.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **split horizon** and **poison reverse** techniques. Illustrate how they resolve the count-to-infinity problem in the same 3-router scenario.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Count-to-Infinity Explanation:**

Consider three routers X, Y, Z connected linearly:

$$
X \ \xrightarrow{\text{cost } 1}\ Y \ \xrightarrow{\text{cost } 1}\ Z
$$

Before the failure, all three routers have converged:

| Node \ To | X | Y | Z |
|---|---|---|---|
| X | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| Y | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| Z | 2 | 1 | 0 |

**At $t=0$:** The YŌĆōZ link fails. Y detects this and sets $D_Y(Z) = \infty$.

**At $t=30s$:** X sends its vector to Y (advertising $D_X(Z) = 2$). Y re-computes:

$$
D_Y(Z) = c(Y,X) + D_X(Z) = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

Y *incorrectly* believes Z is reachable via X with cost 3.

**At $t=60s$:** Y broadcasts $D_Y(Z) = 3$ to X. X recomputes:

$$
D_X(Z) = c(X,Y) + D_Y(Z) = 1 + 3 = 4
$$

**The pattern continues:** the metric inflates by 1 every update cycle (3, 4, 5, 6, ...). It takes 16 cycles (in RIP) to reach the **infinity value of 16** and declare Z unreachable. Total detection time $\approx 16 \times 30s = 480s = 8$ minutes.

**Diagram:**

```
X <--cost 1--> Y <--X (link down) Z

Time: 0    30s   60s   90s   120s  ...  480s
D_X(Z): 2    4     5     6     7    ...   16 (UNREACHABLE)
D_Y(Z): 1    3     4     5     6    ...   16
```

**(b) Split Horizon & Poison Reverse:**

**Split Horizon Rule:** "Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from which the route was learned." 

* When the YŌĆōZ link fails, X will *not* send $D_X(Z) = 2$ to Y, because X originally learned this route *from* Y.
* Y's entry $D_Y(Z)$ therefore remains at $\infty$ after the first update.
* **Convergence time** drops from 8 minutes to **30 seconds** (one update cycle).

**Poison Reverse Rule:** "When advertising back to the source, explicitly mark the route as $\infty$."

* X sends $D_X(Z) = 16$ (infinity) to Y *instead* of suppressing the advertisement entirely.
* This aggressively signals "I no longer have a usable path via you."
* **Advantage over split horizon:** Catches slightly more complex loops involving 3+ routers where split horizon alone is insufficient (e.g., a triangle XŌĆōYŌĆōZŌĆōX).

**Modified 3-router table at $t=30s$ with Split Horizon enabled:**

| Node \ To | X | Y | Z | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X (sent to Y) | 0 | 1 | **omitted / 16** | Split horizon / poison reverse |
| Y (received) | 1 | 0 | $\infty$ | Y keeps Z as unreachable |
| Z (received) | 2 | 1 | 0 | No change |

The infinite-count loop is **completely broken** at the first update.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö DVR Numerical Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute the *same* destination via *all* neighbours** ŌĆö students often update only via the *changed* neighbour, missing better paths via stable ones. **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 2. **Not recording the next-hop** ŌĆö the KTU 2024 marking scheme requires a *next-hop* column in the final table. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing $D_v(y)$ (neighbour's view) with $c(x,v)+D_v(y)$ (your view)** ŌĆö the BellmanŌĆōFord equation must use the *sum*, not the raw neighbour's value. **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 4. **Treating $\infty$ as a numeric value** ŌĆö never perform arithmetic on $\infty$; any $\infty$ operand yields $\infty$. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in count-to-infinity derivations** ŌĆö KTU examiners award partial credit per *round*. Showing only the final state costs you 3ŌĆō4 marks. **(ŌłÆ3 to ŌłÆ4 marks)**
> 6. **Writing "DVR is link-state"** or **"OSPF uses DVR"** in theory answers ŌĆö KTU strictly distinguishes the two; OSPF is link-state, RIPv2 is distance-vector. **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö DVR Algorithm**

* **Definition (1 line):** A distributed, iterative intra-domain routing algorithm where each router maintains a distance vector to all destinations and updates it using only neighbour-supplied information.
* **Core Formula:** $D_x^{\text{new}}(y) = \min_v\{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ ŌĆö **BellmanŌĆōFord equation**.
* **Initialisation:** $D_x(x) = 0$, $D_x(v) = c(x,v)$ for direct neighbours, all others = $\infty$.
* **Update Cycle:** Periodic (30 s in RIP) **OR** triggered (on link failure or table change).
* **Convergence Time:** At most $(N-1)$ iterations for $N$ nodes if no topology change.
* **RIP Timers (must memorise):** Update = 30 s, Invalid = 180 s, Hold-down = 180 s, Flush = 240 s.
* **RIP Infinity:** **16** (anything $\geq 16$ = unreachable). **Max diameter = 15 hops.**
* **Three Reliability Problems of DVR:**
  1. **Count-to-infinity** ŌĆö slow detection of unreachable destinations.
  2. **Routing loops** ŌĆö transient black-holes during convergence.
  3. **BellmanŌĆōFord false paths** ŌĆö outdated information accepted blindly.
* **Three Counter-Measures:**
  1. **Split Horizon** ŌĆö don't advertise route back to source.
  2. **Poison Reverse** ŌĆö advertise $\infty$ back to source.
  3. **Hold-Down Timers** ŌĆö freeze updates for 180 s after a route goes invalid.
* **Complexity:**
  * **Time** per update: $O(\deg(x) \times N)$ for router $x$.
  * **Message** per round: $O(E \cdot N)$ over the whole network.
  * **Space** per router: $O(N)$.
* **Real-World Implementations:** RIPv1 (RFC 1058, classful), RIPv2 (RFC 2453, classless, VLSM, MD5 auth), RIPng (RFC 2080, IPv6).
* **Comparison Anchor for KTU:** DVR is **simple but slow**; Link-State is **fast but complex**; Path-Vector (BGP) is **policy-driven**.
* **KTU Hot-Question Type:** "Show the iteration tables for a 4- or 5-node graph until convergence." Always draw the network, show the $\infty$ to finite transition at $t=1$, then iterate until no changes.
* **BellmanŌĆōFord vs Dijkstra:** DVR uses BellmanŌĆōFord (handles negative weights, distributed); Link-State uses Dijkstra (faster, requires global topology).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link State Routing (Dijkstra's Algorithm)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Link State Routing (Dijkstra's Algorithm)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Link State Routing (LSR)** is a dynamic, intra-domain routing paradigm in which every router independently constructs a complete, identical map of the entire network topology and computes shortest paths to all destinations using the **Dijkstra Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm**.

Each router accomplishes this by performing three well-defined phases:

1. **Link State Discovery** ŌĆö Each router learns its directly connected neighbors and the cost (metric) of the links incident on it, typically using the **Hello Protocol**.
2. **Reliable Flooding** ŌĆö Every router generates a **Link State Packet (LSP)** containing its local connectivity information and broadcasts it to *every other router in the area* using controlled, sequenced, acknowledged flooding.
3. **Shortest Path Computation** ŌĆö Each router constructs a **Link State Database (LSDB)** (an identical topological map of the area), runs the **Dijkstra algorithm** to compute the SPF tree rooted at itself, and installs the resulting shortest paths into the routing table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim standard):** "In Link State Routing, every router possesses complete topology information of the autonomous system. Each router independently runs Dijkstra's algorithm on this topology to construct a shortest-path tree and derive its forwarding table. This is in contrast to Distance Vector Routing, where routers only know their directly connected neighbours."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The GPS Map Analogy

Imagine a **city with no maps**. Everyone only knows the streets immediately around their house. To find a route to a distant place, they would constantly ask neighbours, who would ask their neighbours ŌĆö slow, error-prone, and prone to loops. This is **Distance Vector Routing** (the "rumour-spreading" approach).

Now imagine that the **municipal corporation publishes a complete map of every street in the city to every household**. Each house then independently draws the shortest route from itself to every other location on that same map. No one needs to keep asking neighbours ŌĆö they all hold identical, complete knowledge. This is **Link State Routing**.

The "complete map" is the **LSDB**, and the process of "drawing the shortest route from your house to everywhere else" is **Dijkstra's algorithm**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Expansion | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **LSP** | Link State Packet | The advertisement unit flooded by each router |
| **LSDB** | Link State Database | The synchronised, identical topology map held by every router |
| **SPF Tree** | Shortest Path First Tree | The tree of minimum-cost paths rooted at a given router |
| **Cost** | Routing Metric | The numerical weight of a link (e.g., inversely proportional to bandwidth) |
| **Hello Protocol** | Neighbor Discovery | Forms and maintains adjacencies between directly connected routers |
| **Sequence Number** | LSP Identifier | Prevents stale/flooded packets from causing routing loops |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** In *Distance Vector*, routers exchange **routing tables** with neighbours (partial info, hop-by-hop). In *Link State*, routers exchange **topology maps** with *everyone* (full info, computed locally). This single line of difference is worth 2ŌĆō3 marks in any KTU essay.

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Examples

- **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** ŌĆö the textbook implementation of LSR, used in almost every enterprise ISP backbone.
- **IS-IS (Intermediate System to Intermediate System)** ŌĆö the second widely deployed LSR protocol, common in large carrier networks.
- **TRILL / SPB** ŌĆö modern data-centre LSR variants.

The standard cost metric in OSPF is:

$$ \text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Link Bandwidth}} = \frac{10^8 \text{ bps}}{\text{Link Bandwidth in bps}} $$

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics ŌĆö Five Logical Phases

### Phase 1: Neighbor Discovery via the Hello Protocol
- Each router periodically transmits **Hello packets** out of every interface.
- A neighbour is confirmed only after receiving a Hello that lists its own **Router ID**.
- Hello packets also elect a **Designated Router (DR)** on broadcast networks to reduce $O(n^2)$ adjacencies to $O(n)$.

### Phase 2: Building the Link State Packet (LSP)
An LSP carries four mandatory fields:
- **Router ID** of the originating router.
- **Sequence number** (monotonically increasing).
- **Age** (a countdown timer to discard stale LSPs).
- **List of (neighbour ID, cost) pairs** for all directly attached links.

### Phase 3: Reliable Flooding
- When an LSP arrives with a sequence number *higher* than the local copy, the router:
  1. Stores it in the **LSDB**.
  2. Acknowledges the sender.
  3. Re-floods it out *every interface except the one on which it arrived*.
- This guarantees that, eventually, **every router in the area holds an identical LSDB**.

### Phase 4: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Computation
- The LSDB is viewed as a **graph** $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights = link costs.
- The router executes Dijkstra's algorithm to compute the shortest path from itself (the root) to every other vertex.

### Phase 5: Routing Table Installation
- The second column of the Dijkstra result ("predecessor") is walked to construct next-hop entries.
- The resulting table is installed in the **RIB (Routing Information Base)** and pushed to the **FIB (Forwarding Information Base)** for hardware-accelerated lookups.

## 2.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm ŌĆö Formal Logic

Dijkstra's algorithm computes the minimum-cost path from a source vertex $s$ to every other vertex in a graph with non-negative edge weights.

Let $V$ be the vertex set. The algorithm maintains:

- $N'$ = set of vertices whose shortest path from $s$ is definitively known.
- $D(v)$ = current known shortest distance from $s$ to $v$.
- $P(v)$ = predecessor of $v$ on the current shortest path from $s$.

### Algorithm Pseudocode (textual, not code)

```
INITIALIZATION:
    N' = { s }
    for every vertex v in V:
        if v is adjacent to s:
            D(v) = c(s, v)
            P(v) = s
        else:
            D(v) = infinity
            P(v) = undefined
    D(s) = 0
    P(s) = undefined

LOOP:
    find u not in N' such that D(u) is minimum
    add u to N'
    if N' = V: STOP

    for every vertex w not in N' that is adjacent to u:
        if D(u) + c(u, w) < D(w):
            D(w) = D(u) + c(u, w)
            P(w) = u
    GOTO LOOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Dijkstra work?** Because edge weights are non-negative, the moment a vertex $u$ is added to $N'$, no future relaxation through any other vertex can produce a shorter path to $u$. This is the *greedy optimality property* ŌĆö the foundation of the entire algorithm.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Use Case |
|------------------|---------|----------|
| $N' \subseteq V$ | Set of finalized vertices | Loop termination condition |
| $D(v)$ | Tentative shortest distance from $s$ to $v$ | Updated via relaxation |
| $P(v)$ | Predecessor of $v$ on shortest path | Used to construct SPF tree |
| $c(u, w)$ | Cost of edge $(u, w)$ | Routing metric |
| $D(w) = \min \bigl(D(w),\ D(u) + c(u, w)\bigr)$ | Relaxation step | Updates shortest path estimates |
| $\text{Cost}_{\text{link}} = \dfrac{10^8}{\text{BW}_{\text{bps}}}$ | OSPF cost formula | Converts bandwidth to metric |
| $T_{\text{complexity}} = O\bigl(\vert V \vert^2\bigr)$ | Naive Dijkstra | Standard textbook complexity |
| $T_{\text{complexity}} = O\bigl(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Fibonacci-heap Dijkstra | Optimised implementation |
| $\text{Convergence}_{\text{time}} \approx O(\text{diameter})$ | LSR convergence | Much faster than DVR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When you write `|V|` in a markdown table, write it as `\vert V \vert`, never as a bare pipe. A bare pipe will break the markdown table and your answer sheet may render incorrectly.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Fast Convergence:** Only OSPF LSPs of changed links are flooded, not full tables. Convergence time is **near-instantaneous** compared to DVR's hold-down timers.
- **Loop Freedom:** Because every router computes paths on the same global graph, transient loops are mathematically impossible after LSDB synchronisation.
- **Hierarchical Scalability:** OSPF uses a two-level hierarchy (Area 0 backbone + non-backbone areas) which limits LSDB size and SPF recomputation cost.
- **Industry Footprint:** OSPF is the de facto standard for enterprise WAN, campus networks, and ISP interiors.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Network Topology

Consider the network $G = (V, E)$ shown below, which is a classic KTU problem (Dec 2020 / June 2022 type). Source node = **A**. Compute the shortest paths from **A** to every other vertex.

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| (A, B) | 4 |
| (A, C) | 2 |
| (B, C) | 1 |
| (B, D) | 5 |
| (C, D) | 8 |
| (C, E) | 10 |
| (D, E) | 2 |

> All costs are positive integers ŌĆö Dijkstra's pre-condition (non-negative edge weights) is satisfied.

## 3.2 Full Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution

### Step 0 ŌĆö Initialization

The source $s = A$ is the root. We begin with $N' = \{A\}$.

$$ N'_0 = \{A\} $$

$$ D(A) = 0, \quad D(B) = 4, \quad D(C) = 2, \quad D(D) = \infty, \quad D(E) = \infty $$

$$ P(A) = -, \quad P(B) = A, \quad P(C) = A, \quad P(D) = -, \quad P(E) = - $$

Vertices adjacent to $A$ are $B$ and $C$, both initialised from the edge costs.

---

### Iteration 1 ŌĆö Add the minimum tentative vertex

Among $\{B, C, D, E\}$, the minimum $D$ is $D(C) = 2$.

$$ N'_1 = \{A, C\} $$

$$ D(C) = 2 \ \text{via } A \ \Rightarrow \ P(C) = A $$

**Relaxation through newly added vertex $C$:** neighbours of $C$ not yet in $N'$ are $B, D, E$.

$$ D(B) = \min\bigl(D(B),\ D(C) + c(C, B)\bigr) = \min(4,\ 2 + 1) = 3 $$

$$ P(B) = C $$

$$ D(D) = \min\bigl(D(D),\ D(C) + c(C, D)\bigr) = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 8) = 10 $$

$$ P(D) = C $$

$$ D(E) = \min\bigl(D(E),\ D(C) + c(E, C)\bigr) = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 10) = 12 $$

$$ P(E) = C $$

**End-of-iteration-1 table:**

| Vertex | $D(v)$ | $P(v)$ | In $N'$? |
|--------|--------|--------|----------|
| A | 0 | ŌĆö | Ō£ō |
| B | 3 | C | Ō£Ś |
| C | 2 | A | Ō£ō |
| D | 10 | C | Ō£Ś |
| E | 12 | C | Ō£Ś |

---

### Iteration 2 ŌĆö Add the next minimum tentative vertex

Among $\{B, D, E\}$ (excluding $N'_1$), minimum is $D(B) = 3$.

$$ N'_2 = \{A, C, B\} $$

$$ D(B) = 3 \ \text{via } C \ \Rightarrow \ P(B) = C \ \text{(finalised)} $$

**Relaxation through newly added vertex $B$:** neighbour of $B$ not in $N'$ is $D$.

$$ D(D) = \min\bigl(D(D),\ D(B) + c(B, D)\bigr) = \min(10,\ 3 + 5) = 8 $$

$$ P(D) = B $$

No change to $D(E)$ because $B$ has no edge to $E$.

**End-of-iteration-2 table:**

| Vertex | $D(v)$ | $P(v)$ | In $N'$? |
|--------|--------|--------|----------|
| A | 0 | ŌĆö | Ō£ō |
| B | 3 | C | Ō£ō |
| C | 2 | A | Ō£ō |
| D | 8 | B | Ō£Ś |
| E | 12 | C | Ō£Ś |

---

### Iteration 3 ŌĆö Add the next minimum tentative vertex

Among $\{D, E\}$, minimum is $D(D) = 8$.

$$ N'_3 = \{A, C, B, D\} $$

$$ D(D) = 8 \ \text{via } B \ \Rightarrow \ P(D) = B \ \text{(finalised)} $$

**Relaxation through newly added vertex $D$:** neighbour of $D$ not in $N'$ is $E$.

$$ D(E) = \min\bigl(D(E),\ D(D) + c(D, E)\bigr) = \min(12,\ 8 + 2) = 10 $$

$$ P(E) = D $$

**End-of-iteration-3 table:**

| Vertex | $D(v)$ | $P(v)$ | In $N'$? |
|--------|--------|--------|----------|
| A | 0 | ŌĆö | Ō£ō |
| B | 3 | C | Ō£ō |
| C | 2 | A | Ō£ō |
| D | 8 | B | Ō£ō |
| E | 10 | D | Ō£Ś |

---

### Iteration 4 ŌĆö Finalisation

Only vertex $E$ remains. It is added unconditionally because $N'$ does not yet equal $V$.

$$ N'_4 = \{A, C, B, D, E\} = V $$

$$ D(E) = 10 \ \text{via } D \ \Rightarrow \ P(E) = D \ \text{(finalised)} $$

**Algorithm terminates** since $N'_4 = V$.

---

## 3.3 Final Shortest Path Table (Answer)

| Destination | Shortest Distance from A | Shortest Path | Next Hop |
|-------------|--------------------------|---------------|----------|
| B | 3 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B | C |
| C | 2 | A ŌåÆ C | C |
| D | 8 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D | C |
| E | 10 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E | C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconstruction rule:** The next-hop from $A$ to any destination is the *first step* of the path. For instance, $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ has its first hop = $C$. Therefore the next-hop from $A$ to **every** destination in this example is $C$. This is a common KTU board question: *"List the routing table of A."*

## 3.4 SPF Tree Diagram (textual)

The shortest path tree rooted at $A$ is:

$$ A \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow D \longrightarrow E $$

with branch weights:

$$ c(A, C) = 2, \quad c(C, B) = 1, \quad c(B, D) = 5, \quad c(D, E) = 2 $$

Verify total: $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10 \ \equiv\ D(E)$. Ō£ō

## 3.5 Verification by Enumeration (Cross-Check)

For path $A \to E$, every alternate route:

- $A \to C \to E$: $2 + 10 = 12$
- $A \to C \to D \to E$: $2 + 8 + 2 = 12$
- $A \to B \to D \to E$: $4 + 5 + 2 = 11$
- $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$: $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10$ ŌåÉ **minimum**

This brute-force check confirms the algorithm's output.

## 4.2 Dijkstra Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following block diagram captures the complete Dijkstra execution loop as a functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sInit[START Initialize D source to 0]
    sInf[Set all other D v to infinity]
    sN[Set N prime to empty]
    sP[Set all P v to undefined]

    sLoop{In N prime equal V?}
    sMin[Pick u not in N prime with minimum D u]
    sAdd[Add u to N prime]
    sRelax[For every neighbour w not in N prime]
    sUpdate{D u plus c u w less than D w}
    sSetD[Update D w and set P w to u]
    sNoOp[Skip w keep old D w]

    sOut[OUTPUT shortest paths via P v]

    sInit --> sInf --> sN --> sP --> sLoop
    sLoop -- No --> sMin --> sAdd --> sRelax --> sUpdate
    sUpdate -- Yes --> sSetD --> sLoop
    sUpdate -- No --> sNoOp --> sLoop
    sLoop -- Yes --> sOut
```

## 4.3 OSPF Link State Routing ŌĆö Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps how LSR operates end-to-end at a router. It complements the algorithmic diagram above by showing the *router-level* workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    iface1[Network Interfaces]
    hello[Hello Protocol - Neighbor Discovery]
    neigh[Adjacency Table]
    lspgen[LSP Generator]
    flood[Reliable Flooding Engine]
    lsdb[Link State Database LSDB]
    dijk[Dijkstra SPF Engine]
    spftree[SPF Tree]
    rib[Routing Information Base]
    fib[Forwarding Information Base]

    iface1 --> hello --> neigh
    neigh --> lspgen
    iface1 --> lspgen
    lspgen --> flood
    flood <--> lsdb
    lsdb --> dijk
    dijk --> spftree --> rib --> fib
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Link State Routing. Mention any two of its advantages over Distance Vector Routing. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Link State Routing is an intra-domain routing algorithm in which every router maintains a complete map of the network topology and independently computes shortest paths to all destinations using the Dijkstra SPF algorithm.

**Two advantages over Distance Vector Routing:**

1. **Faster convergence** ŌĆö Because every router immediately floods a link change as a single LSP to all routers in the area, the entire network reconverges in approximately the network diameter time. Distance Vector suffers from hold-down, poison-reverse, and count-to-infinity delays.
2. **Loop-free routing** ŌĆö Since each router computes shortest paths on a globally synchronised topology, transient routing loops cannot form after LSDB synchronisation.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the third mark for either: (a) a crisp one-line definition, or (b) a valid comparison point. Always include **both** for safety.

---

### Question 2: What is a Link State Packet (LSP)? List any four fields contained in it. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Link State Packet (LSP)** is the unit of information generated and flooded by a router to advertise its local connectivity information to all other routers in the routing area.

**Four mandatory fields of an LSP:**

1. **Router ID** of the originating router.
2. **Sequence number** (used to distinguish new LSPs from stale ones).
3. **Age** (a countdown timer; LSP is discarded when it reaches 0).
4. **List of (neighbour ID, link cost) pairs** for every directly attached link.

> [!TIP]
> The fourth mark is sometimes awarded for an *additional* field such as *checksum* ŌĆö adding it strengthens your answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of Link State Routing in detail. Discuss the role of the Hello protocol, LSP flooding, and the LSDB. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`

**A (b)** Using Dijkstra's algorithm, compute the shortest paths from source node **S** to all other nodes in the following graph. Show every iteration in tabular form. (7 marks)

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| (S, A) | 7 |
| (S, B) | 2 |
| (S, C) | 3 |
| (A, B) | 3 |
| (A, D) | 4 |
| (B, C) | 1 |
| (B, D) | 2 |
| (C, T) | 6 |
| (D, T) | 3 |

---

**Model Answer for A (a):**

Link State Routing operates in three sequential phases for every router in the area:

**1. Neighbor Discovery via the Hello Protocol (2 marks):**
- Each router periodically multicasts/sends Hello packets out every interface (default interval **10 s** for OSPF on broadcast networks, **30 s** on point-to-point).
- A bidirectional adjacency is formed when router X receives a Hello from Y that contains X's own Router ID.
- On broadcast networks like Ethernet, a Designated Router (DR) and Backup DR (BDR) are elected to suppress the $O(n^2)$ adjacency explosion.

**2. Building and Flooding Link State Packets (3 marks):**
- Each router constructs an LSP listing (neighbour, cost) for every attached link.
- The LSP carries the **Router ID**, **sequence number**, **age**, and the neighbour list.
- On any topology change, the router floods the new LSP out every interface except the arrival interface.
- Each recipient stores the LSP in its **LSDB**, acknowledges the sender, and re-floods it.
- Sequence numbers prevent old or duplicate LSPs from re-triggering flooding (this is the key anti-loop mechanism).

**3. LSDB Synchronization and Shortest Path Computation (2 marks):**
- After flooding completes, every router in the area holds an **identical LSDB**, which is a logical representation of the entire area's topology.
- Each router runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** on this LSDB with itself as root, producing an SPF tree and hence a routing table.

> **Conclusion:** LSR provides fast, loop-free, hierarchical routing and is the foundation of OSPF ŌĆö the dominant enterprise interior gateway protocol.

---

**Model Answer for A (b):**

**Initialization:**

$$ N'_0 = \{S\}, \quad D(S) = 0, \quad D(A) = 7, \quad D(B) = 2, \quad D(C) = 3, \quad D(D) = \infty, \quad D(T) = \infty $$

$$ P(A) = S, \ P(B) = S, \ P(C) = S $$

**Iteration 1 ŌĆö add B (min D = 2):**

$$ N'_1 = \{S, B\}, \quad D(B) = 2, \ P(B) = S $$

Relax through B:

$$ D(A) = \min(7, 2+3) = 5, \quad P(A) = B $$

$$ D(C) = \min(3, 2+1) = 3 \quad \text{(unchanged)}, \quad P(C) = S $$

$$ D(D) = \min(\infty, 2+2) = 4, \quad P(D) = B $$

**Iteration 2 ŌĆö add C (min D = 3):**

$$ N'_2 = \{S, B, C\}, \quad D(C) = 3, \ P(C) = S $$

Relax through C:

$$ D(T) = \min(\infty, 3+6) = 9, \quad P(T) = C $$

**Iteration 3 ŌĆö add D (min D = 4):**

$$ N'_3 = \{S, B, C, D\}, \quad D(D) = 4, \ P(D) = B $$

Relax through D:

$$ D(T) = \min(9, 4+3) = 7, \quad P(T) = D $$

$$ D(A) = \min(5, 4+\infty) = 5 \quad \text{(unchanged)} $$

**Iteration 4 ŌĆö add A (min D = 5):**

$$ N'_4 = \{S, B, C, D, A\}, \quad D(A) = 5, \ P(A) = B $$

No new relaxations to T.

**Iteration 5 ŌĆö add T (only remaining vertex):**

$$ N'_5 = V, \quad D(T) = 7, \ P(T) = D $$

**Final Shortest Path Table from S:**

| Destination | Distance | Shortest Path |
|-------------|----------|---------------|
| A | 5 | S ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ A |
| B | 2 | S ŌåÆ B |
| C | 3 | S ŌåÆ C |
| D | 4 | S ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D |
| T | 7 | S ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ T |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**B (a)** Differentiate between Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing under the following heads: (i) information shared, (ii) algorithm used, (iii) convergence speed, (iv) scalability, (v) bandwidth overhead, (vi) routing loop risk, (vii) example protocols. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**B (b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm step-by-step to find the shortest path from node **X** to every other node in the given graph. Construct the routing table at X. (7 marks)

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| (X, 1) | 2 |
| (X, 2) | 5 |
| (1, 2) | 1 |
| (1, 3) | 4 |
| (2, 4) | 3 |
| (3, 4) | 2 |
| (3, 5) | 6 |
| (4, 5) | 1 |

---

**Model Answer for B (a):**

| Head | Distance Vector Routing | Link State Routing |
|------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| (i) Information shared | Full routing table with neighbours only | Local connectivity map with *all* routers in the area |
| (ii) Algorithm used | BellmanŌĆōFord | Dijkstra (SPF) |
| (iii) Convergence speed | Slow (hold-down timers, count-to-infinity) | Fast (immediate LSP flood) |
| (iv) Scalability | Poor ŌĆö scales as $O(n)$ memory per router but slow convergence | Excellent via OSPF hierarchical areas |
| (v) Bandwidth overhead | Low (small periodic updates) | Higher (LSP flooding on every change) |
| (vi) Routing loop risk | High (transient loops possible) | None after LSDB synchronisation |
| (vii) Example protocols | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |

---

**Model Answer for B (b):**

**Initialization:**

$$ N'_0 = \{X\}, \quad D(X) = 0, \quad D(1) = 2, \quad D(2) = 5, \quad D(3) = \infty, \quad D(4) = \infty, \quad D(5) = \infty $$

$$ P(1) = X, \ P(2) = X $$

**Iteration 1 ŌĆö add 1 (min D = 2):**

$$ N'_1 = \{X, 1\}, \quad D(1) = 2, \ P(1) = X $$

Relax through 1:

$$ D(2) = \min(5, 2+1) = 3, \quad P(2) = 1 $$

$$ D(3) = \min(\infty, 2+4) = 6, \quad P(3) = 1 $$

**Iteration 2 ŌĆö add 2 (min D = 3):**

$$ N'_2 = \{X, 1, 2\}, \quad D(2) = 3, \ P(2) = 1 $$

Relax through 2:

$$ D(4) = \min(\infty, 3+3) = 6, \quad P(4) = 2 $$

**Iteration 3 ŌĆö add 3 or 4 (min D = 6):** Let us pick 3 (tie-break alphabetically).

$$ N'_3 = \{X, 1, 2, 3\}, \quad D(3) = 6, \ P(3) = 1 $$

Relax through 3:

$$ D(4) = \min(6, 6+2) = 6 \quad \text{(unchanged)} $$

$$ D(5) = \min(\infty, 6+6) = 12, \quad P(5) = 3 $$

**Iteration 4 ŌĆö add 4 (min D = 6):**

$$ N'_4 = \{X, 1, 2, 3, 4\}, \quad D(4) = 6, \ P(4) = 2 $$

Relax through 4:

$$ D(5) = \min(12, 6+1) = 7, \quad P(5) = 4 $$

**Iteration 5 ŌĆö add 5 (only remaining):**

$$ N'_5 = V, \quad D(5) = 7, \ P(5) = 4 $$

**Final Routing Table at X:**

| Destination | Distance | Path | Next Hop |
|-------------|----------|------|----------|
| 1 | 2 | X ŌåÆ 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 3 | X ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 6 | X ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ 3 | 1 |
| 4 | 6 | X ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ 2 ŌåÆ 4 | 1 |
| 5 | 7 | X ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ 2 ŌåÆ 4 ŌåÆ 5 | 1 |

> All entries have next-hop = 1 because the very first hop from X on every shortest path is node 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Failure to update $P(v)$:** Many students update $D(v)$ but forget to update $P(v)$. Both must change together. Cost: **ŌłÆ1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Wrong initialisation:** Setting $D(s) = \infty$ instead of $0$ gives a garbage result. Cost: **ŌłÆ2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to relax newly added neighbours:** Always relax *every* neighbour of the freshly added vertex, even if its $D$ value already looks small. Cost: **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing the routing-table reconstruction step:** KTU often demands the routing table at the source. Walk each $P(v)$ chain back to $s$ and write the first hop as the next-hop. Omitting this loses **3 marks**.
> 5. **Not writing the SPF tree explicitly:** Draw it or describe it in one line. It is worth 2 marks on its own.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Link State Routing = "Tell everyone about my neighbourhood."**
- Every router maintains an **identical LSDB** of the area.
- An **LSP** carries Router ID, sequence number, age, and the neighbour-cost list.
- **Reliable flooding** uses sequence numbers to prevent duplicates and loops.
- **Dijkstra's algorithm** computes shortest paths from a source $s$ to every vertex in a graph with non-negative weights.
- Initialisation: $N' = \{s\}$, $D(s) = 0$, $D(v) = \infty$ for $v \neq s$, $P(s) = -$.
- Loop body: pick minimum $D(u)$ outside $N'$, add $u$ to $N'$, relax all neighbours $w \notin N'$ using $D(w) = \min\bigl(D(w),\, D(u) + c(u, w)\bigr)$ and $P(w) = u$.
- Termination: $N' = V$.
- Reconstruct shortest path from $P$ pointers; reconstruct next-hop from the *first* edge of the reconstructed path.
- Time complexity: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ simple, $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with a Fibonacci heap.
- **OSPF cost** of a link: $10^8 \div \text{link bandwidth in bps}$.
- LSR advantages: fast convergence, loop-free, hierarchical, scalable.
- LSR disadvantages: more CPU/memory per router, larger initial LSP storm.
- OSPF is the canonical LSR protocol; IS-IS is its older cousin used in ISP backbones.
- **Always show the iteration table** in your answer ŌĆö KTU examiners award up to 4 marks purely on tabular clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Protocols: Routing Information Protocol (RIP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Protocols: Routing Information Protocol (RIP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś MODULE 3 ŌĆö Routing Information Protocol (RIP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Routing Information Protocol (RIP)** is an **Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP)** that employs a **distance-vector (BellmanŌĆōFord) routing algorithm** to dynamically exchange reachability information among routers within a single autonomous system (AS). Originally defined in **RFC 1058** (RIPv1) and later refined in **RFC 2453** (RIPv2), RIP uses **hop count** as its sole routing metric and enforces a maximum hop limit of **15**, treating a metric value of **16** as **unreachable (infinity)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCGT302 ŌĆō Module 3)**
> The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes RIP under *Distance-Vector Routing Protocols*. Students must master the **BellmanŌĆōFord distance computation**, the **RIP message format (Request/Response)**, the **timers (Update, Invalid, Hold-down, Flush)**, the **count-to-infinity problem**, and the **loop-avoidance heuristics (Split Horizon, Poison Reverse, Triggered Updates)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Tourist at Every Town"

Imagine a backpacker travelling across Kerala city by city. At each town, the backpacker stops and asks every local: *"Which is the shortest path to my destination, and how many stops will it take?"* Every town records the shortest answer it has heard so far and passes it on to the next town.

- The **backpacker** = a RIP packet.
- Each **town** = a router maintaining its own routing table.
- The **number of stops** = hop count.
- A town that claims *"16 stops"* means the destination is essentially **unreachable**.

This is exactly how RIP operates: every router periodically (every 30 s) shares its *entire* routing table with its immediate neighbours, and the **best (lowest hop count) route wins**.

### 1.3 Why RIP Matters in Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RIP is still widely embedded in **small-to-medium enterprise networks**, **Cisco/Layer-3 switch default gateways**, and **legacy UNIX `routed` daemons**. Although OSPF and EIGRP dominate modern carrier networks, RIP remains the canonical teaching vehicle for understanding **distributed, asynchronous, iterative convergence** of routing algorithms ŌĆö a foundational concept in network engineering.

| Property | RIP Value |
|----------|-----------|
| Algorithm class | Distance Vector (BellmanŌĆōFord) |
| Metric | Hop count (integer) |
| Maximum metric | **15 hops** |
| Unreachable value | **16** |
| Administrative Distance (Cisco) | **120** |
| UDP Port | **520** |
| Update Interval | **30 seconds** |
| Full table broadcast | Yes (RIPv1) / Multicast 224.0.0.9 (RIPv2) |
| Standard | RFC 1058 / RFC 2453 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BellmanŌĆōFord Distance Equation ŌĆö The Heart of RIP

RIP's update rule is a direct implementation of the **BellmanŌĆōFord optimality principle**:

$$
D_x(y) \;=\; \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl[\, c(x,v) \;+\; D_v(y) \,\bigr]
$$

Where:
- $D_x(y)$ = current best distance (in hops) from router $x$ to destination $y$.
- $N(x)$ = set of all neighbours of $x$.
- $c(x,v)$ = cost of the link from $x$ to neighbour $v$. In RIP, this is **always 1 hop** for an active interface.
- $D_v(y)$ = the distance advertised by neighbour $v$ to destination $y$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The minimization is computed **router-locally** for every entry in the routing table. If the computed value is *less than* the previously stored value, the table is updated and the route is flagged as **changed** for the next periodic advertisement.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialization:** On boot, each router's routing table contains only **directly connected networks** with metric = 1 (or 0 for the local interface).
2. **Request Phase (Optional):** A router may broadcast a `RIP Request` on startup asking neighbours for their complete tables.
3. **Response Phase:** Each neighbour replies with a `RIP Response` (its routing table packed into UDP datagrams).
4. **Update Rule:** For every entry $(dest, metric)$ received from neighbour $v$:
   - Compute candidate metric $m' = metric + 1$.
   - If no entry exists for $dest$, install $(dest, m', \text{via } v)$.
   - If an entry exists with a *higher* metric, replace it.
   - If an entry exists via the *same* neighbour $v$, refresh it (reset timer).
   - Otherwise, ignore (unless triggered-update logic overrides).
5. **Periodic Re-advertisement:** Every **30 s**, send the full table to all neighbours (255.255.255.255 for RIPv1; 224.0.0.9 for RIPv2).
6. **Timeout Cascade:** If no refresh arrives within **180 s**, the route is declared **invalid** (metric ŌåÆ 16) and a triggered update is sent.
7. **Hold-down:** For **180 s** afterward, the router refuses to accept a worse route, allowing the network to converge.
8. **Flush:** After **240 s** total, the route is purged from the table.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE FOR THE EXAM HALL**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Default / Numerical Value |
|---------------|---------|---------------------------|
| $D_x(y)$ | Distance from router $x$ to destination $y$ | Hop count (integer $\ge 0$) |
| $c(x,v)$ | Link cost between $x$ and neighbour $v$ | $\mathbf{1}$ (in RIP) |
| $H_{\max}$ | Maximum usable hop count | $\mathbf{15}$ |
| $H_{\infty}$ | "Infinity" / unreachable | $\mathbf{16}$ |
| $T_{update}$ | Periodic update interval | $\mathbf{30\ s}$ |
| $T_{invalid}$ | Invalid timer (route declared dead) | $\mathbf{180\ s}$ |
| $T_{holddown}$ | Hold-down suppression window | $\mathbf{180\ s}$ |
| $T_{flush}$ | Garbage-collection timer | $\mathbf{240\ s}$ |
| $D_{AD}$ | Administrative Distance (Cisco priority) | $\mathbf{120}$ |
| $P_{RIP}$ | UDP port number | $\mathbf{520}$ |
| $\eta$ | Convergence time (worst case) | $\le \mathbf{O}(N \cdot H_{\max})$ rounds |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In operational networks, RIP's simplicity is a feature, not a bug. It is preferred when:
- The topology is **flat** (no more than 15 hops across the diameter).
- **Network engineers need predictable, easy-to-trace** routing behaviour.
- **Hardware constraints** (memory, CPU) preclude link-state protocols.
- **Transition / migration** scenarios between IGP protocols are in progress.

> [!NOTE]
> RIP also serves as the **reference baseline** in the *gtITM* and *ns-2/ns-3* network simulators ŌĆö understanding RIP is essential before tackling the more elaborate **OSPF LSA flood** and **BGP path-vector** mechanisms covered in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of RIP Update from BellmanŌĆōFord

Starting from the **principle of optimality** (Bellman, 1958):

> *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."*

Applying this to a packet-switched network with $N$ routers and per-hop cost $c(x,v)=1$:

$$
D_x^{(k)}(y) \;=\; \min_{v \in N(x)} \Bigl[\, c(x,v) \;+\; D_v^{(k-1)}(y) \Bigr]
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initialization (k = 0):**

$$
D_x^{(0)}(y) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } x = y \quad \text{(self)} \\
1, & \text{if } y \text{ is directly connected to } x \\
\infty \;(=16), & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Iterative Update (k ŌåÆ k+1):** Each router compares the **received** $D_v^{(k-1)}(y) + 1$ with its own $D_x^{(k)}(y)$ and stores the smaller value plus the next-hop pointer $\text{NextHop}(x,y)$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convergence Criterion:** Iteration halts when, for every destination $y$:

$$
\bigl\vert D_x^{(k)}(y) \;-\; D_x^{(k-1)}(y) \bigr\vert \;=\; 0 \quad \forall\, x,y
$$

In the RIP context, "convergence" requires at most $H_{\max} = 15$ asynchronous rounds because any path longer than 15 hops is capped at infinity.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö A 4-Router Topology

Consider routers **R1, R2, R3, R4** with the following adjacency (each link = 1 hop):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Links:} \quad & R1 \leftrightarrow R2,\; R2 \leftrightarrow R3,\; R3 \leftrightarrow R4,\; R1 \leftrightarrow R4 \\
\text{Goal:} \quad & \text{Compute } D_{R1}(R4) \text{ and } D_{R2}(R4) \text{ at convergence.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Round 0 (Initialization)

| Router | To R1 | To R2 | To R3 | To R4 |
|--------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| R1     | 0     | 1     | 16    | 1     |
| R2     | 1     | 0     | 1     | 16    |
| R3     | 16    | 1     | 0     | 1     |
| R4     | 1     | 16    | 1     | 0     |

### Round 1 ŌĆö R1 receives updates from R2 and R4

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{R1}^{\text{candidate}}(R4) \text{ via R2} &= D_{R2}(R4) + 1 = 16 + 1 = 17 \;\;(\text{capped to } 16) \\
D_{R1}^{\text{candidate}}(R4) \text{ via R4} &= D_{R4}(R4) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
D_{R1}^{(1)}(R4) = \min\{17,\, 1\} = 1, \quad \text{NextHop} = R4
$$

### Round 2 ŌĆö R2 receives updates from R1 and R3

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{R2}^{\text{candidate}}(R4) \text{ via R1} &= D_{R1}^{(1)}(R4) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
D_{R2}^{\text{candidate}}(R4) \text{ via R3} &= D_{R3}(R4) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D_{R2}^{(2)}(R4) = \min\{2,\, 2\} = 2, \quad \text{NextHop tie} \rightarrow \text{lowest router-ID wins}
$$

### Convergence Matrix (Final)

| Router | Destination R4 | Metric (hops) | Next Hop |
|--------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| R1     | R4             | 1             | R4       |
| R2     | R4             | 2             | R1 or R3 |
| R3     | R4             | 1             | R4       |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the **shortest path R2 ŌåÆ R4** has length 2 hops, but R2 has *two equal-cost paths*. RIP behaviour on ties is implementation-specific (Cisco uses lowest next-hop IP; some daemons use first-received).

## 3.3 Count-to-Infinity ŌĆö Analytical Bound

Without loop-prevention heuristics, RIP can suffer **count-to-infinity**, where the metric to a failed destination slowly increments from 1 toward 16. The theoretical upper bound is exactly $H_{\infty} = 16$ hops, taking up to:

$$
T_{\text{conv}}^{\text{worst}} \;=\; H_{\infty} \times T_{\text{update}} \;=\; 16 \times 30\;\text{s} \;=\; \mathbf{480\ s}\;(8\;\text{min})
$$

Heuristics reduce this:

| Heuristic | Working Principle | Reduces Convergence To |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Split Horizon** | Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom it was learned. | Halves the loop length |
| **Poison Reverse** | Advertise the route back with metric = 16. | Eliminates 2-node loops |
| **Triggered Updates** | Immediately send an update when the table changes (no 30 s wait). | Cuts delay to ~few seconds |
| **Hold-down Timers** | Refuse worse updates for 180 s after a route goes invalid. | Prevents premature re-acceptance |
| **Route Poisoning** | When a link fails, immediately advertise it as 16 to all neighbours. | Combined with triggered updates ŌåÆ near-instant convergence |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RIP v2 Message Format (RFC 2453)

The diagram below maps the byte-level structure of an individual **RIP entry** carried inside a UDP datagram on port 520.

```mermaid
block-beta
  columns 1
  block:header["RIP MESSAGE HEADER (4 bytes)"]:columns 4
    cmd["Command (1B) Request=1 Response=2"]
    ver["Version (1B) 1 or 2"]
    rsvd["Reserved (2B) Must be Zero"]
  end
  block:rteBlock["ROUTE TABLE ENTRY (20 bytes per route)"]:columns 5
    afi["Address Family (2B) 0x0002 for IP"]
    tag["Route Tag (2B) AS Boundary Info"]
    ip["IP Address (4B) Destination"]
    mask["Subnet Mask (4B) V2 only"]
    nhop["Next Hop (4B) V2 only"]
    met["Metric (4B) 1 to 16"]
  end
  header --> rteBlock
```

> [!NOTE]
> RIPv1 entries occupy only **16 bytes** (no Tag, Mask, or Next-Hop fields). RIPv2 extends the entry to **20 bytes** and introduces subnet masks, supporting **CIDR** and **VLSM**.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö RIP Convergence Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Router Boot: Init Routing Table"] --> B{"Periodic 30s Timer Fired?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Wait for Incoming RIP Response"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D["Serialize Table into UDP 520 Datagram"]
    D --> E{"RIPv2 Multicast 224.0.0.9?"}
    E -- "Yes v2" --> F["Send to Multicast Group"]
    E -- "No v1" --> G["Broadcast 255.255.255.255"]
    F --> H["Neighbour Receives and Decodes"]
    G --> H
    C --> H
    H --> I["Apply Bellman-Ford Min Rule"]
    I --> J{"New Best Path Found?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Update Table, Reset Timers, Trigger Update"]
    J -- "No" --> L["Keep Existing Entry"]
    K --> M["180s Invalid Timer Running"]
    L --> M
    M --> N{"Route Refreshed in 180s?"}
    N -- "Yes" --> B
    N -- "No" --> O["Mark Metric = 16, Send Poison Update"]
    O --> P["Enter 180s Hold-Down"]
    P --> Q{"Better Route Within Hold-Down?"}
    Q -- "Yes" --> K
    Q -- "No" --> R["Flush After 240s Total"]
    R --> B
```

## 4.3 Sample 4-Router Topology with Routing Tables After Convergence

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1["R1"] ---|hop1| R2["R2"]
    R2 ---|hop1| R3["R3"]
    R3 ---|hop1| R4["R4"]
    R1 ---|hop1| R4
    R1 -. "Distance to R4 = 1 via R4" .-> R4
    R2 -. "Distance to R4 = 2 via R1" .-> R4
    R3 -. "Distance to R4 = 1 via R4" .-> R4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Always draw the topology *first*, then build the tables row-by-row starting from routers that have **direct links** to the destination. This visually pre-empts the Bellman-Ford computation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Q: Define RIP. Why is the maximum hop count limited to 15?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** RIP is a **distance-vector interior gateway protocol** standardized in **RFC 1058 / RFC 2453**, which uses **hop count** as the routing metric and advertises the entire routing table to neighbours every **30 seconds** via UDP port **520**.
2. **Hop limit reason (2 marks):** The hop count is capped at **15** with **16** reserved as **infinity (unreachable)**. This bound is enforced to:
   - **Prevent count-to-infinity from looping indefinitely** ŌĆö convergence is guaranteed within 16 update cycles ($\le 480$ s).
   - **Bound memory and convergence time** in networks with diameter $\le 15$ hops.
   - **Keep the metric field compact** as a single 32-bit integer within the RIP message format.

---

### Question 2 ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model]`
**Q: List the four timers used by RIP and state their default values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Timer | Function | Default |
|-------|----------|---------|
| **Update Timer** | Controls periodic advertisement of routing table | **30 s** |
| **Invalid Timer** | Declares a route invalid if no refresh received | **180 s** |
| **Hold-down Timer** | Suppresses acceptance of worse routes after invalidation | **180 s** |
| **Flush Timer** | Removes the route from the routing table | **240 s** |

> Each timer entry: **0.5 mark** for the function, **0.25 mark** for the value.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

### ŌØō Question A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Adapted]`
**Q (a)** Explain the operation of the **Routing Information Protocol (RIP)** with its **message format**, and describe the role of **timers** in route convergence. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** With a neat diagram, illustrate how **count-to-infinity** occurs in a distance-vector network and show how **Split Horizon** and **Poison Reverse** mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **RIP Overview (1 mark):** RIP is a UDP-based (port **520**) **distance-vector** protocol that uses **hop count** as metric, max **15** hops, infinity = **16**. It runs directly over the network layer with no TCP handshake.
2. **Message Format (3 marks):**
   - **Header (4 bytes):** Command field (1 = Request, 2 = Response), Version (1 = RIPv1, 2 = RIPv2), Reserved (2 bytes = 0).
   - **Route Entries:** Up to **25 entries** in a 512-byte UDP packet. Each RIPv1 entry is **16 bytes** (Address Family, must be zero, IP address, Metric). RIPv2 entry is **20 bytes**, adding **Route Tag**, **Subnet Mask**, and **Next Hop**.
3. **Operational Steps (2 marks):**
   - On startup, send a **Request** on every interface.
   - All neighbours reply with their **complete routing tables**.
   - Apply **BellmanŌĆōFord update**; install or refresh entries.
   - Every **30 s** broadcast (v1) / multicast (v2) the full table.
4. **Timers (1 mark):** Update (30 s) ŌåÆ Invalid (180 s) ŌåÆ Hold-down (180 s) ŌåÆ Flush (240 s). Mention **triggered updates** for rapid response to topology changes.

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Count-to-Infinity Scenario (3 marks):**
   - Consider a 3-router linear topology A ŌĆö B ŌĆö C. Suppose link **BŌĆōC** fails.
   - A still advertises distance to C as 2 (via B); B still has stale entry of 2.
   - B receives A's advertisement and updates its distance to C as **3** (A says 2 + 1).
   - A hears B's new metric of 3, updates its own to **4**. Iteration continues until both reach **16**.
2. **Split Horizon (2 marks):** Rule: *A router shall not advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom the route was learned.* In the scenario above, A will never tell B about route-C via B. This prevents the *two-node loop* immediately.
3. **Poison Reverse (2 marks):** Rule: *Advertise the route back to the originating neighbour with metric = 16.* This explicitly signals "this route is unreachable through me" and forces immediate invalidation rather than gradual counting.

---

### ŌØō Question B ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Adapted]`
**Q (a)** Compare **RIPv1 and RIPv2** in terms of features, message format, and addressing support. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Given the network below, build the **RIP routing tables** for all routers after convergence. **(7 marks)**

```
Network:
      10.0.1.0/24
   R1 ----------- R2
   |               |
10.0.2.0/24   10.0.3.0/24
   |               |
   R3 ----------- R4
      10.0.4.0/24
```
Each link is a separate subnet; all routers run RIP.

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks]

| Feature | RIPv1 | RIPv2 |
|---------|-------|-------|
| RFC | 1058 | 2453 |
| Classful / Classless | **Classful** | **Classless (VLSM, CIDR)** |
| Subnet mask in update | **Not carried** | **Carried (4 bytes)** |
| Next-hop field | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Broadcast type | 255.255.255.255 | Multicast 224.0.0.9 |
| Authentication | **None** | Plaintext / MD5 |
| Route tag | **No** | **Yes (16-bit)** |
| Entry size | **16 B** | **20 B** |
| Max entries / packet | **25** | **25** |

> Each correct row: **0.5 mark** ├Ś 8 rows = 4 marks. Add a 3-mark summary describing *why classless support matters* (e.g., enables **192.168.1.0/26** and **192.168.1.64/26** to coexist).

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks]

After full convergence, every router has 4 entries. Direct links have metric 1; one-hop-away subnets have metric 2 (where applicable).

| From / To | 10.0.1.0/24 | 10.0.2.0/24 | 10.0.3.0/24 | 10.0.4.0/24 |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **R1**    | 1 (direct)  | 1 (direct)  | 2 via R2 or R3 | 2 via R3 |
| **R2**    | 1 (direct)  | 2 via R1    | 1 (direct)  | 2 via R4 |
| **R3**    | 2 via R1    | 1 (direct)  | 2 via R4    | 1 (direct) |
| **R4**    | 2 via R2    | 2 via R3    | 1 (direct)  | 1 (direct) |

**Mark allocation:**
- 4 tables correctly drawn ŌåÆ **4 marks**
- Correct metric assignment using **+1 hop rule** ŌåÆ **2 marks**
- Correct next-hop identification ŌåÆ **1 mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤öź KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1":** When applying BellmanŌĆōFord, the *incoming metric* must be **incremented by 1** to account for the link to the neighbour. Omitting this loses **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up timers:** Students often swap *Invalid* (180 s) and *Hold-down* (180 s) ŌĆö both are 180 s but perform different roles. **Write their functions explicitly**.
> 3. **Confusing RIPv1 / RIPv2 message sizes:** RIPv1 = 16 B, RIPv2 = 20 B. **Do not** say RIPv2 is "16 bytes with subnet mask appended" ŌĆö the format is *integrally* redefined.
> 4. **Count-to-infinity diagram missing direction arrows:** You **must** show time progression with multiple rounds; a single static picture earns only partial credit.
> 5. **Ignoring the hop-15 limit:** Whenever a path exceeds 15 hops, you **must** explicitly mark it as **unreachable (metric = 16)**. Skipping this is a common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö RIP**

- ŌŁÉ RIP is a **distance-vector** protocol operating at the application layer over **UDP port 520**.
- ŌŁÉ Metric = **hop count**, integer; max usable = **15**; infinity = **16**.
- ŌŁÉ Full routing table broadcast every **30 s**; RIPv1 uses broadcast, RIPv2 uses **multicast 224.0.0.9**.
- ŌŁÉ **BellmanŌĆōFord equation:** $D_x(y) = \min_v [c(x,v) + D_v(y)]$ with $c(x,v) = 1$.
- ŌŁÉ Four timers: Update (30 s), Invalid (180 s), Hold-down (180 s), Flush (240 s).
- ŌŁÉ **RIPv1** = classful, no mask, no auth; **RIPv2** = classless, carries mask, supports MD5 auth, route tags, next-hop field.
- ŌŁÉ Message format: 4-byte header + up to 25 route entries (16 B in v1, 20 B in v2) inside a 512-byte UDP payload.
- ŌŁÉ **Count-to-infinity** bounded by 16; cured by **Split Horizon**, **Poison Reverse**, **Hold-down**, **Triggered Updates**, **Route Poisoning**.
- ŌŁÉ Convergence is **slow** (asynchronous, iterative) compared to OSPF's link-state approach.
- ŌŁÉ RIP has **Administrative Distance 120** in Cisco IOS ŌĆö lower than OSPF (110), so prefer OSPF when both run.
- ŌŁÉ RIPng (RIP next generation, RFC 2080) extends RIP to **IPv6**, using UDP port **521** and multicast **FF02::9**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Shortest Path First (OSPF)** is a **link-state, hierarchical, classless interior gateway routing protocol** standardized by the IETF in **RFC 2328** (OSPFv2 for IPv4) and **RFC 5340** (OSPFv3 for IPv6). It operates within a single **Autonomous System (AS)** using the **Dijkstra Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** to compute a loop-free topology from a synchronized **Link-State Database (LSDB)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, OSPF is described as:

- A **Type 1 (Interior) Protocol** operating under the TCP/IP Network Layer (Layer 3).
- A **Link-State Routing Protocol** ŌĆö every router maintains a complete map of the area topology.
- An **Open Standard** (vendor-neutral) ŌĆö published, non-proprietary, and freely implementable.
- A **Hierarchical Protocol** ŌĆö supports a **two-level hierarchy** using **Areas** to scale large networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Open"?** The word "Open" simply means the specification is **publicly available** and not owned by any single vendor. This contrasts with Cisco's proprietary **EIGRP** (which was later "opened" in 2013 but is still largely associated with Cisco). OSPF can be implemented by any vendor ŌĆö Juniper, Huawei, MikroTik, Arista, VyOS, etc.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The City Map Analogy"

Imagine you just moved to a new city, **Kochi**. You want to find the shortest route from your home (**Router A**) to the airport (**Router E**). There are two ways to ask for directions:

| Strategy | Real-world analogy | Routing Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **Distance-Vector** ("Rumour routing") | You stop at every petrol pump and ask, "How far is the airport from *here*?" Each person only knows their neighbour. | RIP, IGRP |
| **Link-State** ("Map-based routing") | The Government hands you a **complete, up-to-date city map**. You *yourself* draw the shortest route using a highlighter. | **OSPF, IS-IS** |

> [!TIP]
> **OSPF is the "Government-map" approach:** every router first **builds the same complete map** of the network (the LSDB), then *independently* runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** to highlight the shortest paths. No router is "blind" ŌĆö every router knows every link.

---

## 1.3 Why OSPF? ŌĆö Design Goals vs RIP Limitations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Why is OSPF preferred over RIP in modern networks?"*

| Limitation in RIP (v1/v2) | How OSPF Solves It |
|---|---|
| **Max hop count = 15** (16 = unreachable) | **No hop-count limit** ŌĆö uses cost metric. |
| **Slow convergence** (whole-table updates every 30 s) | **Fast convergence** (sub-second on LSA change). |
| **Routing loops possible** (count-to-infinity) | **Loop-free** by design (Dijkstra SPF on synced LSDB). |
| **Flat network** (no hierarchy) | **Hierarchical Areas** ŌĆö scales to thousands of routers. |
| **Periodic full-table updates** consume bandwidth | **Incremental, triggered updates** only on change. |
| **Classful (v1) / limited summarization (v2)** | **Fully classless** ŌĆö supports **VLSM** and **CIDR**. |

---

## 1.4 Key Characteristics at a Glance

- **Administrative Distance (AD):** **110** (Cisco) ŌĆö second most trusted after static routes (1) and EIGRP (90).
- **Protocol Number:** **89** (IP Protocol field in the IP header).
- **Transport:** Runs directly over **IP** (Protocol 89) ŌĆö no TCP/UDP port numbers.
- **Multicast Addresses:** **224.0.0.5** (AllSPFRouters) and **224.0.0.6** (AllDRouters).
- **Metric:** **Cost** (cumulative, outbound interface cost along the path).
- **Algorithm:** **Dijkstra's SPF** (a.k.a. **Link-State Algorithm**, after E. W. Dijkstra, 1959).
- **Update Mechanism:** **Triggered updates** on link-state change + **LSA flooding** every **30 minutes** (LSRefreshTime).
- **Authentication:** Supports **Null, Simple (Plain-text), and MD5/SHA cryptographic** authentication.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSPF Operational Cycle ŌĆö Three Logical Phases

OSPF behaviour can be decomposed into three distinct phases. KTU examiners love testing these in any order.

### Phase 1 ŌĆö Neighbor Discovery (Hello Protocol)

- Each OSPF-enabled interface periodically transmits **OSPF Hello packets** out of every OSPF interface.
- Default **Hello Interval** = **10 seconds** for Broadcast and Point-to-Point networks; **30 seconds** for NBMA and Point-to-Multipoint.
- **Dead Interval** = **4 ├Ś Hello Interval** = **40 s** (or 120 s) ŌĆö a router is declared *down* if no Hello is received within this window.
- For two routers to become **neighbors**, the following must match inside the Hello packet:
   1. **Hello Interval**
   2. **Dead Interval**
   3. **Area ID**
   4. **Subnet Mask** (on broadcast/point-to-point)
   5. **Authentication type and password**
   6. **Stub Area flag** (must be identical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Neighbour ŌēĀ Adjacency.** Two routers can be *neighbours* (see each other's Hello) without being *adjacent* (exchanged full LSDB). Adjacency is only formed on certain designated networks.

---

### Phase 2 ŌĆö Adjacency Formation & Database Synchronization

Once neighbour relationships are established, routers exchange their **Link-State Advertisements (LSAs)** via four packet types (see 2.3) until their **Link-State Databases (LSDBs)** are identical ŌĆö this is called **Database Synchronization**.

### Phase 3 ŌĆö SPF Computation & Route Installation

Each router runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** against its own LSDB to construct a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** with itself as the root. The result is installed into the **Routing Information Base (RIB)** ŌåÆ the active routing table.

---

## 2.2 The OSPF Hierarchical Model ŌĆö Areas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** OSPF uses a **strict two-level hierarchy**: **Backbone Area (Area 0)** at the top, and one or more **non-backbone areas** below. **All non-backbone areas MUST connect to Area 0** ŌĆö either directly or via a **Virtual Link**.

### 2.2.1 The Four OSPF Router Types

| Router Type | Abbreviation | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Router** | ŌĆö | All interfaces belong to the **same single area**. |
| **Backbone Router** | ŌĆö | Has at least **one interface in Area 0**. |
| **Area Border Router** | **ABR** | Has interfaces in **two or more areas**, one of which **must be Area 0**. Generates **Summary LSAs (Type 3)**. |
| **Autonomous System Boundary Router** | **ASBR** | Imports **external routes** from another AS or routing protocol via **redistribution**. Generates **External LSAs (Type 5)**. |

### 2.2.2 Special Area Types

| Area Type | LSA Allowed | Default Route Injected? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Area** | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 | No | Normal OSPF area |
| **Stub Area** | 1, 2, 3 + Default | **Yes** (cost 1) | Reduces LSDB size; blocks Type 5 |
| **Totally Stubby** (Cisco) | 1, 2 + Default only | **Yes** | Smallest LSDB; blocks Type 3, 4, 5 |
| **Not-So-Stubby Area (NSSA)** | 1, 2, 3, 4, **7** | Optional | Allows ASBR inside stub; converts Type 7 ŌåÆ 5 at ABR |

---

## 2.3 The Five OSPF Packet Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All five packet types share a common **24-byte OSPF header** (Version, Type, Packet Length, Router ID, Area ID, Checksum, AuType, Authentication). The **Type** field (1 byte) distinguishes them.

| Type | Packet Name | Purpose | Sent To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Hello** | Discover / maintain neighbours; elect DR/BDR | 224.0.0.5 |
| **2** | **DBD** (Database Description) | Summarize LSDB contents during sync | 224.0.0.5 (unicast) |
| **3** | **LSR** (Link-State Request) | Request specific LSAs discovered via DBD | Unicast |
| **4** | **LSU** (Link-State Update) | Carry one or more complete **LSAs** in response to LSR or trigger updates | 224.0.0.5 / .6 |
| **5** | **LSAck** (Link-State Acknowledgement) | Acknowledge receipt of LSU/LSA | Unicast / Multicast |

---

## 2.4 The Seven OSPF LSA Types

| Type | LSA Name | Generated By | Flooded In | Carries |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Router LSA** | Every Router | Within an Area | Router's links & state |
| **2** | **Network LSA** | **DR** (Designated Router) | Within an Area | Routers attached to the transit network |
| **3** | **Summary LSA** | **ABR** | Between Areas | Inter-area routes (OSPF) |
| **4** | **ASBR Summary LSA** | **ABR** | Between Areas | Location of the ASBR |
| **5** | **AS External LSA** | **ASBR** | Throughout AS (except stub) | External routes (e.g. from BGP) |
| **6** | **Multicast LSA** | Reserved for MOSPF | ŌĆö | (Rarely used) |
| **7** | **NSSA External LSA** | **ASBR inside NSSA** | Inside NSSA only | External routes inside NSSA |
| **8** | **External Attributes LSA** | ASBR | Opaque | BGP attribute info (RFC 4576) |

---

## 2.5 OSPF States ŌĆö Adjacency Formation Finite State Machine

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Down** | No Hello received from neighbour. |
| **Init** | Hello received from neighbour, but the neighbour has **not** seen *our* Router ID in its Hello. |
| **2-Way** | Bidirectional communication ŌĆö both routers see each other in their Hello. **Adjacency decision** happens here (Full vs 2-Way depending on network type). |
| **ExStart** | Master/Slave election for DBD exchange. |
| **Exchange** | Routers send **DBD** packets describing their LSDB. |
| **Loading** | Routers send **LSR** for missing LSAs and receive **LSU**. |
| **Full** | LSDBs are **completely synchronized**. Adjacency is **fully formed**. |

---

## 2.6 Designated Router (DR) and Backup DR (BDR)

On **broadcast** and **NBMA** networks, forming an adjacency between *every* pair of routers causes an **n┬▓ adjacency explosion** (e.g. 5 routers = 10 adjacencies, 10 routers = 45). To solve this, OSPF elects:

- **DR** ŌĆö represents the multi-access network; all other routers form an adjacency with the **DR only**.
- **BDR** ŌĆö hot standby; takes over if DR fails.
- **DROther** ŌĆö non-DR/BDR routers; remain in the **2-Way** state with each other, but reach **Full** with the DR and BDR.

**Election criteria (highest wins, tie-broken by Router ID):**
1. Highest **OSPF Interface Priority** (default **1**; range 0ŌĆō255; **0 = ineligible to be DR/BDR**).
2. Highest **Router ID** (manually configured loopback, or highest active IP).

> [!NOTE]
> **DR/BDR election is *non-preemptive***. Once elected, a new router with higher priority will **NOT** displace the current DR ŌĆö it must wait until the DR fails.

---

## 2.7 The Link-State Database (LSDB) ŌĆö Single Source of Truth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LSDB is a **collection of all LSAs** the router has learned. Within an area, **all routers must have an identical LSDB**. The LSDB is the input to Dijkstra's algorithm.

Two databases to remember:
- **LSDB** (Link-State Database) ŌåÆ topology map.
- **RIB** (Routing Information Base) ŌåÆ best paths (output of SPF).
- **FIB** (Forwarding Information Base) ŌåÆ fast-CPU forwarding copy of RIB.

---

## 2.8 OSPF Network Types

| Network Type | Default Hello | DR/BDR Election? | Multicast Used? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Broadcast** (e.g. Ethernet) | 10 s | **Yes** | Yes (224.0.0.5/.6) | LAN |
| **Point-to-Point** | 10 s | **No** | Yes (224.0.0.5) | Serial PPP, HDLC |
| **NBMA** (e.g. Frame Relay, ATM) | 30 s | **Yes** | No (unicast only) | Hub-spoke FR |
| **Point-to-Multipoint** | 30 s | **No** | Yes (224.0.0.5) | Hub-spoke over FR |
| **Point-to-Multipoint Non-Broadcast** | 30 s | **No** | No | Hub-spoke, no multicast |

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Every value in the table below has appeared in KTU question papers.

| Formula / Term | Definition / Equation | Units / Default |
|---|---|---|
| **OSPF Cost** | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$ (cumulative along outbound path) | Unitless integer; Cisco default Reference = **100 Mbps** |
| **Total Path Cost** | $\text{PathCost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost}(i)$ where $i$ is each outbound interface | Integer Ōēź 1 |
| **Cost for 10 Mbps link** | $100/10 = 10$ | ŌĆö |
| **Cost for 100 Mbps link** | $100/100 = 1$ | ŌĆö |
| **Cost for 1 Gbps link** | $100/1000 = 0.1 \rightarrow$ rounded up to **1** (configurable `auto-cost reference-bandwidth`) | ŌĆö |
| **Hello Interval** (Broadcast / P2P) | Fixed | **10 s** |
| **Dead Interval** | $4 \times \text{HelloInterval}$ | **40 s** |
| **Dead Interval (NBMA / P2MP)** | $4 \times 30$ | **120 s** |
| **MaxAge (LSA lifetime)** | When a router stops refreshing an LSA it expires | **3600 s** (1 hour) |
| **LSRefreshTime** | Periodic refresh interval | **1800 s** (30 min) |
| **OSPF Administrative Distance** | Trustworthiness vs other protocols | **110** |
| **OSPF Protocol Number** | IP Protocol field value | **89** |
| **Multicast group (AllSPFRouters)** | All OSPF routers listen | **224.0.0.5** |
| **Multicast group (AllDRouters)** | DR and BDR listen | **224.0.0.6** |
| **OSPFv2 RFC** | Specification | **RFC 2328** |
| **OSPFv3 RFC** | IPv6 specification | **RFC 5340** |
| **E = mc┬▓ (just verifying isolation)** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

> **Note on table notation:** The formula sheet above uses `\sum` and `\dfrac` (LaTeX-rendered). For absolute-value notation in tables use `\vert x \vert` not `|x|` to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **fully worked out**, with no skipped algebraic steps. KTU examiners award partial marks for every correctly written step ŌĆö practice this style.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö OSPF Path Cost Calculation

**Problem:** A packet travels from Router **R1 ŌåÆ R2 ŌåÆ R3 ŌåÆ R4**. The outbound interface bandwidths are **10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 1 Gbps** respectively. Using Cisco's default reference bandwidth, compute the **total OSPF cost** of this path.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the formula:**

$$
\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the Reference Bandwidth.** On a default Cisco IOS, the reference is **100 Mbps**. Express all bandwidths in Mbps for uniformity.

$$
B_{\text{ref}} = 100 \text{ Mbps}, \quad
B_1 = 10 \text{ Mbps}, \quad
B_2 = 100 \text{ Mbps}, \quad
B_3 = 1000 \text{ Mbps}, \quad
B_4 = 1000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate the cost of each individual outbound link.**

$$
\text{Cost}_1 = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_2 = \dfrac{100}{100} = 1
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_3 = \dfrac{100}{1000} = 0.1
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Cisco's rule:** OSPF cost must be an **integer Ōēź 1**. Any fractional cost is rounded **up** to 1.

$$
\text{Cost}_3 = \lceil 0.1 \rceil = 1, \qquad
\text{Cost}_4 = \lceil 0.1 \rceil = 1
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sum the costs along the path.**

$$
\text{PathCost} = \text{Cost}_1 + \text{Cost}_2 + \text{Cost}_3 + \text{Cost}_4
$$

$$
\text{PathCost} = 10 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 13
$$

**Final Answer:** The total OSPF cost from R1 to R4 is **13**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** A 1 Gbps link has the *same* OSPF cost as a 100 Mbps link by default. To make OSPF prefer 1 Gbps paths, network engineers issue the global command `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 10000` (in Mbps) to raise the reference to 10 Gbps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Dijkstra's SPF Algorithm (Worked Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE most repeated KTU Module-3 numerical.** Master this step-by-step.

**Problem:** Consider the network in the figure below. Each edge is labelled with its **OSPF cost**. Using **Dijkstra's algorithm**, compute the **shortest path** and the **shortest path cost** from source node **A** to every other node.

**Network topology (undirected weighted graph):**

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| AŌĆōB | 4 |
| AŌĆōC | 2 |
| BŌĆōC | 1 |
| BŌĆōD | 5 |
| CŌĆōD | 8 |
| CŌĆōE | 10 |
| DŌĆōE | 2 |

### Step 0 ŌĆö Initialization

Two sets are maintained:

- $\mathcal{P}$ = **Permanent set** (finalized shortest paths from A).
- $\mathcal{T}$ = **Tentative set** (current best-known distance, not yet final).

Initial state:

$$
