\mathcal{P} = \{A \to 0\}, \qquad
\mathcal{T} = \{B:\infty,\ C:\infty,\ D:\infty,\ E:\infty\}
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Process node A (current = 0)

Relax A's neighbours:

$$
\text{dist}(B) = 0 + 4 = 4
$$

$$
\text{dist}(C) = 0 + 2 = 2
$$

$$
\text{dist}(D) = 0 + \infty = \infty, \qquad
\text{dist}(E) = 0 + \infty = \infty
$$

State:

$$
\mathcal{P} = \{A:0\}, \qquad
\mathcal{T} = \{B:4,\ C:2,\ D:\infty,\ E:\infty\}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Pick the minimum from $\mathcal{T}$: **C = 2** ŌåÆ move to $\mathcal{P}$

Relax C's neighbours:

$$
\text{dist}(B) = \min(4,\ 2+1) = 3
$$

$$
\text{dist}(D) = \min(\infty,\ 2+8) = 10
$$

$$
\text{dist}(E) = \min(\infty,\ 2+10) = 12
$$

State:

$$
\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ C:2\}, \qquad
\mathcal{T} = \{B:3,\ D:10,\ E:12\}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Pick minimum from $\mathcal{T}$: **B = 3** ŌåÆ move to $\mathcal{P}$

Relax B's neighbours:

$$
\text{dist}(D) = \min(10,\ 3+5) = 8
$$

$$
\text{dist}(E) = \min(12,\ 3+\infty) = 12
$$

State:

$$
\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ C:2,\ B:3\}, \qquad
\mathcal{T} = \{D:8,\ E:12\}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Pick minimum: **D = 8** ŌåÆ move to $\mathcal{P}$

Relax D's neighbours:

$$
\text{dist}(E) = \min(12,\ 8+2) = 10
$$

State:

$$
\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ C:2,\ B:3,\ D:8\}, \qquad
\mathcal{T} = \{E:10\}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Pick minimum: **E = 10** ŌåÆ move to $\mathcal{P}$

$$
\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ B:3,\ C:2,\ D:8,\ E:10\}, \qquad
\mathcal{T} = \emptyset
$$

### Final Shortest Path Table (from A)

| Destination | Shortest Cost | Shortest Path (A ŌåÆ ŌĆ”) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 3 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 8 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D |
| E | 10 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E |

> [!TIP]
> **Reverse-engineering the path:** Once a node X is added to $\mathcal{P}$, its predecessor is the node that produced its minimum. Predecessors for this example: BŌåÉC, CŌåÉA, DŌåÉB, EŌåÉD. Reading backwards: EŌåÉDŌåÉBŌåÉCŌåÉA ŌåÆ **AŌĆōCŌĆōBŌĆōDŌĆōE**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Dead-Interval Calculation

**Problem:** On a Frame Relay (NBMA) network, the OSPF Hello timer is set to **30 seconds** by default. What is the **Dead Interval**?

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the relationship:**

$$
\text{DeadInterval} = k \cdot \text{HelloInterval}, \qquad k = 4
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute.**

$$
\text{DeadInterval} = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:** **120 seconds**. A neighbour is declared down if no Hello is heard for two full minutes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Cost vs Bandwidth Quick Reference

| Interface Bandwidth | Default OSPF Cost (Ref = 100 Mbps) | Adjusted Cost (Ref = 10 Gbps) |
|---|---|---|
| 64 kbps (Serial) | 1562 | 156250 |
| 1.544 Mbps (T1) | 64 | 6476 |
| 10 Mbps (Ethernet) | 10 | 1000 |
| 100 Mbps (Fast Ethernet) | 1 | 100 |
| 1 Gbps (GigE) | 1 (after ceil) | 10 |
| 10 Gbps (10 GigE) | 1 (after ceil) | 1 |
| 40 Gbps | 1 (after ceil) | 1 (after ceil of 0.25) |
| 100 Gbps | 1 (after ceil) | 1 (after ceil of 0.1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many 1s?** Because of the $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ rounding rule. To get meaningful differentiation for high-speed links, *always* raise the reference bandwidth to **10 Gbps (10000)** or higher in production networks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** with alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords as standalone IDs) and plain-text labels.

---

## 4.1 OSPF Hierarchical Area Topology (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    backbone["OSPF BACKBONE AREA 0 - Core Network"]
    abr1["ABR1 - Area Border Router"]
    abr2["ABR2 - Area Border Router"]
    asbr1["ASBR1 - AS Boundary Router"]
    asbr2["ASBR2 - AS Boundary Router"]
    area1["AREA 1 - Branch Office North"]
    area2["AREA 2 - Branch Office South"]
    area3["AREA 3 - Data Center"]
    as_ext["EXTERNAL AS - e.g. Internet via BGP"]
    r11["Internal Router R11"]
    r12["Internal Router R12"]
    r13["Internal Router R13"]
    r21["Internal Router R21"]
    r22["Internal Router R22"]
    r31["Internal Router R31"]
    r32["Internal Router R32"]

    backbone --- abr1
    backbone --- abr2
    backbone --- asbr1
    abr1 --- area1
    abr2 --- area2
    abr1 --- area3
    asbr1 --- as_ext
    area1 --- r11
    area1 --- r12
    area1 --- r13
    area2 --- r21
    area2 --- r22
    area3 --- r31
    area3 --- r32
    asbr2 --- area3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Area 0** (backbone) is the *spine* of the network.
- **ABRs** join Area 0 to non-backbone areas ŌĆö every non-backbone area must touch Area 0.
- **ASBR** injects routes from the external world (e.g. from a BGP neighbour).
- **Internal Routers** belong entirely to one non-backbone area.

---

## 4.2 OSPF Adjacency Formation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Down
    Down --> Init : Hello received from neighbour
    Init --> TwoWay : Bidirectional Hello confirmed
    TwoWay --> ExStart : Master Slave election via DBD
    ExStart --> Exchange : DBD packets exchanged
    Exchange --> Loading : LSRs sent for missing LSAs
    Loading --> Full : All LSAs received and acknowledged
    Full --> [*]
    TwoWay --> TwoWay : DROther on broadcast network
```

**Highlights:**
- **Down ŌåÆ Init ŌåÆ 2-Way** is driven entirely by **OSPF Hello (Type 1)** packets.
- **2-Way ŌåÆ ExStart ŌåÆ Exchange ŌåÆ Loading ŌåÆ Full** is driven by **DBD (Type 2)**, **LSR (Type 3)**, **LSU (Type 4)**, and **LSAck (Type 5)**.
- On a broadcast network, **DROther-to-DROther** relationships **stay in 2-Way** ŌĆö they never reach Full.

---

## 4.3 OSPF Packet Interaction Sequence (LSA Flooding)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R1 as Router R1
    participant R2 as Router R2
    participant R3 as Router R3

    R1 ->> R2 : Hello - 224.0.0.5 - Neighbour discovery
    R2 -->> R1 : Hello - 224.0.0.5 - Bidirectional
    R1 ->> R2 : DBD - Database Description
    R2 ->> R1 : DBD - Database Description
    R1 ->> R2 : LSR - Link State Request
    R2 ->> R1 : LSU - Link State Update with LSA
    R1 ->> R2 : LSAck - Acknowledgement
    R2 ->> R3 : LSU - Flood LSA to 224.0.0.5
    R3 -->> R2 : LSAck - Acknowledgement
    R1 ->> R1 : Run Dijkstra SPF on LSDB
    R2 ->> R2 : Run Dijkstra SPF on LSDB
    R3 ->> R3 : Run Dijkstra SPF on LSDB
```

**Reading the sequence:** Once the LSDB is identical on all three routers, each runs Dijkstra independently ŌĆö yet all three arrive at the **same Shortest Path Tree** because they share the same input.

---

## 4.4 OSPF Common Header Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    hdr["OSPF Packet - 24 Byte Header"]
    f1["Version 1 byte"]
    f2["Type 1 byte"]
    f3["Packet Length 2 bytes"]
    f4["Router ID 4 bytes"]
    f5["Area ID 4 bytes"]
    f6["Checksum 2 bytes"]
    f7["AuType 2 bytes"]
    f8["Authentication 8 bytes"]
    payload["LSA Payload variable"]

    hdr --- f1
    hdr --- f2
    hdr --- f3
    hdr --- f4
    hdr --- f5
    hdr --- f6
    hdr --- f7
    hdr --- f8
    hdr --- payload
```

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 1 byte | 2 for OSPFv2, 3 for OSPFv3 |
| Type | 1 byte | 1=Hello, 2=DBD, 3=LSR, 4=LSU, 5=LSAck |
| Packet Length | 2 bytes | Total OSPF packet size in bytes |
| Router ID | 4 bytes | 32-bit unique router identifier |
| Area ID | 4 bytes | 32-bit area the packet belongs to |
| Checksum | 2 bytes | Standard IP-style checksum (not over Auth) |
| AuType | 2 bytes | 0=None, 1=Simple, 2=Crypto (MD5/SHA) |
| Authentication | 8 bytes | Key / cryptographic digest |

---

## 4.5 Conceptual Block Architecture ŌĆö OSPF Functional Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
    app["Upper Layer Protocols - IP Packets - to be forwarded"]
    rib["Routing Information Base - RIB"]
    spf["Dijkstra SPF Engine - Computes SPT"]
    lsdb["Link State Database - LSDB - collection of LSAs"]
    lsa["LSA Management - generate - age - flood - refresh"]
    hello["Hello and Neighbour Subsystem"]
    iface["OSPF Enabled Interfaces - physical and logical"]
    net["Physical Network - Ethernet - Serial - Frame Relay"]

    app --> rib
    spf --> rib
    lsdb --> spf
    lsa --> lsdb
    hello --> lsa
    iface --> hello
    net --> iface
```

This functional view shows how a forwarded packet flows from the **network interface** up through the **LSDB** and **Dijkstra engine** to populate the **RIB**, and how data flows downward from the **RIB** back to the **forwarding engine** to push packets onto the wire.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A tests **recall + 2ŌĆō3 lines of application**. Answers below are written to the exact length expected at the board.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Distance-Vector and Link-State routing protocols. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Distance-Vector | Link-State |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge** | Each router knows only its **direct neighbours** ("routing by rumour"). | Each router maintains a **complete map** of the topology (LSDB). |
| **Algorithm** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra SPF |
| **Updates** | Periodic full-table | Event-triggered (incremental) |
| **Convergence** | Slow (RIP Ōēł 30ŌĆō180 s) | Fast (sub-second) |
| **CPU / Memory** | Low | Higher |
| **Examples** | **RIP, IGRP** | **OSPF, IS-IS** |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 example each.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**List the different OSPF packet types and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Type 1 ŌĆō Hello:** Discover and maintain neighbour relationships; elect DR/BDR.
2. **Type 2 ŌĆō DBD (Database Description):** Summarize the contents of the LSDB during initial sync.
3. **Type 3 ŌĆō LSR (Link-State Request):** Request specific LSAs not yet in the local LSDB.
4. **Type 4 ŌĆō LSU (Link-State Update):** Carry the full LSA(s) in response to an LSR or for triggered updates; this is the **flooding** packet.
5. **Type 5 ŌĆō LSAck (Link-State Acknowledgement):** Reliably confirm receipt of an LSU.

*(3 marks: 0.5 for each packet name + 0.1 for the function = 3.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark questions are split **7 + 7** (sub-parts a and b). Provide **TWO alternative questions** so the student can pick one.

---

### **Question A ŌĆö Option 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the OSPF hierarchical routing model with a neat diagram. List and explain the different router types in OSPF.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Need for Hierarchy (1 mark):**
A single-area OSPF network faces scalability issues ŌĆö every router stores the full LSDB, runs full SPF, and floods every LSA throughout the network. The **two-level hierarchical model** with **Areas** solves this. An **Area** is a logical grouping of routers whose LSDBs are *isolated* from other areas.

**2. Backbone Area ŌĆö Area 0 (1 mark):**
All OSPF networks **must** have a backbone area designated as **Area 0 (0.0.0.0)**. It is the central hub; all inter-area traffic must transit through Area 0. The LSDB of Area 0 is also separated from non-backbone areas.

**3. Router Types (5 marks):**

| Router Type | Definition | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Router** | All interfaces in **one single area** | Maintains one LSDB; runs SPF for that area only. |
| **Backbone Router** | Has at least **one interface in Area 0** | May also be an ABR, ASBR, or just internal to Area 0. |
| **Area Border Router (ABR)** | Interfaces in **two or more areas**, with one being **Area 0** | Maintains **separate LSDBs** for each area; generates **Type 3 Summary LSAs** to advertise inter-area routes. Performs **inter-area summarization**. |
| **AS Boundary Router (ASBR)** | Imports **external routes** (from BGP, static, etc.) into OSPF via **redistribution** | Generates **Type 5 AS-External LSAs** (and Type 7 in NSSA). |

**4. Diagram (1 mark ŌĆö see Section 4.1 above):** Draw the canonical Backbone Ōåö ABR Ōåö Area Ōåö Internal Router diagram.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the network shown in the table below. Using Dijkstra's algorithm, compute the **shortest path** from node **A** to all other nodes. Show every step.

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| AŌĆōB | 4 |
| AŌĆōC | 2 |
| BŌĆōC | 1 |
| BŌĆōD | 5 |
| CŌĆōD | 8 |
| CŌĆōE | 10 |
| DŌĆōE | 2 |

**Model Solution (write each step as a separate row ŌĆö partial marks are given per correct step):**

**Step 0 ŌĆö Initialize:**

- Permanent set: $\mathcal{P} = \{A:0\}$
- Tentative set: $\mathcal{T} = \{B:\infty,\ C:\infty,\ D:\infty,\ E:\infty\}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Process A (cost 0):**
- B: $0+4 = 4$, C: $0+2 = 2$
- $\mathcal{P} = \{A:0\}$; $\mathcal{T} = \{B:4,\ C:2,\ D:\infty,\ E:\infty\}$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Process C (smallest = 2):**
- B: $\min(4,\ 2+1) = 3$, D: $2+8 = 10$, E: $2+10 = 12$
- $\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ C:2\}$; $\mathcal{T} = \{B:3,\ D:10,\ E:12\}$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Process B (smallest = 3):**
- D: $\min(10,\ 3+5) = 8$, E: $12$
- $\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ C:2,\ B:3\}$; $\mathcal{T} = \{D:8,\ E:12\}$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Process D (smallest = 8):**
- E: $\min(12,\ 8+2) = 10$
- $\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ C:2,\ B:3,\ D:8\}$; $\mathcal{T} = \{E:10\}$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Process E (smallest = 10):**
- $\mathcal{P} = \{A:0,\ B:3,\ C:2,\ D:8,\ E:10\}$; $\mathcal{T} = \emptyset$

**Final Shortest Path Table:**

| Destination | Cost | Path |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 3 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B |
| C | 2 | A ŌåÆ C |
| D | 8 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D |
| E | 10 | A ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E |

*(Award: 1 mark for correct initialization, 4 marks for 4 correct step transitions, 1 mark for the final table, 1 mark for the path strings.)*

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Option 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **OSPF neighbour adjacency formation** process with all seven states. List the OSPF packet types exchanged during each state transition.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Need for Adjacency (1 mark):**
In a multi-access network with $n$ routers, forming full adjacencies between every pair would create $n(n-1)/2$ adjacencies. OSPF instead elects a **DR** and **BDR** so non-DR routers form adjacencies only with the DR/BDR ŌĆö reducing the number to $2(n-2)+1$. The *adjacency formation* FSM governs this process.

**2. The Seven States (4.5 marks):**

| # | State | Description | Packet Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Down** | No Hello received. Initial state. | ŌĆö |
| 2 | **Init** | Hello received from neighbour, but neighbour has **not seen** *our* Router ID in its Hello. | Hello |
| 3 | **2-Way** | Bidirectional communication. **Decision point:** if DR/BDR or P2P ŌåÆ proceed; if DROther on broadcast ŌåÆ stay. | Hello |
| 4 | **ExStart** | Master/Slave election for DBD. Higher Router ID becomes Master. | DBD (empty) |
| 5 | **Exchange** | DBD packets sent. Routers compare summaries and build the **Link-State Request List**. | DBD |
| 6 | **Loading** | LSR sent for each missing LSA; LSU received; LSAck sent. | LSR, LSU, LSAck |
| 7 | **Full** | LSDBs are **identical**. Adjacency is fully established. | ŌĆö |

**3. Diagram (1 mark ŌĆö refer to Section 4.2):** Draw the FSM showing transitions.

**4. Significance of 2-Way State (0.5 mark):** On broadcast networks, two **DROthers** stay in 2-Way ŌĆö they have exchanged Hellos but never form a full adjacency.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **LSA types** in OSPF. How is **LSA flooding** carried out and what guarantees its reliability?

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Seven LSA Types (3 marks):**

| Type | Name | Generator | Scope | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Router LSA | Every router | Inside its area | Describes router's links and state |
| 2 | Network LSA | DR | Inside the area | Lists routers on the multi-access segment |
| 3 | Summary LSA | ABR | Between areas | Inter-area route advertisement |
| 4 | ASBR Summary LSA | ABR | Between areas | Tells other areas how to reach the ASBR |
| 5 | AS External LSA | ASBR | Whole AS (except stub) | External (non-OSPF) routes |
| 6 | Multicast OSPF LSA | MOSPF router | ŌĆö | Reserved (rarely used) |
| 7 | NSSA External LSA | ASBR inside NSSA | Inside NSSA | External routes inside NSSA |

**2. LSA Age and Refresh (1 mark):**
Each LSA has a 16-bit **age** field. Incremented each hop and each second. **MaxAge = 3600 s** (1 hour). If not refreshed within this time, the LSA is **purged**. Routers refresh their self-originated LSAs every **30 minutes (LSRefreshTime)**.

**3. The Flooding Procedure (2 marks):**

1. When a router's link changes, it **originates** a new LSA and floods it to **224.0.0.5** (or 224.0.0.6 if going to DR).
2. The receiving router **validates** the LSA checksum, age, and sequence number.
3. If the LSA is **newer** than the local copy, the router:
   - Installs it into its **LSDB**.
   - **Re-floods** it to all other interfaces (except the one it arrived on).
   - Sends an **LSAck** to acknowledge.
4. If the LSA is **duplicate** or **older**, it is silently dropped or an updated copy is sent back.

**4. Reliability Mechanism (1 mark):**
- Each LSU is **acknowledged** (explicitly or implicitly via duplicate suppression).
- **Sequence numbers** wrap from 0x80000001 (initial) to 0x7FFFFFFF (max) to prevent duplicates.
- **Checksum** ensures integrity; aged-out LSAs trigger re-flooding via **LSRefresh**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose 1ŌĆō3 marks per question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "neighbour" with "adjacency".** A *neighbour* is simply "we can see each other's Hello"; an *adjacency* means "we exchanged our full LSDBs".
> 2. **Forgetting the integer-ceiling rule** for OSPF cost. $\lceil 0.1 \rceil = 1$, not 0. Students who write `0.1` or `0` lose a mark.
> 3. **Misidentifying the DR election order.** It is **highest OSPF priority first**, then **highest Router ID as tie-breaker**. The reverse order is incorrect.
> 4. **Writing `DR` and `BDR` as the only routers in a broadcast network.** The correct term for the *others* is **DROther** ŌĆö and they remain in **2-Way**, not Full, with each other.
> 5. **Dijkstra's algorithm skipped steps.** Examiners award **1 mark per step transition**. Skipping Step 2 or Step 3 costs 1 mark each.
> 6. **Confusing LSA Type 3 and Type 4.** Type 3 advertises **networks**; Type 4 advertises the **location of the ASBR**. Both are generated by ABRs.
> 7. **Forgetting that DR election is non-preemptive.** A new router joining with the highest priority does **not** displace the current DR until the DR fails.
> 8. **Stating OSPF uses TCP or UDP.** OSPF uses **IP Protocol 89 directly** ŌĆö no transport-layer port numbers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density 30-second revision checklist for OSPF:**

- ŌŁÉ OSPF = **Open**, **Link-State**, **Classless**, **Hierarchical**, **IGP** ŌĆö RFC 2328 (v2) / RFC 5340 (v3).
- ŌŁÉ **IP Protocol Number = 89**; **Administrative Distance = 110**; **Multicast = 224.0.0.5 / .6**.
- ŌŁÉ **Metric = Cost = $\lceil \text{RefBw} / \text{IntfBw} \rceil$** (default RefBw = 100 Mbps).
- ŌŁÉ **Three operational phases:** Neighbour Discovery (Hello) ŌåÆ Adjacency & DB Sync (DBD/LSR/LSU/LSAck) ŌåÆ SPF Computation (Dijkstra).
- ŌŁÉ **Hierarchical model:** **Backbone Area 0 is mandatory**; non-backbone areas must connect to it (directly or via Virtual Link).
- ŌŁÉ **Four router types:** Internal, Backbone, **ABR**, **ASBR**.
- ŌŁÉ **Five packet types:** Hello (1), DBD (2), LSR (3), LSU (4), LSAck (5).
- ŌŁÉ **Seven states:** Down ŌåÆ Init ŌåÆ 2-Way ŌåÆ ExStart ŌåÆ Exchange ŌåÆ Loading ŌåÆ Full.
- ŌŁÉ **Seven LSA types:** Router (1), Network (2), Summary (3), ASBR Summary (4), AS External (5), Multicast (6), NSSA External (7).
- ŌŁÉ **Timers:** Hello = **10 s** (Bcast/P2P) or **30 s** (NBMA/P2MP); Dead = **4 ├Ś Hello** = **40 s** or **120 s**.
- ŌŁÉ **LSA lifetime:** MaxAge = **3600 s**; LSRefresh = **1800 s** (30 min).
- ŌŁÉ **DR/BDR election:** Highest **Priority** (default 1) wins; tie ŌåÆ highest **Router ID**; election is **non-preemptive**.
- ŌŁÉ **DROther pairs** stay in **2-Way**; only DR/BDR reach **Full** with all others.
- ŌŁÉ **Dijkstra's algorithm** produces a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the local router.
- ŌŁÉ **Convergence** is fast (sub-second) because of triggered LSA updates + reliable flooding + LSAck.
- ŌŁÉ **OSPFv2 = IPv4**, **OSPFv3 = IPv6**; v3 removes IP addressing from the OSPF packet (relies on IPv6 link-local).
- ŌŁÉ **LSDB is identical** across all routers in the **same area**; only ABRs and ASBRs carry information between areas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)** is the **de facto inter-domain routing protocol** of the global Internet, standardized in **RFC 4271**. It is classified as a **Path-Vector Protocol** that enables **policy-based routing** between **Autonomous Systems (AS)**. Each AS is identified by a globally unique **16-bit (later extended to 32-bit) Autonomous System Number (ASN)** assigned by IANA/Regional Internet Registries (RIRs).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, BGP is positioned as the *inter-AS routing protocol* that operates above the **intra-AS protocols** like OSPF and RIP. It is the only protocol used to exchange reachability information between independently administered networks on the public Internet.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Autonomous System (AS)** | A collection of IP networks and routers under the control of a single organization that presents a common routing policy to the Internet. |
| **eBGP (External BGP)** | BGP session established between routers belonging to **two different** ASes. |
| **iBGP (Internal BGP)** | BGP session established between routers **within the same** AS. |
| **Speaker** | A router running BGP is called a BGP speaker. |
| **Peer / Neighbor** | Another BGP speaker with which a TCP connection (port **179**) has been established. |
| **NLRI (Network Layer Reachability Information)** | The set of IP prefixes advertised in a BGP UPDATE message. |
| **Path Vector** | The list of AS numbers a route has traversed, recorded in the `AS_PATH` attribute. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "The International Airline Hub System":**
> Imagine you want to fly from **Kochi (COK)** to **Toronto (YYZ)**. No single airline operates the entire route. You must transfer through hubs (e.g., **Dubai ŌåÆ London ŌåÆ Toronto**). The *airline alliance* (Star Alliance, OneWorld) decides which hubs to use, based on **commercial agreements (policy)** rather than shortest distance. The list of hubs you traverse is stamped on your ticket ŌĆö this is exactly what BGP does. Each AS is an "airline alliance," and the `AS_PATH` is the sequence of hubs your packet crosses. BGP does **not** pick the geographically shortest path ŌĆö it picks the **policy-compliant** path.

### 1.4 Why BGP is NOT OSPF or RIP

| Feature | RIP | OSPF | **BGP** |
|---------|-----|------|---------|
| Type | Distance Vector | Link State | **Path Vector** |
| Metric | Hop Count | Cost (bandwidth) | **Policy + Path Attributes** |
| Scope | Intra-AS | Intra-AS | **Inter-AS (and Intra-AS via iBGP)** |
| Algorithm | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra | **Best-Path Selection (per-policy)** |
| Convergence | Slow | Fast | **Slow but policy-correct** |
| Transport | UDP | IP | **TCP (Port 179)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Highlight:** BGP is a **path-vector protocol** ŌĆö it carries the *complete sequence of AS numbers* in its updates, which prevents the count-to-infinity problem of distance-vector protocols and enables loop detection across the entire Internet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Position of BGP in the TCP/IP Stack

BGP operates as an **Application Layer** protocol because it uses **TCP port 179** for reliable transport. However, its purpose is strictly **network-layer routing**.

### 2.2 BGP Message Types (KTU High-Yield)

| Message Type | Code | Purpose | Frequency |
|--------------|------|---------|-----------|
| **OPEN** | 1 | Establishes TCP session; negotiates hold-time, BGP version, AS number, BGP Identifier. | Once at session start |
| **UPDATE** | 2 | Advertises new routes, withdraws unreachable routes, or both. | As needed |
| **KEEPALIVE** | 3 | Confirms that the peer is alive; prevents hold-timer expiry. | Every **60 seconds** (default) |
| **NOTIFICATION** | 4 | Reports errors and immediately tears down the BGP session. | On error only |
| **ROUTE-REFRESH** (Optional, RFC 2918) | 5 | Requests a peer to resend its full Adj-RIB-Out. | On policy change |

### 2.3 BGP Path Attributes (The Heart of BGP ŌĆö KTU Most Important)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A BGP UPDATE message carries **three components**: withdrawn routes, NLRI, and **path attributes**. Path attributes describe the route's properties and are used in the BGP best-path selection algorithm.

| Attribute | Type Code | Category | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------|----------|---------|
| **ORIGIN** | 1 | Well-known Mandatory | IGP (0), EGP (1), or INCOMPLETE (2) |
| **AS_PATH** | 2 | Well-known Mandatory | Sequence of ASes the route has traversed |
| **NEXT_HOP** | 3 | Well-known Mandatory | IP address of the next-hop router |
| **MULTI_EXIT_DISC (MED)** | 4 | Optional Non-Transitive | Hint to external neighbors about preferred entry point |
| **LOCAL_PREF** | 5 | Well-known Discretionary | Preference within the local AS (higher is better) |
| **ATOMIC_AGGREGATE** | 6 | Well-known Discretionary | Indicates that aggregation has been performed |
| **AGGREGATOR** | 7 | Optional Transitive | AS + Router ID that performed aggregation |
| **COMMUNITY** | 8 | Optional Transitive | Tag for applying routing policies |
| **ORIGINATOR_ID** | 9 | Optional Non-Transitive (iBGP) | Router ID of the route originator (RR loop prevention) |
| **CLUSTER_LIST** | 10 | Optional Non-Transitive (iBGP) | List of Route Reflector cluster IDs |

### 2.4 The Four Attribute Categories

1. **Well-known Mandatory**: Must be present in every UPDATE (e.g., ORIGIN, AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP).
2. **Well-known Discretionary**: Must be recognized, but not required in every UPDATE (e.g., LOCAL_PREF).
3. **Optional Transitive**: May be passed to other BGP peers even if not understood (e.g., COMMUNITY).
4. **Optional Non-Transitive**: Discarded if not understood (e.g., MED, ORIGINATOR_ID).

### 2.5 BGP Path Selection Algorithm (KTU Favourite)

When multiple feasible routes exist to the same destination, the BGP decision process runs in the following **strict order** until a winner is found:

1. Highest **WEIGHT** (Cisco proprietary, local to router).
2. Highest **LOCAL_PREF**.
3. Locally originated route (next-hop = 0.0.0.0).
4. Shortest **AS_PATH**.
5. Lowest **ORIGIN** code (IGP < EGP < INCOMPLETE).
6. Lowest **MED** (only compared if from the same neighboring AS).
7. Prefer **eBGP** over iBGP paths.
8. Lowest IGP metric to the NEXT_HOP.
9. Oldest path (minimizes route flapping).
10. Lowest **Router ID** of the BGP speaker.
11. Lowest **IP address** of the neighbor.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| AS_PATH loop prevention | If a router sees its own AS in `AS_PATH` ŌåÆ reject the route | Avoids inter-AS routing loops |
| NEXT_HOP on eBGP | Set to the IP address of the advertising router | Standard behavior |
| NEXT_HOP on iBGP (3rd party) | Unchanged from eBGP advertisement | Requires `next-hop-self` configuration |
| Hold-Time | **180 seconds** (default) | Must be **0 seconds** or **at least 3 seconds** |
| Keepalive Interval | **60 seconds** (default) | One-third of hold-time |
| TCP Port | **179** | BGP transport port |
| IPv4 prefix encoding | `/n` where $n \in \vert 0, 32 \vert$ | Standard CIDR |
| IPv6 prefix encoding | `/n` where $n \in \vert 0, 128 \vert$ | Extended CIDR |
| AS Number (legacy) | 16-bit ŌåÆ $2^{16} = 65536$ values (0ŌĆō65535) | Historical 2-byte ASN |
| AS Number (extended) | 32-bit ŌåÆ $2^{32}$ values (RFC 6793) | Modern 4-byte ASN |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

BGP is the **backbone protocol of the global Internet**. It is used by:
- **ISPs (Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3)** to peer with each other.
- **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)** like Akamai, Cloudflare, AWS to announce their IP prefixes.
- **Data Centers** running anycast services (DNS root servers, cloud regions).
- **Enterprise WANs** for multi-homing to two or more ISPs (active-active or active-passive).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 BGP Finite State Machine (FSM) ŌĆö Exhaustive Transition Analysis

BGP peers traverse **six states** before they can exchange routing information. Below is the **analytical derivation** of each transition.

#### State 1: `Idle`
- **Initial state.** The router has **no BGP session** with this peer.
- The router refuses all incoming BGP connections.
- The router initializes resources, starts the **ConnectRetry timer (120 s default)**, and initiates a TCP connection to the configured peer.
- **Trigger to leave:** TCP connection initiated successfully OR a `Start` event from the operator (manual reset).

#### State 2: `Connect`
- TCP connection is **in progress**.
- If TCP succeeds ŌåÆ transition to `OpenSent` and send an **OPEN message**.
- If TCP fails ŌåÆ transition to `Active` state.
- If `ConnectRetry timer` expires ŌåÆ restart TCP, remain in `Connect`.

#### State 3: `Active`
- The router is **actively trying** to acquire a peer by initiating a new TCP connection.
- If TCP succeeds ŌåÆ transition to `OpenSent`.
- If `ConnectRetry timer` expires ŌåÆ return to `Connect`.
- If the peer resets the connection ŌåÆ stay in `Active` and retry.
- **Symptom of "stuck in Active"**: Wrong IP, ACL blocking TCP 179, or mismatched source IP.

#### State 4: `OpenSent`
- TCP connection is established; BGP is **waiting for the peer's OPEN message**.
- The local router has sent its OWN OPEN message.
- Validation begins: check peer's AS number, BGP version, BGP Identifier, and optional parameters.
- If OPEN is received and valid ŌåÆ transition to `OpenConfirm`.
- If error detected ŌåÆ send **NOTIFICATION** ŌåÆ transition to `Idle`.

#### State 5: `OpenConfirm`
- Local router has sent **KEEPALIVE** in response to a valid OPEN.
- The router is waiting for the peer's KEEPALIVE or NOTIFICATION.
- If KEEPALIVE received ŌåÆ transition to `Established`.
- If NOTIFICATION received ŌåÆ transition to `Idle`.

#### State 6: `Established`
- **Peering is fully active.** UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, and NOTIFICATION messages can be exchanged.
- **Hold-Timer:** Reset every time any BGP message (including KEEPALIVE) is received.
- If Hold-Timer expires (default **180 s** without any message) ŌåÆ send NOTIFICATION ŌåÆ `Idle`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total transition formula** for a healthy session: `Idle ŌåÆ Connect ŌåÆ OpenSent ŌåÆ OpenConfirm ŌåÆ Established`. The minimum time to reach `Established` is governed by the TCP three-way handshake plus OPEN/KEEPALIVE exchange ŌĆö typically **under 3 seconds** on a healthy link.

### 3.2 Derivation of BGP Path Selection Tie-Break

Consider a scenario where two routes to prefix **203.0.113.0/24** are received from different peers. The decision process is:

$$
\text{Winner} = \arg\max_{r \in R} \Big[ W(r),\ L(r),\ O(r),\ S(r),\ M(r),\ E(r),\ I(r) \Big]
$$

Where:
- $W(r)$ = WEIGHT of route $r$ (highest wins)
- $L(r)$ = LOCAL\_PREF of route $r$ (highest wins)
- $O(r) = 0$ if locally originated, else $\infty$ (lower wins)
- $S(r)$ = length of AS\_PATH of route $r$ (lowest wins)
- $M(r)$ = ORIGIN code of route $r$ (lowest wins: IGP=0 < EGP=1 < INCOMPLETE=2)
- $D(r)$ = MED of route $r$ (lowest wins, only when neighbors are in same AS)
- $E(r) = 0$ if eBGP, $1$ if iBGP (eBGP preferred)

The selection is performed in a **lexicographic order**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{If } W(r_1) \neq W(r_2) \rightarrow \text{pick the higher} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Else if } L(r_1) \neq L(r_2) \rightarrow \text{pick the higher} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Else if origin differs} \rightarrow \text{pick the lower ORIGIN code} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Else pick the shorter AS\_PATH} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Else pick the lower MED (same-neighbor-AS rule)} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Else prefer eBGP over iBGP} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Else pick the lowest IGP cost to NEXT\_HOP} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Else pick the oldest route} \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Else pick the lowest Router ID} \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Else pick the lowest peer IP} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: eBGP vs iBGP Behavior

| Behavior | **eBGP** | **iBGP** |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Peers belong to | Different ASes | Same AS |
| AS_PATH modification | Prepends local AS | Does **not** modify AS_PATH |
| NEXT_HOP | Set to advertising router's IP | Preserved from eBGP (or rewritten with `next-hop-self`) |
| LOCAL_PREF propagation | Sent to iBGP peers | Propagated within the AS only |
| MED handling | Sent to eBGP neighbors | Exchanged within the AS |
| IGP requirement | Not strictly required | Required (iBGP routers must be IGP-reachable) |
| Loop prevention | AS_PATH check | Split-horizon / Route Reflector / Confederation |
| Default TTL | **1** | **255** |
| TCP session | Direct between ASes | Can traverse IGP-routed path |

### 3.4 Worked Example: AS_PATH Loop Detection

Suppose AS **65010** receives a route from AS **65020** with the following AS_PATH:

$$
AS\_PATH = [65020, 65030, 65040, 65050]
$$

When AS 65010 considers inserting its own AS, it would produce:

$$
AS\_PATH = [65010, 65020, 65030, 65040, 65050]
$$

If AS 65010 then re-advertises this to AS **65040** (somehow), AS 65040 scans the AS_PATH, finds **its own AS number (65040)** embedded, and **rejects** the route. This is the fundamental inter-AS loop prevention mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BGP Finite State Machine Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stIdle

    stIdle --> stConnect : TCP connect initiated
    stConnect --> stOpenSent : TCP established / send OPEN
    stConnect --> stActive : TCP failed
    stActive --> stConnect : ConnectRetry expired
    stActive --> stOpenSent : TCP established
    stOpenSent --> stOpenConfirm : OPEN received and valid
    stOpenSent --> stIdle : NOTIFICATION or error
    stOpenConfirm --> stEstablished : KEEPALIVE received
    stOpenConfirm --> stIdle : NOTIFICATION received
    stEstablished --> stIdle : HoldTimer expired / NOTIFICATION
    stEstablished --> stEstablished : KEEPALIVE / UPDATE exchanged
    stIdle --> stIdle : Operator reset
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: eBGP and iBGP Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AS100
        R1[Router R1 - AS100]
        R2[Router R2 - AS100]
        R3[Router R3 - AS100]
    end

    subgraph AS200
        R4[Router R4 - AS200]
        R5[Router R5 - AS200]
    end

    subgraph AS300
        R6[Router R6 - AS300]
        R7[Router R7 - AS300]
    end

    R1 ---|eBGP TCP 179| R4
    R2 ---|eBGP TCP 179| R5
    R4 ---|iBGP TCP 179| R5
    R5 ---|eBGP TCP 179| R6
    R6 ---|iBGP TCP 179| R7
    R1 ---|iBGP TCP 179| R2
    R2 ---|iBGP TCP 179| R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** `eBGP` links cross AS boundaries (R1ŌåöR4, R2ŌåöR5, R5ŌåöR6). `iBGP` links stay inside a single AS (R1ŌåöR2ŌåöR3 inside AS100; R4ŌåöR5 inside AS200; R6ŌåöR7 inside AS300). This is the canonical "AS-graph" model that the global Internet follows.

### 4.3 BGP Message Exchange Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SP as Speaker A
    participant TC as TCP Port 179
    participant PP as Speaker B

    Note over SP,PP: TCP Three-Way Handshake
    SP ->> TC: SYN
    TC ->> PP: SYN
    PP ->> TC: SYN-ACK
    TC ->> SP: SYN-ACK
    SP ->> TC: ACK
    TC ->> PP: ACK

    Note over SP,PP: BGP Session Negotiation
    SP ->> PP: OPEN (AS, RouterID, HoldTime)
    PP ->> SP: OPEN (AS, RouterID, HoldTime)
    SP ->> PP: KEEPALIVE
    PP ->> SP: KEEPALIVE

    Note over SP,PP: Established State - Route Exchange
    SP ->> PP: UPDATE (NLRI + Path Attributes)
    PP ->> SP: UPDATE (NLRI + Path Attributes)

    loop Every 60 seconds
        SP ->> PP: KEEPALIVE
        PP ->> SP: KEEPALIVE
    end
```

### 4.4 BGP Best-Path Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Multiple BGP routes to same prefix] --> B{Step 1: Highest WEIGHT}
    B -->|Yes| Z[Install in RIB - Winner]
    B -->|Tie| C{Step 2: Highest LOCAL_PREF}
    C -->|Yes| Z
    C -->|Tie| D{Step 3: Locally originated}
    D -->|Yes| Z
    D -->|Tie| E{Step 4: Shortest AS_PATH}
    E -->|Yes| Z
    E -->|Tie| F{Step 5: Lowest ORIGIN}
    F -->|Yes| Z
    F -->|Tie| G{Step 6: Lowest MED}
    G -->|Yes| Z
    G -->|Tie| H{Step 7: Prefer eBGP over iBGP}
    H -->|Yes| Z
    H -->|Tie| I{Step 8: Lowest IGP metric to NEXT_HOP}
    I -->|Yes| Z
    I -->|Tie| J{Step 9: Oldest route}
    J -->|Yes| Z
    J -->|Tie| K{Step 10: Lowest Router ID}
    K -->|Yes| Z
    K -->|Tie| L{Step 11: Lowest neighbor IP}
    L -->|Yes| Z
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Border Gateway Protocol (BGP). Why is it classified as a path-vector protocol rather than a distance-vector or link-state protocol?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
BGP is the standardized inter-domain routing protocol of the Internet, defined in **RFC 4271**, used to exchange reachability information between **Autonomous Systems (ASes)**.

**Path-Vector Justification (2 marks):**
- BGP advertises the **complete sequence of AS numbers** (the `AS_PATH` attribute) that a route has traversed.
- This is unlike **distance-vector** (RIP) which carries only a hop count.
- It is unlike **link-state** (OSPF) which floods a complete topology database.
- The `AS_PATH` serves **two purposes**: (i) loop prevention (reject routes containing own AS), and (ii) policy-based path selection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*List any four BGP message types and state the purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Message Type | Purpose |
|---|--------------|---------|
| 1 | **OPEN** | Establishes BGP session; negotiates AS number, hold-time, BGP version, Router ID. |
| 2 | **UPDATE** | Advertises new feasible routes (NLRI) and/or withdraws unreachable routes. |
| 3 | **KEEPALIVE** | Sent every **60 s** by default to maintain the BGP session. |
| 4 | **NOTIFICATION** | Sent when an error is detected; immediately terminates the BGP session. |

*(1 mark for listing four correctly, 2 marks for brief purpose explanation of each.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing eBGP NEXT_HOP with iBGP NEXT_HOP** ŌĆö students frequently write "NEXT_HOP is always the advertising router" ŌĆö this is FALSE for iBGP unless `next-hop-self` is configured. **Lose 1ŌĆō2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting that LOCAL_PREF is propagated only within the AS** ŌĆö it is sent to **iBGP** peers, NOT to **eBGP** peers. Cross-AS influence is via MED only.
> 3. **Writing "BGP uses UDP"** ŌĆö this is a deadly mistake. BGP uses **TCP port 179**. Zero marks for protocol/transport.
> 4. **Skipping the FSM states** in order ŌĆö the FSM is graded step-by-step. Missing `Active` state loses a mark.
> 5. **Not stating default timers** (Keepalive=60s, Hold=180s) ŌĆö examiners specifically test these values.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **BGP Finite State Machine (FSM)**. List all six states and explain the conditions that cause a transition from `Idle` to `Established` state. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **BGP path attributes** in detail. Differentiate between **Well-known Mandatory**, **Well-known Discretionary**, **Optional Transitive**, and **Optional Non-Transitive** attributes with two examples each. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BGP Finite State Machine (7 marks):**

BGP uses an FSM with **six states** to establish and maintain a peering session. The state diagram is as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**State-by-state explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Idle** ŌĆö The router is not ready to establish a session. It refuses incoming connections. After operator intervention or auto-start, it transitions to `Connect` and initiates a TCP connection on port **179**.

2. **Connect** ŌĆö Waiting for the TCP three-way handshake to complete. If successful ŌåÆ moves to `OpenSent` and sends an **OPEN** message. If TCP fails ŌåÆ moves to `Active` state.

3. **Active** ŌĆö The router is trying to acquire a peer by initiating/retrying the TCP connection. If TCP succeeds ŌåÆ `OpenSent`. If `ConnectRetry timer (120 s)` expires ŌåÆ returns to `Connect`.

4. **OpenSent** ŌĆö TCP is up; the router has sent its OPEN and is waiting for the peer's OPEN. Upon receiving a valid OPEN, it transitions to `OpenConfirm`. On error ŌåÆ `Idle`.

5. **OpenConfirm** ŌĆö The router has sent a KEEPALIVE in response to a valid OPEN. It now waits for the peer's KEEPALIVE or NOTIFICATION. On KEEPALIVE ŌåÆ `Established`.

6. **Established** ŌĆö Peering is fully active. UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, and NOTIFICATION can be exchanged. The Hold-Timer (180 s default) must be reset by incoming messages; if it expires, the session is torn down.

**Transition summary from Idle to Established (2 marks):**

$$
Idle \xrightarrow{\text{TCP SYN}} Connect \xrightarrow{\text{TCP success + OPEN sent}} OpenSent \xrightarrow{\text{OPEN received + valid}} OpenConfirm \xrightarrow{\text{KEEPALIVE received}} Established
$$

---

**(b) BGP Path Attributes (7 marks):**

Path attributes are encoded in **TLV (Type-Length-Value)** format within the UPDATE message. They describe the route and influence the best-path selection.

**Category definitions (4 marks ŌĆö 1 mark per category):**

| Category | Rule | Examples |
|----------|------|----------|
| **Well-known Mandatory** | Must be present in every UPDATE; must be supported by all BGP implementations. | `ORIGIN`, `AS_PATH`, `NEXT_HOP` |
| **Well-known Discretionary** | Must be recognized by all implementations, but need not be present in every UPDATE. | `LOCAL_PREF`, `ATOMIC_AGGREGATE` |
| **Optional Transitive** | May be passed to other peers (iBGP/eBGP) even if not understood by the local router. | `COMMUNITY`, `AGGREGATOR` |
| **Optional Non-Transitive** | Discarded if not understood; not passed to other peers. | `MED`, `ORIGINATOR_ID`, `CLUSTER_LIST` |

**Detailed attribute functions (3 marks):**

- **`ORIGIN`** ŌĆö Indicates the origin of the NLRI: IGP (0), EGP (1), or INCOMPLETE (2). Lower value preferred.
- **`AS_PATH`** ŌĆö Sequence of ASNs traversed. Used for loop prevention and path length comparison.
- **`NEXT_HOP`** ŌĆö IP address of the next-hop router to reach the NLRI.
- **`LOCAL_PREF`** ŌĆö Used within an AS to select the outbound exit point; higher is preferred; propagated only to iBGP peers.
- **`MED (MULTI_EXIT_DISC)`** ŌĆö Hint sent to a neighboring AS to influence their inbound traffic engineering; lower is preferred.
- **`COMMUNITY`** ŌĆö A tag (e.g., `no-export`, `no-advertise`) used to apply routing policies in bulk.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **BGP best-path selection algorithm** in detail. Discuss why a **shorter AS_PATH is preferred** and how **LOCAL_PREF** influences route selection within an AS. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **eBGP and iBGP** in terms of peering, AS_PATH modification, NEXT_HOP behavior, default TTL, and loop-prevention mechanism. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BGP Best-Path Selection Algorithm (7 marks):**

When multiple feasible routes exist to the same destination prefix, BGP applies a **strictly ordered decision process**. The decision is made using a **lexicographic comparison** of attributes:

1. **Highest WEIGHT** (Cisco proprietary, locally significant).
2. **Highest LOCAL_PREF** ŌĆö used within the AS to choose the preferred exit point.
3. **Locally originated** route wins.
4. **Shortest AS_PATH** ŌĆö fewer AS hops preferred.
5. **Lowest ORIGIN code** ŌĆö IGP (0) < EGP (1) < INCOMPLETE (2).
6. **Lowest MED** ŌĆö compared only when both routes come from the same neighboring AS.
7. **Prefer eBGP** over iBGP.
8. **Lowest IGP cost** to the NEXT_HOP.
9. **Oldest** route (to minimize churn).
10. **Lowest Router ID** of the advertising BGP speaker.
11. **Lowest neighbor IP** address.

**Why shorter AS_PATH is preferred (2 marks):**
AS_PATH length is a proxy for path "cost" in terms of AS-level hops. A shorter AS_PATH implies fewer autonomous systems are traversed, which generally translates to lower latency and fewer policy restrictions. However, the *real* reason is policy-driven ŌĆö operators configure AS_PATH prepending to artificially lengthen a path and steer traffic away from a particular link.

**How LOCAL_PREF influences selection (2 marks):**
`LOCAL_PREF` is set on a router and **propagated only to iBGP peers** within the same AS. It allows the local AS to choose its preferred outbound exit point. The route with the **highest** LOCAL_PREF is selected. For example, an AS multi-homed to two ISPs can set LOCAL_PREF = 200 for ISP-A and LOCAL_PREF = 100 for ISP-B, forcing all outbound traffic to use ISP-A.

---

**(b) eBGP vs iBGP Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | **eBGP** | **iBGP** |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Peering scope** | Between two different ASes | Within the same AS |
| **AS_PATH modification** | Local AS is prepended to AS_PATH | AS_PATH is NOT modified |
| **NEXT_HOP behavior** | Set to IP of the advertising router (eBGP peer) | Preserved from eBGP; can be overwritten with `next-hop-self` |
| **Default TTL** | **1** (peers must be directly connected) | **255** (can traverse IGP-routed path) |
| **Loop prevention** | AS_PATH scan (reject if own AS appears) | Split-horizon rule + Route Reflector (ORIGINATOR_ID, CLUSTER_LIST) or Confederation |
| **LOCAL_PREF propagation** | Sent to iBGP peers (received from iBGP, not sent to eBGP) | Exchanged between all iBGP speakers within the AS |
| **MED propagation** | Received from eBGP and forwarded to iBGP | Forwarded to eBGP peers |
| **TCP connection** | Usually direct (multi-hop possible via `ebgp-multihop`) | Indirect via IGP-routed path |

**Key conceptual difference (1 mark):**
eBGP is used to advertise **reachability** to external ASes (inter-domain), while iBGP is used to **propagate** the external routes learned via eBGP to all other BGP speakers *within* the same AS ŌĆö so that consistent policy decisions are made.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö BGP**

- Ō£ģ **BGP = Path-Vector Protocol** (RFC 4271); carries full `AS_PATH` in updates.
- Ō£ģ Uses **TCP port 179** ŌĆö never UDP, never IP directly.
- Ō£ģ **Two flavors:** eBGP (between ASes) and iBGP (within an AS).
- Ō£ģ **Five message types:** OPEN, UPDATE, KEEPALIVE, NOTIFICATION, ROUTE-REFRESH.
- Ō£ģ **Six FSM states:** Idle, Connect, Active, OpenSent, OpenConfirm, Established.
- Ō£ģ **Default timers:** Keepalive = **60 s**, Hold-Timer = **180 s**, ConnectRetry = **120 s**.
- Ō£ģ **Default TTL:** eBGP = **1**, iBGP = **255**.
- Ō£ģ **AS_PATH loop prevention:** reject any update containing your own ASN.
- Ō£ģ **Path attributes** are categorized into 4 types: Well-known Mandatory, Well-known Discretionary, Optional Transitive, Optional Non-Transitive.
- Ō£ģ **LOCAL_PREF:** higher is better; propagates only to **iBGP** peers.
- Ō£ģ **MED (MULTI_EXIT_DISC):** lower is better; propagates only to **eBGP** peers.
- Ō£ģ **Best-path selection** is a strict ordered 11-step lexicographic process.
- Ō£ģ **iBGP full-mesh** requirement can be relaxed using **Route Reflectors** (with `ORIGINATOR_ID` and `CLUSTER_LIST`) or **Confederations**.
- Ō£ģ **AS numbers** are 16-bit historically (65,536 values); modern 32-bit (RFC 6793) supports 4 billion ASNs.
- Ō£ģ BGP does **not** internally use metrics like bandwidth or delay; it is **policy-driven**.
- Ō£ģ BGP convergence is **slow** (minutes to hours) compared to OSPF (seconds) ŌĆö this is by design for stability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MPLS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** MPLS # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Multiprotocol Label Switching (MPLS)** is a packet-forwarding technique standardized by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** in **RFC 3031 (2001)** that operates at a layer generally considered to lie between **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)** and **Layer 3 (Network Layer)** of the OSI reference model ŌĆö hence it is often described as a **"Layer 2.5" protocol**. Instead of making forwarding decisions by inspecting the full IP destination address (a long, variable-length operation), MPLS uses **short, fixed-length labels** (typically **32 bits**) appended to packets. A router performing MPLS (called a **Label Switch Router, LSR**) makes its entire forwarding decision based purely on the label value, enabling extremely fast and deterministic packet switching. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MPLS is *multiprotocol* because its label-switching mechanism is independent of the Layer 3 protocol ŌĆö it can carry IPv4, IPv6, IPX, AppleTalk, ATM, Frame Relay, and Ethernet payloads. This protocol-agnosticism is its defining architectural feature. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Highway Toll-Booth System" Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** on a national highway. You do not need to re-read the entire route map at every crossroad. Instead: - At the **city entry (LER ŌĆö Label Edge Router)**, an officer stamps a **"Toll Pass"** on your windshield with a number, say **"NH-44"**. This number represents the entire route. - At every **interchange (LSR ŌĆö Label Switch Router)**, the toll operator only needs to **read the pass number**, look it up in a small register, and direct you onward ŌĆö *no map reading required*. - When you approach the **destination city (exit LER)**, the officer **removes the sticker** (label pop) and hands you to local street navigation (conventional IP routing). In the same way, MPLS separates **"decide the route once"** (at the edge) from **"forward fast using a tag"** (in the core), giving both speed and predictability. > [!NOTE] > **Why MPLS was invented:** Traditional IP routing requires longest-prefix-match lookups in routing tables ŌĆö a process that grows increasingly slow and power-hungry at backbone speeds. MPLS reduces this to a single, constant-time table lookup. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU Exam) | Term | Standard Value / Definition | |---|---| | MPLS Label Length | **32 bits** total | | Label value field | **20 bits** (up to ~1.04 million labels) | | EXP / TC field | **3 bits** (for QoS / Class of Service) | | Bottom-of-Stack (S) bit | **1 bit** (0 = more labels, 1 = last label) | | TTL field | **8 bits** (identical semantics to IP TTL) | | MPLS Shim Header position | Between L2 header and L3 payload | | Reserved Label `0` | IPv4 Explicit NULL | | Reserved Label `1` | Router Alert | | Reserved Label `2` | IPv6 Explicit NULL | | Reserved Label `3` | Implicit NULL (PHP signal) | | Operating Layer | OSI Layer 2.5 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Plane Architecture of MPLS MPLS cleanly **separates** two functions that traditional IP routing combines: 1. **Control Plane** ŌĆö Runs routing protocols (OSPF, IS-IS) and a **label distribution protocol (LDP / RSVP-TE)**. Its job is to *build* the forwarding tables and exchange label-binding information between adjacent routers. 2. **Data Plane (Forwarding Plane)** ŌĆö Uses the label tables produced by the control plane to *forward* actual user traffic at line rate. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU:** "Control plane **decides**; data plane **delivers**." ## 2.2 Core MPLS Network Components | Component | Full Form | Role in Network | |---|---|---| | **LER** | Label Edge Router | Sits at MPLS network boundary. **Pushes** (imposes) the first label when a packet enters, and **pops** (removes) the last label when it leaves. | | **LSR** | Label Switch Router | Core router. Performs **label swap** operations based on incoming label + incoming interface. | | **LSP** | Label Switched Path | The unidirectional tunnel through the MPLS cloud from ingress LER to egress LER, following the label-switched route. | | **FEC** | Forwarding Equivalence Class | A group of packets (e.g., all packets to prefix `10.0.0.0/8`) that receive **identical forwarding treatment** along the same LSP. | | **LDP** | Label Distribution Protocol | RFC 5036 ŌĆö the standard signaling protocol that lets LSRs exchange label-to-FEC bindings. | ## 2.3 The MPLS Shim Header ŌĆö Bit-Level Anatomy When an IP packet enters an MPLS network, the ingress LER **inserts a 32-bit shim header** between the Layer 2 header and the Layer 3 (IP) header. The format (from MSB to LSB) is: | Label (20 bits) | EXP / TC (3 bits) | S (1 bit) | TTL (8 bits) | |---|---|---|---| - **Label (20 bits):** Index into the incoming label map (ILM) of the receiving LSR. - **EXP (3 bits):** Used for QoS marking, DiffServ PHB (Per-Hop Behavior) selection, and ECN. Also called **Traffic Class (TC)**. - **S ŌĆö Bottom of Stack (1 bit):** `1` means this is the *innermost* label; `0` means another label sits below it. Enables **MPLS label stacking** for VPNs and MPLS-TP. - **TTL (8 bits):** Decremented at every LSR hop. Prevents looping. At the egress LER, this value is *copied back* into the IP TTL field (if PHP was not performed). ## 2.4 The Three Fundamental Label Operations Every LSR can only perform one (or a combination) of three label actions: 1. **PUSH (Impose):** Insert a new label on top of the label stack. Performed by the **ingress LER**. 2. **SWAP:** Replace the top label with a new outgoing label and forward out the correct interface. Performed by **core LSRs**. 3. **POP (Dispose):** Remove the top label. Performed by the **egress LER** (or one hop earlier ŌĆö see PHP below). > [!NOTE] > **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP):** To avoid two label lookups at the egress, the LSR *immediately before* the egress LER pops the label and forwards the packet as a plain IP packet (using the `Implicit NULL` label `3` advertised by the egress LER). This optimizes the egress router's workload. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Expression / Rule | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | MPLS Header Size | $H_{MPLS} = 4 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ bits}$ | Constant overhead per label | | TTL Decrement | $TTL_{out} = TTL_{in} - 1$ | Same rule as IP TTL on every LSR | | Maximum Label Stack Depth | $D_{max} = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_{service}$ | Practical limit ~3ŌĆō4 due to MTU | | Label Value Range | $0 \le L \le 2^{20} - 1 = 1{,}048{,}575$ | `0` to `15` reserved by IANA | | EXP Field Range | $0 \le E \le 7$ | 8 QoS classes (0=BE, 7=NC) | | LSP Setup Time (LDP) | $T_{LSP} \approx T_{IGP-convergence}$ | No resource reservation | | LSP Setup Time (RSVP-TE) | $T_{LSP} \ge T_{IGP} + T_{reservation}$ | Supports Traffic Engineering | | Total Forwarding Time at LSR | $T_f = T_{lookup} + T_{switch}$ | Lookup is O(1) ŌĆö exact-match | | Effective Packet Size | $L_{eff} = L_{orig} + 4 \cdot k$ | $k$ = number of stacked labels | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-pitfall:** Use `\vert` in LaTeX instead of the pipe symbol `|` inside any markdown table to avoid breaking KTU's PDF rendering pipeline. ## 2.6 Why MPLS Matters in Real Engineering - **Speed:** Reduces the per-packet processing time at core routers from O(log n) (LPM trie walk) to O(1) (exact match). - **Traffic Engineering (TE):** With RSVP-TE, operators can force traffic along non-shortest paths to balance load ŌĆö impossible in pure OSPF. - **Layer 3 VPNs (L3VPN):** MPLS enables service providers to offer isolated virtual routing tables to enterprise customers (RFC 4364). - **Layer 2 VPNs (L2VPN / VPLS):** Ethernet frames transported across an MPLS backbone. - **QoS Differentiation:** EXP bits allow end-to-end QoS across heterogeneous Layer 2 networks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö End-to-End TTL Behavior Across an MPLS LSP **Problem context:** An IP packet enters an MPLS network with initial IP TTL = $T_0$. It traverses $n$ LSRs, and the egress LER finally forwards it as native IP. Compute the IP TTL seen by the final destination. **Step 1 ŌĆö Ingress LER action:** The ingress LER copies the IP TTL into the MPLS TTL field (preserving the value) and pushes the first label. $$TTL_{MPLS}^{(0)} = T_0$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Decrement at every core LSR:** For each hop $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \dots, n-1$ for non-egress LSRs), the standard TTL semantics apply: $$TTL_{MPLS}^{(i)} = TTL_{MPLS}^{(i-1)} - 1$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Egress LER action (No PHP case):** The egress LER pops the label and **copies** the MPLS TTL back into the IP TTL header. The final TTL the destination sees is: $$TTL_{IP}^{final} = TTL_{MPLS}^{(n-1)} - 1 = T_0 - n$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Egress LER action (With PHP case):** When the penultimate LSR pops the label and forwards a plain IP packet, the IP TTL is set by the penultimate hop's decrement: $$TTL_{IP}^{final} = T_0 - (n-1)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the difference matters for Traceroute:** The number of MPLS hops is *hidden* in the IP TTL when PHP is used. Tools like `MPLS LSP Ping` (RFC 8029) and the `router-alert` label (`1`) are required to expose internal LSRs to traceroute. **Summary expression:** $$ TTL_{IP}^{final} = \begin{cases} T_0 - n, & \text{if no PHP} \\ T_0 - (n-1), & \text{if PHP enabled} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Label Stack Depth vs. MTU Budget **Problem context:** An Ethernet interface has a standard MTU of **1500 bytes**. A core LSR is configured to push a *service label* on top of the transport label (typical for L3VPN). Determine the maximum safe label stack depth. **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify overheads:** - Original IP packet (pre-MPLS): $L_{IP}$ bytes - Transport label: $H_1 = 4$ bytes - Service label: $H_2 = 4$ bytes - Ethernet header: $H_{L2} = 14$ bytes (no VLAN), or $18$ bytes (with 802.1Q VLAN tag) **Step 2 ŌĆö Total frame size equation:** $$L_{frame} = H_{L2} + k \cdot 4 + L_{IP}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö MTU constraint:** $$L_{frame} \le MTU_{interface}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for maximum stack depth $k$:** $$ k \le \left\lfloor \frac{MTU_{interface} - H_{L2} - L_{IP}}{4} \right\rfloor $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical example (typical case):** Assume $MTU = 1500$, $H_{L2} = 18$ (VLAN-tagged), $L_{IP} = 1480$ (full-size IP packet before MPLS): $$ k \le \left\lfloor \frac{1500 - 18 - 1480}{4} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2}{4} \right\rfloor = 0 $$ This result is **negative** because the original packet already exceeds the MTU once we add 2 labels. The remedy is **MTU path discovery** (RFC 1191) or **MPLS MTU configuration** at each interface (commonly set to $1508$ or $1518$). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö MPLS vs. Conventional IP Routing | Performance Dimension | Conventional IP Routing | MPLS | |---|---|---| | Forwarding Decision Basis | Destination IP prefix (longest match) | Exact-match label value | | Lookup Complexity | $O(\log n)$ to $O(W)$ ŌĆö $W$=address width | $O(1)$ ŌĆö direct table index | | Per-Packet Header Modification | TTL only (and checksum recalc) | TTL + full label swap | | Path Determination | Distributed, hop-by-hop (OSPF/IS-IS) | Predetermined LSP (could be explicit) | | Traffic Engineering Support | Limited (equal-cost multi-path only) | Full (RSVP-TE with constraint-based routing) | | QoS Granularity | DSCP field in IP header | EXP bits in shim header + DSCP preservation | | VPN Scalability | Requires GRE/IPsec overlays | Native (L3VPN via RFC 4364) | | Loop Prevention Mechanism | TTL-based; OSPF/IS-IS SPF | TTL + LSP setup-time loop detection | | Failure Recovery | Sub-second (with BFD) | Sub-50 ms possible (with FRR ŌĆö Fast Reroute) | | Header Overhead | 0 extra bytes | 4 bytes per label | ## 3.4 Derivation 3 ŌĆö LSP Establishment Time Bound (LDP) LDP binds labels to FECs *after* the IGP has converged. Therefore, the **upper bound** for LSP setup latency is: $$ T_{LSP} \ge T_{IGP-converge} + T_{LDP-Hello} + T_{LDP-Label-Mapping} $$ A practical engineering estimate for a stable backbone: $$ T_{LSP} \approx 1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ seconds} $$ For traffic-engineered LSPs using RSVP-TE, the lower bound is: $$ T_{RSVP-TE} \ge T_{IGP-converge} + T_{CSPF-compute} + 2 \cdot T_{prop-max} + T_{RESV} $$ The factor of $2 \cdot T_{prop-max}$ exists because RSVP uses a **PATH message** (forward) followed by a **RESV message** (reverse reservation) before the LSP is operational. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level MPLS Network Architecture The diagram below illustrates the canonical MPLS topology referenced in every KTU textbook: a packet entering the cloud at the ingress LER, traversing core LSRs, and leaving at the egress LER. ```mermaid flowchart LR HOST_A["Host A\n10.1.0.0/24"]:::edge LER1["Ingress LER\nLER1\nPUSH Label 25"]:::ler LSR1["Core LSR1\nSWAP 25 to 47"]:::lsr LSR2["Core LSR2\nSWAP 47 to 33"]:::lsr LER2["Egress LER\nLER2\nPOP Label"]:::ler HOST_B["Host B\n10.2.0.0/24"]:::edge HOST_A -->|IP Packet| LER1 LER1 -->|MPLS Label 25| LSR1 LSR1 -->|MPLS Label 47| LSR2 LSR2 -->|MPLS Label 33| LER2 LER2 -->|Native IP| HOST_B classDef edge fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ler fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lsr fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 MPLS Shim Header ŌĆö Bit Layout The 32-bit shim header inserted between the Layer 2 and Layer 3 headers is structured as follows. ```mermaid block-beta columns 5 L1["Label\n20 bits"] L2["Label\ncont."] L3["EXP\n3 bits"] L4["S\n1 bit"] L5["TTL\n8 bits"] space:2 R1["Reserved\nbottom 32 bits of label"] R2["QoS\nClass"] R3["Stack\nMarker"] R4["Loop\nGuard"] style L1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style L2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style L3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32 style L4 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828 style L5 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0 ``` ## 4.3 End-to-End Label Lifecycle ŌĆö Push, Swap, Pop The lifecycle of a label as a packet travels through the MPLS cloud is shown below. The numbers on the links represent the **top-of-stack label value** the downstream router must read. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant EdgeIn as Ingress LER participant Core1 as Core LSR1 participant Core2 as Core LSR2 participant EdgeOut as Egress LER Note over EdgeIn: PUSH action
Insert Label 25 EdgeIn->>Core1: Label = 25 Note over Core1: SWAP action
Replace 25 with 47 Core1->>Core2: Label = 47 Note over Core2: SWAP action
Replace 47 with 33 Core2->>EdgeOut: Label = 33 Note over EdgeOut: POP action
Remove label EdgeOut-->>EdgeOut: Forward as native IP ``` ## 4.4 Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP) Flow In PHP, the label is removed *one hop before* the egress LER, allowing the egress router to perform only an IP lookup (not a label lookup). This is signaled using the **Implicit NULL label (value 3)**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ingress LER
PUSH label 25] --> B[Core LSR1
SWAP 25 to 47] B --> C[Core LSR2
SWAP 47 to 3 Implicit NULL] C --> D[Egress LER
Receives IP packet
No label lookup needed] style A fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style B fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style C fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style D fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD ``` ## 4.5 Label Distribution Protocol (LDP) ŌĆö Discovery & Session Setup LDP uses **UDP port 646** for Hello discovery and **TCP port 646** for reliable label exchange. The following diagram captures the four-message handshake. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant LSR_A as LSR A participant LSR_B as LSR B Note over LSR_A,LSR_B: Hello Discovery (UDP 646) LSR_A->>LSR_B: LDP Hello LSR_B->>LSR_A: LDP Hello Note over LSR_A,LSR_B: TCP Session Establishment LSR_A->>LSR_B: SYN LSR_B->>LSR_A: SYN-ACK LSR_A->>LSR_B: ACK Note over LSR_A,LSR_B: Label Binding Exchange LSR_A->>LSR_B: Label Mapping Message LSR_B->>LSR_A: Label Mapping Message ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define MPLS. List any four advantages of MPLS over conventional IP routing.** **Model Answer (Ōēł 80 words):** **Multiprotocol Label Switching (MPLS)** is a packet-forwarding technique defined in **RFC 3031** that uses short, fixed-length **32-bit labels** to make forwarding decisions, operating between Layer 2 and Layer 3 of the OSI model. **Four advantages:** 1. **Faster forwarding** ŌĆö O(1) exact-match lookup instead of longest-prefix match. 2. **Traffic Engineering** ŌĆö Explicit LSPs allow non-shortest-path routing. 3. **Native VPN support** ŌĆö L2VPN and L3VPN scale better than GRE/IPsec overlays. 4. **QoS differentiation** ŌĆö EXP bits enable end-to-end Class-of-Service. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Explain the role of the EXP field in the MPLS shim header. How many bits does it occupy?** **Model Answer (Ōēł 60 words):** The **EXP (Experimental) field**, also called the **Traffic Class (TC) field**, occupies the **3 bits** immediately preceding the Bottom-of-Stack bit in the 32-bit MPLS shim header. It is used to mark the QoS class of a packet (8 possible values, 0ŌĆō7), influencing **Per-Hop Behavior (PHB)** at each LSR ŌĆö typically mapped to DiffServ PHBs such as **EF (Expedited Forwarding)** and **AF (Assured Forwarding)** classes. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts are compulsory within the chosen question. You must answer **either Question A OR Question B** as per the internal choice rule. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of an MPLS network**. Identify and describe the role of LER and LSR. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **format of the MPLS shim header** with a neat sketch. Discuss the function of each field. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A **(a) MPLS Network Architecture:** An MPLS network consists of three logical components: 1. **Ingress Label Edge Router (Ingress LER):** The entry point into the MPLS cloud. It classifies incoming IP packets into **Forwarding Equivalence Classes (FECs)** and **pushes (imposes)** the initial MPLS label onto each packet. 2. **Transit Label Switch Routers (LSRs):** Core routers that perform **label swap** operations. They consult their **Incoming Label Map (ILM)** indexed by the top label and forward the packet out the corresponding interface with a new outgoing label. 3. **Egress Label Edge Router (Egress LER):** The exit point from the MPLS cloud. It **pops** the final label and forwards the packet as a native IP packet to its destination. **Control plane functions** (routing + LDP/RSVP) run in parallel with the data plane. The control plane populates forwarding tables, while the data plane uses them to switch packets at line rate. ``` Host A --- Ingress LER --- LSR1 --- LSR2 --- Egress LER --- Host B (PUSH) (SWAP) (SWAP) (POP) ``` **(b) MPLS Shim Header Format:** The 32-bit MPLS shim header sits between the L2 and L3 headers and is structured as: | Label (20 bits) | EXP (3 bits) | S (1 bit) | TTL (8 bits) | - **Label (20 bits):** The identifier used for the exact-match lookup in the ILM. Values `0`ŌĆō`15` are reserved (e.g., `0` = IPv4 Explicit NULL, `3` = Implicit NULL for PHP). - **EXP (3 bits):** Traffic Class / QoS marking. Copied from or mapped to the IP DSCP field. - **S ŌĆö Bottom of Stack (1 bit):** `1` indicates this is the innermost label; `0` indicates another label is stacked below. Enables **label stacking** (used in MPLS VPNs). - **TTL (8 bits):** Prevents forwarding loops. Decremented at each hop. On egress (or PHP), the value is restored to the IP TTL header. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **LDP (Label Distribution Protocol)**. How does it differ from RSVP-TE? **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)** mechanism in MPLS. What is the role of the Implicit NULL label? --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B **(a) LDP ŌĆö Label Distribution Protocol:** **LDP**, standardized in **RFC 5036**, is the primary MPLS signaling protocol used to establish label bindings between LSRs. It operates in four phases: 1. **Discovery** ŌĆö LSRs send **LDP Hello** messages over **UDP port 646** to discover neighbors. 2. **Session Establishment** ŌĆö A reliable **TCP session on port 646** is established between peers. 3. **Label Advertisement** ŌĆö When an LSR assigns a label to an FEC, it distributes the binding to its peers using **Label Mapping** messages. 4. **Withdrawal** ŌĆö When a binding becomes invalid, an LSR sends a **Label Withdrawal** message. LDP is **downstream-unsolicited** by default and follows the IGP's best path. It does **not** support constraint-based routing or explicit path setup. **LDP vs. RSVP-TE:** | Parameter | LDP | RSVP-TE | |---|---|---| | Standard | RFC 5036 | RFC 3209 | | Path Selection | IGP shortest path only | Constraint-based (CSPF) | | Resource Reservation | No | Yes (bandwidth, affinities) | | Transport | TCP/UDP | Raw IP (protocol 46) | | Traffic Engineering | Limited | Native support | | Failure Recovery | IGP convergence | Fast Reroute (FRR) < 50 ms | **(b) Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP):** PHP is an **optimization technique** in which the **second-to-last LSR** (penultimate hop) of an LSP pops the label, instead of leaving the task to the egress LER. This saves the egress LER from having to perform a *second* lookup (label ŌåÆ IP) ŌĆö it receives a plain IP packet and performs a single, native IP forwarding decision. **Mechanism:** 1. The egress LER advertises the **Implicit NULL label (value 3)** for each FEC it owns. 2. The penultimate LSR, upon receiving a packet with label = 3, recognizes the special meaning. 3. It **pops** the label, **decrements the IP TTL by one** (since it still counts as a hop), and forwards the resulting IP packet to the egress LER. 4. The egress LER performs only an IP routing lookup. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write that the egress LER also performs a TTL decrement *in addition to* the penultimate hop. **Correct rule:** The penultimate hop decrements the MPLS TTL by 1 and copies that value directly into the IP TTL. The egress LER then performs a **standard IP TTL decrement** exactly as for any IP packet. Net effect on the destination's TTL: **$T_0 - n$** where $n$ is the total number of MPLS hops (including the penultimate hop). Failing to state both decrements explicitly costs **2 marks** in KTU valuations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Must-Know for KTU 2024 Scheme Exam:** - **MPLS** = Layer 2.5 protocol, defined in **RFC 3031**, uses **32-bit labels**. - The shim header has exactly four fields: **Label (20) + EXP (3) + S (1) + TTL (8)**. - **LER** sits at the edge (Push/Pop); **LSR** sits in the core (Swap). - **LSP** is the unidirectional path through the MPLS cloud; **FEC** groups packets getting identical treatment. - **LDP** (RFC 5036) is the default signaling protocol; **RSVP-TE** (RFC 3209) is for traffic engineering. - **PHP** uses **Implicit NULL label (value 3)** advertised by the egress LER. - Reserved labels: `0` IPv4 Explicit NULL, `1` Router Alert, `2` IPv6 Explicit NULL, `3` Implicit NULL. - **EXP bits** are copied/mapped to/from IP **DSCP** for QoS continuity. - **MPLS TTL = 8 bits**, decremented at every LSR, copied into IP TTL on egress (or at PHP hop). - **Stack depth** typically $\le 3$ŌĆō$4$ due to MTU constraints; total label overhead = $4k$ bytes for $k$ labels. - **MPLS supports** L2VPN (VPLS, VPWS), L3VPN (RFC 4364), Traffic Engineering, and DiffServ QoS. - **LDP** uses UDP/TCP port **646**; **RSVP** uses IP protocol number **46**. - **LSP types:** Static (manually configured) vs. Dynamic (signaled via LDP/RSVP-TE). - **Loop prevention:** TTL + LDP loop-detection mechanism (max label hop count). - **Comparison keywords for KTU essays:** *exact-match, O(1), shim header, LSP, FEC, LDP, RSVP-TE, PHP, EXP, LSP Ping (RFC 8029).* > [!WARNING] > **Top 3 KTU Mark-Loss Traps:** > 1. Confusing **LDP** with **LSP** ŌĆö they are *not* the same. LDP is the *protocol*; LSP is the *path*. > 2. Forgetting that **PHP hop still decrements the TTL** by 1 (it counts as a regular hop). > 3. Writing "MPLS is a Layer 3 protocol" ŌĆö it is a **Layer 2.5** protocol. Examiners deduct for this.

topics.title: Firewalls and Their Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT302 - COMPUTER NETWORKS & PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Firewalls and Their Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Firewall?

A **firewall** is a network security device (hardware, software, or a combination of both) that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's previously established security policies. It acts as a **barrier** between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (such as the Internet), controlling the flow of data packets according to predefined security rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (RFC 4949 / KTU Standard):**
> A firewall is a *security gateway* that enforces the security policy of an organization between two or more networks by controlling the passage of traffic based on a set of rules. It is the first line of defense in any network perimeter security architecture.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a firewall as a **security guard at the entrance of a gated apartment complex**:

- **Residents (trusted internal users)** carry identity cards and can pass freely.
- **Visitors (incoming packets)** are stopped at the gate.
- The guard checks a **rulebook (access control list)** ŌĆö *"Is the visitor on the approved list? Is the delivery truck allowed at this hour? Is this parcel suspicious?"*
- Based on the rules, the guard **allows, denies, or inspects** the visitor before letting them in.
- The guard also **logs** who entered and who was turned away.

In the same way, a firewall examines every packet entering or leaving the network, compares it against a **set of rules (security policy)**, and decides whether to **forward (ACCEPT)** or **drop (DROP/REJECT)** the packet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> A firewall is NOT an anti-virus, NOT an intrusion detection system (IDS), and NOT encryption. It is purely a **traffic-filtering mechanism** operating at various layers of the OSI model.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Firewalls? (Goals of a Firewall)

The primary goals of a firewall are summarized below:

1. **Traffic Filtering** ŌĆō Block unauthorized or unwanted traffic from entering the private network.
2. **Access Control** ŌĆō Enforce "least privilege" by restricting users to only the services they need.
3. **Prevention of IP Spoofing** ŌĆō Drop packets that claim to originate from internal IP addresses but arrive from outside.
4. **Defense Against DoS / DDoS Attacks** ŌĆō Rate-limit or block traffic floods targeting internal servers.
5. **Logging and Auditing** ŌĆō Maintain logs of all access attempts for forensic analysis.
6. **Network Address Translation (NAT)** ŌĆō Hide internal IP address topology from the outside world.
7. **Policy Enforcement** ŌĆō Implement a centralized, organization-wide security policy.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Firewalls are deployed in every enterprise, data center, cloud VPC (e.g., AWS Security Groups, Azure NSG), and even in personal computers (e.g., Windows Defender Firewall, iptables in Linux).

## 1.3 Limitations of a Firewall

A firewall **cannot** protect against:

- Attacks that **bypass** it (e.g., a modem dial-in inside the network).
- **Internal threats** (a malicious employee with authorized access).
- **Application-layer logic flaws** in allowed protocols (e.g., a permitted HTTP request carrying SQL injection).
- **Social engineering** attacks (users tricked into revealing passwords).
- **Fully encrypted traffic** that it cannot inspect without deep-packet inspection capability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Types of Firewalls

## 2.1 Classification of Firewalls

Firewalls can be broadly classified based on **how they inspect traffic** and **at which OSI layer they operate**. The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on the following five categories:

### 2.1.1 Packet Filtering Firewall (Stateless)

**Operational Layer:** OSI Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport).

A packet-filtering firewall examines each packet **individually** (in isolation) against a set of Access Control List (ACL) rules. It inspects the header fields such as:

- Source IP Address
- Destination IP Address
- Source Port
- Destination Port
- Protocol type (TCP / UDP / ICMP)
- ACK / SYN flag bits (for TCP)

**How it works ŌĆö step by step:**

1. The firewall receives a packet at its interface.
2. It parses the **header fields** (Layer 3 + Layer 4).
3. The header is matched sequentially against each rule in the ACL (top-down).
4. The first matching rule decides the action: **ACCEPT**, **DROP**, or **REJECT**.
5. If no rule matches, the default policy (usually DROP) is applied.

**Advantages:**

- Very **fast** (low latency) ŌĆö only header inspection, no payload processing.
- **Cheap** and easy to implement (e.g., Linux `iptables`, Cisco ACLs).
- Transparent to the end user.

**Disadvantages:**

- **Stateless** ŌĆö cannot correlate multiple packets of the same session.
- Easily fooled by **IP spoofing** (forged source addresses).
- Cannot inspect **application-layer payload** (vulnerable to application attacks).
- Logs are minimal and difficult to interpret for forensics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Rule Set (ACL):**
> - `permit tcp 192.168.1.0/24 any eq 80` ŌåÆ Allow internal LAN to browse any web server.
> - `deny ip any any` ŌåÆ Block everything else (default deny).
